He wrote on Truth Social: “The People of Aberdeen, in Scotland, are dancing in the streets because the new Prime Minister, Andy Burnham, has stated that he will be opening up, all the way, the invaluable North Sea Oil!”Labour’s deputy leader, Lucy Powell, had earlier said Burnham would take a more pragmatic approach and that it was a “change of emphasis” rather than a change of policy.Burnham’s greater openness on North Sea oil and gas was one factor in Miliband falling from favour as frontrunner to be chancellor, given his opposition to more drilling as energy secretary.He is being tipped instead for foreign secretary and could take a more pro-Gaza position and greater steps towards setting the UK on a pathway towards restoring foreign aid to 0.7% of national income from its current level of 0.2%. The idea was immediately seized on by Reform UK. Its leader, Nigel Farage, said: “Andy Burnham plans to spend an extra £10bn of your money on foreign aid. I will always put British people first.”Burnham’s team also declined to be drawn on his plans for Thames Water after the Sunday Times reported he was expected to push the London and south-east England utility company into a special form of administration in an intervention that could mean the state has to pay some of its running costs until a buyer is found.Powell spoke on Sunday about Burnham’s plans for increased public ownership, saying the economy had been “overly privatised” and the “utilities and essentials of life don’t work in the interest of ordinary people.”She said bills were “going up and up because they are run in the interests of private interest, not for the consumers or for taxpayers”.The Conservatives have accused Burnham of planning to put up taxes in order to fund areas that will need more money, from social care to defence. When asked about a wealth tax, the new Labour leader said he did not want to create divisions, but that in the future an option “might be having to ask for a little more”.Kemi Badenoch criticised Burnham on Sunday for not setting out his plans before Monday and not having done in-depth media interviews before taking office, describing his comments so far as “airy-fairy stuff”.The Conservative leader told the BBC she thought Burnham was “a people pleaser” rather than someone who would make tough decisions.As he prepared to hand over power, Keir Starmer released a video saying he was “so proud” to have left the country “in a much better state than we found it two years ago”.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamLabourLabour party leadershipnewsShareReuse this content

title: Rise in overseas surrogates ‘increases risk of stateless babies’ | Society | The Guardian
description: Calls grow for global regulation as rising numbers of westerners use surrogates in countries with different laws
Author: Jessica Murray
Headline: Calls grow for global regulation as rising numbers of westerners use surrogates in countries with different laws
Content: Surrogates normally carry pregnancies from donor eggs or the egg of someone who wishes to become a parent.Composite: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSurrogates normally carry pregnancies from donor eggs or the egg of someone who wishes to become a parent.Composite: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesSocietyRise in overseas surrogates ‘increases risk of stateless babies’Calls grow for global regulation as rising numbers of westerners use surrogates in countries with different lawsWhy Mexico is at the heart of ethical debate over global surrogacyJessica MurrayandRachel HallSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe rise in the use of surrogates abroad is leaving more babies at risk of becoming stateless, experts have said, amid growing calls for urgent global regulation of the practice.The warning follows the suspension of a 15-year effort by The Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH) to establish a global surrogacy convention, which was paused due to divisions among member states.With more older women, gay couples and people affected by infertility seeking to become parents, increasing numbers of babies are being born to surrogates around the world. Surrogates normally carry pregnancies from a donor egg or the egg of someone who wishes to become a parent.Laws differ from country to country, with some such as Italy banning the practice outright, while others, including some US states, allow commercial surrogacy. The UK only allows “altruistic” surrogacy, where surrogates cannot be paid more than their expenses from carrying the pregnancy.UK surrogacy agencies argue that an outdated legal framework at home is pushing couples to seek surrogates abroad, where regulations to protect surrogates from exploitation can be less robust or do not exist at all. Anti-surrogacy campaigners say the practice is fundamentally abusive in all cases and are pushing for a global ban.Prof Michael Hellner, who chaired the HCCH working group, said hugely differing opinions on the ethics of surrogacy between the member states involved meant the project to create a global framework on legal parentage in surrogacy cases had proved impossible.“I invested 10 years of my life into this project. But if there’s no political will, there’s absolutely no point in us spending more of people’s time and money,” he said. “The lack of coordination between states on this issue leads to problems for the children, and the risk of statelessness is increasing.”Hellner said the patchwork of cross-border surrogacy arrangements put babies at risk of “limping parentage” – where a child has different legal parents in different countries – and statelessness.Experts said statelessness could happen in cases where a country does not automatically grant nationality to a child born there, and where surrogacy is not legally recognised in either the country of birth or the child’s final destination.View image in fullscreenThere is no data on the number of births by surrogacy globally, but they have increased rapidly in the UK over the past decade.Photograph: Andrew Matthews/PAHellner said the project had proved difficult when some countries were “violently opposed to anything that could be seen as legitimising surrogacy, which they think is a violation of human rights and dignity”.There is no data on the number of births by surrogacy globally, but they have increased rapidly in the UK over the past decade, doubling since 2015 to more than 981 in 2025.Most of these children were born abroad, mostly in the US, Ukraine, Nigeria, Georgia, Colombia and Mexico. British people using surrogates abroad to have babies are forced to apply to the UK family courts for a parental order to become the legal parents of their child and, in some cases, also need to apply for British citizenship for the child.Maud de Boer-Buquicchio, the president of the Geneva-based nonprofit Child Identity Protection and a former UN special rapporteur on the sale and sexual exploitation of children, said the HCCH was a missed opportunity to have explicit international standards on the recognition of legal parentage in surrogacy, which can be a requirement for establishing nationality.“There are lots of situations where these children are just left in a limbo and that affects their legal status, which is something that is crucial to having an identity,” she said. “The differences in legislation between different countries is still a big, big puzzle.”The subject of international surrogacy came to prominence this week when Jens Spahn, a former German health minister and ally of the chancellor, Friedrich Merz,was forced to resignhis position in his party after revealing he and his husband used a surrogate mother to become parents – a practice he had criticised in the past and his party is vehemently opposed to.Member states involved in the HCCH included the US, UK, Australia and Canada, where surrogacy is permitted in some form, as well as France, Germany, Italy and Spain, which have heavily restricted or banned surrogacy domestically.Bhaskar Mishra, a child protection specialist at Unicef, said the UN agency was pushing for better guidelines to protect the rights of children in international surrogacy.“If the government doesn’t issue a passport in either the country where the baby is born, or where it is going home to, then there are chances the baby will become stateless,” he said. “That’s a serious risk in cases of international surrogacy agreements, and numbers are growing, and growing very fast.”In 2025 a senior UK judge issued a warning about risky surrogacy arrangements abroad after a couple faced a four-year legal battle to bring two children born via surrogacy to Ukrainian women in Cyprus back to the UK, during which time they were essentially stateless.The then president of the family division, Andrew McFarlane, said the case should act as a warning that courts may refuse to grant a parental order in cases where a child was born via commercial surrogacy abroad and this may leave the child “permanently stateless and legally parentless”.Last year, Denmark was forced to change its laws to allow legal parentage recognition in foreign surrogacy arrangements, after the European court of human rights ruled that preventing this violated children’s rights.The UN’s special rapporteur on violence against women and girls, Reem Alsalem, has called for surrogacy to be abolished globally, calling it a “system of violence, exploitation and abuse against women”.Alsalem said women and girls who were “impoverished, destitute and affected by crisis and conflict” were more likely to be exploited by surrogacy arrangements, and commissioning individuals usually came from “western wealthier states”.“The vulnerability to exploitation and abuse through surrogacy would not be possible if it were not for the existence of demand by commissioning individuals, who are usually from western wealthier states, a dynamic that reinforces colonial and discriminatory practices,” she said.“There is a push to normalise and legalise surrogacy worldwide, given that it is lucrative and there are many strong and well-financed powerful lobbies behind it.”View image in fullscreenThere have been calls from some to ban surrogacy as a practice globally.Photograph: Danny Lawson/PAMarie-Josèphe Devillers, who runs the International Coalition for the Abolition of Surrogate Motherhood (CIAMS), said she worked with surrogates who regretted their decision, and said they felt “cheated, lied to and exploited”.One woman was a victim of human trafficking from Latin America to France, and another, from the US, said her child was sold to people other than those she had believed to be the intended parents. Devillers said she had also looked at cases where women had experienced health complications during pregnancy or birth, including some deaths.Michael Johnson-Ellis, the founder of MySurrogacyJourney, which facilitates surrogacy in the UK, US and Mexico, said as long as it was done ethically and with proper regulation, international surrogacy worked well.“People should be allowed to make a choice on where and how they do surrogacy, providing it’s safe and there’s no risk of people being exploited or coerced,” he said. “The wait time is about two years for a UK surrogate, and for some people who have been on a lengthy journey with infertility, that time might be something they don’t have.”He said his agency only recommended the US and Mexico for parents seeking surrogates abroad, and was vocal in advising people on where to avoid. “We see the horror stories through the high courts of people being stuck in places like Cyprus or Georgia or Ukraine, or some of these emerging destinations that clearly are exploitative and dangerous,” he said.Rachel Westbury runs Nappy Endings, a UK-based surrogacy agency, and has been a surrogate four times since 2011. She said desperate parents were increasingly looking abroad for surrogates but the number of suitable countries was narrowing.“People need to stop thinking that it’s just celebrities doing it and glamourising it. It’s not just for millionaires. We have people who remortgage their home to do it,” she said. “It’s a privilege to be a surrogate. If more people knew the magic of surrogacy, when it’s done right, more surrogates would come forward.”Explore more on these topicsSocietySurrogacyChildrenParents and parentingFamilynewsShareReuse this content

title: Pay gap widens as UK bosses get 130 times average worker’s salary | Executive pay and bonuses | The Guardian
description: In its final report the High Pay Centre says the median remuneration of a FTSE 100 CEO has risen to a record £5.06m
Author: Mark Sweney
Headline: In its final report the High Pay Centre says the median remuneration of a FTSE 100 CEO has risen to a record £5.06m
Content: AstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot was the FTSE 100’s highest-paid boss last year, receiving £17.7m. The median pay for a full-time UK worker is £39,000.Photograph: Carlos Osorio/ReutersView image in fullscreenAstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot was the FTSE 100’s highest-paid boss last year, receiving £17.7m. The median pay for a full-time UK worker is £39,000.Photograph: Carlos Osorio/ReutersExecutive pay and bonusesPay gap widens as UK bosses get 130 times average worker’s salaryIn its final report the High Pay Centre says the median remuneration of a FTSE 100 CEO has risen to a record £5.06mMark SweneySun 19 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe bosses of Britain’s largest listed companies received record pay last year, fuelling the widest earnings gap with workers in eight years.Median pay forFTSE100 chief executives hit £5.06m in the last financial year, according to data released by the High Pay Centre, an 8.6% increase on £4.66m the previous year and the highest level on record.Executive remuneration has been rising steadily since the pandemic, when CEOs took pay and bonus cuts as the lockdowns affected business performance.Analysis found that listed company bosses are now paid 130 times the salary of the average full-time UK worker, up from 124 times in the previous financial year. It is the biggest gap since the ratio hit 137 in the year to the end of March 2018.TheHigh Pay Centresaid FTSE 100 firms had spent £856.6m on pay during the last financial year, including £550m remunerating chief executives. The thinktank, founded in 2011 by Deborah Hargreaves, a former Guardian business editor,is to close after 15 years campaigning for fairer pay for workers.Graph showing Median FTSE 100 CEO pay is now 130 times that of the median UK full-time workerIts interim director, Andrew Speke, said: “The substantial growth in the gap between executive and worker pay in the last year should be a wake-up call to those who’ve turned a blind eye to rising executive pay.“We hope that a change in prime minister and a renewed focus on economic fairness will lead to economic inequality and corporate excess returning up the political agenda.”This thinktank exposed fat cats and obscenely high pay. Guess what has happened to it? | Polly ToynbeeRead moreAndy Burnham, who will become the prime minister on Monday, has previously said thereneeds to be a public debateabout high and excessive pay, and has promised to give“breathing space”as soon as he can to families struggling with the cost of living.The median pay for a full-time UK worker is £39,000, according to the UK’s Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings, based on estimates in October.The amount paid out to FTSE 100 executives has dropped to £857m,from £1bn in the previous report, while mean pay fell from £6.09m to £5.89m.The High Pay Centre said this fall was the result of theexceptionally high pay award of £212mhanded out by the engineering firm Melrose Industries the previous year, as executives reaped the rewards after itbought the aerospace and automotive group GKN for £8bnin a hostile takeover in 2018.Sixty-six of the 94 large listed companies in the analysis increased their chief executive’s pay package from the previous year.The mean long-term incentive payment (LTIP) increased by a fifth to £2.7m, and the mean short-term incentive payment (STIP) rose 14% to £1.8m.Pascal Soriot, chief executive of the pharmaceutical company AstraZeneca, was the FTSE 100’s highest-paid boss last year, taking home £17.7m.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSoriot has been the highest-paid head of a listed company for three of the last four years. He wasdisplaced by the bosses of Melrosein the previous report.list showing FTSE 100 companies with the highest CEO pay in 2025-26GSK’s Emma Walmsley is the only woman in the top 10, although she stood down as boss of the pharmaceutical company at the beginning of the year. Walmsley surged into second place thanks to anear-50% pay rise to £15.6min her final year in charge.CS Venkatakrishnan, chief executive of Barclays, was paid £15m last year after EU rules limiting bonus pay were scrapped for UK banks. It was the largest package for a Barclays boss since Bob Diamond took home £17m in 2011.The top five earners last year were rounded out by Shell’s boss, Wael Sawan, whoreceived a 60% pay rise to £13.7m, despite a slump in profits, and the chief executive of Standard Chartered, Bill Winters, who received £12.7m.The HighPayCentre argues that “excessive spending” on bosses by large listed companies comes at the expense of pay increases for the rest of the workforce.The thinktank is calling for reforms to the regulations that govern the pay-setting process companies follow, including a “fat-cat tax”, appointing two workers as board directors, and the full implementation of Labour’s employment rights bill, which includes measures under which employers must inform their workers of their trade union rights.Speke said: “As our findings show, this is the fourth year in a row that FTSE 100 executive pay has risen, and this growth is starting to substantially outstrip growth in worker pay.“A failure to tackle such disproportionate and inefficient levels of inequality will only further reduce faith in our current economic model and help to accelerate the rise of rightwing populism.”Explore more on these topicsExecutive pay and bonusesFTSEPayFamily financesWork & careersUK cost of living crisisInequalitynewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New York  | Zohran Mamdani | The Guardian
description: NYC mayor says in Times interview that’s he’s in ‘active conversation’ with city’s law department about the matter
Author: Lucy Campbell
Headline: NYC mayor says in Times interview that’s he’s in ‘active conversation’ with city’s law department about the matter
Content: Mayor Zohran Mamdani says on The Interview: ‘Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do.’Photograph: Camara Porter/AdMedia/MediaPunch/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenMayor Zohran Mamdani says on The Interview: ‘Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do.’Photograph: Camara Porter/AdMedia/MediaPunch/ShutterstockZohran Mamdani‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New YorkNYC mayor says in Times interview that’s he’s in ‘active conversation’ with city’s law department about the matterLucy CampbellSun 19 Jul 2026 11.38 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 11.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNew York City mayorZohran Mamdanihas said he was reviewing whether his administration could arrestBenjamin Netanyahuif as expected the Israeli prime minister visits the city for the UN general assembly in September.Mamdani’s comments on Saturday’s episode of the New York Times’sthe Interview podcastechoed ones he had made to the publication as he successfully ran for mayor last fall.“I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said, referring to the Dutch city that is home to the international criminal court, whichissuedan arrest warrant for Netanyahu in November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza. “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.”Mamdani said it was unclear to him whether he has the legal authority to order theNew Yorkpolice department (NYPD), which he oversees, to detain a foreign leader like Netanyahu. But he said he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s law department about the matter.“Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” Mamdani added. “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.”Thewarrantwhich the ICC issued for the arrest of Netanyahu and other Israeli leaders accused them of war crimes and crimes against humanity inGaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”.During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani characterized Israel’s military actions in Gazaas a genocideand Netanyahu as a war criminal,telling the New York Timesin September that he would order NYPD to arrest Netanyahu in the event he ever set foot there. He alsocondemnedthe 7 October 2023 Hamas attacks on Israel as a “horrific war crime”.The Times report back then cited legal experts who judged that having Netanyahu arrested “would be a practical impossibility” for Mamdani – and could bring him into direct conflict with the US’s federal government.The US is not a party to the ICC and does not recognize its authority. And in February 2025, early in his presidency,Donald Trumpordered economic sanctions against the court, maintaining that it lacked jurisdiction over the US or Israel.Trumps’s US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, then recentlyvowed to “dismantle” the court, accusing it of “waging a war against our country, not with bullets or missiles” but with “the force of so-called international law”.Netanyahu, who facesa tough re-election campaignin October, brushed off Mamdani’s arrest threats in a recent radio appearance, saying he was not concerned.Though Mamdani denies the accusation, Netanyahu also alleged Mamdani supports Hamas.“He’s with the terror actors,” Netanyahu toldNew York’s WABC. “And I think the problem is that he doesn’t recognize and he doesn’t care that those who hate the Jews and Israel ultimately hate America.”Israel’s ambassador to the UN, Danny Danon, accused Mamdani – New York’s first Muslim mayor – of failing to confront rising antisemitism in his city. Mamdani has countered such accusations by noting that he has increased funding for an office to combat hate crimes.“Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu will come to New York, address the United Nations General Assembly with pride, and stand before the world to state Israel’s truth and its unwavering right to defend its citizens,” Danonsaid.Trump’s ambassador to the UN, Mike Waltz, alsodismissedMamdani’s threat as “pure political theater”, saying the US is not a member of the ICC.World leaders from United Nations member-states are expected to travel to New York in September for the body’s annual session. Netanyahu has often participated in this event in previous years.“We’re not talking about a personal assessment ofBenjamin Netanyahu,” Mamdani told the Times’ podcast. “We’re talking about the international criminal court and the fact that they have a warrant out for this prime minister’s arrest, and I think it’s important to talk about the weight that that has as a charge and that also, as the mayor of New York City, I’ll be following the laws of New York City.”Mamdani’s comments on Saturday came as his stance on Israel isbecoming less fringewithin the Democratic party.Close to half of US House Democrats on Wednesdayvotedto strip $3.3bn in military aid to Israel. It was not enough to pass the measure. But it signifieda buckling of unconditional supportfor Israel, a shift that is alsoreflected in public opinionpolling.Asked by the Times about the political weight he places on Israel and support for it by the US, Mamdani said ongoing war in Gaza was motivating voters throughout the country, including whenhis endorsed candidates swept House racesin New York in June.“It is hard to find a more bankrupt policy approach than what our country has done to Gaza and to Palestine,” he said.Explore more on these topicsZohran MamdaniNew YorkBenjamin NetanyahuUS politicsIsraelMiddle East and north AfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: Nigel Farage admits George Cottrell paid for filming and let him use his home | Nigel Farage | The Guardian
description: Reform leader says support from fraudster before last election is ‘totally undeclarable in every single way’
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: Reform leader says support from fraudster before last election is ‘totally undeclarable in every single way’
Content: Nigel Farage with George Cottrell (right in dark glasses) in 2019.Photograph: Mark Kerrison/AlamyView image in fullscreenNigel Farage with George Cottrell (right in dark glasses) in 2019.Photograph: Mark Kerrison/AlamyNigel FarageNigel Farage admits George Cottrell paid for filming and let him use his homeReform leader says support from fraudster before last election is ‘totally undeclarable in every single way’Rowena MasonWhitehall editorSun 19 Jul 2026 08.21 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNigel Farage has admitted his close friend the fraudster George Cottrell let him use one of his London homes and paid for social media filming before the last election but insisted it was “totally undeclarable in every single way”.TheReform UKleader spoke in depth for the first time about his support from Cottrell and the £5m gift from the Thailand-based businessman Christopher Harborne in an interview with the anti-woke Triggernometry podcast.He acknowledged the parliamentary standards system could find against him but claimed he had sought advice from a “top international lawyer” who told him the £5m did not need to be declared because it was unconditional.In relation to Cottrell, Farage said his friend had given him nothing since he was elected in July 2024 and that financial support before then was “irrelevant” and unrelated to politics.The Reform UK leader also claimed his party was being unfairly targeted over a £500,000 donation to the party from Cottrell’s mother, Fiona, which is under investigation by the police over whether she is the true source of the funding.However, in an apparent slip of the tongue, Farage referred to it as being a donation from Cottrell himself, before correcting himself to say it was from his mother, as he made the case that it was a conspiracy against the party.“The idea that his donation – it was his mother’s donation – this was reported to the NCA [National Crime Agency] in September 2024. The police followed up in the spring of 2025. Why is it now a story? It’s now a story because everything is being coordinated together to try and knock us out of the game. That’s why I’m fighting this byelection. I’m saying: let the people decide.”Farage’s support in kind from Cottrell and the £5m from Harborne just before the last election are currently under investigation by the standards commissioner over whether they should have been publicly registered.Financial support has to be declared within 12 months of an MP being elected if it has any link to their political activities.Although the standards commissioner has not yet reported back, Farage has called a byelection in his constituency of Clacton to let voters decide if they want him as MP, although it is being boycotted by the major parties. He could face a second byelection if the commissioner finds against him and the penalty is enough to trigger a recall petition.On the financial support he took from Cottrell before July 2024, Farage told the podcast: “At that period of my life, from 2021 to 3 June 2024, I was a broadcaster, I was someone that appeared on celebrity TV programmes, I was somebody that promoted some financial market products, I was an influencer, I had a considerable 5 million following, so the fact that George did me a favour with those things is not a matter of public interest or record. It’s irrelevant. I wasn’t in politics, it’s literally no one’s business and it is totally undeclarable in every single way.”He also defended his decision to stay loyal to Cottrell, who is a member of his inner circle and who Farage described as an “enormously colourful character”.On Cottrell’s conviction and imprisonment for fraud in the US in 2017, Farage said: “George, as a young man, got into trouble in America. If you are involved in tax avoidance schemes, it is very easy in America to get into very, very big trouble.”Reform UK is also under scrutiny over £1m sent by Fiona Cottrell before the last election to Britain Means Business, a company controlled by the Reform UK deputy leader, Richard Tice. Of this, £500,000 was later sent to Reform UK by Britain Means Business.The Guardian has previously reported that Fiona Cottrell did not send the money straight to the Britain Means Business account. It was routed via a UK bank account of an Australian money exchange platform called Oneify.The Sunday Timesreported this weekendthat George Cottrell opened an account with a money transfer firm called Oneify in May 2024.Asked this month if Cottrell transferred money to his mother before she made donations to Reform, his lawyers at Carter-Ruck said: “Our client has confirmed his instructions that any donations by his mother have been entirely her own decision.” The lawyers added: “He therefore declines to engage with your [wider] questions.”In a previous letter to the Guardian, the lawyers stated that “his mother’s donations have been entirely her own decision, and are a matter for her”. They added that suggestions he “has donated impermissibly to Reform UK are unfounded”. Fiona Cottrell has declined to speak to the media about the donations.Explore more on these topicsNigel FarageReform UKnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘What has the BBC ever done for me?’ Romesh Ranganathan reboots corporation’s 1985 licence fee plea | BBC licence fee | The Guardian
description: As with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David Jason
Author: Tara Conlan
Headline: As with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David Jason
Content: Romesh Ranganathan in the Queen Vic with punters including David Jason, Adam Woodyatt  and Sue Perkins.Photograph: BBCView image in fullscreenRomesh Ranganathan in the Queen Vic with punters including David Jason, Adam Woodyatt  and Sue Perkins.Photograph: BBCBBC licence fee‘What has the BBC ever done for me?’ Romesh Ranganathan reboots corporation’s 1985 licence fee pleaAs with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David JasonTara ConlanSun 19 Jul 2026 12.02 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJohn Cleese’s 1985 advert for the BBC in which he asks a pub full of stars “What has the BBC ever given us?” is one of the most famous examples of the corporation making the case to the public for the value of paying the licence fee.In the advert, Cleese is complaining to the bartender that he has just bought his TV licence. “Fifty-eight quid for the privilege of sitting in your own home, watching your own television set. It’s diabolical. I mean, what’s the BBC ever given us for 58 quid?” he says, before being put right by a string of punters, ranging from David Attenborough and John Humphrys to Terry Wogan and David Jason.Now, over 40 years later, as the corporation cranks up its campaign for the charter that allows it to operate to be renewed, it has rebooted the advert with anew musical versionfronted by Romesh Ranganathan and directed by Dougal Wilson, the man behind Paddington in Peru and the John Lewis Christmas ad campaigns.The comedian stars as a disgruntled licence fee payer grumbling in EastEnders’ Queen Vic pub to a host of household names including Chris Martin, Claudia Winkleman, David Jason and Greg James.View image in fullscreenClaudia Winkleman pulls a pint of The Traitor’s Ale.Photograph: BBCThe comedian then has a whistle-stop song and dance tour of the BBC’s shows and services, featuring about 90 famous faces, from Alan Carr to Cate Blanchett, from Hey Duggee to Clive Myrie.The BBC faces what its director general, Matt Brittin, described recently as“a moment of real jeopardy”due to a decline in licence fee payments and the rise of rivals such as YouTube, TikTok and the streamers. The number of people paying the BBC’s licence fee fell faster than expected in the last year, with half a million more households opting out of the payment.To illustrate what could be lost, near the end of the advert Ranganathan is propelled to the backstage of Strictly Come Dancing to meet Louis Theroux, who says: “You know, none of this has to exist,” as he puts his hand on the lever of a giant on/off switch marked BBC, “if we don’t want it to”.View image in fullscreenLouis Theroux has his hand on the BBC’s on/off switch.Photograph: BBCThe film is being premiered on TV during the World Cup final and was launched on social platforms on Sunday morning. It has had some positive reaction from viewers, but a lukewarm response from some quarters, withthe Telegraphcomplaining that the selection of BBC offerings presented were “notably middlebrow” and theTimes complainingthat – as is “sorely emblematic of so much TV content these days on the BBC, but also elsewhere” – it was a rehash of an old idea.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWhat's OnGet the best TV reviews, news and features in your inbox every MondayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRomesh Ranganathan: ‘My greatest fear? My children ending up anything like me’Read moreThere have been a number of BBC promotional films over the years aimed at encapsulating its public service offering in a way that informs, educates and entertains.They include star-studded versions of theBeach Boys’ God Only KnowsandLou Reed’s Perfect Day. But Cleese’s ad (itself based on Monty Python’s Life of Brian sceneWhat Have the Romans Ever Done For Us?) washighlighted recently by Chris Packhamas “genius” while promoting an open letter in defence of the corporation.Ranganathan defended the rationale behind the new version, saying: “I strongly, strongly believe that the BBC should exist and, look, don’t get me wrong, I don’t think it’s without its flaws.”“There have definitely been times and issues where I wished it could have dealt with things differently – but I feel incredibly passionate about the fact that we should have it and it adds so much to our lives. And I’m really proud of it.”“I really do feel strongly that we should have a BBC and I think that there is nothing else that does what the BBC does.”He said when he was asked to front the film, “I was very surprised that they’d chosen me and very honoured, but I thought it was a no-brainer, really.”Growing up, it was the BBC’sGoodness Gracious Methat made him believe he could have a performing career, he said: “I didn’t think people that looked like me could do comedy. I was doing standup as a little kid and then you watch something like Goodness Gracious Me and you think, yeah, I could do that.”The advert acknowledges that the BBC has done a lot for Ranganathan himself, with Myrie pointing out he fronts The Weakest Link, Sara Cox reminding him he hosts a Radio 2 show and Chris McCausland highlighting his “hip-hop podcast on Sounds”.Explore more on these topicsBBC licence feeBBCTelevision industryTelevisionRomesh RanganathanMatt BrittinnewsShareReuse this content

title: Burnham’s vow to end Labour briefing wars will test his grip on the party | Labour | The Guardian
description: Tensions between his aims are already there including disputes over key appointments such as chancellor
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: Tensions between his aims are already there including disputes over key appointments such as chancellor
Content: Andy Burnham (left) with Ed Miliband outside No 10 in July 2024. Miliband has been the target of vicious briefing over his prospects of becoming chancellor.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham (left) with Ed Miliband outside No 10 in July 2024. Miliband has been the target of vicious briefing over his prospects of becoming chancellor.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersLabourAnalysisBurnham’s vow to end Labour briefing wars will test his grip on the partyRowena MasonWhitehall editorTensions between his aims are already there, including disputes over key appointments such as chancellorSun 19 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.32 EDTShareAndy Burnham’s vision for cleaning up Westminster politics is a utopian one: ending the dominance of the whipping system, Whitehall power hoarding and the “insidious briefing culture” of the bubble.In his first speech on becoming Labour leader, he warned his party it“won’t beat Britain’s new right if we are consumed by infightingand pulling in different directions”.Burnham is not wrong to try to weed out some of the endemic problems of factionalism in British politics that result in power struggles, rather than a focus on where agreement can be found.But the tensions between his aims are already there: to end the culture of briefing between politicians and factions jostling for ascendancy would need some extremely tough command-and-control discipline and control of the narrative from the centre.The new leader’s fledgling operation has also so far proved no different to any other when it comes to ending factionalism. Indeed, Burnham’s supporters have not got their candidate to the very top without a very large amount of briefing and organising to undermine Keir Starmer – despite his claim to have been loyal to the outgoing prime minister.Six political headaches Andy Burnham must tackle in his first weeks as PMRead moreSince then, there have been disputes between the right and soft left of the party, with key players falling out over who has the most input into policy, who should get which job and how policies, from North Sea drilling to immigration controls, should proceed.Ed Miliband, who helped propel Burnham to the leadership, has been the target of vicious briefing by those who feared he would be too antagonistic to business and could spook the markets if chosen as chancellor.Likewisethere has been a counter-briefing operation against Shabana Mahmood, the alternative choice for chancellor, from some on the soft left who are eager to paint her as too far on the Blue Labour right to win over the party.And rival ministers have not been shy to pick up the phone to journalists to tell them why they would be better candidates for the job instead.Lucy Powell, the deputy party leader and possible deputy prime minister, told the BBC’s Sunday with Laura Kuenssberg that some of the speculation around whether Miliband would be chancellor had been “really quite horrible, to be honest, and unedifying”, blaming political journalists for whipping up a storm by reporting on the tensions.She claimedLabourhad handled the leadership crisis with “consensus” around Burnham, adding: “I think we’ve given a really good account of ourselves over these last few weeks.“And maybe because political journalists haven’t had all that to write about, they’re looking for other personalities and other disagreements to write about.”However, the implication that reporting on political disagreements within the party should be suppressed runs counter to Burnham’s other desire to make Labour a broad church that can accept differing views without the need to start withdrawing the whip or expelling party members.When a leadership transfer has been made from Starmer to Burnham without the big debate of a proper contest or an election, it is not realistic to think all discussions about strategy and big jobs can be kept behind closed doors without any of the arguments being publicly surfaced. Whether on welfare changes, immigration controls or public spending, the right and left of Labour have very different views.There may be room to find common ground, but not without an airing of the disagreements and struggles over who gets to control those policy areas first.Burnham may be all powerful within Labour at the moment, and idealistic about ending factionalism and briefing wars. But he will need to maintain a very tight grip on his party and government if he thinks he will be able to decide on the future direction of the country without some big battles first.Explore more on these topicsLabourAndy BurnhamEd MilibandShabana MahmoodEconomic policyanalysisShareReuse this content

title: After fixing its engine problems, Rolls-Royce is turning to its next big challenge | Rolls-Royce | The Guardian
description: The aerospace giant is seeking UK government support for its re-entry into the huge narrowbody jet market
Author: Jasper Jolly
Headline: The aerospace giant is seeking UK government support for its re-entry into the huge narrowbody jet market
Content: A worker inspects a Trent 1000 engine. Rolls-Royce is on track to fit new blades to all of these engines in service by next June.Photograph: Newscast/Rolls-Royce/PAView image in fullscreenA worker inspects a Trent 1000 engine. Rolls-Royce is on track to fit new blades to all of these engines in service by next June.Photograph: Newscast/Rolls-Royce/PARolls-RoyceAfter fixing its engine problems, Rolls-Royce is turning to its next big challengeThe aerospace giant is seeking UK government support for its re-entry into the huge narrowbody jet marketJasper JollySun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn a 100-year-old hangar at Rolls-Royce’s factory in Derby, aircraft engines lie on their noses as technicians strip them down after a couple of years circling the world.Cranes lift and flip the engines, before engineers separate different modules to be cleaned, treated in acid baths if necessary, repaired or replaced. The acrid smell of kerosene signals the part of the factory where engineers handle the metre-diameter core of the engine, in which fuel and air combine at high pressure to drive the turbines and propel 200-tonne planes through the air.The operation is a crucial part of the effort to keep the global aircraft fleet in the air, but for Rolls-Royce visits to the overhaul shop became all too frequent in recent years amidpersistent durability problems with its Trent engines, including cracking in turbine blades. That led to disputes with airline customers and weighed on the share price of one of Britain’s best known manufacturers.Rolls’s chief executive, Tufan Erginbilgiç,described the company as a “burning platform”when he arrived in 2023. Yet any problems appear to have been extinguished: underlying profits for 2025 soared by 40% to £3.5bn, and its share price is above £13 – 10 times what it was three years ago.The global aviation industry is gathering this coming week in Farnborough, 30 miles west of London, for a biannual airshow (alternating with another in Paris) that serves as a chance for executives to take stock. Engine manufacturers have little cause for complaint. They are still enjoying the gradual recovery of flying after the Covid pandemic, and increased orders from the boom in defence spending started by Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022. In one of its last big moves, Keir Starmer’s government published aplan to spend more on weaponryafter more than a year of wrangling.Rolls-Royce has benefited further from huge demand for generators for AI datacentres, and shareholders are also excited by its plans to re-enter theenormous market for narrowbody jet engines. But before that Rolls-Royce needed to fix the problems in its main business.When Erginbilgiç took over, dealing with the Trent troubles and rolling solutions out to airline customers was one of several priorities. Erginbilgiç apportioned £1bn to improving Trent durability and expanding capacity for engine maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO).View image in fullscreenTufan Erginbilgiç took over as the chief executive of Rolls-Royce in 2023, describing the company as a ‘burning platform’.Photograph: Hollie Adams/EPARolls-Royce’s Trent engines – named after the river that flows to the south ofDerbyin England’s East Midlands – are used on bigger, twin-aisle planes, also known as widebodies: Airbus’s A330, A350 and A380, and Boeing’s 787 Dreamliner and 777. Each one of the 68 blades inside the engine – about 10cm long – endures the force of a doubledecker bus on its end while generating the same power as a Formula One car. All that happens at nearly 1,800C, well above the melting point of steel and enough to turn sand in the air into shards of glass.The blades to withstand such a hellish environment have to be made from a single crystal ofsuperalloysof nickel and aluminium, which get stronger as temperatures increase. But even with those properties, the blades eventually wear down, with the surface turning from a dull grey to white, and signs of wear along the edges of the blades exposed in the Derby factory.Stopping the cracking included changes to the pattern of tiny holes in the blades to increase air flow – helping to increase the cooling for the blades by 40% – and trimming some weight off the top of the blades to reduce the forces they must endure.The changes would allow the company to triple the engine’s time on aircraft wings, rather than being dismantled in hangars, said Rachel Walker, the Trent 1000 engine programme director, speaking to reporters at the Derby factory. Just short of half of the Trent 1000 engines have received the new blades, putting the company on track to replace all of them by next June.Airline customers might be entitled to wonder why those changes were not made before. Celine Bouas, Rolls-Royce’s senior vice-president for customers, said the upgrades were held back because of delays to certification prompted by the crisis at Boeing over two deadly crashes of its 737 Max. Although Rolls-Royce does not make engines for the Max, the US Federal Aviation Administration facedaccusations of being too close to Boeing, so all certification work slowed to allow for increased scrutiny.Rolls-Royce has a tricky balance to strike. Its financial success is plain to see, but airlines have been asking why the engine-maker should not be sharing more of those profits with them, given how long they suffered from the reliability problems.“We needed the fix, we needed the MRO set-up so that all customers could start feeling the difference,” said Bouas. “Have we caused pain to our customers? Yes, it caused pain. It has also cost us a great deal. I mean, in terms of cost, loss of revenue, investment in the product and the MRO. So it was necessary for Rolls-Royce and for our customers to invest.”The work is helping to persuade new customers to choose Rolls-Royce engines on their planes, with a 1% improvement in fuel consumption translating to cost reductions of $500,000 (£3732,000) a year. Bouas said the company was very close to a deal with an airline which has “for decades” only used engines from US rival GE and its CFM joint venture with France’s Safran.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRob Watson, Rolls-Royce’s president for civil aerospace, said: “I think we have delivered our commitments in many areas, and in some areas we’ve outperformed what we said two years ago.”View image in fullscreenA Rolls-Royce UltraFan engine moves from the preparation shop to a testbed at the Derby factory.Photograph: Jonathan Green/Rolls-Royce Archive/GettyAlong with fixing the Trent programme, another of Erginbilgiç’s priorities when he took over was cutting investments in distractions from its main business. For instance,out went an electric propulsion arm, in a blow to hopes it could contribute to decarbonising aviation. (Some of the electrical lessons could be applied to hybrid systems that add electric propulsion to gas turbine jet engines, although it was likely only when a new plane was announced, Watson said.) However, the company continued to spend heavily on its next-generation engine technology, UltraFan.Rolls-Royce executives from Erginbilgiç down insist that the company does not need a return to the narrowbody market to continue to grow. But nobody can deny the size of the attraction. Rolls-Royce left the market for the jet engines used on medium-sized, narrowbody jets in 2011 with the sale of its stake in a joint venture, but most of the growth since then has been in that market as airlines concentrate on point-to-point journeys.Two UltraFan engines are being developed: a widebody version with 80,000 pounds of thrust, and a 30,000-lb narrowbody version. The 30 is still in a concept stage, with an aim of building and testing by 2028. The 80 is the first priority, with Rolls-Royce last month successfully linking the engine’s power gearbox to the high-pressure core.Rolls-Royce ispushing for UK government supportfor the investments – despite having cash for billions in shareholder payouts – although it is braced for a small delay while a new government, under Andy Burnham, reviews options. Both engines are being designed with new, as yet unannounced aircraft in mind, suggesting that Rolls-Royce will probably not try to fit the UltraFan under the wing of existing planes.“The reality now on the large engine is going to be driven by the next widebody platform,” said Watson. “That step change in technology, step change in performance, probably warrants a new aircraft.”Erginbilgiç has made clear that Rolls-Royce would like to share the massive investment needed in returning to the narrowbody market. Watson said the company could “do that alone, OK, but we’d rather de-risk it. We’d rather do the partnership and make sure we’re offering the very best technology and the best commercial structure to the market.”Explore more on these topicsRolls-RoyceManufacturing sectorAerospace industryDerbyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘There was absolutely no chance of being a kid’: new exhibition explores how war shapes children’s lives | Children | The Guardian
description: Childhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilities
Author: Nadia Khomami
Headline: Childhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilities
Content: John Hajdu outside the Imperial War Museum in London. John is loaning his beloved teddy for the Childhood in War exhibition, which opens on 31 July.Photograph: Richard Ash/© IWMView image in fullscreenJohn Hajdu outside the Imperial War Museum in London. John is loaning his beloved teddy for the Childhood in War exhibition, which opens on 31 July.Photograph: Richard Ash/© IWMChildren‘There was absolutely no chance of being a kid’: new exhibition explores how war shapes children’s livesChildhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilitiesNadia KhomamiArts and culture correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen John Hajdu was three, his parents gave him a teddy bear small enough to fit into his hands: 86 years later, it is the only surviving object from his childhood.The bear, which the 89-year-old simply calls “teddy”, stayed with him as his father was taken to a labour camp, his mother sent to a concentration camp, and he hid in a non-Jewish neighbour’s cupboard before being forced into the Budapest ghetto.From 31 July, teddy will go on public display for the first time as part ofChildhood in War, a new exhibition at the Imperial War Museum, London, examining how conflict shapes children’s lives.Displayed in a recreated second world war bedroom, the bear sits among toys, books and personal possessions that ask visitors to consider what childhood looks like when play, education and family life are disrupted by war.View image in fullscreenJohn Hajdu and his family. Now a grandfather of four, John spends much of his time speaking in schools about surviving the HolocaustThe exhibition combines hundreds of objects with immersive environments, including a full-size railway carriage, a wartime classroom and a cold war living room and kitchen, alongside testimonies from children living through conflicts in places including Ukraine, Sudan and Afghanistan.For Hajdu, teddy represents a childhood interrupted. Before the war, his life was typical of a well-to-do, middle-class Jewish family. By 1944, after years of antisemitic persecution in Hungary, his father had been sent to a labour camp and Hajdu and his mother had been forced into a designated yellow-star house.double quotation markTeddy did, and still does, mean so much to me. It was my only connection to a family life I barely rememberedJohn Hajdu“We could only take with us what we could carry, and that meant just a few pieces of clothing and teddy,” he says. “There was absolutely no chance of being a kid. I had no other toys or books. We were only allowed out in the afternoon wearing the yellow star. I couldn’t meet other children. We weren’t allowed in parks, cinemas or swimming pools or cafes. I was completely isolated.”The exhibition explores exactly those everyday losses. A reconstructed classroom shows how war disrupts children’s education through objects from conflicts including the second world war, Northern Ireland, Afghanistan and the former Yugoslavia.View image in fullscreenThe red knitted jumper given to Jochewet Heidenstein by her parents before she left Berlin for Britain.Photograph: © IWM (EPH 3909).There are comics warning children about landmines and unexploded ordnance. Visitors can also climb into an evacuee train carriage containing letters sent home and Kindertransport objects, including a red knitted jumper given to Jochewet Heidenstein by her parents before she and her sisters fled Berlin for Britain.In October 1944, Hajdu’s mother was arrested and taken to Mauthausen concentration camp in Austria. Believing she would never return, Hajdu’s aunt rushed him into the cupboard of a non-Jewish neighbour.“I had teddy in my pocket,” he says. “I was in complete darkness and had to keep completely quiet. It seemed like a very long time, which was an incredibly frightening experience for a seven-year-old … By that time I was able to comprehend what happened without fully understanding the enormity of the situation.”View image in fullscreenA child refugee’s drawing depicting the 1998–99 conflict in Kosovo.Illustration: © The rightsholder (EPH 3008)He later moved with his aunt into the Budapest ghetto, sharing a flat with more than 20 strangers. “There were no children. We survived on horse meat and bread. Dead bodies lay piled up on the streets, having died of cold, starvation or disease. I was always hungry. Again, I had teddy in my pocket.”The ghetto was liberated by Soviet troops in January 1945, just before retreating German forces planned to destroy it. Miraculously, Hajdu was reunited with both his parents. His mother survived Mauthausen after US troops liberated the camp, but returned with broken ribs, smashed teeth and lasting psychological scars. “I was convinced I’d never see my parents again,” he says.A decade later, after the Soviet suppression of the Hungarian uprising, Hajdu and his mother walked 40km (25 miles) and crossed an icy river under cover of darkness to reach Austria. They arrived in Britain in 1957 on a Red Cross train, with teddy travelling with them.Throughout the exhibition, stories such as Hajdu’s sit alongside contemporary accounts from children living through war today. Visitors can explore a 360-degree virtual tour of an underground school in Ukraine created by one of its pupils, read poems written by Ukrainian children for Unicef’sPoems for Peace project, and seedrawings by children in Sudanthat were later submitted as evidence of alleged war crimes to the international criminal court.A poem by a 15-year-old Afghan girl describes the moment the Taliban returned to power in Kabul in August 2021: “I witnessed how that day darkened not only Kabul’s sky, but also the dreams and hopes of so many.”View image in fullscreenAlina and Artem, aged nine and from the city of Kharkiv in Ukraine, sit on a makeshift sofa in the underground car park that serves as a bomb shelter. Their parents have done their best to make it homely.Photograph: Aleksey Filippov/Unicef/UN0642196/Aleksey Filippov. All rights reservedThe exhibition also examines how childhood can become politicised, with material from the Hitler Youth and Soviet Komsomol movements.“We wanted to talk about the impact of war on children’s lives,” says curator Fran Singleton. “With more conflicts in the news at the moment, it felt like the right time to create an exhibition that offers families a space to come together and discuss these important topics.”‘Into the arms of strangers’: child refugees of Nazi Germany rememberRead moreFor Hajdu, allowing teddy to leave his home for the first time has been surprisingly difficult.“Teddy did, and still does, mean so much to me. It was my only connection to a family life I barely remembered when I was three,” he says. “I find it difficult to look at the desk where he normally sits.”Now a grandfather of four, Hajdu spends much of his time speaking in schools about surviving theHolocaust. As the number of survivors dwindles, he hopes younger generations will continue telling these stories.“I tell them, ‘When you go home, talk to your grandparents and parents. Find out whether they’ve gone through traumas, whether it’s in Asia or Africa.’“My small story is an example that you can survive. Unfortunately, people are suffering all over the world. We are getting older, and fewer and fewer Holocaust survivors are here to tell these stories. It’s absolutely vital that the younger generation takes on what we’re doing.”Explore more on these topicsChildrenMuseumsHolocaustSecond world warfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Corded vacuums are supposed to be outdated – so why is the Shark Detect Lift-Away XL so good? | Homes | The Guardian
description: Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve tested
Author: Andy Shaw
Headline: Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve tested
Content: ‘The thing I love most about this vacuum is its ability to clean up every mess thrown at it.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘The thing I love most about this vacuum is its ability to clean up every mess thrown at it.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianThe FilterHomesReviewCorded vacuums are supposed to be outdated – so why is the Shark Detect Lift-Away XL so good?Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve testedThe best vacuum cleanersAndy ShawSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Cordedvacuum cleanersaren’t as glamorous as their cordless cousins, but swapping battery power for a plug socket has its advantages. The device will never be short of power, so it can keep cleaning for as long as you need it to. Prices also tend to be lower because there’s no expensive battery to factor into the cost.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.On the downside, there’s a cable to manage while you clean, and even the longest cords usually need to be switched from one plug socket to another if your home is any larger than a small flat.The Shark Detect XL LA791UKT is a versatile corded upright that carries many of the best elements you’ll find on Shark’scordless vacuums. Chief among these is the Duo Clean dual-roller floor head, which combines a fluffy roller and a regular brush bar into an all-in-one unit. That makes it an expert on carpetsandhard floors, without needing to switch heads.What’s relatively new to this model is its Detect function. This is a sensor on the underside of the floor head that works out what surface it’s on and adjusts the speed of its rollers accordingly. That means you don’t have to flip a switch when transferring from the hard floor of your kitchen or utility room to carpets and rugs in your living areas.View at SharkNinjaView at AmazonHow I testedView image in fullscreenFor suck’s sake: writer Andy testing the Shark Detect XL on a rug and hard floor.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianCorded vacuum cleaners aren’t as difficult to test as cordless models, as there’s no battery life to take into consideration. I tested the suction by attaching a gauge to the end of the extension hose and recording the measurements on minimum and maximum modes.I also put the vacuum through a series of cleaning tests, challenging it to collect pre-weighed amounts of cat litter, flour and pet hair using the Duo Clean floor head. I ran these tests on short-pile carpet and hard floor, weighing the collection bin before and after each test to find out how much mess is transferred from the floor to the bin.What you need to knowView image in fullscreen‘The classic two rollers and anti-tangle system are unchanged from previous models, and rightly so.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianView at SharkNinjaView at AmazonThere’s little here that’s particularly new or exciting, but frankly, that’s the way I like Shark’s vacuum cleaners. They’ve been blitzing my cleaning tests for several generations now, and change isn’t always for the better, as we saw with theDyson V16 Piston Animal.The Duo Clean floor head is a refined and effective piece of engineering that Shark is sensibly tweaking without revolutionising. The surface detection is the newest addition to the corded range, but many of Shark’s cordless vacuums (such as our favourite, theShark PowerDetect Clean & Empty IP3251) already have that feature. The classic two rollers and anti-tangle system are unchanged from previous models, and rightly so – it carries on a fine tradition here, performing well in my tests.The collection bin can be released from the floor head by pressing a foot pedal, then lifted away. You can then release the handle from the extension wand to use it as a handheld device (albeit on the end of the flexible hose). And the bin is a good size, with a capacity of 2.5 litres. That isn’t as voluminous as some corded devices, witha regular-size bagged Henrytypically swallowing six litres of dirt before it needs emptying, but it’s still more than twice the capacity of mostcordless vacuum cleaners.As a result, it’s large and heavy to lug around, weighing 5.2kg on my bathroom scales. Leave the floor head behind in Lift-Away mode, however, and it drops to a more manageable 2.9kg, including the 8m cable, handle and extension.Despite its weight, it’s surprisingly nimble when it’s on the floor and switched on. Its centre of gravity is low, and the floor head pulls forwards slightly on carpet. If anything, you need to rein in its enthusiasm, rather than struggle to get it moving.Shark Detect XL LA791UKT specificationsWeight:5.2kgMax suction power (quoted):not statedMax suction power (measured):17kPaCord length:8mDust capacity:2.5lAdditional filters supplied?NoDimensions:31 x 43 x 115cm (WDH)What we loveView image in fullscreenTest materials: the Shark Detect XL was given several challenges.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianWith the combination of the floor head and an excellent variety of attachments, the thing I love most about this vacuum cleaner is itsability to clean upevery mess thrown at it. I was particularly impressed with the floor head’s performance in my flour and pet hair tests. On carpets and hard floors, the vacuum easily lifted everything I spilt.The cat litter proved marginally more elusive, with a small number of pieces falling out of the vacuum when I finished cleaning, having not made it into the collection bin. It’s beside the point, though, because I managed to clean up the remainder quickly and easily using the extension wand without any attachments. The cat litter flew up the tube, a better way to gather larger particles than forcing them through the floor head.Andtheattachments really are excellent, including the ones I always look for: a motorised upholstery tool, and a combination crevice tool and dusting brush. I tested the floor head’santi-tangle capabilityon my daughter’s bedroom floor. This has proved to be a tricky test for many rival vacuum cleaners because of her long hair, which is highly prone to wrapping itself around a vacuum’s rollers. Shark’s system has a comb that lifts out the hair before it gets tangled – and it performed its job perfectly here. I could see hair coming off the floor and saw it pass safely into the collection bin. I inspected the rollers closely afterwards, and couldn’t see a single strand left behind.View image in fullscreenNot hard to handle: the Shark Detect XL has an easy-to-use Min and Max control to select suction power.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianIt’s great to be able to lift the collection bin away from the floor head when you want toaccess hard-to-reach areas, whether they’re high up, such as cobwebs in the corners, or you’re cleaning the stairs. This makes it a bit lighter, less bulky, and leaves the wheels behind. I found the motorised upholstery tool particularly effective for the stairs, though I did need to follow it up with the crevice tool to tackle the edges and corners. With the main vacuum section in one hand and the released handle and hose in the other, accessing difficult areas is a breeze.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe FilterGet the best shopping advice from the Filter team straight to your inbox. The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThecontrolsare incredibly simple, with no screen or menus to navigate. The vacuum is switched on via a button on the main unit. This doubles up as a mode switch, which can either be left in Detect mode or forced to stay in either carpet or hard-floor modes. The only downside is that you have to reach down to the collection bin to operate it, whereas many cordless vacuums have their controls more readily available at the handle.It’s also possible tocontrol the suction power with a flick of a switch. This is up at the handle, so it can be controlled with a thumb while you’re vacuuming. There are two modes, Min and Max, with the Min setting essentially opening up a vent that lets through more air, thus weakening the suction.What we don’t loveView image in fullscreenBig issue? The size and position of the collection bin sometimes make it tricky to get the cleaner under low furniture.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianCordless vacuum cleaners are reasonably good at getting under low furniture, but the Shark’s floor head is held back bythe bulk of the collection binthat sits above it. You can certainly use one of the attachments to reach difficult places, but you don’t quite get the flexibility and manoeuvrability of a cordless here.I also didn’t lovethe cord. While eliminating the chance of running out of power, cord management while you’re vacuuming is an extra thing to think about. The Shark doesn’t have fancy retractable cord storage either – instead you’re required to wrap the cord around two hooks on the side when you’ve finished your cleaning.I’ve used a few similar upright Sharks extensively for everyday cleaning jobs and found that theycan suffer blockagesdue to the slightly convoluted path that debris has to pass through. Even when the floor head is attached, dirt is sucked up the extension wand, almost up to the handle, then back down the bendy tube to the collection bin. I didn’t experience any blockages during testing, but have witnessed it in similar designs. Fortunately, everything is easy to dismantle, so blockages are simple to clear. They also only really occur when you’re tackling particularly problematic messes, such as clumps of pet hair.Warranty, repairability and sustainabilityShark’s corded vacuum cleaners have a generous five-year guarantee, provided you register the product within 28 days of purchase. The guarantee covers parts and labour, and Shark offers repair or replacement if things go wrong with your machine. It doesn’t cover blockages, misuse or everyday wear and tear – such as worn-out rollers or blown fuses – but most other types of fault are covered.Although it doesn’t use bags, the LA791UKT does have a consumable in the form of an anti-odour capsule. These aren’t essential to replace, but they keep your vacuum smelling a little more pleasant, and last for about six months. Shark sellsreplacement cartridgesfor £7.99 each.The filters are easy to remove and wash under cold running water. There are two pre-motor filters, which should be rinsed through and left to dry once a month. The post-motor filter is an annual cleaning job. If any of these get too bunged up, you can buy replacements from Shark.When your vacuum finally gives up, it can be recycled at a local electrical recycling centre – find your nearest atrecycle-more.For more, read our guide onhow to make your vacuum cleaner last longerShark Detect Lift-Away XL: should I buy it?View image in fullscreenHeads you win: ‘Easily the best corded vacuum cleaner I’ve tested.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianThe Shark Detect Lift-Away XL LA791UKT is easily the best corded vacuum cleaner I’ve tested, with excellent performance in my tests, all the add-ons and attachments you could ask for, and no-fuss operation and maintenance. At an RRP of £349.99, it’s at the higher end of Shark’s price scale for corded upright vacuum cleaners, but it comes with everything you might need and a guarantee that should ensure it’s built to last.View at SharkNinjaView at AmazonFor more:The best vacuum cleaners for every floorThe best cordless vacuum cleaners for a spotless homeThe best robot vacuums to keep your home clean and dust freeAndy Shaw is a consumer journalist and technology addict. Having reviewed tech products professionally for more than 30 years, his favoured working environment is a small desk surrounded by big boxes. His greatest weakness is that he never, ever remembers how things came out of their packaging, so they rarely fit back in again when it’s time to return themExplore more on these topicsHomesThe FilterGadgetsInteriorsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The truth about migraines: what causes them – and how to find relief | Life and style | The Guardian
description: Over the last decade, the understanding of these devastating headaches has grown enormously, as has the understanding of what can be done to treat them
Author: Joel Snape
Headline: Over the last decade, the understanding of these devastating headaches has grown enormously, as has the understanding of what can be done to treat them
Content: ‘Our understanding of what causes severe headaches has progressed enormously’ …Composite: Guardian Design; draganab/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Our understanding of what causes severe headaches has progressed enormously’ …Composite: Guardian Design; draganab/Getty ImagesSecrets of the bodyLife and styleThe truth about migraines: what causes them – and how to find reliefOver the last decade, the understanding of these devastating headaches has grown enormously, as has the understanding of what can be done to treat themJoel SnapeSun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWe’ve been trying to cure migraines – without much success, until relatively recently – for as long as we’ve been able to identify them as a distinct ailment.The Ebers Papyrus, a scroll dating back to 1550BC that mentions a “disease of one half of the head”, suggests anointing the scalp with fried catfish; Galen, the second-century Roman physician whose term “hemicrania” evolved into the modern word, recommended blood-letting (this, to be fair, was his answer to a lot of things). By the 20th century, doctors were suggesting repressed emotions might be at fault,leading to suggestions that women– who suffer from migraines far more than men – were simply too fragile and neurotic. As recently as a couple of decades ago, “vascular theory” – the idea that blood vessels in the brain expanding and contracting were to blame – was still the standard explanation in many medical schools.Over the last decade, though, our understanding of what causes these neurological disorders, which are technically a sub-category of headache, has progressed enormously, alongside the options for treating them. One of the biggest problems, as in centuries past, is a lack of sympathy for sufferers. The biggest difference, these days, is that we actually understand what is happening, and why.View image in fullscreenHeadaches fit into two broad categories.Photograph: Posed by model; Guido Mieth/Getty ImagesBefore we get into the brain science, it’s helpful to understand that headaches, more generally, fit into two broad categories: primary headaches, which don’t come from any underlying structural cause, and secondary headaches, which could be caused by anything from stroke, meningitis or a tumour, to bleeding on the brain. A hangover, which is caused by a number of factors (some of them related to dehydration), is secondary – but a cluster headache, a very unpleasant neurological condition that can be triggered by alcohol in a matter of minutes, is primary. Migraines are primary, but are frequently mistaken for secondary: one of the many reasons that sufferers have often been poorly served.“As doctors, we’re taught to ask the right questions to determine which type of headache it is,” says Alexandra Sinclair, a professor of neurology at the University of Birmingham and chair of the British Association for the Study of Headache. “We use the International Headache Society classification,which you can see online– but actually, for the individual patient, many headaches feel quite similar, so the precise classification can be difficult to understand. Generally, though, a headache that is moderate to severe, where a patient wants to lie down, keep away from light, and noise, keep still, may feel nauseated, and feels pounding pain, often on one side – that’s most often going to be migraine.”double quotation markMigraines typically occur over four phases, including a warning period that might include excess yawning, urination or fatigueMigraines, we now understand, come from the activation of the trigeminovascular system – a network of nerves and blood vessels that detects pain in the brain. The core of this system is the largest and most complex sensory nerve in your head: the trigeminal nerve, which deals with sensory information (including touch, pain, temperature) and motor control (chewing). When something goes wrong, the nerve fibres in this system pump out a chemical called CGRP (calcitonin gene-related peptide), which activates the brain’s pain network and causes headaches that range from moderate to crippling.View image in fullscreenAlcohol can trigger cluster headaches.Photograph: Yana Iskayeva/Getty ImagesSomething else that’s now better understood is that migraines typically occur over four phases, including a warning period that might include excess yawning, urination or fatigue. A common occurrence at this stage is odd food cravings – which can lead to an element of victim-blaming. “Historically, there used to be a sense of ‘Well, you may have caused your headache by eating a piece of cheese or chocolate,’” says Prof Sinclair. “But actually, the research has completely turned that upside down. Functional brain imaging shows activation of the brain areas that might make you crave food in the 24 hours or so before the actual pain. Hence, you didn’t cause the migraine – the migraine caused the craving.”After this period – technically called the “prodrome” – about 30% of sufferers experience what is known as the “aura”, which can include visual disturbances, numbness in the face or arms or legs, or speech difficulties. Then there’s the migraine itself, which typically lasts anywhere from four to 72 hours. Then finally the “postdrome”, where you may feel washed out or exhausted for up to two days. It’s typically only after that, after several days of disruption to your body’s typical way of functioning, that things go back to normal.Like other systems in the body, this network of sensations probably evolved for good reasons. “There are schools of thought that would say that when you get meningitis or when you have a head injury or something going on in your brain like a tumor, you may be primed to get more headaches, which actually may be good – because it alerts you that something’s happened within your brain,” says Prof Sinclair. “There are also schools of thought that say that back in our caveman days, if you were primed to have migraines, you’d be quite vigilant, to noise, light, or movement – you probably wouldn’t be sleeping deeply – and so those genes might have stayed with us by giving humans a survival advantage.”double quotation markFor some people, their ability to function properly is impaired for much of the monthThis, in a nutshell, is the issue for severe migraine sufferers. Hormonal, lifestyle and even environmental changes can change your susceptibility to what is technically a neurological disease, but you can also have a brain that’s genetically primed to be more headache-prone. These days, of course, more of us are working in fluorescent-lit offices than keeping watch around a campfire – and if a migraine hobbles our ability to get any work done for most of the week, then it’s no longer a survival advantage (the WorldHealthOrganization, for instance, lists migraine in the top 10 “most disabling” diseases globally).“It affects a huge number of people for what should be the most productive years of their lives, frequently between puberty and menopause,” says Prof Sinclair. “For some people, their ability to function properly is impaired for much of the month – and then there’s the stigma around it not being a ‘real’ issue, and being sort of pushed to the back of the queue for medical treatment.” Abouttwice as many womenseem to suffer from migraine as men, for reasons that run from hormonal differences to differences in brain susceptibility to pain chemicals.
