That might be another reason it traditionally hasn’t been treated seriously enough.View image in fullscreenDehydration can contribute to migraines.Photograph: Posed by model; Ekaterina Goncharova/Getty ImagesThere are things sufferers can do to mitigate the likelihood of migraine: lack of sleep, too much sleep, caffeine withdrawal, dehydration and significant stress all play a part. Screen time, according to the available evidence, presents a murkier picture: migraine sufferers are typically sensitive to light, but it’s not totally established that there’s a correlation between phone or monitor use and the onset of attacks.What causes runner’s high – and how can you boost your chances of an ecstatic 5k?Read moreThe good news? The identification of CGRP means that migraine treatments are now improving rapidly, consisting of inhibitors built specifically to block the pain chemical. “We have a class of drugs usually taken as a tablet called Gepants that work by blocking the CGRP receptor,” says Prof Sinclair. “We also have CGRP monoclonal antibodies, often referred to as Mabs, which are typically administered via monthly injection. Together, they have truly revolutionised headache care over the last four or five years.” The problem is, these treatments are expensive, and can be difficult to access. “We need more healthcare professionals able to provide headache management, alongside a simpler prescribing process, so patients can access treatment when they need it and recover faster,” says Prof Sinclair. “But we also need to educate patients to seek help. Effective management is essential and can sometimes be the difference between losing your job or keeping it. So I think it’s really about understanding that, if you’re a migraine sufferer, things really have changed.”Headaches might not have altered in 2,000 years, but thankfully we’ve moved past seeing them as a badge of character.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsLife and styleSecrets of the bodyHealth & wellbeingHealthfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: This is how we do it: ‘My ex didn’t find me attractive after I put on weight. With Denzel, I finally feel loved’ | Life and style | The Guardian
description: Both Denzel and Sarita left sexless relationships before they met, but together they have discovered toys and Tantric sex
Author: Naomi Larsson Piñeda
Headline: Both Denzel and Sarita left sexless relationships before they met, but together they have discovered toys and Tantric sex
Content: Illustration: Ryan Gillett/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Ryan Gillett/The GuardianThis is how we do itLife and styleThis is how we do it: ‘My ex didn’t find me attractive after I put on weight. With Denzel, I finally feel loved’Both Denzel and Sarita left sexless relationships before they met, but together they have discovered toys and Tantric sexHow do you do it? Share the story of your sex life, anonymouslyAs told toNaomi Larsson PiñedaSun 19 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDenzel, 46double quotation markShe’s brought a different side to my life I didn’t know existedI think Sarita brings the best out in me. From the beginning, we’ve been very open when talking about sex and what we like to do. Nothing is taboo and there’s no judgment. I feel free to be myself and she’s very open and understanding. I know she’s got insecurities around her body, but I try to reassure her as much as I can. I find her sexy and I love her for who she is. I’ve also got insecurities – we all do. But we talk very openly about this and don’t feel judged. We are just happy to be naked in front of each other.I had previously been married for almost 20 years, but the relationship broke down and towards the end was sexless. After that, I made sure I was mentally ready to move forward and happy in myself, and wasn’t coming into a new relationship with too much baggage. I wanted to start with a clean slate. When you feel happy in yourself, that affects your sex life.double quotation markThe way she talks during sex – she could read the shopping list and I’d still be turned onI think Sarita has helped me understand myself, sexually. She’s brought a different side to my life I didn’t know existed, with new experiences like Tantric sex and using toys. We talk about sex all the time and I don’t feel afraid to ask her anything. I haven’t always had this attitude; with Sarita it’s different because we get on so well.For example, I don’t have a foot fetish, but I like hers. We also massage each other a lot, which was new to me. It’s so relaxing and I find I can’t stop touching her. I’ve never felt like that before. Once we tried Tantric sex where you just stare at each other. It was weird at first, but then I felt this emotion I’d never felt before, like I wanted to cry. And the way she talks during sex – she could read the shopping list and I’d still be turned on.We’ve been together for two years, and have our individual lives, but we come together and work well as a team, both in and out of the bedroom. As we live separately, most times we meet we’ll have sex – about six times a week. It’s always sensual and never feels rushed.Share your experienceIf you’re keen to talk to us about your sex lives you can get in touch by filling in the form below. It is very important that both sexual partners are happy to participate.Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish. For more information please see ourterms of serviceandprivacy policy.Tell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.First name of partner oneFirst name of partner twoLength of relationshipContact number or email address of partner onePlease include your country's area code if outside of the UK.Contact number or email address of partner twoPlease include your country's area code if outside of the UK.Do both parties agree to being contacted by a journalist from the Guardian?YesBy submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreSarita, 44double quotation markFrom the beginning Denzel would tell me every single part of my body that he likedDenzel and I met online two years ago. Some years before that I’d come out of a sexless marriage. My ex admitted that he no longer found me attractive after I’d gained some weight, which made me feel disgusting, undesirable and completely rejected. Not only this, but I did all the childcare, housework and cooking, and I was an emotional support. So I knew I didn’t want to be with someone who was draining or who didn’t respect me. I’ve always had quite a high sex drive and really enjoyed sex, though never had the opportunity to have much of it. When I was ready to start dating again, I wanted a bit of excitement.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markI bought him nipple clamps, and he was really into it. I suggested mirror sex, and the next minute we were in front of the mirror doing itThis is how we do it: ‘I expected to be a little old spinster, but kinky sex broadened my horizons’Read moreWithin about half an hour of meeting Denzel I really wanted to kiss him. We didn’t have sex on the first date, and the eight days between the first and second meetings felt like a lifetime. When we did have sex, it was absolutely mind-blowing. I remember him saying to me: “What do you like? I’ll do it.” And I thought: bloody hell, that’s such a turn on. He absolutely loves giving oral sex, which is so refreshing. I had so many hang-ups about my appearance, but from the beginning Denzel would tell me every single part of my body that he liked, such as my feet, and the spot above my bum which he thinks is amazing and I think looks like flab. I feel really cared for and loved.Our sex life has continued to get better and better. We talk about sex like we talk about what we’re having for dinner. We go shopping for sex toys together, or buy things and surprise each other with them. Once I suggested we try Tantric sex, and he was instantly up for it. I bought him some nipple clamps, and he was really into it. I suggested we should have some mirror sex, and the next minute we were in front of the mirror doing it. I don’t feel like anything’s out of bounds.Explore more on these topicsLife and styleThis is how we do itSexRelationshipsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why Mexico is at the heart of ethical debate over global surrogacy | Surrogacy | The Guardian
description: Many say lack of regulation allows people to exploit women’s bodies, but others argue it offers financial freedom, and a child to those with no other option
Author: Oscar Lopez
Headline: Many say lack of regulation allows people to exploit women’s bodies, but others argue it offers financial freedom, and a child to those with no other option
Content: One expert described the business of providing surrogacy for foreigners as a ‘form of neo-colonisation of women’s bodies’.Photograph: Posed by model; Alejandro Franco Garcia/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOne expert described the business of providing surrogacy for foreigners as a ‘form of neo-colonisation of women’s bodies’.Photograph: Posed by model; Alejandro Franco Garcia/Getty ImagesSurrogacyWhy Mexico is at the heart of ethical debate over global surrogacyMany say lack of regulation allows people to exploit women’s bodies, but others argue it offers financial freedom, and a child to those with no other optionRise in overseas surrogates ‘increases risk of stateless babies’Oscar LopezSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDiogo Amaro met his future husband, Loup, in Paris and from the first date, he made one thing clear: he wanted to have children.“Right off the bat, I was like: ‘If I’m going to be in a relationship with somebody, I really want to build a family,’” he said. “‘That’s important to me, so please tell me if you want to walk.’”But Loup didn’t walk – in fact, the couple moved to New York together a few months later and after five years they were married in a ceremony back in Paris.Surrogacy is banned in France, as well as in Germany, Italy, Spain, Sweden and Switzerland. In the UK, surrogacy can only happen on altruistic terms; paying a surrogate is illegal, but intended parents are permitted to cover expenses.The rules in Amaro’s native Brazil were restrictive and complicated, and the United States was prohibitively expensive.Instead, like an increasing number of Europeans from countries with surrogacy bans, Amaro and his husband choseMexico, where the practice is widespread and largely unregulated. Only two states have banned it and another three have regulations governing the practice.Because it is not regulated at a federal level, there are no reliable numbers for how many surrogate pregnancies take place in Mexico. This lack of regulation can also become deeply problematic for those involved, according to advocates.“The current model – characterised by uneven or nonexistent regulation – does not offer sufficient guarantees for pregnant people,” said Verónica Esparza, from Mexican reproductive rights group GIRE. “Surrogacy agreements are frequently carried out in contexts of cultural, economic and social inequality.”Keith López Nares is a professor at the University of the Valley of Mexico and co-author ofa 2024 studyon the practice of surrogacy in Tabasco, one of the only states where the practice is regulated.The study found that surrogacy was “a patriarchal practice of reproductive exploitation that affects the most vulnerable women living in poverty, leading them to rent their wombs as a survival strategy.“This situation of economic vulnerability is exploited by the international and national markets as an extractive practice justified by the argument that women ‘freely’ decide to sign the surrogacy contract.”According to Lopez Nares, some poor women in Tabasco were coerced by their husbands into undergoing surrogacy multiple times. In some cases, husbands took over the surrogacy process outside the clinic or agency structure.“It became a personal business: instead of being under the strict orders of a clinic’s instructions, they would be directly able to organise, manage and control the resources obtained from the exploitation of their wives’ bodies.”As far as the business involving foreigners, Lopez said it was a “form of neo-colonisation of women’s bodies”.“You take children from Mexico, and then you don’t know what happens to them,” she said. “I mean, nobody, nobody demands any accountability regarding the whereabouts and wellbeing of these children.”GIRE has also documented numerous irregularities in Mexico, including contracts with vague or ambiguous clauses that left women vulnerable, as well as “problems for the legal registry of children born under this agreement, which affects their right to an identity”.But while somefeminist groups in Mexicohave called for the prohibition of surrogacy, the issue remains under debate, with many also feeling that greater regulation is the way forward.“Far from eradicating the practice, prohibition tends to push it underground, increasing the risks of abuse,” said Esparza.Oriana López Uribe, co-director of feminist organisation Hilar Talentos, said that, as with sex work, women should be allowed to choose what they want to do with their own bodies.“The conditions of inequality in which we live aren’t going to change just because a practice is prohibited,” she said. “On the contrary, we are reducing the possibilities that [women] have of obtaining an income.”And while many argue exploitation is widespread in Mexico, others insist not all surrogacies are exploitative.Amaro says he and his husband were aware of “the horrible things that happen to surrogates” in Mexico, but once they started talking to other parents who had used the same clinic as them, and who had had “delightful experiences” with their surrogates, their fears were assuaged.“It was something that worried us a little bit in the beginning but very quickly it became much less relevant,” he said.The clinic gave them a catalogue “with different profiles of egg donors, with different phenotypes primarily from Latin America”. They chose a young woman from Colombia who the couple felt resembled them physically.They met their surrogate when they went to sign the surrogacy agreement before a notary, and kept in touch throughout the pregnancy. When their daughter, Gisele, was born, Loup was in the delivery room and both men spent the night in the maternity ward.After two months in Mexico, the couple returned to France, but are still in contact with the surrogate.“I send her pictures of Gisele whenever I send pictures to my grandmother every week or two,” Amaro said. “She’s a great person, and we’re really in touch.”Explore more on these topicsSurrogacyWomen's rights and gender equalityParents and parentingMexicofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Trump’s rebranding of Palm Beach airport hasn’t taken off as he planned | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: Changes inside the Florida international airport are hardly evident as state and county hold back funds for full remodel
Author: Richard Luscombe
Headline: Changes inside the Florida international airport are hardly evident as state and county hold back funds for full remodel
Content: A sign displays the name of the rebranded Donald J Trump international airport on 13 July in West Palm Beach, Florida.Photograph: Marta Lavandier/APView image in fullscreenA sign displays the name of the rebranded Donald J Trump international airport on 13 July in West Palm Beach, Florida.Photograph: Marta Lavandier/APDonald TrumpAnalysisTrump’s rebranding of Palm Beach airport hasn’t taken off as he plannedRichard Luscombein MiamiChanges inside the Florida international airport are hardly evident as state and county hold back funds for full remodelSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTShareOne ofDonald Trump’s most grandiose vanity projects, a multimillion-dollar makeover ofFlorida’s Palm Beach international airport into aself-aggrandizing, gold-plated transportation hub, is running into turbulence, barely a week after the first plane’s wheels touched the runway.At first glance, therebrandingof the airport less than 5 miles from his opulent Mar-a-Lago resort appears to be complete. Itswebsitebears the name and oversized logo of the President Donald J Trump international airport, and state transportation workers were quick to erecthighway signsleaving drivers in no doubt where they were heading.Yet inside the terminals, at the ticket counters and baggage halls, the change is far less noticeable. Digital signage reflects the switch, but many physical signs and infrastructure still refer to its old name of Palm Beach International (PBI).There is no clear timeline of when, or even if, new signs will be installed and PBI fully becomes DJT, the three-letter airport code using the president’s initials thatwill be itsformal aviation industry designation starting on 18 August.Trump speech lays groundwork for him to tamper with midterm results, critics warnRead moreThe issue is money. A state lawsigned in MarchbyRon DeSantis, Florida’s Republican governor and intermittent Trump ally, mandated the name change on a reluctant county commission, whichvoted4-3 in May to approve a trademark deal with the Trump Organization as some members feared consequences if they did not.But Florida’s$117.6bn budgetsigned off by DeSantis last month contained only $2.75m to fund the switch, half of the estimated $5.5m overall cost. And Palm Beach county is refusing to make up the shortfall, with some commissioners insisting taxpayers cannot and should not be left on the hook.“We will be able to make the baseline, necessary changes to signage with the $2,750,000. But for the full extensive signage, we will be needing the other half of those funds from the state,” Joseph Abruzzo, the county administrator,told the Miami Herald.Abruzzo was speaking on 9 July, the official handover day when “Trump Force One” carrying the president’s son Ericlandedjust after 5am to become the first to do so under the airport’s new name.Astatementon the airport’s website, meanwhile, says that, beyond the $2.75m received in state money: “The remaining costs will be funded through the [Palm Beach county] Department of Airports’ operating budget and capital improvement program.”It is an apparent contradiction to Abruzzo’s remarks to the media last week that no county money will be used, and has raised fears among some critics that funds earmarked for safety and other crucial improvements at the department’sfour airports, which include Palm Beach international, will be siphoned off to support Trump’s glorification.Joel Flores, one of three county commissioners to vote against the unorthodoxtrademark and licensing agreementin May that critics say willfurther enrichthe president and his family, said he would attempt to block any such move.“I will not personally be voting to utilize any operational funds towards this rebranding,” he said.“This should be solely on the state to be able to fund this. This is their initiative. This is their imposition, their rule over a local municipality over a local airport, so they should be responsible.“We’ve also heard that, federally, they might be sending some dollars down, but I have not seen any bills or budgets that address that. So until I see the money in the bank, I’m not going to count with it.”Flores said he agreed with Abruzzo’s assessment that the rebranding would move forward in phases, and stall until more funds were found.“That’s the kind of conversation I’ve had with our administrator,” he said. “If we don’t get to rebrand entirely, then that’s on them. I don’t feel that our dollars should be going to a rebrand that this board did not approve.”Rebeca Krogman, the public information officer for the Palm Beach department of airports, said a phased approach “ensures continued progress while aligning each step with available resources”.In a statement, she said: “As with any facility of this size, it would not be practical to list every individual sign or area that has or has not yet been updated.“We have begun updating all digital touchpoints, including the airport’s website, social media platforms, and digital signage within the terminal. Temporary signage is also being deployed across the campus pending installation of permanent signage, including the large welcome sign on Turnage Boulevard.”Krogman did not rule out drawing on the airports’ operating budget, but said it was not the department’s preference.“It is our intent to continue these improvements through the airport’s normal budgeting process, primarily utilizing available external funding sources, such as the recent state allocation for signage upgrades, while actively pursuing additional opportunities, including legislative appropriations and grant funding,” she said.Trump airport branding deal opens new route to profit for familyRead moreTheFloridadepartment of transportation did not return a request for comment.Two lawsuits have also been filed to try to prevent the rebranding from advancing. One is byGeorge Poncy, a retired pilot who said that changing the airport’s identifier from PBI, which has been embedded in air traffic control and aviation navigation and management software for decades, will cause confusion that could create a safety hazard.Theother, by the trademark attorney Victoria Doyle, argues the state had no authority to impose the name change on a county-owned and -operated airport.Doyle, who is running for the Democratic nomination for a Palm Beach congressional seat in next month’s primary election, said her case has been granted a trial date next year.“Trump’s ego is going to demand upgrades to that airport as fast as possible,” she said.“He’s going to want it to be the best, most beautiful, mostgold-ladenairport on earth, and we could be on the hook. I am suspicious of all statements about funding and wouldn’t be surprised if the county ends up paying the money.“The airport is an extremely upsetting topic to many people here. We are a majority Democratic county, and we were not asked our opinion of this renaming. It was shoved down our throats from the state on high, and the money is a big part of why they’re so upset. Not only did we not want this rename, now we’re going to have to pay for it? It’s highly offensive to people who live here.”Explore more on these topicsDonald TrumpFloridaUS politicsRon DeSantisanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before US president attempts to crash Spain celebrations | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday
Author: Alexander Abnos
Headline: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday
Content: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino during the trophy presentation ceremony after the World Cup final.Photograph: Ashley Landis/APView image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Gianni Infantino during the trophy presentation ceremony after the World Cup final.Photograph: Ashley Landis/APWorld Cup 2026Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before US president attempts to crash Spain celebrationsUS president watches Spain v Argentina matchMatch report: Spain beat Argentina in extra-timeAlexander Abnosat New York New Jersey StadiumSun 19 Jul 2026 19.27 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump andGianni Infantinowere booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony before the US president attempted to gatecrash Spain’s victory party.Trump arrived at the stadium on Marine One, with the president’s helicopter first flying over the stadium about 45 minutes before kick-off. He sat in a glassed-in section of luxury seats for the match, along with Infantino. Other dignitaries and former players sat nearby, including Spain’s king, Felipe VI, Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum.The 2026 World Cup final: Spain v Argentina – in picturesRead moreTrump was briefly shown on the video screens at the stadium after the US national anthem was played. There were scattered boos from some in the crowd but nothing like the hostile reception he has received at other sporting events during his presidency. However, when the US president and Infantino came on to the field for the presentation after Spain clinched victory, boos rang out around the stadium. The White House pool report said that ambient crowd noise, which had been sitting at approximately 78 decibels before their introduction, rose to 84 decibels when Trump entered the field.Then, after Trump presented Spain with theWorld Cuptrophy, he attempted to stand with the team at the side of the podium as they celebrated before Infantino quietly ushered him away.It was a repeat of last summer’s Club World Cup at the same stadium after Trump presented the trophy to champions Chelseaand tried to crash the celebrationsto the bemusement of the players.Trump has made several appearances at sporting events since his re-election. Last year, he attendedthe Super Bowl,tennis’s US Open,the Daytona 500and golf’s Ryder Cup. This year, he has attended several golf events, college football’s national championship game and Game 3 of the NBA finals, where he wasmet with boosfrom the New York Knicks home crowd.View image in fullscreenLionel Messi receives his runner-up medal from Donald Trump.Photograph: Alex Pantling/FIFA/Getty ImagesTrump’s appearance was the first time he has attended a match at this summer’s World Cup, which the have US co-hosted along with Canada and Mexico.At the 2022World Cupfinal, the emir of Qatar, Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani, joined Infantino to award the trophy to Lionel Messi and his Argentina teammates. Vladimir Putin awarded it to Kylian Mbappé and France after their win at the 2018 World Cup in Russia.View image in fullscreenRodri lifts the World Cup Trophy alongside Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino.Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty ImagesTrump is the first sitting US president to attend a World Cup final on home soil. When the US hosted the tournament in 1994, Trump’s predecessor Bill Clinton did not attend the game. The trophy was instead presented by Clinton’s vice-president, Al Gore.Trump hasa close relationshipwith Infantino, andcalled the Fifa presidentto lobby for a review of USA star Folarin Balogun’s controversial red-card suspension before their last-16 match. Fifa did reverse Balogun’s ban, but the political circus cast a pall over the US team, who exited a promising World Cup with defeat to Belgium.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupDonald TrumpUS politicsGianni InfantinonewsShareReuse this content

title: At the World Cup of superstars, a team take home the trophy | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a system
Author: Sid Lowe
Headline: The European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a system
Content: Ferran Torres scored the only goal of the final, becoming Spain’s seventh different scorer at the tournament.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAView image in fullscreenFerran Torres scored the only goal of the final, becoming Spain’s seventh different scorer at the tournament.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAWorld Cup 2026AnalysisAt the World Cup of superstars, a team take home the trophySid Loweat New York New Jersey StadiumThe European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a systemSun 19 Jul 2026 19.05 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.39 EDTShareRelease the pressure, let it out. All of it and all of them. Again and again they had come, again and again they had been halted but they would not be denied. A dozen different players had taken aim at Emi Martínez’s goal but somehow he was still standing and time was slipping away, yetSpainrefused to do so, so they came some more.And then, suddenly, only this wasn’t really sudden, the result instead of the purest logic, the clearest justice, Pedro Porro’s deep cross found Nico Williams at the far post and he headed back. The ball dropped deep inside theArgentinapenalty area, bounced on the turf and just kind of sat there: waiting, willing someone take this moment.Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra timeRead moreSo at 5.42pm eastern daylight time, 37 seconds into the second period of extra time, Ferran Torres did. And with Spain’s 20th shot of this final, with all his might and all his soul, he won theWorld Cup. The ball travelled at 102km/h, tearing into the net, and Torres turned and tore in the other direction, sprinting to the corner where he leapt into the air and volleyed that too. Not quite so hard as he had hit the shot, but hard. From the bench and the pitch his teammates raced towards him, liberated at last, the joy flooding out. Oh, the release.Andrés Iniesta once said that the moment the ball sat up for him in the 116th minute of the final in South Africa in 2010, he heard the silence. Maybe Torres heard it too; maybe he heard history call. More often he has heard noise. Here there was an explosion, everyone at that end of New York New Jersey Stadium going absolutely mad. Torres stood there, doing a familiar gesture: jibber-jabber hands, ears “listening” wondering where the critics were now. Then he got the biggest hug Lamine Yamal has ever given.Late in Spain’s victory over Saudi Arabia on the day that Luis de la Fuente celebrated his 65th birthday, Torres had come on as a substitute and scored the final goal – or thought he had – only for the video assistant referee to take it from him. As they reviewed the footage, Ferran had stood with his hands in front of his mouth in a praying position, muttering to himself:por favor,be a goal; por favor,be a goal.This seemed like a portrait of the pressure, of what players put up with sometimes, a picture perhaps of desperation too. Spain were four goals up already, but here he waspleadingfor it to count.In the previous game against Cape Verde, he had hit the bar. In the next game, against Uruguay, he did it again. There was something that his critics took as confirmation, something cruel in how close it was. Too often he had felt that fate somehow was conspiring against him. Turned out, it was saving something for him: the greatest goal, the greatest moment in any player’s career. Justice had been done, not just in the game, which Spain had dominated Spain’s way – at that point Argentina had not had a shot – but to this team. This family, as De la Fuente calls them, all in it together.It felt right almost that it should be Torres, a symbol of all of them – like this was another moment shared, the collective expressed in one man who hasn’t always felt it but will now be for ever beloved. Torres became the seventhdifferent Spain player to score in the US. At the World Cup of the stars, ateamtook the trophy. The European champions were the best team here, lifting that trophy having conceded just one goal, and this was a victory for a system, an ideal, a commitment to an identity. In that, it can feel almost inevitable but it is not. This was also a victory for vindication, for the belief their manager has in them and they have in each other, even if others do not. “People have been very unfair with him but he has a spectacular mentality, he keeps going, keeps working, and today life has paid him back,” Pedro Porro said of Torres. “This is for all 26 of us.”Coming into the tournament, Rodri, the captain, had not completed his rehabilitation yet. Lamine Yamal, the star, was returning from injury too, everyone waiting for him until they realised that maybe they didn’t have to. Williams, who had provided the assist, thought he wouldn’t get there: injured for much of the season, he fought back then feared Uruguay had kicked him out of the World Cup.Fabián Ruiz had missed most the season injured. Aymeric Laporte, imperious again, suffered with Athletic. Behind him, the debate around Unai Simón wouldn’t relent. Eric García and Martín Zubimendi came on here to win it: neither had had a minute yet, meaning Spain finished the competition with only two outfielders who have not played. Who had Pedro Porro and Marc Cucurella down as the world’s best full-backs before the tournament? Plenty do now. Mikel Merino, the superest of super subs, had played only 28 minutes since March, but appeared to rescue them. Not once, but twice.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAnd then, at the biggest moment of all, there was Torres.De la Fuente said that Spain had been “stung” by the doubts in those early games, the lack of recognition. “They are the best players in the world,” he kept saying, and it turned out he was right. Spain’s coach is a man of faith; faith in his footballers, his “family”. Every day over the last month he has exchanged messages with a friend Antonio, who is like a son to him, expressing the conviction they both felt. “It is written,” the messages say, and now it is.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026SpainArgentinaWorld CupanalysisShareReuse this content

title: ‘It was crap’: Wayne Rooney’s candour helps BBC in World Cup final TV battle | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: BBC scored a late goal in head-to-head with ITV over the World Cup by being in the stadium for the final
Author: Will Unwin
Headline: BBC scored a late goal in head-to-head with ITV over the World Cup by being in the stadium for the final
Content: ITV’s World Cup final panel of Roy Keane, Gary Neville and Ian Wright opted to stay in their studio by the Hudson River rather than travel to the stadium.Photograph: ITVView image in fullscreenITV’s World Cup final panel of Roy Keane, Gary Neville and Ian Wright opted to stay in their studio by the Hudson River rather than travel to the stadium.Photograph: ITVWorld Cup 2026Review‘It was crap’: Wayne Rooney’s candour helps BBC in World Cup final TV battleBBC scored a late goal in head-to-head with ITV over the World Cup by being in the stadium for the finalWill UnwinSun 19 Jul 2026 18.32 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google“Ithought it was crap,” Wayne Rooney surmised onBBCafter enduring the half-time show. Amid a tense final, the interval was always going to be prominent, theoretically bringing light to the shade. Why the two UK broadcasters felt the need to transmit the karaoke session in full we will never know but at least the pundits struggled through it, too.Ally McCoist said it had “mentally scarred” him as someone in attendance, unlike many of his ITV colleagues who were sat in Brooklyn. The studio has rightly earned them kudos, creating a home alongside the Hudson River with the Brooklyn Bridge providing the dramatic backdrop. With the game only an expensive train ride away, it was a surprise to see Roy Keane, Gary Neville and Ian Wright in familiar surroundings.Hits, misses and Muppets: the star-studded World Cup half-time show nobody asked forRead moreMark Pougatch scripted a safe opening, which is what you expect from the ITV presenter, while BBC made more effort by hiring an American voiceover artist and captured the mood of the match as Lionel Messi v Lamine Yamal. Both sides were subtly seething with rage over England’s failure to reach this stage, which would have made it a greater spectacle for the domestic audience, with a lot of editorial proposals floating past Manhattan.The BBC was playing catchup, having spent most of the tournament in Salford rather than San Francisco. With the authoritative Gabby Logan hosting from New York New Jersey Stadium and coaxing the best out of Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards – a trio that provide plenty of energy stood up inside a stadium that was bursting into life as the minutes ticked by – ITV appeared more subdued in absentia. Rooney proved he is not a regular in San Sebastián pintxos bars with his mangled pronunciation of Mikel Oyarzabal, much to the amusement of his colleagues.A backup team of Laura Woods, Karen Carney and Emma Hayes were in situ but reduced to cameos. Seeing the venue, the noise and the colour that comes with it is always part of the occasion, something missed pre-match by ITV, whose main interest came in Robbie Williams singing the ironically named Fifa anthem Desire which no one wanted to hear. Twenty minutes of potential insight was lost on both sides to national anthems and Phoenix Nights-style entertainment.View image in fullscreenThe BBC opted to send Gabby Logan, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards to the stadium to be closer to the action.Photograph: BBCGuy Mowbray and Alan Shearer are fine professionals, knowledgable and engaging, but lack the cutting edge of the ITV trio on the gantry. Sam Matterface is not always to everyone’s taste but he keeps the viewer on their toes because he can throw in an unexpected line without notice, while Lee Dixon plays McCoist’s straight man, a necessary addition amid such a turgid match, joking that the final was so bad it would turn the Americans back to baseball. The host broadcaster spent far too much time finding every single celebrity in attendance. Mowbray did not seem to recognise Matt Damon, while Matterface quipped “the talented Mr Scaloni” as the camera cut to the Argentina coach.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSpain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra timeRead moreNo requirement for adverts is always a benefit for BBC, even if pushing the licence fee continued, allowing it to do some proper analysis before Ronaldo was released by Madonna from his kidnapping, looking confused at re-entering society wearing a shellsuit and driving a dune buggy. ITV had to appease its paymasters and did its best to sell everything under the sun but when back in the studio everything was rushed, providing vague insight on a muted opening half.There were ITV giggles over the referee’s shaving foam spontaneously ejaculating to keep spirits up to counteract the tedious football, while BBC stayed stony silent. “There’s not more shows?” a concerned Dixon asked as extra time loomed, as ITV brought the greater mirth, but it mustered only a few seconds of punditry after the ads. “It was awful,” Matterface said of normal time, a hard piece of honesty on what is a career highlight for any journalist.Quick GuideBob the Cat earns mention in BBC's World Cup creditsShowBob the Cat was given a special mention in the BBC's closing credits after the World Cup final. Danny Murphy's tales of his former pet lit up some of the duller games of the tournament, with the commentator first mentioning Bob when his namesake, Norway winger Oscar Bobb, got involved against Côte d'Ivoire in the last 32. 'I used to have a cat called Bob. He jumped into the back of a Royal Mail van, and we lost him,' Murphy revealed without further context.After the clip went viral, Murphy added more background during Spain's 3-0 win over Austria. 'We did get him back ... unfortunately he had gone feral. He ended up living with my friend's parents in Preston. That was the life he had and now he has passed away, unfortunately, many years ago.'Guardian sportWas this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Amid the joviality Matterface immediately knew Enzo Fernández was heading for an early bath but Mowbray did not realise a second yellow was incoming, indicating who had the calmer mind. ITV’s referee analyst, Christina Unkel, was clear with her view on what happened, while Darren Cann’s quiet review was less convincing.ITV rested on laurels from earlier praise and should have had a greater presence at New York New Jersey Stadium rather than taking the safe route. BBC’s pundits were sharper and given the space to air their views but ITV’s more entertaining commentary balanced it out. No one will be too eager to rewatch the match but, more than anything, the public should not be subjected to another half-time show.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026Sport TVITV1BBC OneBBCWorld CupTelevisionreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Kane says Tuchel will ‘learn a lot’ from England pressures after exit to Argentina | England | The Guardian
description: Harry Kane has backed Thomas Tuchel to learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat and says upcoming Nations League campaign is key to improving
Author: Ed Aarons
Headline: Harry Kane has backed Thomas Tuchel to learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat and says upcoming Nations League campaign is key to improving
Content: Harry Kane said Thomas Tuchel played ‘a big part’ in creating positive atmosphere within squad.Photograph: Catherine Ivill/AMA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHarry Kane said Thomas Tuchel played ‘a big part’ in creating positive atmosphere within squad.Photograph: Catherine Ivill/AMA/Getty ImagesEnglandKane says Tuchel will ‘learn a lot’ from England pressures after exit to ArgentinaGerman created ‘best England group for togetherness’Captain underlines importance of Nations League gamesEd Aaronsin MiamiSun 19 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHarry Kane has backedThomas Tuchelto learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat against Argentina and believes they must use the forthcoming Nations League campaign to gain experience of playing against the top teams.Kane was an unused substitute as England sealed third place with anentertaining 6-4 victory against Franceon Saturday – their best finish at a World Cup on foreign soil. Tuchel, who signed a contract extension before the tournament, has been criticised heavily for his tacticsagainst Argentinawhen he opted to switch to a back five to try to defend a 1-0 lead.England 2026 World Cup squad: player-by-player ratingsRead moreKane admittedEnglandhad fallen short against Lionel Scaloni’s side but described the environment Tuchel has created since succeeding Gareth Southgate in 2025 as “the best England group I’ve been a part of in terms of togetherness”.The captain said: “I know there are a lot of questions after the other night. We have to process it as players. I’m sure the manager will. It’s his first major tournament. He’ll learn a lot from the players, the situations, the pressures, the travel.“There are also a lot of things as players we can learn from the other night. For now it’s just trying to process everything. We had a lot of good things, a lot of tough situations we overcame. The group is incredible, the best England group I’ve been a part of in terms of togetherness. The boss was a big part of creating that as well.“Fans are going to be angry and we understand it. It’s a major tournament and that’s why you dream so big. When it doesn’t go your way you have to take the criticism. We’re all willing to do that, to dare to reach the biggest goal of all. We know over the next two years until we’re facing that situation again there is always going to be a question mark. It’s something we have to take and use as motivation.”England missed out on facing regular top-class opposition in the buildup to theWorld Cupafter being relegated from the top Nations League group in 2022 under Southgate. They were promoted in 2024 and will face Spain, Croatia and Czechia, starting with a rematch of the Euro 2024 final against Luis de la Fuente’s side on 26 September at Wembley. After beating France – albeit in a largely meaningless game – Kane thinks England must take the competition seriously if they are to thrive in tournaments.View image in fullscreenDefeat to an established opponent was a familiar feeling for England.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty Images“The last 30 minutes of Argentina is not the team we want to be,” he said. “We spent a lot of time over the last two years talking about that situation, to attack those moments. The bottom line is we lost anyway. It’s easy to go away and talk about it. We need to get better at actually being in those situations. We can train, play the qualifiers … I think the Nations League will be big for us, against Spain and Croatia, big teams.“Getting used to those big environments and finding our best form against those teams, like we did against France, albeit not in the most competitive match. We have to learn, improve. The coach will have improved from these learnings. I can talk as much as I want but ultimately it’s about showing it next time we’re in that situation.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionKane has been on the losing side in two World Cup semi-finals and twoEuropean Championshipfinals and he believes Tuchel is the man to help England to make the final step.“The enthusiasm he has, the emotion he brings, the tactical experience he has … it doesn’t mean you’re going to get it right every time. We want to get it right on the biggest occasions and he expects that from himself as well. He’s a fantastic coach. He not only gave the players belief but he gave the country belief that it was our year. That is why it hurts even more than other years because everyone believed we were going all the way.“Everyone has to process it. He has to process it himself. We’re still in that grieving stage, the emotional stage, and we’ll be in that for the next few weeks. It won’t be until the next camp where you think about what the future holds and what the next target is. We’ll have conversations as captain and manager and with other teammates and staff, to see where we can be better.”Explore more on these topicsEnglandHarry KaneThomas TuchelEuro 2028European ChampionshipWorld Cup 2026World CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: Are money and soft power draining World Cup football of its magic? | Richard Partington | The Guardian
description: Fifa’s orchestration of incompatible or frankly absurd corporate sponsorship suggests the balance may have tipped too far
Author: Richard Partington
Headline: Fifa’s orchestration of incompatible or frankly absurd corporate sponsorship suggests the balance may have tipped too far
Content: England’s Bobby Moore lifts the World Cup at Wembley in 1966, when pitch-side advertising – for brands including Brylcreem and Gordon’s Gin – first crept into the tournament.Photograph: APView image in fullscreenEngland’s Bobby Moore lifts the World Cup at Wembley in 1966, when pitch-side advertising – for brands including Brylcreem and Gordon’s Gin – first crept into the tournament.Photograph: APEconomics viewpointAdvertisingAre money and soft power draining World Cup football of its magic?Richard PartingtonFifa’s orchestration of incompatible or frankly absurd corporate sponsorship suggests the balance may have tipped too farSun 19 Jul 2026 07.16 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 13.34 EDTShareIt’s almost all over. No more hydration breaks, no more obligatory pans of the TV cameras to Hollywood A-listers, or dread of the crushing inevitability of English disappointment. The FifaWorld Cup2026 is at an end, after another month of planet-straddling drama laced with significance and symbolism, both on and off the pitch.Before the kick-off between Spain and Argentina on Sunday evening, the result is already clear: football’s greatest prize is more than a sporting event; it is a geopolitically charged economic juggernaut.After a month of fixtures, billions of dollars have been spent in an extravaganza of consumerism and corporate largesse, fuelled by advertising spend, ticket sales, merchandise, gambling, travel and hospitality.Fifaestimates that the tournament will add $40.9bn (£30.4bn) to global GDP, with the US – ahead of Canada and Mexico as the leading economy among the co-host nations – accounting for almost half of the total.There are good reasons for the World Cup’s economic impact: no other sporting event – not even the Olympics, Tour de France or Wimbledon – can match its global reach. Sunday’s final is expected to draw an audience at least 10 times that of theSuper Bowl.According to Fifa, more than half the world’s population – about 5 billion people – were estimated to have watched at least part of the lastWorld Cupin Qatar in 2022. With the competition’s expanded format this year, total engagement is expected to be higher still.Big sporting and cultural events can be significant money-spinners able to send ripples through the wider economy. Taylor Swift’s2024 Eras toursupposedly pushed up quarterly GDP in Britain and Singapore, and aBeyoncéconcert was blamed for driving up inflation in Sweden.But the impact is not always entirely clear-cut. Using data from the 1982 World Cup onwards, analysts at Goldman Sachs found only a marginally positive effect on economic output for the host nation. The long-run effects are also virtually zero: while activity is boosted short term, much of the spending is often simply redirected from other activities, and any boom during the finals is often followed by a decline afterwards.Early analysis by theUS Federal Reserveshows that, while some bars inBoston ran out of boozeas Scotland fans descended on the city, the positive impact was mitigated by economic weakness elsewhere as consumers cut back amid rising prices linked to the Iran war. Regular tourists have also avoided host cities because of anticipated congestion and sky-high prices.There are intangible benefits, however. Goldman’s analysts point out that many fans would pay over the odds to see their country win. The sense of national pride, happiness and cultural heft is large, and difficult to quantify on the cold-hearted GDP ledger.Riding on the coat-tails of the World Cup is also, clearly, big business. The corporate sponsors have cashed in, with airlines, bars, restaurants, hotels and retailers across the 48 nations involved also ringing in profits. But this is about more than just money: it is about soft power and who wields it.Just look at Fifa’s official sponsors. Each shines a spotlight: Qatar Airways and Saudi Aramco, among the top-tier “partners” – believed to be paying up to $200m for the privilege – underline the dominance of fossil fuels in powering global output, and the influence seeking of the energy-rich Gulf states. Adidas, Coca-Cola and Visa cling on for the old guard of western capitalism, while the fast-growing economies of Asia are represented by the South Korean carmaker Hyundai-Kia and the Chinese technology company Lenovo.View image in fullscreenA World Cup replica trophy displayed in a Louis Vuitton case.Photograph: Amy Tennery/ReutersBelow them, the cadre of “sponsors” and commercial “supporters” range from a crypto exchange to tech companies, banks, and even an “official dairy sponsor” in China’s Mengniu Dairy. Not all sound that compatible with elite sport – not least the “quick-service restaurant sponsor” McDonald’s, or official snack food sponsor Frito-Lay (known better in the UK as Walkers). Some border on the absurd: does the World Cup need a bespokeLouis Vuitton trophy trunkto be carried on to the pitch in New York? Even the clothes worn by Fifa’s officials are a money-spinning opportunity, for “official formal wear supplier”Boggi Milano, an Italian fashion house.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSixty years ago, when England lifted the trophy in 1966, pitch-side advertising – for brands includingBrylcreem and Gordon’s Gin– crept into the tournament for the first time. A year earlier, theRolling Stoneslambasted the dawn of the modern consumerist age (“He can’t be a man ‘cause he doesn’t smoke the same cigarettes as me”). Today? The band has a “landmark collaboration” with world football’s governing body to sell three limited-edition vinyl album covers, and a remixed song for the Official Fifa World Cup 2026™ Album.As the ringmaster, Fifa is expected to earn a small fortune – with projections it will rake in more than the $7.6bn it earned at the 2022 Qatar World Cup.Part of the reason for the commercial whirl is understandable. Fifa’s mission is to grow and develop football in each of its 211 member associations – from the grassroots to elite competition; youth, women’s football and education programmes. It is laudable stuff. For all of the rivalry in football, the game still brings people together – despite our fragmenting world.It would also be foolish to pretend that money and power have no place in the beautiful game. The kicking of apig’s bladder in medieval Britainwould not have developed into a global sport without the spread of empire. Money has also long played an outsize role in the business of winning trophies, in a relationship that, although riven with tension, is ultimately symbiotic.But has the balance tipped too far? Such corporate encroachment, into a game with deep working-class roots, is increasingly fraught in an age when globalisation is being questioned and the fruits of trickle-down economics have turned sour.Explore more on these topicsAdvertisingEconomics viewpointBusiness (Football)FifaEconomicsEconomic policyWorld Cup 2026World CupcommentShareReuse this content

title: Going offline is fun. Living offline was not – just ask a child of the 90s | Emma Beddington | The Guardian
description: The neo-luddites are fighting the good fight. But my life before smartphones felt lonelier and more dangerous, writes Emma Beddington
Author: Emma Beddington
Headline: The neo-luddites are fighting the good fight. But my life before smartphones felt lonelier and more dangerous, writes Emma Beddington
Content: A frightening reminder of the dark days before satnavs.Photograph: Posed by models; Tim Hall/Getty Images/Image SourceView image in fullscreenA frightening reminder of the dark days before satnavs.Photograph: Posed by models; Tim Hall/Getty Images/Image SourceOpinionTechnologyGoing offline is fun. Living offline was not – just ask a child of the 90sEmma BeddingtonThe neo-luddites are fighting the good fight. But my life before smartphones felt lonelier and more dangerousSun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.07 EDTShareWelcome to the Summer of Ludd. That’s the name of an anti-smartphone festival recently held in New York. It was slightly under the radar for obvious reasons – there was no Instagram, TikTok or website; advertisements were on “wheatpasted monochrome posters”. Those attending followedthe rules: “Be present, and absolutely no phones, recording, or photos allowed.”That’s perhaps why,the Economist reported, “crowds were small” – but they seemed to be having fun, with events including “Google in Real Life” (festival attenders answering questions on their specialist subjects), “flirting rehabilitation” and “hanging out with rats”. I was particularly charmed by the festival’s“media spokespuppet”, Gowanus: more events should be fronted by carpet offcuts with sock arms and button eyes.A wider neo-luddite movement is gathering momentum. You may have read about gen Z prioritisingIRL socialising, “going 90s” and adoptingoffline, old-fashioned hobbies. The likes ofOffline Cluband New York University’sIRL initiativeare combating smartphone addiction with phone-free spaces and events.There’s a gentle longing for analogue fun – reading big books, dreaming, making things, being silly. I see it in my gen Z sons: they have paper diaries, CDs and old-school hobbies (playing instruments, embroidery). And they’re much more disciplined about screen time than their parents: one brought his Brick (a device you tap with your phone, blocking access to time-sucking apps) on holiday.It’s combined with a strong strand of starkertechno-pessimismthat warns we’reteetering on the brink of AI-engineered oblivion; that smartphones and social media are tools of oppression and surveillance, and tech companies are engaged in “systematic fracking of human beings”, extracting our attention for profit. Hopefully, they’re wrong about the imminent extinction bit, but it’s increasingly evident they’re right about the rest.So is this the summer we copy the original luddites with automated looms and grind our smartphones to piles of glass shards, copper and lithium? Realising how much of my life is dominated by my phone makes me itch to hang out with rats and dance with Gowanus.View image in fullscreenHang on – they’re not filming anything!Photograph: Posed by models; Klaus Vedfelt/Getty ImagesBut hang on: I wastherebefore phones, and some of it was absolutely crap – the friction for whichfriction-maxxersyearn was abundantly, luxuriantly available. If we’re heading back in that direction – and I accept we should – let’s go clear-eyed.For a start, it was dangerous. Driving anywhere new meant navigating with a giant, floppy map book precariously propped on the dashboard and scribbled handwritten directions on the passenger seat. I’m an awful driver at the best of times, and flailing around with paper at every motorway exit and junction placed me and other road users in constant peril. Another example: when I was working in Normandy in 1995, getting home from an inexplicably popular nightclub located in a field meant riding in a dilapidated banger driven by someone who had been drinking for six hours straight. In both cases – and so many more – I survived thanks to luck alone.It was also lonely. If you were awkward or socially inept, if your life was restricted by illness, caring responsibilities or geography, if you just never quite found your people, life could feel awfully empty and isolated. I spent years lacking connection (the nightclub trips were a rare exception, and I didn’t actually enjoy them) and thinking I was unlikable – maybe I was! But my life blossomed in the “blogosphere” and on Twitter (as it then was); it was improved and enriched beyond recognition by people I met online.Are middle-aged women really invisible? I see them everywhere – and not just in the mirror | Emma BeddingtonRead moreAnd God, it was sometimes so boring. That student who said she would “stare at a wall” when the under-16 social media ban came in was accurately describing most of my adolescence. I think you might be less inclined to say “boredom is good for you” if you’d spent every Saturday night for years watching Casualty with your mum (no shade, she was great company, butCharlie Fairheadwasn’t). The tech-shunners may well find that a Summer of Ludd is an easier sell than a winter, when it’s rainy, cold and dark before 4pm.I still think the neo-luddites are right and fighting the good fight. But switching off will be hard. Maybe hardest for my generation – the people who lived the before-and-after-smartphones eras most vividly, feeling the world open up in wild, digital Technicolor in the palms of our hands.Emma Beddington is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsTechnologyOpinionMobile phonesSocial media banscommentShareReuse this content

title: Aperol, late-night laughs – and some gritty life truths: why girls’ trips aren’t just fluffy fun | Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett | The Guardian
description: From Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discovery, says Guardian columnist Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Author: Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Headline: From Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discovery, says Guardian columnist Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Content: Women swimming at Portobello beach, Edinburgh, March 2026.Photograph: Jane Barlow/PAView image in fullscreenWomen swimming at Portobello beach, Edinburgh, March 2026.Photograph: Jane Barlow/PAOpinionFriendshipAperol, late-night laughs – and some gritty life truths: why girls’ trips aren’t just fluffy funRhiannon Lucy CosslettFrom Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discoverySun 19 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 13.45 EDTShareIhave just come back from a much-anticipated girls’ holiday to Puglia, and to say that it fulfilled expectations would be an understatement. We swam, we napped, we reminisced, we ate little bits of crudo alongside lovelysips of fiano, we danced around the table of the Airbnb while singing along toSarà perché ti amo. Most of all, we laughed, sometimes until our stomachs hurt and our faces were wet with tears.What a joy the girls’ trip is (despite our ages, “women” doesn’t feel right here somehow). Maybe that’s because at times during the holiday I was transported back to when we were indeed “girls” and studying together in Italy (although this time with far less street harassment – a realisation that prompted mixed feelings until a belated “Che bellissime!” saved the day).Powered by Aperol – who may as well sponsor them at this point – thousands of girls’ trips will be taking place as I write. At this very moment, I can almost guarantee that somewhere in southern Europe, a woman is detailing her husband’s domestic habits with the meticulous comic precision of a standup routine, as hercrochet-cladaudience cackle, rattle their ice cubes supportively and holler the occasional “Boo!”The girls’ trip has become an institution, a central tenet of“hun” cultureand the subject ofmyriad films, books and TV shows, most recentlyThe Five-Star Weekend, in which a widowed Jennifer Garner brings together friends from different phases of her life for a holiday. My own novel,Female, Nude, uses a girls’ trip to a Greek villa as the backdrop for an examination of friendship between four women in their early 30s who are all starting to ask themselves questions about ambition, marriage and motherhood. Sophie, my protagonist, is kicking hardest against the sacrifices she feels she will have to make in the next phase of her life, which is partly why she embarks on an explosive affair.View image in fullscreenPauline Collins in the 1989 film Shirley Valentine.Photograph: PARAMOUNT/AllstarHer story is not new. Ever since oppressed and controlled housewifeShirley Valentinetook herself off to Greece with her friend Jane in 1989, the girls’ trip has become a byword for emancipation (even for those who don’t stray outside their relationships), and the deep need for women to have time away together. Rewatching Shirley as an adult woman feels powerfully poignant. Not all of us get the chance to travel, or feel so liberated. “It’s great you’re doing this,” another woman said to me before I went on my trip, as we discussed how hard it can be to organise a holiday with so many competing demands at home.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn a world determined to isolate us, friendship may be the best way to keep each other alive | Rebecca ShawRead moreI have known my travel companions for almost 20 years and we now have seven children between us, all under the age of eight. To swim out into the ocean without worrying about needing to get back to them, to have a conversation that was uninterrupted by them, where men were also not present, felt rare and precious. Luxurious, even. We talked about everything: birth, death, health, our bodies and senses of identity, the men we love and have loved and didn’t love, our children, and our hopes and fears for them. We covered fashion, the football, whether or not to get life insurance, how long it’s safe to keep an arancino on the beach before eating it, getting older. The conversation ebbed and flowed – we dipped in and out as we wanted – and somewhere in that very feminine mix of comedy and heartache, profound truths were arrived at (or maybe it was just the spicy margs).There’s a tendency to trivialise girls’ holidays – “oh look, the lasses are getting on the wine!” – and indeed, I just did it with that crack about margaritas. There are certainly some who don’t seem to think that the girls’ holiday is a subject worthy of serious cultural or literary inquiry (the terrible Sex and the City films didn’t help). Yet I knew I wanted to write about one because it is often during those opportunities to step away from your life for a little while, alongside other women who have known you for years and about whom you care deeply, that your emotions are cast in high relief. Perhaps the revelations that follow will bring about change, perhaps they won’t, but the chance to mix freedom, fun and serious self-inquiry should always be taken when it’s presented. I’ve certainly returned home with a new perspective. Time to start planning the next trip.Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett is a Guardian columnist. Her new book,Female, Nudeis out nowExplore more on these topicsFriendshipOpinionWomenFictioncommentShareReuse this content

title: When Maga’s power fades, we cannot abandon those ICE killed | Moira Donegan | The Guardian
description: When this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountable
Author: Moira Donegan
Headline: When this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountable
Content: ‘Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.’Photograph: Shannon Stapleton/ReutersView image in fullscreen‘Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.’Photograph: Shannon Stapleton/ReutersOpinionICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)When Maga’s power fades, we cannot abandon those ICE killedMoira DoneganWhen this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountableSun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.12 EDTShareOne day, when Maga is out of power and the conditions of political possibility have changed, there are going to be people who tell you that the best thing for the country is to move on. There are going to be people who tell you that attempts to hold the purveyors of Trumpism accountable are petty, vindictive, engaging in a kind of petulant recrimination and score-settling. There are going to be people who tell you that there must not be investigations, that it would be a waste of time to have trials. There are going to be people who tell you that it is not wise to change the law to allow for prosecutions and lawsuits; that too great an emphasis on the past will only divide the country, and keep us from looking toward the future. This argument was made after Watergate; it was made after the civil war. It was wrong back then, and it will be wrong again when it is delivered, with piety and smugness, after Trump.When Maga leaves power, all of the officers, bureaucrats and leaders of ICE – from the uniformed thugs in masks on the streets, to the contractors guarding concentration camps, to the suited vultures directing the operation in Washington and drafting legal memos to justify it – all of them must be held accountable. Their immunity from criminal prosecution and civil suits must be eradicated, and their liability must be made retroactive. They must be investigated, tried, fined, punished and ostracized. Needless to say, ICE must be abolished – attempts to merely reform it must be resisted as betrayals of democratic values. But it is not enough to abolish ICE. Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.This much is clear from a slew of recent deaths. Last Tuesday, a business owner and father of three namedLorenzo Salgado Araujowas shot dead in his car by an ICE agent in Houston while he was on his way to work. At 52, he had made an honorable and prosperous life for himself in Texas after immigrating from Mexico 35 years ago, at age 17. His three co-workers who were in the car – he had picked them up to give them a ride to work – survived the shooting, and were disappeared into ICE custody. Later, it turned out that the ICE agents had been looking forsomebody else.Before Salgado’s body was even cold, ICE killed again. Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero was a 25-year-old resident of Biddeford, Maine, who had been born in Colombia. According to his father, he had a work permit, a condition typical of asylum claimants. He worked two jobs – one as a food delivery driver, and another as a custodian at a veterinary office – to support his wife and three-year-old daughter.According to witnesses, his little girl witnessed the ICE agents ram into her father’s car, approach his driver’s side window and then shoot her father dead. The incident occurred at about 7am; the little girl was still in her Bluey pajamas. Angus King, a senator from Maine, was told by the DHS secretary, Markwayne Mullen, that ICE was looking for somebody else when they killed Durán, too.After the agents killed him, ICE proclaimed that Salgado had been trying to run them down with his car, a statement thatwitnesseshave contested. It’s also exactly what ICE said aboutRenee Good, the mother of three they killed in Minneapolis early this year. Video quickly disproved ICE’s account of the Good killing; there is no reason to think that their account of Salgado’s death is true, either.What seems more likely: ICE, a group of reckless, trigger-happy bigots tasked with carrying out the president’s mass deportation and ethnic cleansing regime, is terrorizing communities, stalking disfavored populations, targeting innocent, hardworking people on the basis of their race, and often escalating their interactions to deadly violence.Lorenzo Salgado Araujo is dead. How long will ICE be allowed to hide its face? | Jamil SmithRead moreWe have been here before. The needless, cold-blooded shootings of Salgado and Durán come months after ICE killed two US citizens in Minneapolis, first Good and then the Veterans Affairs hospital nurse Alex Pretti. Their deaths galvanized an already robust civilian anti-ICE resistance in Minneapolis, and sparked protests across the country against ICE’s kidnapping and ethnic cleansing operations. The bad PR clearly got to the president; Greg Bovino, the high-profile border patrol commander who became famous for his Nazi-like attire, lost his job, apparently as a concession to the unpopularity of the raids; Kristi Noem, the Homeland Security secretary who ran the department at the time, didn’t last long, either. But after a brief de-escalation, ICE has now resumed its raids with their former ostentatious cruelty, needless violence and terrorizing gore. “ICE hunts us like animals,” Maria Cervantes, a Mexican immigrant who lives in the Houston neighborhood where ICE killed Salgado,told the Guardian. “I can’t help but think, who’s next? Am I going to get a bullet to the head for being brown?”If we are to rebuild America as a new kind of country – if we are to make it fulfill its promise to be a free and pluralistic democracy of equals – then we have an obligation to make sure that no one ever has to ask that question again, and that those who do receive justice for the suffering, violence and indignity that this regime has inflicted on them. When this is all over, people will tell you to move on. What they will not tell you is that moving on requires forgetting about Salgado, and Durán, and about Good and Pretti, and about everyone else that ICE has killed and will kill. It will require you to leave the insult and injustice of their deaths unanswered. We must refuse to.Moira Donegan is a Guardian US columnistExplore more on these topicsICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)OpinionUS immigrationTrump administrationUS politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Want a relaxing start to your summer holiday? Get an airport divorce | Polly Hudson | The Guardian
description: My husband has a personality transplant within a five-mile radius of any airport. There is no chatting, no dawdling, no time to buy water or go to the loo. But I have happened upon a better way to travel, writes Polly Hudson
Author: Polly Hudson
Headline: My husband has a personality transplant within a five-mile radius of any airport. There is no chatting, no dawdling, no time to buy water or go to the loo. But I have happened upon a better way to travel, writes Polly Hudson
Content: You go your way, I’ll go mine.Photograph: Posed by models; izusek/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenYou go your way, I’ll go mine.Photograph: Posed by models; izusek/Getty ImagesOpinionLife and styleWant a relaxing start to your summer holiday? Get an airport divorcePolly HudsonMy husband has a personality transplant within a five-mile radius of any airport. There is no chatting, no dawdling, no time to buy water or go to the loo. But I have happened upon a better way to travelSun 19 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 05.10 EDTShareIf we are being observed by aliens, they probably wonder why couples on Earth invent so many ways to be apart while together. Thesleep divorce,screen divorce, meal divorce, chore divorce, hobby divorce … “Just split up already!” the beings from Nerfleurg 7 undoubtedly yell at their surveillance screens. However, there’s a new relationship hack in town and it could be the best yet: the airport divorce.In the run-up to holiday season, is there anybody who didn’t read those words and feel wistful? Even if you’re single, going away with a friend, you can still have an airport divorce. This strategy is as all-inclusive as a resort package.An airport divorce entails going your separate ways as soon as you arrive at the terminal, navigating what then occurs in your preferred style, then meeting up again at the gate, ready for boarding, fresh as daisies, without having witnessed the very worst of each other.Yet if some adopt this method, we all have to. What makes the frustration, bewilderment and anger you experience with your airport companions bearable is seeing others who feel the same about theirs, only more so – those visibly seething or openly arguing. But if airport divorces take off, consoling yourself by looking at bickering pairs and thinking: “Well, at least we’re not that bad,” will no longer be an option, because such situations won’t exist. One for all and all for one.My husband – usually easy-going and relaxed – has a personality transplant the moment he’s within a five-mile radius of an airport. He takes charge, even if begged not to do so. Troops have been led into battle more calmly and with less barking of orders: there’s a strict plan, a timeline calculated to the second and zero permitted deviations.There is, of course, no chatting, nor dawdling. Any wild ideas of stopping for even a second before we’re through security – to buy some water or go to the loo – dare not be suggested. He strides ahead, speed-walking in triple time like a clockwork toy wound to max while we scramble behind him. If any of our party had an accident, he would leave us for dead. Hesitation is not in the schedule.If there is a long queue, he’s devastated and incensed, even though, obviously, per his insistence, we have arrived at the airport a full 72 hours ahead of travel. He will shift from foot to foot impatiently, sigh and tut. He cannot be distracted or drawn into conversation about anything else. There is nothing else. Only the airport.Once we’re through to the other side, you might assume he would loosen up a little, but no. He’s now even more focused and vigilant, because what if there’s a sudden switcheroo that catches us out? A gate change, a time change, an airport change? This normally rational man becomes convinced that the entire aeronautical industry is plotting to sabotage our journey. In reality, it’s probably grateful to him, because if there is a gate change he will yelp, then shriek the breaking news so loudly and urgently that he informs far more passengers more quickly than the information board ever could.By the time we arrive at our destination, I have aged two decades and feel more in need of a padded cell than a holiday. My husband, conversely, instantly unwinds, fully embracing yet another personality transplant, this time into chilled-out vacation dude. Of course he does.So although an airport divorce may seem an alien concept, it might just be the answer to a harmonious married life on Earth.Polly Hudson is a freelance writerDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsLife and styleOpinionRelationshipsAirline industryAir transportMarriageFamilycommentShareReuse this content

title: Trump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his own | Jamil Smith | The Guardian
description: The president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects
Author: Jamil Smith
Headline: The president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects
Content: ‘The president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026.’Photograph: Saul Loeb/UPI/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘The president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026.’Photograph: Saul Loeb/UPI/ShutterstockOpinionDonald TrumpTrump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his ownJamil SmithThe president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspectsSun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.04 EDTShareThere is a version of this country in whichDonald Trumptells Americans the truth he has been handed: that their elections are secure. Once, he apparently wanted to.The Atlantic reportedafterThursday night’s addressthat a February 2020 election-security briefing pleased Trump so much he wanted to announce the news himself.
