The press conference never happened. The election did, and Trump lost it.More than six years later, he mocked the assurances he once wanted his name on, describing a system “so broken and so vulnerablethat no one can possibly defend it”. The evidence hadn’t changed. Its usefulness had.Trump is dangerous twice over: in what he does and what he tells us to fear while doing it. Factcheckers agreed he proved nothing. Failing as evidence, his address succeeded as permission – for federal intervention already under way in Fulton county.In his speech, Trump assembled old intelligence, vulnerabilities andliesinto a conspiracy:220 millionUS voter records in Chinese hands, “extremely exposed” voting machines,278,000 supposed noncitizenson the rolls and a deep-state burial of proof. Yet some documents he released contradicted him.Five claims Trump made in primetime address not backed up by evidenceRead moreA 2021 intelligence-community assessmentdistinguished foreign influence from interference with registration, ballot casting, tabulation or results, and found no indication that any foreign actor attempted the latter. A newly released document judged tabulation systemsdifficult to manipulateat election-changing scale;a report on Venezuelan votingnever confirmed large-scale electronic fraud even there. The filesinstead documenteda Russian influence campaign intended to defeat Joe Biden: allegations related to the Ukrainian gas company Burisma spread through American intermediaries, plans for a corruption scandal and fraud narratives involving mail ballots. Trump omitted the foreign influence that aided him and inflated vulnerabilities into interference that did not occur.Trump gave Bill Pulte – the loyalist installed as acting intelligence chief to investigate supposedly “rigged” elections – broad declassification authority. The conservative media figure John Solomonhelped search government filesfor the speech. Yet after millions of documents were reviewed, a White House official told the Atlantic that no evidence showed a foreign power changed votes; Solomonconceded the samefor 2020, 2022 and 2024.The speech named Michigan, but omitted Georgia.In January, FBI agentsseizedroughly 700 boxes of 2020 election materials from Fulton county, including about 150,000 mail ballots, 116,000 cast for Biden. The bureauredirected260 analysts nationwide to work on an investigation into that year’s election. One day before Trump’s speech,ProPublica reportedthat the FBI had explored using artificial intelligence to re-examine ballot-envelope signatures, possibly against only one registration signature. Whoever builds such a system decides how much natural variation counts as suspicion.The predicate was never sound. The allegations behind the seizure had been found false; Georgia’s ballots were countedthree times, once by hand. Yet an FBI source told ProPublica the hunt would continue “whether they find anything or not”. Signature-matching errors fall hardest on voters of color and other vulnerable groups.This is what Trump’s own interference means. He cannot alter a completed count. He can use federal power to place voters, workers and institutions under permanent suspicion,weakening the legitimacyof the next count before it begins.Fulton county was no random choice. Georgia is where Black opposition to Trump became governing power. Black votersmade upabout 29% of the 2020 electorate; nearly nine in 10 supported Biden. Fulton gave Bidenan advantage approaching 243,000 votesin a state hewon by 11,779. That coalition then elected Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff, transferring the Senate to Democrats.Trump’s falsehoods made targets ofRuby Freeman and Shaye Moss, two Black women who counted ballots; the federal project now extends suspicion to their county. Trump carried Georgia in 2024. That victory did not end his pursuit of Fulton. Theracismof this project requires no window into his heart. It is visible in the jurisdiction selected, the method applied and the voters who will bear its errors.Trump has declared war on elections in the name of protecting them | Austin SaratRead moreThe president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026. His justice department haslost its first 15 district-court attemptsto obtain states’ unredacted voter rolls, containing driver’s license numbers and partial social security numbers – more sensitive information than Trump showed China possessing. Having failed in court and struggled in Congress, he propagandized on television.His intelligence office identified voting-machine vulnerabilitiesbut found no hacking. The White House shelved its report as the safe installation window closed, thenemptied the agencysupporting voting-system certification. The day before the speech, House RepublicansattachedTrump’sSafeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, or Save America Act, to a state department spending bill and passed it, 217–209. The bill would impose new identification requirements on mail voting, require documentary proof of citizenship to register and compel every state to send its complete federal voter list to the Department of Homeland Security within 30 days.Trump neither declared an emergency nor claimed control over state elections. But he ordered DHSto direct statesto remove alleged noncitizens, and by Friday enforcement had arrived. A week earlier, the Department of Justice hadwarned election officials nationwideof criminal liability for knowingly retaining noncitizens or facilitating their votes.On Friday, the DHS secretary,Markwayne Mullin, claimedthat a database known as Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements had identified more than 250,000 potential noncitizens registered in California, New Jersey, Nevada and Pennsylvania. His department offered no evidence and gave those four states two weeks to confirm cooperation. Mullin separately threatened election officials nationwide withcriminal chargesif they refused to run their rolls through the database. The database canmisidentify naturalized citizens; last month, afederal judge blockedthe revamped system from checking voter rolls, finding it less accurate and a threat to privacy and voting rights.The speech failed to prove an emergency. Yet within a day, Trump’s government was acting as though it had. Fulton was the laboratory. The Save Act is thearchitecture for taking it national. Trump can neither erase his loss nor the Black political power essential to it. So he no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects. To this president, any election he loses is a crime scene.Jamil Smith is a Guardian US columnistExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionTrump administrationUS politicsUS midterm elections 2026GeorgiaLaw (US)RacecommentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on pubs: football gave them a boost – now they need another | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Communal spaces where people can share big moments are a vital part of our shared life
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Communal spaces where people can share big moments are a vital part of our shared life
Content: Fans watch the World Cup semifinal between England and Argentina at Tailors pub in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, on 15 July.Photograph: Oli Scarff/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFans watch the World Cup semifinal between England and Argentina at Tailors pub in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, on 15 July.Photograph: Oli Scarff/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionPubsThe Guardian view on pubs: football gave them a boost – now they need anotherEditorialCommunal spaces where people can share big moments are a vital part of our shared lifeSun 19 Jul 2026 12.25 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTShare“People can’t get enough of football fever,” apub landlord, Sam Hale,told the Guardian last week. The World Cup provided the UK’s struggling hospitality industry with a much-needed boost, withpubs benefitingfrom extended opening hours and one estimate suggesting that an extra 5.5m pints were sold in the group stages alone. The tournament lifted customers’ spirits too – even if it ended in disappointment for England and Scotland fans – as millions of people relished the communal spectacle.These sociable few weeks could not have come at a better time for pubs, which areclosing at a rate of two a day, with about 2,000 gone since 2020. This reflects a long-term trend away from going out, combined with pressures from rising costs including energy bills, business rates, wages and national insurance contributions. The hope in the sector, and among its advocates, is that the temporary uplift of the past few weeks will focus minds on the importance of venues, including pubs, in promoting social cohesion and “watercooler moments”.The wider industry is campaigning for a halving of VAT on hospitality from 20% to 10%, and more than 288,000 people have signed a petition. Given the likely cost to the Treasury of at least £10bn, this proposal is unlikely to be taken up. A thinktank, Tax Policy Associates, has pointed out that multinational businesses, including McDonald’s, would be thebiggest beneficiaries. But with a recent survey finding that almost a quarter of pubs and restaurants arelosing money, ministers should be looking for ways to help.This is not just a matter of owners’ finances. Spaces where people can meet and talk to each other, and share special occasions, are an important part of our collective life. The American sociologist Ray Oldenburg coined a name for them, “third places”, and pubs are not the only ones that fit the bill. His point was the value of the public realm more widely – parks, libraries, youth clubs. And it is important to recognise that pubs’ overall impact includes harms linked to alcohol. But sales ofno- and low-alcohol beerare booming and, particularly in rural areas, pubs are vital facilities at a time where loneliness is also a serious public health issue.Theshrinking number of entry-level jobsin sectors including hospitality is another concern. In his interim report for the government on young people and work, Alan Milburn argued that this is one factor behind the rising number of 16- to 24-year-olds who are neither in work nor education.It is easy to be nostalgic about pubs, as it is about many things that are associated with the past. They are not the be-all and end-all of thriving neighbourhoods. One reason why people are staying in more is that there is more to watch on television than there used to be. It is good if people enjoy being in their homes.But there is real value in making an effort to be around others, having face-to-face conversations rather than tapping out messages. Recent research has pointed tosocial isolation among young adultsas a growing problem. Experts differ as to explanations, but the pandemic, home working and social media have all been suggested. A worthwhile legacy of this World Cup would be a strengthened recognition of the value of shared, in-person experiences after the fever has died down.Explore more on these topicsPubsOpinionWorld Cup 2026Consumer spendingEconomicsRetail industryHospitality industryHealtheditorialsShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on perceptions of superpowers: the world isn’t Chinamaxxing, it’s America-avoidant | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Startling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to Washington
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Startling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to Washington
Content: Chongqing. ‘China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future of cyberpunk cities.’Photograph: zhangshuang/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChongqing. ‘China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future of cyberpunk cities.’Photograph: zhangshuang/Getty ImagesOpinionChinaThe Guardian view on perceptions of superpowers: the world isn’t Chinamaxxing, it’s America-avoidantEditorialStartling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to WashingtonSun 19 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTShareEarlier this year, a spate of western TikTok and Instagram creatorsannounced that they were at “a very Chinese time in my life”– drinking hot water and wearing slippers around their homes. TheChinamaxxing phenomenonprimarily reflected the constant need of influencers for novel content. But social media trends also reflect a growing fascination with China, as the economic historian Adam Toozehas noted.While it broods over China’s economic, military andtechnicaladvance, the Trump administration is accelerating Beijing’s diplomatic progress, as striking new polling by the Pew Research Center shows. For the first time, China and Xi Jinping areseen more favourably than the USand its president in most of the 36 major countries surveyed – including Canada, Mexico and Australia. Only in six countries did people rate the US more highly. While Beijing has seen significant improvements in some places, the slump in Washington’s standing is more dramatic. Across 20 countries, the median percentage with a favourable view of Chinarose from 32% to 46% between 2023and this year; for the US, it fell from 58% to 36%. Views of China were much more positive inLatin America, Africa and parts of south and south-east Asiathan in wealthier European and east Asian countries.On Thursday, Mr Trumpaccused China of interfering with the 2020 election– casting doubt on polls ahead of midterms that are forecast to hit Republicans hard. Never mind the lack of evidence; heoffered Argentinians a $20bn economic incentiveto vote in line with his preference last October. In nearly every country surveyed, more people thought the US interfered in other countries than thought China did so. Mr Trump’s demands totake over Greenland, the abduction of Venezuela’s then leader Nicolás Maduro and the illegal war on Iran have all left their mark. Even the World Cup, which should have been an easy US soft power win, has been tarred by hisexclusionandbullying.View image in fullscreenXi Jinping and Donald Trump.Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/AP Pool/APGrave concerns about Beijing remain. Though it has dialled back displays of aggressive “wolf-warrior” diplomacy, it is forceful in its dealings. But it may benefit from lower expectations, and it looks predictable beside Trump-created turmoil. Famously, Beijing has just one ally: North Korea. Washington has more than 50 – but seems to accord them less respect than adversaries. Beijing detained two Canadian citizens for more than 1,000 daysin retaliation for Ottawa’s arrestof the Huawei executive Meng Wanzhou due to a US warrant. But Mr Trump announced that Canada will be the 51st state.China is increasingly investing inoverseas influence operations, including working with or amplifying local social media content creators. Content may range from paid-for Xinjiang travelogues intended to erase concerns about the persecution of Uyghurs, to softer material about food or technology.The Pew findings may furtherconvince it of Washington’s hegemonic decline. China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future ofcyberpunk cities, while the west struggles with a declining standard of living – not least because it is useful in the face of domestic disillusionment.But public perceptions of other countriesare often complexand volatile. US standingreboundedafter dropping sharply over the Iraq war; China’s favourability ratings fell during the pandemic, reflecting its handling of the outbreak. The lasting lesson for both superpowers should surely be that presentation shapes opinion, but others will judge them on their actions – and respond accordingly.Explore more on these topicsChinaOpinionXi JinpingAsia PacificDonald TrumpSocial mediaDigital mediaeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham must raise the spirits of a disillusioned public | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: Letters: Readers give their views on Britain’s new prime minister
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Readers give their views on Britain’s new prime minister
Content: Andy Burnham on a visit to Gravesend town pier in Kent on Friday.Photograph: Gareth Fuller/PAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham on a visit to Gravesend town pier in Kent on Friday.Photograph: Gareth Fuller/PAAndy BurnhamLettersAndy Burnham must raise the spirits of a disillusioned publicReaders give their views on Britain’s new prime ministerSun 19 Jul 2026 11.46 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTShareHow fortuitous for Andy Burnham that just as Keir Starmer departs from Downing Street, the threat from the menacing presence of Nigel Farage is also beginning to dissolve (Editorial, 15 July). Burnham is an unknown quantity to most of the public so he needs to define who he is, what he believes in and what he will do about the things people care about most – the cost of living, the housing market and the parlous state of public services.In stark contrast to Keir Starmer’s cautious and forensic persona, Burnham is a skilled operator, familiar with the rough cut and thrust of politics, and a track record of fusing the energy and talents of people from diverse perspectives to work towards a common good.Every one of these attributes, together with a great deal of good fortune and Burnham’s renowned skills in communicating, will be needed to raise the spirits of an embattled, disillusioned and sceptical public. Starmer’s departure from office will be to no avail if it is not followed swiftly by the public feeling an increasing sense of hope and wellbeing.Peter RiddleWirksworth, DerbyshireAs a former Labour member who resigned this year following the announcement of reforms to refugee status, I felt Andy Burnham’s Labour leadership speech was inspiring and full of hope (Burnham declares he is ready to ‘beat Britain’s new right’ as he becomes Labour leader, 17 July). I noted that the speech didn’t provide any deep insight into his foreign policy thinking.Keir Starmer’s two-year reign has improved EU relationships, played a key role in shaping Europe’s policy towardsUkraineand improved trade agreements with several nations. Mr Burnham and the new foreign secretary need to maintain the pace and focus on these three critical issues.Improved trade with the EU provides new opportunities for young people and businesses. Ukrainian citizens, the EU and the UK need to know the war is not placed on a lower level of priority by the UK government given Burnham’s overflowing in-tray.Paresh MotlaThame, OxfordshirePeter Walker (Analysis, 17 July) identifies “an almost Johnsonian sense of mischief” in Andy Burnham’s acceptance speech, but which Johnson is he referencing? Until relatively recently, I would have assumed that he meant the 18th-century lexicographer Samuel Johnson. If it’s a latter-day Boris Johnson, perhaps the dictionary definition of “Johnsonian” needs updating. Pity Dr Johnson isn’t around to do the job.Steve NewmanSunderlandBefore Andy Burnham starts nationalising stuff “to undo the Thatcherism of the 1980s” and we party like it’s 1966, perhaps he could have a word with the likes of Greg Jackson at Octopus Energy and Jeff Bezos at Amazon instead of assuming that the state running utilities is the holy grail. I for one I don’t want my energy to come from Ed Miliband.Charles FosterChalfont St Peter, BuckinghamshireIf Andy Burnham is serious about recovering the Labour values of 40 years ago, I look forward to the return ofclause IVto the party constitution.Steve KingLondonShouldAndy Burnhamappoint a minister for drought? It worked in 1976.Hilary BirdKilndown, KentSo Andy Burnham has “a plan”. Or is that a cunning plan?Dr Phil DawsonNewcastle upon TyneHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamKeir StarmerLabourLabour party leadershipReform UKUkrainelettersShareReuse this content

title: Israel’s brutality cannot continue in plain sight | Israel | The Guardian
description: Letters: Gwyn Daniel and Phil Gaunt respond to an article by Nesrine Malik about the horrors taking place in Israel’s prisons
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Gwyn Daniel and Phil Gaunt respond to an article by Nesrine Malik about the horrors taking place in Israel’s prisons
Content: Eighteen months ago Dr Hussam Abu Safiya was seized by Israeli forces and has since been held without charge or trial.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyView image in fullscreenEighteen months ago Dr Hussam Abu Safiya was seized by Israeli forces and has since been held without charge or trial.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyIsraelLettersIsrael’s brutality cannot continue in plain sightGwyn DanielandPhil Gauntrespond to an article by Nesrine Malik about the horrors taking place in Israel’s prisonsSun 19 Jul 2026 11.45 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTShareNesrine Malik’s incisive piece (In Israel’s prisons, torture and death have become a norm that it barely tries to hide, 13 July) lays bare the fact that all the illegal detentions, deaths, torture and sexual abuse in Israeli prisons are not aberrations from a legal system, but deeply embedded in the practices and intentions of the state. She mentions the psychological dimension to deliberately eroding the capacity of Palestinian civil society to exert any kind of autonomy.As mental health practitioners, working alongside our Palestinian co-professionals, we have long recorded the ways in which practices of degradation, humiliation and debilitation constitute attacks on minds and relationships as well as bodies. They are designed to undermine the fabric of family, social and community life, and to destroy those systems of connectivity upon which all citizens, especially children, are dependent if they are to flourish. The carceral system is part of a set of state interventions which not only detain, murder, maim and mutilate Palestinian bodies, but also conduct an unrelenting assault on their growth, dignity, personhood and identity.Malik is right to highlight the extent to which western governments and most mainstream media act as if it is the “extremist fringes” rather than the state itself that should be criticised or sanctioned. In doing so, they fail to heed the pronouncements from Israeli government representatives themselves and do a profound disservice to the victims of this violent, racist and unrestrained regime.Gwyn DanielUK and InternationalPalestineMental Health NetworksThank you once again for Nesrine Malik’s continued efforts to draw attention to the brutality that has been inflicted on the Palestinian people. While Andy Burnham has acknowledged that the British government’s response to the Israel bombardment and mass killing in Gaza “didn’t get it right”, there have since been innumerable atrocities in Gaza and the West Bank, even after a ceasefire was declared.Sanctions on illegal settlers who attack and kill Palestinians will not be enough – the Israeli government provides them with immunity, leaves the people of Gaza camped amid devastation and detains thousands of Palestinians without trial in reportedly dreadful conditions. The UK and Europe need to act far more decisively regarding trade withIsraeland take meaningful steps to ending the illegal occupation of Palestine.Phil GauntDumbartonHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsIsraelGazaPalestineBenjamin NetanyahuMental healthHealthAndy BurnhamlettersShareReuse this content

title: I’ve seen first-hand how to divert children away from lives of crime | Youth justice | The Guardian
description: Letters: In the 1980s, I was director of the Northampton juvenile liaison bureau, which became a model nationwide, writes Jim Crook
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: In the 1980s, I was director of the Northampton juvenile liaison bureau, which became a model nationwide, writes Jim Crook
Content: A young offenders institution in Aylesbury, Buckinghamshire.Photograph: Andrew Aitchison/Corbis/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA young offenders institution in Aylesbury, Buckinghamshire.Photograph: Andrew Aitchison/Corbis/Getty ImagesYouth justiceLettersI’ve seen first-hand how to divert children away from lives of crimeIn the 1980s, I was director of the Northampton juvenile liaison bureau, which became a model nationwide, writesJim CrookSun 19 Jul 2026 11.41 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 11.43 EDTShareI completely agree with Kirsty Brimelow that the age of criminal responsibility should be raised from the age of 10 to 14 (Jailing children does not make us safer – we need to get rid of this Dickensian delusion, 11 July). Also, that many more children could be diverted away from the criminal justice system. In the 1980s, I was director of the Northampton juvenile liaison bureau, a multi-disciplinary team that was successful in diverting more than 80% of the children between the ages of 10 to 17 who were offending away from the criminal justice system by employing informal methods of control, support and care, including restorative justice.It gained a national reputation and became a model for the creation of youth-offending teams. Research revealed no increase in youth offending or repeat offences as a result of this policy. Due to the reduction in the number of juvenile court sittings and fewer children being processed through the criminal justice system, it saved in today’s money nearly £2m per year in Northampton alone and avoided children acquiring criminal convictions and criminal records. Research also found that the biggest impact on preventing further offending by the majority of children was being caught, spending a night in police cells, and the consequences of having to face parents.Jim CrookStillington, North YorkshireExplore more on these topicsYouth justiceUK criminal justiceYoung peopleChildrenlettersShareReuse this content

title: A rhyme to recall rising temperatures | Climate crisis | The Guardian
description: Brief letters: The new normal | Cool school | Italian idiom | Word Wheel | Covid failings
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Brief letters: The new normal | Cool school | Italian idiom | Word Wheel | Covid failings
Content: A sweltering day on a scorched Wimbledon Common, London.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA sweltering day on a scorched Wimbledon Common, London.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockBrief lettersClimate crisisLettersA rhyme to recall rising temperaturesThe new normal | Cool school | Italian idiom | Word Wheel | Covid failingsSun 19 Jul 2026 11.37 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTShareYour article (‘Unprecedented’ changes in UK climate are normalizing extremes, report says, 15 July) reminded me of the rhyme to help people convert from fahrenheit to centigrade when I was a schoolgirl in the late 60s. This was “five, 10 and 21 – winter, spring and summer sun”, denoting the then average temperatures. Maybe this could be updated for the 2020s. I was thinking of “10, 15, 28 – reduce emissions before too late”.Liz HaggartBearsted, KentOf all the inequalities in education, access to tree shade would not be high on my list (Private schools offer 41% more tree shade than state schools in England, 16 July). It’s a poor metric, as if a state school is forced to sell an open playing field, the proportion of its site covered by trees could increase. I would look at the extent of air-conditioned classrooms first.Peter WestLondonRhiannon Lucy Cosslett writes that learning languages slows brain ageing (12 July). I enjoy learning Italian idioms. The one to match “it’s another kettle of fish” ise un altro paio di maniche(“it’s another pair of sleeves”). Still trying at 88.Les BonneyCheltenham, GloucestershireAs aCrystal Palacefan, I was perturbed that among the words I found in the Word Wheel (13 July print edition) were “eagle”and “relegated”.Is this a worrying portent?John BryantTopsham, ExeterThe letter from Laura Matthew (As a frontline worker, my anger over the Tories’ handling of Covid has not abated, 16 July) should be engraved in stone and made a national monument.Leila RobertsMickley, North YorkshireHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsClimate crisisBrief lettersLanguagesExtreme weatherSchoolsTrees and forestsCovid inquiryPrivate schoolslettersShareReuse this content

title: Ryan Fox crowned Open champion as heavyweights wilt under pressure | The Open | The Guardian
description: On a dramatic final day of the Open, Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of a former Black Caps captain, emerged victorious
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: On a dramatic final day of the Open, Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of a former Black Caps captain, emerged victorious
Content: Ryan Fox holds the Claret Jug after his victory in the 2026 Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreenRyan Fox holds the Claret Jug after his victory in the 2026 Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe OpenRyan Fox crowned Open champion as heavyweights wilt under pressureUSA’s Cameron Young falls short by a single shotRory McIlroy hints at DeChambeau joining Irish OpenEwan Murrayat Royal BirkdaleSun 19 Jul 2026 14.57 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat a way to enhance a family dynasty. Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of one of the country’s cricket captains, is the Open champion. Three generations, three sports, one Claret Jug. Fox displayed the fortitude of Grant, his old man, to snatch victory from Cameron Young on such a dramatic Open Championship Sunday.Fox took to the 18th tee level with Young at nine under par. The American had finished more than two hours earlier, his total looking ever-more promising as others took it in turn to wilt under the heat of Royal Birkdale. Fox laughed in the face of adversity. He crashed a drive 330 yards down the fairway, flicked an iron to 11ft and holed out for a birdie three. This will trigger quite the party, including in his homeland. This Open needed something to switch the conversation fromBryson DeChambeau and a rules breach. In Fox, the tournament found a bold and deserving champion.View image in fullscreenRyan Fox celebrates after winning the Open on the final green.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianFor Young, there will be pain. A par at the last would have earned him 10 under par and, as it was to transpire, a playoff. He found the bunker from the 18th tee. A six-iron, intended to reach the front of the green, rebounded from the top of the trap and into thick grass. Young, who is becoming a major nearly man, scrambled superbly for a bogey five. It was not enough, alas.The Open 2026: Ryan Fox wins the Open – as it happenedRead moreOn a week dominated by sunburn and grass burn, some believed it was fate that Sam Burns would triumph. Instead, the 54-hole leader wobbled after reaching 11 under inside two holes. He regressed to minus eight at the turn, where he was to stay. It was Fox who was to deal best with golf’s version of scorched-earth syndrome.At one point, it looked as if matters were conspiring against Fox. His tee shot to the perilous par-three 15th hit the bank of a bunker, from where it flew back to a position where the 39-year-old had no scope to even hit his ball forward. Fox escaped with a four. He had an opportunity to reach double digits under par on the penultimate hole yet missed. In the clubhouse, a pensive Young was edging ever closer to extra holes. He was denied that by a Fox attack.Kim Si-woo had taken the lead when holing from off the green at the 6th. He remained in the equation until a poor closing stretch. Kim shared sixth with Casey Jarvis and Lucas Herbert.Tommy Fleetwood, the hometown hero, could not end his major wait at Birkdale. Still, the moment of Sunday belonged to him. He kissed his hand, waved and touched his heart in front of adoring galleries after holing out for a 72nd-hole birdie. If there really are golfing gods, he will win an Open. His finish this time was a tie for fourth. Things had started so promisingly on Sunday for Fleetwood, who triggered roars that reverberated around the course with a birdie from 73ft on the 1st.View image in fullscreenTommy Fleetwood acknowledges the love of the crowd as he walks up the fairway on his 72nd hole.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianScottie Scheffler should really have become the first man to win back-to-back Opens since Padraig Harrington here in 2008. The taking of 32 putts in round two fatally harmed the world No 1’s cause. Scheffler flew from the traps on Sunday, throwing darts towards flags and playing the first five holes in three under. He had slipped to six under on the 15th but roared back again; birdies at the 16th and 17th left him one from the lead while playing the last. Scheffler carved his tee shot right, missed the green and took three from there. A finish beside Fleetwood at minus seven was a real tale of missed opportunity. “I guess it just wasn’t my week,” Scheffler said. His consistency of results remains quite incredible.Bob MacIntyre’s latest tantrum, this time slamming his driver into the ground, received a code of conduct warning from tournament officials. “I’m going to do it for the rest of my career,” the Scot said. “I try and toe the line, but when you feel you’re playing a golf course that you can hit it pretty much anywhere and there’s no real punishment and then you do hit one slightly off line and you get the biggest punishment you can possibly get, it’s just a sore one. I’m going to react aggressively at times. Part of my DNA.” Perhaps it shouldn’t be. Professional golfers set examples.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDeChambeau was hovering on the leaderboard before a disastrous triple-bogey seven on the 11th. The record books will show a tie for 14th as his best major finish of 2026. The R&A quietly breathed a sigh of relief that he did not close only two from the lead, that being the penalty for his errant footwork on the 5th during Friday’s round.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau, hit with a two-stroke penalty on Friday, tied for 14th.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe leaving of a bunker shot by the 18th green in sand rather summed up Rory McIlroy’s Open. The Masters champion signed off with a 71 for a one-under total. McIlroy will now take a holiday before returning to competitive action in the PGA Tour’s playoffs. He was asked rather mischievously whether he would welcome DeChambeau at the Irish Open this year, as has been rumoured as a possibility. The event takes place at Donald Trump’s Doonbeg resort. “I think that would be great for the tournament, absolutely,” McIlroy said. “Having someone like Bryson at the Irish Open, it would be great for the tournament.”McIlroy’sscathing sentiment towards DeChambeauhad been the theme of Saturday at the Open. Day four proved just as eye-catching. Inside the ropes, this time. Fox has just sampled a life-changing moment.Explore more on these topicsThe OpenRory McIlroyScottie SchefflerEuropean TourPGA TourGolfUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Glory eludes hometown hero Tommy Fleetwood in his dream made reality | The Open | The Guardian
description: Southport local said the ovation he received on the 18th was ‘incredibly special’ after his title chances fizzle out
Author: Andy Bull
Headline: Southport local said the ovation he received on the 18th was ‘incredibly special’ after his title chances fizzle out
Content: ‘The atmosphere walking down 18 was beyond anything I ever dreamed,’ said Tommy Fleetwood, who finished three off the lead.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘The atmosphere walking down 18 was beyond anything I ever dreamed,’ said Tommy Fleetwood, who finished three off the lead.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe OpenGlory eludes hometown hero Tommy Fleetwood in his dream made realitySouthport local said the ovation he received on the 18th was ‘incredibly special’ after his title chances fizzle outAndy Bullat Royal BirkdaleSun 19 Jul 2026 15.55 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTwo o’clock on a sunny Sunday afternoon, five hours or so to go in the 154th Open Championship, and here comes Tommy Fleetwood up the 1st fairway, five shots off the lead. A delay in one of the groups up ahead meant that Fleetwood has had to stand and wait 15 minutes at the tee box, which is maybe why, when he finally got up to play his opening shot, he pulled it left into the rough stuff behind a fairway bunker. It was the first time he’d missed that shot all week.From there, Fleetwood had caught his second shot a little thinner than he wanted. He shrugged as he followed its flight, twisted his lips into a grimace as he watched the ball drop and stop about 20 metres short of where it needed to be. “Head up, kid!” someone cried out. “Head up!”Ryan Fox crowned Open champion as heavyweights wilt under pressureRead moreOn Saturday night, Fleetwood said he had been imagining this Sunday at the Open just about as long as he can remember. “I’ve done it most days since I was seven years old, and it still hasn’t stopped,” he said. “That’s what life’s about, right? You dream about the biggest things possible and then you try and make it a reality.”Now here it was at last, real as it gets. Sunday at Royal Birkdale, a clear blue sky over his head, parched brown grass beneath his feet, a shot at the Open Championship in front.And his home crowd all around, every step of the way. “Walking up to every green, it’s like the most amazing ovation that you can imagine,” Fleetwood said. These aren’t strangers on the other side of the ropes, they’re friends, and friends of friends. They’re people he was at school with, used to play football with, people who worked with his dad, or used to get their hair cut by his mum, people who worked with him sweeping leaves at Southport & Ainsdale, who shared a drink with him in the clubhouse at Formby Hall.“I still try to stay in my bubble,” Fleetwood said. “It just happens that there’s like thousands of people in my bubble with me that are willing me on.” However many times he pictured the moment in his head, he can’t ever have been standing over this putt in front of him on the 1st, 73ft from the front of the green to a pin set tucked into the very back corner.But here it is. And now the ball is rolling and all of a sudden people are starting to shout out and the ball’s closing in and now even the marshals who are supposed to be controlling the crowd have turned around to watch and as it drops into the cup everyone’s up and screaming and leaping and Fleetwood is pumping his fists and the roar they made in that moment must have carried clean over the beach and out across the Irish Sea.View image in fullscreenTommy Fleetwood draws a crowd as he tees off at the 7th.Photograph: Richard Heathcote/Getty ImagesFour shots back became three shots back when Fleetwood drove the green and chipped his second shot on for a tap-in birdie at the 5th. By the time he made an 8ft putt for birdie on the 8th he was only two shots behind. He was eight under now, and in that moment everyone there on the course, and watching on TV, listening on the radio, felt that maybe, just maybe, he really was going to do it. Like Fleetwood said himself: “Sometimes dreams do come true, we watch it all the time.”And sometimes they don’t.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAt the 9th Fleetwood overhit his approach and it skittered off the green over the back up into the lee of the grandstand. Everyone watching winced. It got worse. His next shot was his ugliest yet. It blew 30ft past the hole, and this time, as hard as everyone around hoped for him, he wasn’t able to hole it. It was his first bogey, and it seemed to break the spell. He dropped a second shot at the 10th, when he blew his tee shot wide right, and a third at the 11th, where he took three putts. That meant he had dropped three shots in as many holes.He would say later that the par-five 14th was the one that finished him. He was greenside with a chip for birdie, but it burned the hole and passed 3ft by.“My dream might come true, it might not,” Fleetwood had said last week, “but you’ll never find out if yours will unless you chase it.” By the time he got to the 18th, just before six o’clock, the gallery had made peace with the idea that it wasn’t going to happen for him this year. Maybe he had too. He scored a birdie on 17, was an inch off holing his approach on 18. That left him seven under, three shots off the winning score, tied for fourth.“There’s disappointment that I didn’t win when I was in a good position, but the atmosphere walking down 18, that was beyond anything I ever dreamed,” Fleetwood said. “As a kid, when you’re dreaming of having putts to win the Open, that’s one thing, but I could never imagine something like that ovation on 18. That was incredibly special.”Explore more on these topicsThe OpenPGA TourEuropean TourGolfUS sportsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping control | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Jonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine climb, having been woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Jonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine climb, having been woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls
Content: Remco Evenepoel celebrates as he crosses the finish line ahead of Tadej Pogacar at Plateau de Solaison.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPAView image in fullscreenRemco Evenepoel celebrates as he crosses the finish line ahead of Tadej Pogacar at Plateau de Solaison.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPATour de France 2026Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping controlDane was woken by doping control at 2am on day of stageRemco Evenepoel outsprints Pogacar to claim victoryJeremy Whittleat Plateau de SolaisonSun 19 Jul 2026 12.15 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine stage on Sunday, only hours after being woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls. He will have collarbone surgery in the next few days.The double Tour winner, who alsowon the Giro d’Italia this year, climbed gingerly into a race ambulance, his right arm in a sling, after falling as the main peloton exited a roundabout in Bonneville.Tour de France 2026: Vingegaard crashes out before Evenepoel wins on stage 15 – liveRead moreVingegaard andTadej Pogacarwere subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls, outside the normal window of 6am to 11pm, before stage 15, as the race headed to Plateau de Solaison in the Savoyard Alps. Vingegaard was tested at 2am by the International Testing Agency (ITA), while Pogacar’s unexpected knock on the door came at 5am.Pogacar described Vingegaard’s 2am wake-up call as “inhuman” and suggested that it “ruined” his longstanding rival’s sleep and could have been a factor in his crash. Before the stage start on Sunday, Vingegaard said: “I was sleeping very well. It’s good that they test, but when it’s affecting performance and your sleep, I don’t think it’s so good.”Pogacar said the disturbance could “be a small percentage in why he crashed because he probably didn’t sleep good”, adding: “I know how it feels. They woke me up at five in the morning also, but at least I got my deep sleep. It’s better than if they come at two.”Pogacar’s own performance seemed unaffected by the nocturnal disturbance. Once again he led the way up the final climb, this time with his UAE Emirates XRG teammate Isaac del Toro and Remco Evenepoel in tow, moving to the front of the race on the steep final climb, just as Vingegaard, cradling his right arm, gingerly climbed the steps to the Tour’s mobile hospital.But for once Pogacar, the four-time winner, did not have the upper hand and was unable to come past Evenepoel, as the Belgian led home the sprint for the stage victory. Behind the trio, Paul Seixas and Florian Lipowitz – Evenepoel’s Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe teammate – gave chase but both lost almost a minute in the overall standings.In a statement the ITA said: “The ITA is conscious that night-time testing may be disruptive to riders’ rest and recovery. Due consideration has therefore been given to minimise as much as possible the impact testing activities have on riders.“However, the ITA, on behalf of the UCI [the governing body], has the responsibility to ensure the effectiveness of the cycling anti‑doping programme and the integrity of cycling races.”View image in fullscreenJonas Vingegaard descends earlier on stage 15.Photograph: Anne-Christine Poujoulat/AFP/Getty ImagesThe statement added that “effective testing must, in limited and justified circumstances, be capable to occur outside standard daytime hours. All testing activities conducted at the Tour are subject to the safeguards established under the world anti-doping code, the UCI anti-doping rules and French law, with due consideration for riders’ welfare, equity and fundamental rights.”While nocturnal tests are highly unusual, the UCI’s rules state that tests outside the normal hours of 6am to 11pm may be allowed if there is a “serious and specific suspicion that the rider may be engaged in doping”.Other teams confirmed that during the Tour they have also undergone tests as early as five in the morning. Pogacar said that recently the pattern of controls he had experienced had been “strange”, adding: “They’re doing different testing than usual. For example, I was counting how many controls I had at home since the first of January.“When I was at home, I was already waking up every day at six to check the doorbell because I had so many visits. I didn’t want to go for dinner or the supermarket even, because they were coming at random hours.“If this is necessary to still prove that we are riding clean, then I totally accept it and I commit to this, but it’s a bit shit when you sleep four hours in one day.“If Jonas was tested at two in the morning, he slept less than four hours, it ruined his deep sleep and then completely ruined the night. But it is what it is, and if this is what we have to do, we do it.”Evenepoel, meanwhile, who has now risen to second in the overall standings behind Pogacar, was typically outspoken. “To be honest, I would send my team tonight to wake them [anti-doping officials] at three in the night, to let them feel how it is, and to take blood,” the double Olympic champion said.“It’s inhuman and this is something we actually should not accept as athletes, because everybody knows sleep is the only recovery that we have in the night. And if that gets disturbed, it makes it even harder. I hope it doesn’t happen any more in the future because it’s quite disrespectful.”Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingJonas VingegaardTadej PogacarShareReuse this content

title: England take India ODI series after Duckett’s ton and Bethell’s brilliant star turn | Cricket | The Guardian
description: Rohit Sharma made 138 for India but England’s 387-3 secured a 27-run win as Ben Duckett hit 141 and Jacob Bethell made 91 and bowled seven overs for 49
Author: Ali Martin
Headline: Rohit Sharma made 138 for India but England’s 387-3 secured a 27-run win as Ben Duckett hit 141 and Jacob Bethell made 91 and bowled seven overs for 49
Content: Ben Duckett and Jacob Bethell made explosive contributions to England’s record score.Photograph: Graeme Wilcockson/Focus Images Ltd/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenBen Duckett and Jacob Bethell made explosive contributions to England’s record score.Photograph: Graeme Wilcockson/Focus Images Ltd/ShutterstockCricketEngland take India ODI series after Duckett’s ton and Bethell’s brilliant star turn3rd ODI: England, 387-3, bt India, 360-7, by 27 runsRohit Sharma hits searing century but India fall shortAli Martinat Lord'sSun 19 Jul 2026 14.55 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter a cascade of wickets during the much-criticised Test here six weeks ago came a landslide of one-day international runs. In the end, despite a ferocious century from Rohit Sharma that turned Lord’s into his personal playground, England had enough on the board to seal a 2-1 series victory against India.Harry Brook must have thought it was mission accomplished for his side at the halfway stage, with Ben Duckett’s 141 from 135 balls having powered England to 387 for three. Both were men’s ODI records at Lord’s, with Duckett having surpassed Viv Richards and the unbeaten 138 he made during the World Cup in 1979.England beat India by 27 runs in third and deciding men’s cricket ODI – as it happenedRead moreBut amid speculation about his international future – reports of a recent tap on the shoulder – Sharma erupted with a display of controlled brutality. His 138 from 110 balls, first allied with another glossy 77 from Shubman Gill, had India tracking with the target and their vocal supporters dreaming of a famous heist.That was until Jacob Bethell, fresh from 91 with the bat, finally slid one of his left-arm tweakers past a sweep shot from Sharma and into the stumps to leave India at 260 for two in the 39th over. It was the key moment in a run chase in which the tourists finished on 360 for seven and lost by 27 runs.Brook later credited the head coach, Brendon McCullum, for the introduction of Bethell, having nipped off the field for an over to seek his advice. The pair have built up a strong rapport, with T20 and ODI series wins against India this summer. But it has also pushed Brook forward as a candidate for the Test captaincy under the next head coach in that format.“It’d be tough to do [all three jobs] but I’m not saying it’s impossible,” Brook said. “I’m extremely pleased with the way we’ve performed in this series after going 1-0 down – the fight, camaraderie and belief we have shown to win is credit to the team and the coaches.”One of the differences on the day was Jos Buttler’s incendiary, unbeaten 41 from 13 balls at the back end of England’s innings, while Brook was also blessed with more bowling options than his opposite number. Among them was Sam Curran, whose array of cutters and moon-balls claimed four for 75 and shut down Virat Kohli’s maiden half-century at Lord’s for 74.Brook had also won a handy toss, even if Gill, without Jasprit Bumrah due to a knee injury sustained in ­Cardiff, said he would have bowled first. In Bumrah’s absence, only Prasidh Krishna adjusted quickly enough first thing – and even then his figures were set alight at the tail end by Buttler’s ultimately match-winning cameo.This dizzying match began with a minute’s silence for the late Sir Garfield Sobers and during the first couple of hours it looked like a fitting century might follow. Bethell, who swapped Barbados for a scholarship at Rugby School with a personal ­reference from Sobers, fell nine runs short of his second in the format.View image in fullscreenRohit Sharma’s swashbuckling innings proved in vain.Photograph: Henning von Jagow/Action Plus/ShutterstockStill, as one half of a 192-run ­opening stand with Duckett, Bethell had helped to set a record by an ­England pair against India – any wicket – and the platform for this monster total. Bethell struck 11 fours and pulled two mighty sixes for his 93-ball 91, dampening concerns about his recent promotion to opener in the process.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe SpinSubscribe to our cricket newsletter for our writers' thoughts on the biggest stories and a review of the week’s actionPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDuckett was at his impish best, the more passive initially – 64 balls for his half-century compared to Bethell’s 52 – before kicking on with Joe Root in a 101-run second‑wicket stand. His only real flirtation with danger, before plopping a catch to Prince Yadav, was also his only six as KL Rahul tumbled over the rope with ball in hand.The England of old might have pushed out Buttler ahead of Root but the latter’s busy unbeaten 74 from 48 balls proved no impediment. Following scores of 76 not out at Edgbaston and 99 not out in Cardiff, England’s No 3 has been no average Joe in this series – and has scored 360 runs since his last dismissal in the format.And the fireworks followed regardless. After Brook came and went for 14, Buttler arrived in the mood for mischief. He slapped four fours and cleared the rope three times to see 82 runs flow from the last five overs. At that stage, India looked as cooked as Gooram Brar’s figures of none for 97 – the costliest in a Lord’s ODI.It was clear, however, that unlikeEngland’s crapshoot victoryin whites against New Zealand last month, wickets would be tricky to come by and even containment was going to be a struggle. Gill was in sublime touch from the outset, holding the pose after lacing his cover-drives as he and Sharma put on 147 for the first wicket.Adil Rashid eventually prised out Gill lbw in the 25th but it was not until Sharma’s demise – followed by Curran’s quickfire removals of Ishan Kishan and Shreyas Iyer – that Brook could breathe a little easier. The final overs may have been an anticlimax but the journey to get there was both brilliant and bonkers.Explore more on these topicsCricketEngland cricket teamIndia cricket teamnewsShareReuse this content

title: Russell’s Mercedes problems ‘100% on us’, says Toto Wolff after Belgium blow | Formula One 2026 | The Guardian
description: Toto Wolff has admitted Mercedes let George Russell down after he crashed out of the Belgian Grand Prix following a collision with Lewis Hamilton
Author: Giles Richards
Headline: Toto Wolff has admitted Mercedes let George Russell down after he crashed out of the Belgian Grand Prix following a collision with Lewis Hamilton
Content: George Russell departed the Belgian Grand Prix after a first-lap crash with Lewis Hamilton.Photograph: Christian Hartmann/ReutersView image in fullscreenGeorge Russell departed the Belgian Grand Prix after a first-lap crash with Lewis Hamilton.Photograph: Christian Hartmann/ReutersFormula One 2026Russell’s Mercedes problems ‘100% on us’, says Toto Wolff after Belgium blowRussell clipped by Hamilton on lap one after power lossBritish driver 50 points behind Antonelli in standingsGiles RichardsSun 19 Jul 2026 15.06 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleToto Wolff, the Mercedes principal, has admitted the team let George Russell down at the Belgian Grand Prix. The British driver failed to score a point after a clash withLewis Hamiltonthat Russell attributed to his car’s performance not being up to scratch.The racewas won by Kimi Antonelli, who now leads his teammate Russell by 50 points in the world championship, a serious blow to the latter’s title hopes.Russell was clipped by Hamilton on the opening lap, sending him off into the gravel and ending his race. Russell said it had been a racing incident but insisted he would not have been alongside Hamilton but for his Mercedes failing to deploy sufficient power on the run to the Les Combes corner. Russell had been outspoken all weekend about a deficiency in electrical energy deployment and felt it had been hugely costly, which Wolff addressed.Kimi Antonelli tightens F1 title grip with Belgian GP win after Russell crashes outRead more“We had an issue on all Mercedes engines that we lacked the power out on the straights,” Wolff said. “That bit him [Russell] badly and it goes 100% on us.“George has 100% our support. George had a difficult weekend. Half of it is to be attributed to the power unit that wasn’t as good as it should have been and the other half may be on the driving. But we are in this together, we all make mistakes, today is the mistake of the team and another day is going to be the driver. We just need to minimise it and support each other.”Russell said the result had left him numb with disappointment, while insisting he felt he should not have been competing for the corner with the Ferraris, who had swept past him on the Kemmel straight. Wolff said he understood his driver’s strong reaction.“It’s completely understandable that the moment you’re out of the race, it’s so frustrating and the emotions boil,” he said. “We are trying to solve the issues, he’s doing everything he can to get on top of his driving. I’m going to try to put my arm around him.”Hamilton was found by the stewards to be culpable and given a five-second penalty, but he too felt it was only a racing incident and that he would speak to Russell about it.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“I was in the position where George obviously looked like he lost power and then he swooped around the outside and just had a small coming together,” he said. “I will text George later on. The last thing you ever want to do is come together with your teammate or with your old team who you highly respect.”Explore more on these topicsFormula One 2026Toto WolffGeorge RussellLewis HamiltonMercedes GPFerrariFormula OnenewsShareReuse this content

title: Feyi-Waboso gives England more bite but there is no ignoring elephant in the room | England rugby union team | The Guardian
description: Returning world-beater has made real difference but Fin Smith laments continuing ill discipline after Argentina win
Author: Robert Kitson
Headline: Returning world-beater has made real difference but Fin Smith laments continuing ill discipline after Argentina win
Content: England gather on the pitch after their 31-24 win against Argentina on Saturday.Photograph: Juan Gasparini/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenEngland gather on the pitch after their 31-24 win against Argentina on Saturday.Photograph: Juan Gasparini/AFP/Getty ImagesEngland rugby union teamFeyi-Waboso gives England more bite but there is no ignoring elephant in the roomReturning world-beater has made real difference but Fin Smith laments continuing ill discipline after Argentina winRobert Kitsonin Santiago del EsteroSun 19 Jul 2026 13.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe most impressive analysis of England’s fluctuating July fortunes, as is so often the case nowadays, came from their fly-half Fin Smith. Where others choose to obfuscate, deflect or shoot the messenger, the admirably clear‑eyed Smith prefers to tell it as it is. And at the end of a long, draining season, his view is that England are still frustratingly short of where they need to be.Yes,they beat Argentina 31‑24 on Saturdayin a turbulent game containing eight tries and the tightest of finishes. It means they have claimed back-to-back Test wins for the first time since November and, in Manny Feyi-Waboso, they have a genuine world-beater. Equally, though, there can be no ignoring the bright yellow elephant in the dressing room. Four more yellow cards has taken England’s tally to 14 in 10 games this year, a habit they still seem unable to break.Schmidt leaves repaired Wallabies with gifts of Canham and Lonergan for Kiss | Daniel GallanRead moreAfter the match the head coach,Steve Borthwick, sought to criticise “the negativity committee” of travelling media when the subject inevitably cropped up. Smith, to his credit, was much more upfront. He gave England only a modest seven out of 10, reflective of the fact England ended up millimetres away from blowing a 19-3 half-time lead. In his opinion, too, England fans should expect more than five defeats in eight Tests this calendar year, regardless of results in the past fortnight.Those who insist England are held to unfairly high standards of public expectation were also politely put straight. “I think it comes with being an England rugby player. The Rugby Football Union is a massive organisation and Twickenham is full every week. People just want to see us win, which I think is fair. There’s no reason we shouldn’t be a powerhouse in international rugby and there’s no reason we shouldn’t be winning every week.”How refreshing to hear that particular nettle being grasped. Because, ultimately, what has been the abiding memory of the 26,000‑mile trek this month across three continents, aside from the various airport terminals and Liverpool train hell? Without doubt it was the start and finish of their defeat against South Africa at Ellis Park wherethey lost 45-21. Bouncing back against the lower-ranked sides Fiji and Argentina may have bolstered Borthwick’s immediate employment prospects but his side’s calling card remains their reliable inconsistency.Smith, for one, has had enough of it. “Yeah, it is frustrating because I think we’re a very good team with 15 men on the pitch. I think back to the last five or six games I’ve played for England, I reckon I’ve spent at least 25-30% of the game without that. That is frustrating because I don’t think we’re allowing ourselves to show the best of us. There’s probably a bit of personal responsibility to not keep doing stuff that gets you pinged.”The good news – welcome back, Steve – is that England are developing a better balanced game and will have even more depth when Maro Itoje, George Furbank, Will Stuart and Fin Baxter return to bolster the matchday squad. George Martin, Ollie Chessum and Joe Heyes have all impressed up front and Henry Slade’s recall has visibly improved the backline fluency. “When we’re at our best we look like a smart team that makes good decisions and we’ve got an awesome forward pack that can beat teams up,” Smith said.View image in fullscreenFin Smith (centre) said England ‘are not allowing ourselves to show the best of us’.Photograph: Joaquin Camiletti/RFU/The RFU Collection/Getty Images“I think we owe it to ourselves to show that a lot more than we do. We wanted to go to South Africa and win and we didn’t do that. I thought we played well against Fiji and I thought for about 50 minutes this weekend we were good … I might look back in a few weeks and think we were better or worse but at this moment that’s how I feel.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe BreakdownThe latest rugby union news and analysis, plus all the week's action reviewedPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBingo. There is an argument that England also wasted an opportunity to give a solitary minute of game time to fringe squad members such as Theo Dan, Greg Fisilau, Max Ojomoh and Arthur Clark but Borthwick’s selections were clearly driven by short-term imperatives rather than longer-term considerations. Happily Noah Caluori, Benhard van Rensburg, George Kloska and Manny Iyogun did make their debuts and rewarded the faith shown in them.The single biggest plus, though, has to be the return of Feyi-Waboso, who missed the entire Six Nations before breaking his jaw playing for Exeter late in the domestic campaign. While the 23‑year‑old still cannot chew properly – “After games I’m in a lot of pain but it’s worth it” – his ability to break tackles and score special tries gives England a different attacking dimension when they have sufficient players on the pitch. “It started not great in South Africa, really not great at all,” Feyi‑Waboso said. “Then we fixed some things against Fiji and managed to get away with one today. It’s consistency, isn’t it? Obviously we’re trying to find that … not having 15 men on the pitch for the majority of the time is a huge factor.”A disappointing Argentina could only lament the final seconds of the game on Saturday when a diving Bautista Delguy was fractionally denied in the corner by the never-say-die Slade. The Pumas argued Caluori’s previous high grab of Dulguy’s collar should have yielded a penalty and one last chance while their head coach, Felipe Contepomi, also slammed England for “a lack of respect” as their noisy celebrations next door drowned out his press conference. Given a certain recent football result, however, visiting sympathy was somewhat limited. England now need to recharge fully over the summer and heed Smith’s wise words.Explore more on these topicsEngland rugby union teamSteve BorthwickArgentina rugby union teamRugby unionfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Thames Water creditors seek talks with Burnham as nationalisation looms | Thames Water | The Guardian
description: Consortium preparing for potential legal battle amid reports new PM could put the company into temporary public ownership
Author: Mark Sweney
Headline: Consortium preparing for potential legal battle amid reports new PM could put the company into temporary public ownership
Content: London & Valley Water, a consortium of 100 institutional investors, has said it is open to government involvement but not to public ownership.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersView image in fullscreenLondon & Valley Water, a consortium of 100 institutional investors, has said it is open to government involvement but not to public ownership.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersThames WaterThames Water creditors seek talks with Burnham as nationalisation loomsConsortium preparing for potential legal battle amid reports new PM could put the company into temporary public ownershipMark SweneySun 19 Jul 2026 12.17 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe group of investors pursuing a rescue bid for Thames Water have said they are willing to discuss greater public control but are also preparing for a potential multi-billion pound legal battle amid reports thatAndy Burnhamcould temporarily nationalise the company.London & Valley Water (L&VW), a consortium of 100 institutional investors that hold £17bn of the company’s £21bn debt, has said it is open to government involvement withThames Water, but indicated that this does not include public ownership in the struggling company.Mike McTighe, the corporate troubleshooter who is leading the governance overhaul and building the new board proposed for Thames, Britain’s biggest water company, said the consortium wanted to work constructively with Burnham when he takes over as prime minister.“We are keen to meet new ministers as soon as possible to discuss how we can work together in the best interests of customers, including by enhancing public control of the company’s operations,” he said. “We will work with Andy Burnham, his government and local authority leaders to rebuild confidence in Thames Water and the wider sector.”The comments by McTighe, who is likely to take over as Thames Water’s chair if the consortium is able to get its £10bn rescue deal approved by the government, follow reports that Burnham is planning to transfer the company into a special administration regime (SAR), a form of temporary public ownership, after he takes over as prime minister.Burnham said last month that there should be“greater public control”of Thames Water and told the Guardian this could mean nationalisation.The SAR proposal would transfer the costs of running the company to the taxpayer, with Thames Water’s creditors claiming the bill could come to £2bn.“If it is going to cost the taxpayer £2bn to keep the company afloat then the taxpayer needs to receive something in return; that means control, so that we can fix the company and secure the water supply for thousands of families and businesses,” a Burnham ally told The Sunday Times.L&VW, which includes fund managers such as Apollo Global Management, Elliott Management, Farallon Capital Management and Silver Point Capital, has also moved to strengthen its legal position in order to be prepared if Thames is nationalised.The consortium has hired top litigation and disputes firm Pallas Partners to work alongside Akin Gump, the law firm advising the consortium on the terms of their restructuring proposals.“Creditors are assessing all potential routes that the situation regarding Thames Water may play out,” said one person close to the consortium. “They want, and need, to be ready. There is no legal action being taken at this point. This is purely a precautionary measure.”Earlier this month, Thames Water’s creditors were said to be still willing to pursue their bid for the debt-laden company, even if Burnhamwere to bring it into temporary nationalisation.“The consortium is trying to pursue a solvent restructuring,” said the person. “This would avoid a taxpayer funded administration process and help creditors recover as much as possible. It can bid alongside any others, but that prolongs everything.”The future of Thames, which serves 16 million customers inLondonand the Thames valley and is buckling under interest payments on debt it has run up since privatisation, will be one of the most pressing issues in Burnham’s in-tray when he enters Downing Street.“We remain ready and willing to recapitalise Thames Water, return it to investment grade, and begin the long process of turning it around,” said McTighe. “We urgently need government engagement to begin that process.”McTighe is the chairman of Openreach, BT Group’s infrastructure arm, and previously chaired the Daily Telegraph’s publisher.The lenders have been trying to take ownership and bring it out of administration after a failed attempt tosell the utility to the US investment group KKRlast year.However, the creditors’ plans were thrown into doubt last month when Emma Reynolds, the environment secretary, wrote to the regulator, Ofwat,voicing concerns about the terms of the deal.Explore more on these topicsThames WaterWater industryAndy BurnhamUtilitiesCorporate governanceNationalisationLondonnewsShareReuse this content

title: Five family members found dead after flash flood in Utah canyon, police say | Utah | The Guardian
description: Victims were Provo’s fire captain, his wife and their three sons, who were hiking in Bicknell amid heavy rains
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Victims were Provo’s fire captain, his wife and their three sons, who were hiking in Bicknell amid heavy rains
Content: Sunglow campground in Bicknell, Utah, where the family was on a day trip.Photograph: User: runt35View image in fullscreenSunglow campground in Bicknell, Utah, where the family was on a day trip.Photograph: User: runt35UtahFive family members found dead after flash flood in Utah canyon, police sayVictims were Provo’s fire captain, his wife and their three sons, who were hiking in Bicknell amid heavy rainsAssociated PressSun 19 Jul 2026 18.24 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFive family members who had set out for a day of hiking were killed after a flash flood swept through canyons of southernUtahon Friday, law enforcement officials said.The victims were a captain in the Provo fire department, his wife and their sons, according to fire officials. A daughter who was not on the trip survived.The family arrived on Friday at Sunglow campground in a scenic red rock canyon near Bicknell,Utah, for their day excursion, the Wayne county sheriff’s office said.“It is believed the family fell victim to flooding caused by heavy rains,” the sheriff’s office said in a news release.View image in fullscreenCaptain Spencer Long of the Provo fire department in Utah.Photograph: Provo Fire & RescueAlthough the sheriff’s office did not identify the victims, the Provo fire department confirmed in a social media post that a fire captain, Spencer Long, and his family members were killed.“We are absolutely heartbroken by the death of Captain Spencer Long and his family. Our thoughts are with his family, friends, and colleagues during this difficult time,” officials with the fire department wrote.On Friday night, deputies were called to the campground after the body of a young man was found in the flood wash. The bodies of the other family members were recovered later on Friday and Saturday.Meanwhile, tributes poured in for the family.Long; his wife, Katrina; and their sons Reid, Thayne and Gage died in the devastating floods, according to a GoFundMe site set up by a family friend to support the Longs’ daughter as she navigates the loss of her family.“Spencer and Katrina were loving, devoted parents whose greatest joy was their family. They touched the lives of so many with their kindness, generosity, and unwavering love,” Bree Nielson wrote on the page.Katrina Long was the co-founder of a medical spa. “Our hearts are with all of their loved ones as we grieve the unimaginable loss of Katrina, her husband, and their three beautiful boys,” Summit Medical Spapostedon Facebook.Explore more on these topicsUtahUS weatherFloodingExtreme weathernewsShareReuse this content

title: Dozens missing after passenger ferry sinks off coast of Guyana | Guyana | The Guardian
description: Emergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disasters
Author: Natricia Duncan
Headline: Emergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disasters
Content: The Coast Guard, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers helped rescue passengers after the MV Barima sank on Saturday night.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Coast Guard, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers helped rescue passengers after the MV Barima sank on Saturday night.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesGuyanaDozens missing after passenger ferry sinks off coast of GuyanaEmergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disastersNeil Marks in Essequibo andNatricia Duncan, Caribbean correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 15.39 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA ferry carrying 116 passengers and crew sank ⁠off the coast ofGuyanain South America ⁠late Saturday, with 67 ⁠people, ​including 15 children, rescued so far, according to Guyana ⁠authorities.A massive search-and-rescue operation continues for dozens of missing passengers in what has been described by officials as one of the country’s worst maritime disasters.The ferry MV Barima left Georgetown, the capital of the English-speaking South American country, on Saturday at about 3.15pm for Port Kaituma, located in the oil-rich Essequibo region and just off the border with Venezuela.A distress call was received shortly after 11pm, prompting an emergency response involving the Guyana Defence Force Coast Guard and Air Corps, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers.Juan Edghill, the minister of public works, said preliminary investigations indicate the vessel was operating within its legal limits.“There was no engine problem or any mechanical problem. It had nothing to do with overload. It had to do with the tide,” Edghill told local reporters.He said the MV Barima was certified to carry 397 passengers and 284 tonnes of cargo. Its manifest showed it was transporting 268 tonnes of cargo, below its authorised capacity.The ship was equipped with 250 lifejackets, two lifeboats and six inflatable rafts, Edghill said.In a statement on Sunday, Guyana’s prime minister, Mark Phillips, said the search and rescue coordination centre had expanded the search area from 400 sq km (154 sq miles) to 1,070 sq km, with additional aircraft and vessels deployed alongside local fisherfolk familiar with the waters.View image in fullscreenThe emergency response involved the Coast Guard, the Air Corps and volunteer fishing vessels and private craft.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThe 67 survivors include 41 men, 11 women and 15 children.As the rescue effort continues, survivors recounted the moments when the ferry overturned in darkness.Adam Matabheek, who was travelling with his wife, brother, sister-in-law and three young nieces and nephews, said he noticed the vessel leaning several hours before it capsized.“We dismissed it as rough water and went to sleep,” he said.Moments later, the vessel rolled over. “I ended up in the water,” he recalled, saying he managed to grab a lifejacket before drifting through the night.His wife, Carlita Matabheek, who cannot swim, said she became trapped inside the overturned vessel before a lifejacket fell within her reach. “I started to pray,” she said.After calling repeatedly for her husband in the darkness, she felt someone tugging at her hair. “It was Adam,” she said.The couple and Adam’s younger brother, Nathaniel, spent the night drifting in the sea until rescuers found them after daybreak.“God cleared the skies and saved us,” Carlita said.Their relief, however, was overshadowed by tragedy.Nathaniel’s wife, Rachel Welch, and their three children – Nathaniel Jr., 8, Natalia, 5, and Ezekiel, 3 – remain among the missing. According to the family, Rachel was thrown from her hammock as the ferry capsized and tried desperately to reach her children before they disappeared into the water.Another survivor, Loureen Singh, a trader who regularly travels to Port Kaituma to sell clothing, survived by holding on to the same lifejacket as Carlita after telling her she could swim “a little”.Carlita urged her: “Hold on to me.”The two women remained together throughout the night until they were rescued.Authorities say families of all recorded passengers and crew members have been contacted as efforts continue to account for everyone aboard.Explore more on these topicsGuyanaWater transportAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Molten salt and human sweat: the weird batteries that could store renewable energy  | Renewable energy | The Guardian
description: From Nevada to Manchester, developers are trialling innovative solutions to clean energy’s biggest challenge
Author: Jillian Ambrose
Headline: From Nevada to Manchester, developers are trialling innovative solutions to clean energy’s biggest challenge
