The high point of the show is Locked Out of Heaven, Mars’ 2012 hit and a near-perfect pop song that he delivers with as much enthusiasm as if it were brand new. It’s a fun show but unforgettable? That prayer went unanswered.Various dates at Wembley Stadium until 28 JulyExplore more on these topicsBruno MarsPop and rockSoulR&BreviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘People say they’ve conceived to my music’: Tricky’s honest playlist | Tricky | The Guardian
description: The trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?
Author: Rich Pelley
Headline: The trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?
Content: ‘Songs I loved years ago I can still listen to now’ … Tricky.Photograph: Steve GullickView image in fullscreen‘Songs I loved years ago I can still listen to now’ … Tricky.Photograph: Steve GullickHonest playlistTricky‘People say they’ve conceived to my music’: Tricky’s honest playlistThe trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?As told toRich PelleySun 19 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe first song I fell in love withI was going out with this Jamaican girl when we were about 15, and would listen to Night Nurse by Gregory Isaacs all the time. We had a daughter together when we were 17 – she’s 35 now and works as a social worker in Bristol – so that song has always stayed with me.The song that makes me cryAstral Weeks by Van Morrison, because it reminds me of my mum and growing up in Bristol. He sings about his mum dressing him and showing him pictures, and my mum was exactly like that. In every childhood photo I’m in a shirt and tie and looking proper.The first single I boughtI remember getting on the bus from Knowle West into town to buy the Specials album. I was obsessed with the artwork and the sleeve notes. I sat on the bus reading everything on the way home and listened to it for three days straight.The song I know all the lyrics toI can still rap every word of Check Out My Melody by Eric B and Rakim off the top of my head, 40 years later.The best song to play at a partySong 2 by Blur, because of that riff. I still listen to it now. I was listening to it yesterday in my hotel room. I put on my headphones, smoked some hashish and played Song 2.The song I can no longer listen toThere honestly isn’t one. Songs I loved years ago – Purple Rain by Prince or Fight the Power by Public Enemy – I can still listen to now.The song I do at karaokeI can’t say I’ve ever done karaoke, but if I had to, I’d probably do True Colors by Cyndi Lauper.The song that gets me up in the morningRelease Yo’ Delf by Method Man gets me moving.The best song to have sex toPeople have been telling me for years: “Me and my partner have sex to your music.” Some people have even said they’ve conceived to my music, which is all very flattering. But I don’t think sex and music even go together, so it’s always seemed a bit weird to me.The song that changed my lifeI loved Prince, but I knew I could never sing like him. Then I heard Too Much Too Young by the Specials and saw them live. Terry Hall and Neville Staple were singing about the life I knew. Suddenly it felt possible. They made me realise I could do that, too.The song I secretly like, but tell everyone I hateBreathe Me by Sia is fucking gorgeous. I don’t really know any of her other stuff, which is bad, because I probably should.The song I’d like played at my funeralI’m never going to die, so there’s no need to think about that.Tricky’s album When It’s Silent is outnow.Explore more on these topicsTrickyHonest playlistHip-hopDance musicElectronic musicfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Hole! review – filthy musical fable enchants … and makes you blush | Stage | The Guardian
description: Jake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper are vivid storytellers and fine musicians and foley artists
Author: Holly O'Mahony
Headline: Jake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper are vivid storytellers and fine musicians and foley artists
Content: Plugged in … Jake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper in Hole!Photograph: Tristram Kenton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenPlugged in … Jake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper in Hole!Photograph: Tristram Kenton/The GuardianStageReviewHole! review – filthy musical fable enchants … and makes you blushSoho theatre, LondonJake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper are vivid storytellers and fine musicians and foley artistsHolly O'MahonySun 19 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 10.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfilthy fable about a god-fearing Christian sect in rural Nebraska who, in a racy, sci-fi twist, are forced to wear butt plugs to avoid being sucked up into the ether, could be utterly naff. But the sheer artistry behind this hard-working two-hander ensures every second is surprisingly enchanting – even when it makes you blush.It’s written and acted by Jake Brasch and Nadja Leonhard-Hooper, who perform together as New York-based outfit American Sing Song. They are not only vivid storytellers but fine musicians and foley artists – creating their own sound effects on the fly. Over the course of this 80-minute show, every pop, suck and ominous whoosh (as a plug is fatefully removed) is heard, and every innuendo lands, sometimes in harmony.There’s lots of intricate world building here, but essentially we’re in an apocalyptic landscape, where only the congregation of the prophesying Garth and a spattering of others who happened to have their arseholes plugged at the time of The Great Sucking have survived.The bulk of it follows teen friends Connor (Leonhard-Hooper) and Luke (Brasch) as they venture out of their corn-fed community like a pair of Book of Mormon missionaries, only with the murderous intent of finding, de-plugging and thereby killing members of a gay community. But after a sexual awakening sees the boys do some converting of their own, a return to their conservative theocracy no longer beckons.Stood behind a worktop that looks a little like a spaceship, with their hair twisted into identical gaping holes above their heads, Leonhard-Hooper and Brasch are impressively in sync and never lose us as they switch between characters and manipulate an impressive array of sound-making tools. Even their between-scenes swigs from comically large water bottles are synchronised. The songs are witty earworms you’ll want to stream after (albeit with a robust set of headphones given the x-rated lyrics). Brasch’s accompaniment on keys often makes the whole thing sound like a jaunty musical, and in a particularly inspired number, Leonard-Hooper provides a tap interlude with her hands manoeuvring the shoes.Impish and brimming with originality, this is fringe theatre at its most scintillating and inventive.Explore more on these topicsStageTheatreSoho theatreEdinburgh festivalreviewsShareReuse this content

title: For Dolores review – dependency and desire in portrait of obsessive friendship | Stage | The Guardian
description: Eva O’Connor’s intense new play explores the lives of spiky Glaswegian Mo and naive Dubliner Réaltín through competing monologues, blunt humour and dance moves
Author: Helen Meany
Headline: Eva O’Connor’s intense new play explores the lives of spiky Glaswegian Mo and naive Dubliner Réaltín through competing monologues, blunt humour and dance moves
Content: Contrasts … Catriona Faint as Mo and Lara McDonnell as Réaltín in For Dolores.Photograph: Pat RedmondView image in fullscreenContrasts … Catriona Faint as Mo and Lara McDonnell as Réaltín in For Dolores.Photograph: Pat RedmondStageReviewFor Dolores review – dependency and desire in portrait of obsessive friendshipMick Lally theatre, GalwayEva O’Connor’s intense new play explores the lives of spiky Glaswegian Mo and naive Dubliner Réaltín through competing monologues, blunt humour and dance movesHelen MeanySun 19 Jul 2026 05.45 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 05.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleStaying together for the sake of a child takes on additional weight in Eva O’Connor’s intense new play for FishambleTheatreCompany. In their mid-20s, Mo (Catriona Faint) and Réaltín (Lara McDonnell) initially seem like bickering young parents; “chosen family”, attending a therapy session, with the audience in the role of therapist. As the pair’s story spools back to their comical first meeting as new university students in Edinburgh, a portrait of an obsessive friendship emerges.Playwright and actor O’Connor haswritten previously about compulsion, and here she vividly conveys the confusing mix of love, dependency, admiration and desire that can be poured into young women’s friendships: “the wild magnet pull of us,” Réaltín calls it. Initially it was the contrasts between the spiky, politicised Glaswegian, Mo, and the naive, middle-class Dubliner, Réaltín, that cemented their bond – captured in Faint and McDonnell’s committed, passionate performances.As director Jim Culleton sensitively brings out, in tiny moments and gestures, Réaltín may be oblivious to the differences in their feelings; the distinction between loving and being “in love”. It hardly matters in their messy drinking and clubbing years, but post-college, when an emotionally lost Réaltín gets engaged to a wealthy Finnish man, the fallout is catastrophic.Alternating between monologues for their competing memories, dance moves and bluntly humorous dialogue, the leisurely exposition of early scenes begins to tighten around the events of a single night, with tension underscored by Denis Clohessy’s sound design. At a party where sex, alcohol and jealousy slam together, the violent climax is all the more vivid for not being directly described.Set designer Alyson Cummins’s simple raised platform rests on a layer of discarded bottles, books and junk, as if allowing us to see the detritus that has been swept under the carpet. O’Connor is very good at ensuring that we can’t look away.At the Pavilion theatre, Dún Laoghaire, 22 to 26 July;Traverse theatre, Edinburgh, 6 to 30 August.Galway international arts festivalcontinues until 26 JulyExplore more on these topicsStageTheatreIrelandreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Here Comes J Edgar! A Musical Comedy review – show tunes, Mel Brooks zing and real human anguish | Stage | The Guardian
description: Camp comedy from The Simpsons’ Harry Shearer and Seinfeld writer Tom Leopold explores the relationship between FBI boss Hoover and protege Clyde Tolson
Author: Arifa Akbar
Headline: Camp comedy from The Simpsons’ Harry Shearer and Seinfeld writer Tom Leopold explores the relationship between FBI boss Hoover and protege Clyde Tolson
Content: Exuberant … Bryan Batt as Hoover in Here Comes J Edgar! A Comedy Musical.Photograph: Mark DouetView image in fullscreenExuberant … Bryan Batt as Hoover in Here Comes J Edgar! A Comedy Musical.Photograph: Mark DouetStageReviewHere Comes J Edgar! A Musical Comedy review – show tunes, Mel Brooks zing and real human anguishKing’s Head theatre, LondonCamp comedy from The Simpsons’ Harry Shearer and Seinfeld writer Tom Leopold explores the relationship between FBI boss Hoover and protege Clyde TolsonArifa AkbarSun 19 Jul 2026 07.53 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 07.55 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThis queering of FBI history does not seem like the stuff of fringe theatre. Created by American comedy royalty –Harry Shearer, a long-time cast member of The Simpsons, pens the book and lyrics with Tom Leopold, of Cheers and Seinfeld writing fame – the music is by the late Peter Matz, known for his work with Barbra Streisand. Alongside those commercial chops, Mad Men actor Bryan Batt plays the lead. It is even more curious that, as a very American story about the life and times of the eponymous first director of the bureau, it premieres in Britain.It has been decades in the making too (beginning life on radio in the 1990s). How has it ended up here?The production deals with its own much-debated question of whether Hoover was gay. It answers it with a resounding “of course”, showing his longtime friend, Clyde Tolson (Hugo Bolton, fabulous), to be his closeted lover under cover of protege and fellow law enforcement agent.An initially ailing Hoover, on his deathbed, is taken through his own Christmas Carol style reckoning, seeing his life re-enacted as a jazz-hands, Broadway musical. Cue chorus line numbers and songs that both send up and celebrate the musical theatre form.View image in fullscreenUnder cover … Hugo Bolton and Bryan Batt in Here Comes J Edgar! A Comedy MusicalPhotograph: Mark DouetHis former child self (Jess Pratley) is the ghost of the past. We see how Hoover goes from precocious schoolboy, who knows how to blackmail his headteacher with compromising personal information, to doing exactly the same with four presidents during his tenure as head of theFBI.Exuberantly directed by Josh Seymour, there is bags of irony, campiness and some sparkling satire as Hoover becomes the anti-hero of his life. His uglier side is left unvarnished – the dirt he digs up on Martin Luther King, his grubbily close relationship to press man Walter Winchell (Marc Elliott), his friendship with Joseph McCarthy, his clampdown on “commies” and even a passing reference to stamping out homosexuality in American society.At its best, the comedy has a Mel Brooks zing to it. But alongside the humour there is anguish that feels real and human, particularly in the love between Hoover and Tolson. Their first encounter in a toilet brings gorgeously funny romance and some lovely songs accompany their relationship (Once Upon a Face and I’ll Never Miss Him). Repressed desire also comes in the form of Hoover’s secretary, Helen (Lucy O’Byrne) who gets one of the most riotous numbers in Helen’s Blues.Sometimes it feels too big for the venue, with its parodies of show tunes and chorus lines. A character jokes that the second-half is not as good as the first and there is some truth in it. But this is a ridiculously fun ride of a musical, with entertaining, outre and imaginative set-pieces. You can see its West End ambitions, and with a bit of brushing up it deserves to be there.At King’s Head theatre, London, until 16 AugustExplore more on these topicsStageTheatreFBIHarry ShearerComedyreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Sphinxes, movie stars and 3m books: inside Los Angeles’s beloved art deco library | Los Angeles | The Guardian
description: The showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institution
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institution
Content: The library opened in 1926 and has been described as ‘a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences’.Photograph: Damian Dovarganes/APView image in fullscreenThe library opened in 1926 and has been described as ‘a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences’.Photograph: Damian Dovarganes/APLos AngelesSphinxes, movie stars and 3m books: inside Los Angeles’s beloved art deco libraryThe showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institutionJames Bartlett in Los AngelesSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe central library in downtownLos Angeleshas seen its fair share of colorful moments.The architectural gem, which turned 100 this week, has been the backdrop to the epic street battle between Pacino, De Niro et al in Heat, and waswhere the originalGhostbusterscame across their first ghost, a friendly older lady librarian who was anything but. Television shows such as Moonlighting; Murder, She Wrote; and LA Law filmed there too, when characters were researching or investigating a case.‘A counter-narrative of history’: Schomburg Center’s 100 years of celebrating Black cultureRead moreBut the library has also been touched by the darker side of LA life. The “Night Stalker” serial killer Richard Ramirezcame here in 1985and asked for books on astrology and torture. Ever helpful, the librarian directed him to the correct department and thought little more about the unsettling man with rotten teeth – until he recognized him when he was arrested several days later.Then there was the still-officially unsolved 1986 arson, which destroyed 400,000 volumes and shut the library for seven years.“People think that libraries are quiet, but they really aren’t. They rumble with voices and footsteps and a whole orchestral range of book-related noises,” wrote Susan Orlean in The Library Book,her 2018 bestseller about the central library.That was certainly true this past week, when the author was one of the estimated 12,000 people who swelled through the corridors, halls, courtyards, rotunda, reading rooms and sculpture garden in celebration of its 100th anniversary.View image in fullscreenThe Los Angeles central library has been a set location for films and TV shows, and a research center for a serial killer.Photograph: Eric Draper/APThe art deco-era library, according to L Is for Librarian: The ABCs ofLos AngelesCentral Library, houses nearly 3m books and 89 miles of shelves.The library’s most dazzling and Instagram-worthy feature is the central rotunda – cathedral-like and lined with Dean Cornwell’s romanticized murals of California history. Visitors’ eyes are drawn up to the zodiac chandelier with its stained-glass globe and zodiac signs.“It remains one of the city’s great civic spaces, a place that’s used, loved and valued every single day,” says Stephen Gee, the author of Los Angeles Central Library: A History of Its Art andArchitecture.“It’s one of the few places in modern Los Angeles where everyone is welcome, regardless of their background or circumstances. It doesn’t ask who you are or what you earn.”After outgrowing several smaller downtown locations, the municipal dream of a monumental downtown library was financed through a $2.5m municipal bond measure approved in 1921.View image in fullscreenThe Bison Hunt frieze on the exterior of the Los Angeles central library.Photograph: University of Southern California/Corbis/Getty ImagesDesigned by the architect Bertram Goodhue, the central library officially opened its doors in 1926.“Goodhue promised a library that was ‘Spanish in style – at least in spirit’,” noted Gee in his book, “but what he ultimately created was something far more original: a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences.”View image in fullscreenThe Lodwrick M Cook Rotunda inside the LA central library.Photograph: Barry Winiker/Getty ImagesThat fusion is apparent whether you approach the library from Hope Street, which was the original, mural-lined tunnel entrance, or through the sculpture and fountain-spotted Maguire Gardens.Towering over the main entrance are Hesper and Phosphor, the ancient Greek morning and evening stars; Hesper holds a scroll with the names of eastern philosophers, Moses, Buddha and Mohammad.Most noticeable to the first-time visitor is the colored mosaic pyramid on the building’s roof, which features a sunburst and is topped by a handheld torch symbolizing the light of learning.The Egyptian theme is also seen in two black-gold sphinxes, which rest at the top of a staircase on either side of a female statue representing civilization.One of the longest lines at this week’s centennial festival was for a vintage injection-mold vending machine, whichproduced small replica wax modelsof those sphinxes.Christina Rice, the senior librarian of the library’s vast photo collection and a Central 100 co-chair, christened the whirring, jukebox-like machine “Mold-A-Central”, and obtaining one had been on her wishlist for years.“About a dozen years ago, we received an inquiry in the photo collection from a patron looking for images of these machines,” Rice said.It unleashed a flood of childhood memories for Rice, who recalled getting animal models at the LA zoo and admits that she “became a little bit obsessed with them”. After finally tracking down one of the machines, she had it installed for the Central 100 event. “It will be a permanent fixture, and we’ll be changing out the molds periodically,” she said.View image in fullscreenA crowded reading room in the LA central library in 1933.Photograph: University of Southern California/Corbis/Getty ImagesElsewhere, people took former catalogue cards and used them to clack out poems on vintage typewriters, while down in the history and genealogy department the cards in the huge family names cabinet were being eagerly thumbed through.“People do love card catalogues,” says Kelly Wallace, the department’sCaliforniahistory specialist. “I find that Los Angeles has come of age in terms of taking an interest in its own history. Every day, people come to the library to research the history of their families, homes, neighborhoods and the city around them.”View image in fullscreenLos Angeles central library on 19 July 2010.Photograph: Ted Soqui/Corbis/Getty ImagesRice explains that the library is as relevant as ever, despite those who might declare libraries “obsolete”. She mentions how the central library provides services for job hunters and those seeking a path to citizenship or looking to learn a new language. “We have a program for adults to earn their high school diploma, loads of resources to get teens prepared for college and literacy assistance for patrons of all ages.”On the science, technology and patents floor is the Octavia lab, which offers 3D printing, digitizers, converters, vinyl cutters, large-format printers – and sewing and embroidery machines. The facility is named after a former frequent patron, the late Octavia Butler, the renowned science fiction author who said: “Public libraries … are the open universities of America.”The daily business of the library continued during the events, but the most popular book of the day was Luceros y Penumras (roughly translated as “starlight and shadows”) by Daniel González. At 31-by-20ft and 11ft tall, it’sthe world’s largest pop-up book, according to the library, and the 1,800lb work wascarefully assembledin the center of the rotunda.But perhaps the best news of the day? Five hundred and eighty-eight people were issued new library cards.Explore more on these topicsLos AngelesLibrariesArchitectureWest CoastCaliforniafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Readers reply: Why is there no rugby culture in Germany? | Life and style | The Guardian
description: The long-running series in which readers answer other readers’ questions answers a sporting puzzle
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The long-running series in which readers answer other readers’ questions answers a sporting puzzle
Content: France and Italy compete in this year’s men’s Six Nations in February.Photograph: Matthieu Mirville/DPPI/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenFrance and Italy compete in this year’s men’s Six Nations in February.Photograph: Matthieu Mirville/DPPI/ShutterstockNotes and queriesLife and styleReaders reply: Why is there no rugby culture in Germany?The long-running series in which readers answer other readers’ questions answers a sporting puzzleThis week’s question:Some people want to break up the EU. Why don’t they want to break up the US?Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFrance and Italy play rugby to a very high standard, butGermanyhas no comparable rugby culture. Why?Tim Hare, by emailSend new questions tonq@theguardian.com.Readers replyMaybe Germans did once give rugby a try, but not being converted declined to tackle it and instead elected to pass and pack it in?ThereisnoOwlGermany is actually the oldest rugby nation in continental Europe, the game being played there since the 1890s.However, the second world war (especially) decimated the player base, and the following separation (and banning) and reunification projects were a bit too much for the governing bodies, and it never took off to the same degree as it did with their continental neighbours.allthenamesaregoneGermany has the oldest national rugby union in continental Europe. Look up Deutscher Rugby-Verband for information on the three league divisions and the German national team.Peter Dewar, by emailBremen has a rugby club. It used to do a lovely exchange scheme with English clubs in the 1980s where you’d stay with a German player, play some rugby and then they would come to England the following year!Lurch8823There is rugby in Germany but mainly in regions where the British were the occupying force after the second world war and in some university towns.Germany has a national team which plays in the European Nations Cup at level four with the likes of Sweden and Switzerland.Barry J Hake, Narbonne, FranceView image in fullscreenA rugby match between France and Germany in Paris on 24 March 1935.Photograph: AFP/Getty ImagesIn the period before the second world war, the Germans provided serious opposition for the French rugby team after France were excluded from the Five Nations championship in 1931 due to concerns over professionalism. The Germans beat the French at rugby in 1938. The game was particularly popular in Heidelberg, Hanover and Frankfurt. The war destroyed rugby in Germany with most of the national team being killed. For some reason, rugby never recovered its popularity after that. Maybe it was associated too much with Britain and France to make it attractive to postwar Germans.The country has about 125 active clubs with more than 12,000 active players, who are organised into two national leagues and various regional leagues. The men’s national team are ranked 35th in the world. In the Men’s Rugby European Championship in 2026, the top tier of rugby in Europe after the Six Nations, Germany came eighth out of eight behind Georgia (first), Portugal (second), Spain (third), Romania (fourth), Belgium (fifth), Switzerland (sixth) and Netherlands (seventh).Parisse010924I think there are a few main reasons why Germany never developed a major rugby culture. First, unlike countries such as Wales, England, Ireland, Scotland, France, New Zealand and South Africa, rugby was never fully integrated into Germany’s school or community systems. In many of those nations, generations of players were introduced to rugby through schools, local clubs and family traditions, helping the sport become part of the national culture.Second, Germany already has a sport that occupies a very similar space: handball. It offers the same elements of physicality, teamwork, strong club structures and local rivalries, so many athletes who might have been drawn to rugby instead gravitated towards handball.Finally, timing played a big role. Germany established a rugby federation as early as 1900, but football’s growth quickly outpaced that of rugby. By the time of the world wars and the political upheaval that followed, football had become firmly established as the country’s dominant team sport. Rugby was left competing for attention against football, handball and several other established sports, making it difficult to gain nationwide momentum.That said, I think Germany has huge potential as a rugby nation. The country has a strong sporting culture, a large population, and a competitive mindset. If participation were to grow through schools and clubs, Germany could eventually become a significant force in European rugby and help strengthen the game beyond its traditional heartlands, alongside emerging rugby nations such as Portugal and Georgia.David Rossiter, by emailThere are a few German internationals who play in England – including a couple who serve in the British military (I assume kids from when there was a big military presence in Germany).SouthChapelOne of the German TV channels started to show the lastRugby World Cupmatches live and this proved so popular that it has continued to show international matches, including the Six Nations and the latest Nations Championship games. This has sparked an increase in the game in Germany and there are numerous clubs now competing at various levels. There is indeed a rugby culture in Germany. It is just not yet at the same level as in France or England – although outside the big professional clubs, rugby in England has become far less popular as a participant sport as many schools no longer play the game and junior clubs that used to field four or five senior teams on a Saturday now struggle to field two teams on a regular basis.There are also individual German players who are making their mark on the international scene, the most recent being Anton Segner, who went to New Zealand in 2017 as a 16-year-old on a rugby scholarship and who now qualifies for the All Blacks, for whom he made his debut againstItalyon 11 July.HoofitoffIt wasn’t specifically a rugby scholarship, although clearly it was Segner’s ambition to play rugby.MckenziesBayI’m 30 and moved to Germany four years ago specifically to play rugby for a team in the Bundesliga 2. Your statement about rugby having no comparable culture is both correct and incorrect for Germany.View image in fullscreenRugby thrives in Heidelberg.Photograph: querbeet/Getty ImagesFor: there are small pockets of Germany where rugby is thriving. Those being Heidelberg, where they have five rugby teams and are exclusively a rugby city. Munich, where there are four teams and rugby is ever growing, and Berlin.Against: everywhere else in Germany rugby is fighting for player participation for several reasons. I play/coach for Neckarsulm Sport Union. We have the home of Lidl with lots of expats from rugby-playing countries but it is drowned out by lack of awareness and the options for so many other sports. Last season we started training with 20 players: by Christmas this was down to four.This is not just a problem with Neckarsulm, but with all teams outside these rugby hubs. In the last two seasons three men’s teams have pulled out of the Bundesliga and Bundesliga 2 due to players leaving for work or family reasons or to play other sports.This was mainly due to how the Bundesliga 2 is formed, with the closest game being one hour away and the longest being up to four hours away in Luxembourg. It is also a problem with the main German rugby board as clubs used to bolster their numbers with foreign imports but now, in an effort to focus on homegrown talent, players are in a point system where you can only have so many points: 20 points per team per game, plus two for foreign players, plus one for players playing in Germany for four years or a German who has changed clubs. Zero points for German internationals. One point for players who have been trained by their current club since they were 10. This has massively hindered the small clubs relying on people who have moved to Germany to work or even change clubs.My final point is I’ve recently created a women’s rugby team and we’ve had up to 15 people attend but I’m already fighting with other sports to keep them interested and having to show YouTube videos as most have never heard of rugby until they arrive. I have moved now to a new club in Heidelberg as they have absorbed all teams within an hour’s drive as we simply can’t compete.Harry Tanner, by emailMost team sports were codified in the mid 19th century in the UK. Many came from public schools, especially Rugby Union. As Britain built its empire it also exported its sports. The first empire Britain built was its physical empire, the ground a flag was planted on. This empire was driven by wealthy investors and land owners, ex-public school boys who played cricket and Rugby Union.View image in fullscreenHistorical influence … the Sunderland strip.Photograph: Stu Forster/Getty ImagesThe second British empire was the empire of influence, investment and trade driven by the Industrial Revolution. This empire needed British workers to travel the world showing locals how to dig mines or build railways – working-class men who played football. If there’s a football team that plays in red and white strips somewhere in the world it’s because an engineer from Sunderland helped build their railway.This was organic ground-up development. France reeling from the defeat in the Franco-Prussian war looked to Britain, the world superpower, for a way to improve its military capabilities. Aware that the battle of Waterloo was won on the playing fields of Eton, the French investigated public schools and imported Rugby Union. Top-down development. Germany followed the bottom-up path. It was not colonised by rugby-playing public schoolboys but working-class footballers who built their railways. Italy followed both pathways. A very sketchy answer but why countries or even cities and towns play particular sports is a complex mix of history and environment.Glenn Cruddas, by emailI lived and worked in Germany in the early 1990s.I needed to find some local social connections, and having played rugby at school and university it was an obvious choice. The sports editor of the local paper gave me the phone number of the local team.A couple of weeks later I landed up at their first pre-season practice. As a Brit, I was made me very welcome. We were very fortunate, Bad Homberg had a very successful semi-pro football team which bankrolled the rugby team – so even our Christmas dinners were free! It was started by an exchange English teacher from Wales.View image in fullscreenA handball semifinal game in Germany in the 1990s.Photograph: Martin Rose/Bongarts/Getty ImagesThe simple answer is that football (and also handball) are incredibly popular in Germany and swamp out other sports. Germany does have local and national leagues, and their national team play in qualifiers for the World Cup. But with a small player base they have yet to make it to the main event.I spoke pretty fluent German before moving there. I can now also swear proficiently in German!Mark Lucas, West Chester, PennsylvaniaThe second world war is an answer. But more fitting is the fact that Germany was closer to the US than to the Brits. We have more clubs and players in American football than in rugby. You can earn more money with AF and it brings more money into TV channels. And No 1 sports is football. There you have more idols if you’re a young kid.I’ve played for 40 years actively and for 20 years I have been coaching as well. I’ve seen some development over the last two decades. But that’s not enough. Sadly. Because rugby is the best sport in the world.Frank Elsholz, SCS Rugby BerlinRugby was steadily on the way up in Germany, led by Heidelberg and a few other university towns. A key missed opportunity for Germany was the 2018 ruling that there was a conflict of interests with Hans-Peter Wild investing in both Heidelberg Rugby Club (RK) and Stade Français.While a conflict of interests should be taken seriously, that was arguably one that should have been reviewed and managed constructively, given that Heidelberg RK’s European success was a mighty achievement stemming from a successful investment in a potential challenger nation. The ruling, quite possibly, made Germany less attractive for investment in semi-professional rugby and there has clearly been a decline with Germany falling out of contention for the World Cup repechage of late, even within an expanded format. Surely, investors in the clubs of tier-one nations should be encouraged to invest also in growth in the third tier, and be celebrated when the investment successfully raises a team to compete a couple of levels above where they might be expected to compete.Thanks to the World Rugby Nations Cup, now 24 teams have a sustainable pathway, but an investment strategy will be needed soon to keep that newly minted second-tier competitive, by keeping open pathways for the next best teams across Europe, Asia, Africa and the Americas. Learn from Germany and Heidelberg RK and create packages for tier-three investment, and a plan for positive management of any resulting conflict of interests in the future, since the long-term health of the second tier, and hence the growth of rugby internationally, relies on these teams eventually doing well.Ross, Glasgow, by emailNot sure it’s accurate to say that there is no rugby culture in Germany: my boyfriend’s team (Battersea Ironsides) played on tour there in about 1986, and one of my German cousins won a tournament as a member of a Heidelberg team much more recently.Germany men won the first leg of the Rugby Europe Rugby Sevens tournament in Hamburg last month, although the women only managed fifth place in theirs.Rugby Europe matches can be watched online on Rugby Europe TV.DB, by emailI have lived in Berlin for 16 years. I have seen a rapidly growing interest in the game. There are youth teams, men’s teams and women’s teams here. Excellent places such as Piri’s in Neukölln are packed with people watching the World Cup and Six Nations. The men’s national team has also had growing success recently and I believe Germany will be continue to compete more and more in the coming years.Alex from Dorset, by emailView image in fullscreenThe Bauhaus city of Dessau has hosted a rugby team.Photograph: fhm/Getty ImagesI played for about 12 years starting in the early 80s to the beginning of the 90s in East Germany. There have been around 10 clubs in and around East Berlin and two in Leipzig. And one in Dessau. Two leagues, which consists of mostly teams from the area around Berlin (East) and one or two teams from Leipzig. My team was some kind of an elevator team. One year trashing everyone in the 2nd league, the other year losing every game in the first league.But everything was only on a non-professional level. In West Germany it was probably a bit better, but not much, as I know. And after the unification, it still remains on a amateur level. No media coverage.And non-rugby fans confuse it with American football. It would be a hard task to make it more popular in Germany!Hendrik, by emailThere is. Just don’t approach it based on the (endlessly tiresome) assumption that “rugby” equals rugby union. Pop “NRLD” into your browser.Simon Green, Westcliff-on-seaThere is indeed a rugby culture in Germany, but it is centred around university cities in western/southern Germany in the ex-British and French occupation zones and mostly played by students. My ex-team in Aachen in the 80s and 90s had weekly matches with the UK Rhein army teams and the Awacs, as well as other university teams but also Dutch and Belgian teams.View image in fullscreenFlying high … an Awacs early warning system plane.Photograph: JamesReillyWilson/Getty ImagesOur trainer at the time was Willi, an East German who defected in 1986, leaving his team in East Berlin, Stahl Hennigsdorf, a steel-workers’ team. After the Wall fell, we played East German teams, too, where, surprisingly, clubs had sprung up earlier, not seeded by UK/French forces as under Russian occupation.The hurdles to rugby catching on in modern Germany are many: fathers and grandfathers might have played football but very, very rarely rugby, so sons and daughters lack exposure both as spectators and in teams. Both my sons tried rugby for some months but returned to football because they could cycle to their local football team/pitch, their friends were all there and rugby entailed more bumps and bruises and parents having to provide transport.Further, rugby has to compete with American football which has all the big shoulders, helmets, glitter and girlies that attracts adolescent boys (and girls). German mothers worry more about injury rates in rugby, but let their kids have BMX bikes and skateboards.The final and great hurdle is narrow-mindedness on how urban green spaces are allocated as they become increasingly rare. The football lobby is very strong and it sees sports with oval balls as competition for space; added to which German town hall resource allocators can easily swayed by comments like “that sport will shred your green playing fields” or “think of all the injuries and rough behaviour norms that will follow”.Rob Hain, ex Aachen, GermanyThere’s a rugby culture – it’s just simply not popular and it’s a dedicated group of individuals. I played for Viadana at the Munich Oktoberfest sevens in 1991 and Viadana won the cup v Ealing. Teams were military and expat with a scattering of German players plus touring sides.Playing Italian rugby at the start of its exponential development was a fantastic experience. Since that time I’ve coached u14, u16 and u18 for Coq Mosan, near Liège Belgium with games v Aachen, Köln, Frankfurt and many games in Luxembourg with tournaments in the Netherlands plus the regular league in Belgium.The same exists in Germany. It’s just the UK rugby fraternity do not understand or explore what is possible just over the Channel, especially for potential tours and development of rugby camaraderie in Belgium, Germany, Luxembourg and the Netherlands.I know many teams over here would scare the hell out of UK amateur teams. Hopefully, there could be more of a future for UK rugby touring teams playing in Europe but not just France and Italy.Kevin Pycroft, by emailWhy does Britain have no handball culture – a sport widely played in Germany and other European countries.Patrick Murtagh, by emailHaving played rugby in Germany for 20 years, up to second division level (and now watch my club playing at first division level), I can confirm that there is rugby culture, a good grassroots club scene, and some high-quality players who qualify for Germany as well as other nations (see All Blacks and Canada debutants from this month as examples), as well as a population base built for the sport (athletic, big people who aren’t shy of a bit of contact).Unfortunately, football, handball and basketball receive more state support and media coverage. Additionally, the DRV (German rugby union) has historically focused support only on the Heidelberg-based clubs and financial and people management scandals devolving from that Heidelberg clique engulf the sport every few years to the point where investors in other clubs – Frankfurt and Pforzheim to name a couple of examples – have pulled support due to that Heidelberg bias coming out of the DRV.If they ever committed at a national level from the top down then they’d likely be very good within just a few years.elbourgThere are German rugby players playing but the good ones are outside Germany.MacaumikeApparently, Germany targeted participating in the 2015 Rugby World Cup but didn’t qualify. Not sure what happened in 2019 or 2023, but it seems they have yet to qualify for any of them despite the national team playing at the second level of European rugby.andya2015It’s not entirely true that rugby is absent in Germany. As a tour guide with an English school group, we chanced to be at a sports field outside the Olympic Stadium a few years ago where the Berlin Rugby Club were having their annual barbecue. We were invited to join them. We discovered that they were very enthusiastic about the game and had no trouble finding opposing teams in Germany. The fact that the Olympic Stadium was in the British zone of occupation between 1945 and 1989 doubtless had something to do with this.AnschauerWhen I was stationed with the RAF in West Berlin in the 1980s, we regularly played German clubs – very sociable occasions involving loads of beer. Mind you, all our rugby matches involved loads of beer.Murphy15There assuredly is as my brother-in-law would point out as a regular club player in Potsdam. It’s just a lot smaller than the UK.F1SocialistExplore more on these topicsLife and styleNotes and queriesRugby World CupRugby unionGermanyFranceItalyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Dining across the divide: ‘I was very surprised to meet someone committed to Conservatism at such a young age’ | Life and style | The Guardian
description: A social media manager and a​ transport psychology ​researcher disagreed over Palestine Action, but who ​changed their view on the triple-loc​k?
Author: Zoe Williams
Headline: A social media manager and a​ transport psychology ​researcher disagreed over Palestine Action, but who ​changed their view on the triple-loc​k?
Content: Hugo (on left) and William. All photographs: Martin Godwin/The GuardianView image in fullscreenHugo (on left) and William. All photographs: Martin Godwin/The GuardianDining across the divideLife and styleDining across the divide: ‘I was very surprised to meet someone committed to Conservatism at such a young age’A social media manager and a​ transport psychology ​researcher disagreed over Palestine Action, but who ​changed their view on the triple-loc​k?Want to meet someone from across the divide? Clickhereto find out howZoe WilliamsSun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleView image in fullscreenHugo, 21, BirminghamOccupationSocial media manager for the Conservative partyVoting recordAlways ConservativeAmuse boucheHugo took up competitive powerlifting as a teenager to deal with stress during the pandemicView image in fullscreenWilliam, 39, BirminghamOccupationResearcher in transport psychologyVoting recordWilliam can vote in both the UK and France, and usually opts for a leftwing party – here, either Labour or GreenAmuse boucheWilliam was once attacked by geese while running along the canal. In revenge, he added a question about geese to his transport research, and it turned out roughly 30% of runners have had a goose incidentFor startersHugoI thought he seemed like a really reasonable, nice guy. He told me about the work he does in transport; it’s really respectable, useful stuff.WilliamI was very surprised to meet someone committed to Conservatism at such a young age. But he was very articulate. He’s knowledgable, he knows the policies much better than I do. You can’t judge a book by its cover.HugoI had buttermilk chicken tenders, for the main I had fish and chips, and for dessert I had a chocolate souffle.WilliamStarter I had prawn cocktail, and the fish and chips. It’s a British pub.View image in fullscreenThe big beefHugoWe talked about Palestine Action, in light of the four who were sentenced recently. He wasn’t aware that one of those quote-unquote protesters hit a policewoman with a sledgehammer. A lot of the points I made were in his media blind spot. So he’d say: “If what you’re saying is true, I agree with you.”WilliamThat was our spiciest disagreement. He said there were a number of facts that had been cherrypicked by the media, and other things that weren’t covered. If someone has been attacked from behind with a hammer, the perpetrator should be in jail, but four of them sent to jail for at least four years and eight months, I think it’s a bit harsh. They’ve been destroying military goods funded by public money. They believed these weapons, these drones, were being used for a genocide. We agreed that it was a genocide.HugoPalestine Action undoubtedly is a terrorist organisation. If the courts are saying it is, if politicians are saying it is, then it is. It’s violent action with the intention of influencing government. The sentencing was absolutely just. If you do have objections to what the government’s doing, you can take out that frustration at the ballot box, not with a sledgehammer.WilliamRather than being imprisoned, they should have been made to do community service, and it would be very tongue-in-cheek and ironic, since they destroyed military equipment, to make them do military service.View image in fullscreenSharing plateHugoI started to explain some of the moral issues around the triple lock – you’re taking the wealthiest of the generations, and plying them with more and more cash, while loads of graduates are going on the dole because they can’t find any work – loads of young families are really struggling.WilliamHe said something that struck me: “I’m not even counting on having a pension.” He’s not saying: “We should remove the triple lock, so I can benefit from it.” He’s saying: “We should have something less expensive and invest elsewhere,” assuming that he wasn’t personally going to benefit from it either way. So his point of view wasn’t selfish, it wasn’t centred on his own generation. I didn’t realise the triple lock was so expensive, and now I’m not sure it is viable.View image in fullscreenFor aftersHugoThe Rwanda scheme was a popular policy with the electorate, but Rishi Sunak’s government was initially unable to carry it out because of the ECHR. We should be setting the standards with a British bill of rights.WilliamI didn’t know the Rwanda scheme was popular, but I didn’t contest it. I still think you need an international gatekeeper of inalienable rights. Sometimes what the population expresses isn’t what’s best for the population.View image in fullscreenTakeawaysDining across the divide: ‘I had an idea he was a Tommy Robinson fan and was thinking, Oh my God’Read moreHugoHe said that when you sit down with someone from the Conservative party, it was much closer to what he believed than he realised. I was proud of myself for getting him to that point.WilliamHe’s a gentleman. We connected on LinkedIn.View image in fullscreenAdditional reporting: Kitty DrakeHugo and William ate atThe Old Joint Stockin BirminghamWant to meet someone from across the divide?Find out how to take partExplore more on these topicsLife and styleDining across the divideSocial trendsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Vieni, Birmingham – ‘A happy hubbub – you cannot pipe this in’ – restaurant review  | Food | The Guardian
description: Vieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often fail
Author: Grace Dent
Headline: Vieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often fail
Content: Vieni, Birmingham: ‘It isn’t remotely intimidating, and you are actually being fed’.Photograph: Jack Spicer Adams/The GuardianView image in fullscreenVieni, Birmingham: ‘It isn’t remotely intimidating, and you are actually being fed’.Photograph: Jack Spicer Adams/The GuardianGrace Dent on restaurantsFoodReviewVieni, Birmingham – ‘A happy hubbub – you cannot pipe this in’ – restaurant reviewVieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often failGrace DentSun 19 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 05.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVieni, an Italian restaurant in the Jewellery Quarter of Birmingham, is small, Sicilian and scrappy. The Instagram bio of its chef and founderAngelina Adamoreads, “Bite off more than you can chew and learn how to fucking chew”, which feels like a battle cry for almost everyone stepping into hospitality, especially independent places like Vieni, where the waters are choppy and your skillset needs to be varied. Arancini with one hand, signing off payroll and ordering loo paper with the other.Vieni, which is about a mile or so from the city’s Bull Ring, is a million miles fromAlbert’s Schloss, Big Mamma’sLa Bellezzaand the multitude of vast, impersonal pleasure machines that have spread their legs in Brum city centre. Of course, a giganticRosa’sserving pad thai andTattuwith a plastic purple flower ceiling all have their place, and make people happy, but I have joy in my heart for places like Vieni.View image in fullscreen‘Hearty, not trendy’: Vieni’s barbecued whole sea bass, with courgette and basilPrior to this opening, Adamo was running the kitchen atThe Circle Loungeinside Birmingham Hippodrome, serving weary thesps and parents seeking refuge from Robin Hood the pantomime, with plates of marvellous arancini. She has now moved to this tiny, beloved nook on the edge of the newly emergingGoodsyarddevelopment, serving coils of Sicilian fennel sausage withcacio e pepebutter beans, fregola with clams, and an eccentric, ever-changingdolceof the day that is worth the train fare alone. Note: many property developers claim to be building in the quaintly named Jewellery Quarter but are actually miles away; Vieni is just beside the tiny station. En route I bought vintage clip-on earrings from a man in a bullet-proof kiosk, while on the corner a sandwich board offered gold grills for your teeth from £60, with free moulding and postage.Vieni’s menu is hearty, not trendy – it’s not glorified picky bits and puzzling sharing plates. It is, instead, 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne in slices as a starter; Pyefleet oysters with British strawberry and champagne vinegar; seabass crudo with peaches, basil and green chilli; and ruffled ribbons ofmafaldinewith vodka tomato sauce. We snuck in on a Saturday at around half past four, myself and the weary Charles, and grabbed a window table for a Sicilian Slapper, draught Poretti and Limonata Radler, and a glass of Luscombe’s lightly sparkling rhubarb pop. Soon we were ordering a round of 1946 gildas and a plate of majoram polenta bites. We’d popped in for snacks; now we were begging them to serve us dinner.I needed one of the pea, broad bean and pecorino arancini that Adamo is so adept at; these aren’t your piddly quail’s egg-sized arancini so popular in Britain. This was a magnificent object, perched in a puddle of tomato sauce and heaped with pecorino. Next up, that 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne: lots of depth, lots of softness. The ultimate cuddle on a plate.View image in fullscreen‘An ultimate cuddle on a plate’: Vieni’s 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne.Vieni has that thing that pleases me most in restaurants: a happy hubbub. You cannot pipe this in or orchestrate it. A happy restaurant hubbub manifests when diners are genuinely happy with their s’mores toasted marshmallow gelato, or, as we had, a large barbecued sea bream with peperoncino ’nduja butter. Diners were chuffed with their burrata, melon and Brummie honey, littered with mint and pistachio, or their whole globe artichoke with hazelnut pangrattato. This wasn’t likeKawan, who you may remember from last week, where the atmosphere was of people who had been very politely mugged.View image in fullscreenDolce of the day: Vieni’s Sicilian lemon and  vanilla cannolo.Vieni dances a fine line where British-Italian restaurants often fail. It’s not the Spaghetti House or a red-sauce mum-and-pop restaurant; it sits closer in spirit toMoroon Exmouth Market, London, but it isn’t remotely intimidating and you are actually being fed. The menu is ever changing – that dolce of the day, a s’mores toasted marshmallow gelato when we visited, for example, seems now to be a maraschino cherry gelato with a lattice shortcrust. That’s one of the sexiest things I’ve written this year.Grab a table. Bite off more than you can chew. Then eat the bloody lot.Vieni, 41 Pitsford Street, Goodsyard, B18 6LJ, 0121 806 0080; vieni.co.uk. Open lunch Weds–Sat noon-2pm, dinner Tue-Sat 5pm-9pm, plus brunch Sat 9.30am-11.45am (10am-2pm Sun). About £50 a head for three courses, plus drinks and service.Explore more on these topicsFoodGrace Dent on restaurantsRestaurantsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: How to make Russian salad – recipe | Food | The Guardian
description: Cooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dish
Author: Felicity Cloake
Headline: Cooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dish
Content: Felicity Cloake's Russian salad.Photograph: Robert Billington/The Guardian. Food styling: Loïc Parisot.View image in fullscreenFelicity Cloake's Russian salad.Photograph: Robert Billington/The Guardian. Food styling: Loïc Parisot.Felicity Cloake's masterclassFoodHow to make Russian salad – recipeCooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dishFelicity CloakeSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn much of the world, including Russia itself, this richly satisfying collation is known assalade olivier, after the 19th-century Belgian-born chef credited with its creation. Its wide popularity is testament to its adaptability and essential deliciousness: robust enough for picnics and packed lunches, it also keeps well and is great paired with just about anything, from fish to a simple green salad.Prep40 minCook15 minServes4For the salad200g waxy potatoes(ie, about 4 small ones)100g carrots(ie, 1 medium one)100g turnip(about ½ medium one, or 2 extra potatoes, if you prefer)Salt50g green beans2 eggs(optional)75g frozen peas2 large gherkins, plus 2 tbsp of their pickling liquid1 tbsp capers, rinsed if necessary50g ham, or pressed tongue or cooked chicken (optional, see step 9)1 small bunch dill, or chives, finely choppedFor the homemade mayonnaise (alternatively, use 3 tbsp ready-made)1 egg2 tsp dijon mustard1 generous pinch salt1 tbsp white-wine vinegar, or lemon juice180ml groundnut oil, or light olive or sunflower oil25ml extra-virgin olive oil(optional)1 A note on the ingredientsMayonnaise aside, the exact make-up of this salad is entirely up to you, though I would recommend including something starchy (potatoes are the usual base, but you might prefer to use sweet potato, parsnips or yam) and a selection of other flavours and textures, with a preference for cooked, rather than raw ingredients.View image in fullscreen2 Prep the root veg and beansStart by peeling the potatoes, carrot and turnip, and top and tail the beans. Cut the carrot and turnip into large chunks, but keep the potatoes whole, unless any are particularly large. Other common additions include cauliflower, broad beans and beetroot (though add the latter as a garnish at the end, or it will turn the mayonnaise pink).3 Boil the potatoesCooking each vegetable separately might seem a faff, but in such a simple dish it’s really worth doing to ensure that each element is perfect. Put the potatoes in a pan of cold salted water, bring to a boil and cook until tender; try not to overcook them or they’ll be hard to cut neatly. Drain and leave to dry in the hot pan.View image in fullscreen4 Cook the rest of the veg and eggsMeanwhile, bring another pan of salted water to a boil, drop in the beans and cook for two minutes. Scoop out into a large bowl filled with cold water (or the sink). In the same pan of boiling water, repeat with the carrots and turnip, then hard-boil the eggs for 10 minutes. Put the peas in a colander or sieve and pour boiling water over them to thaw.5 A note on the mayoThis salad is bound only by mayonnaise (a vegan one, if necessary), so it’s important that it tastes good. If there’s a jarred sort you like, by all means use that, but I really think it’s worth making from scratch for this dish. These days, I’m more likely to go for the stick-blender option than the traditional sort (though do feel free to get out the whisk if you wish).View image in fullscreen6 Start the mayoTo make stick-blender mayonnaise (this will give you more than you need here, but it keeps well), put the whole egg, mustard, salt and vinegar in a jar or glass that’s just wider than the head of the blender. Pour the lighter oil on top, leave for a minute to settle, then plunge in the blender so the head sits on the base of the jar.7 Finish the mayoTurn on to high speed and, without lifting the blender head, whizz until you see mayonnaise billowing out from the sides of the head. Gently tilt the jar this way and that, and gradually introduce more oil. Very slowly lift up the head of the blender through the oil until it’s all blended in. Whisk in the extra oil, taste and adjust the seasoning.View image in fullscreen8 Chop the veg and eggsDrain, dry and chop all the vegetables (except the peas, of course) into roughly 1cm dice or lengths, then mix these in a large bowl. Cut the gherkins (or other pickles) into 1cm dice and add to the bowl with two tablespoons of their pickling vinegar, the capers and peas. Peel the eggs (doing so underwater makes the process easier) and coarsely grate or dice into the salad.View image in fullscreen9 Finishing touchesCut the meat, if using, into strips (tofu or drained beans or pulses would be a good plant-based alternative; cooked fish or seafood such as brown shrimp, smoked trout or white crabmeat are also a pleasing addition). Finally, stir in the chopped herbs and about three tablespoons of the mayonnaise – you need only just enough to coat – then serve, or cover and store in the fridge.Explore more on these topicsFoodFelicity Cloake's masterclassSaladVegetablesPotatoesEggsHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The kindness of strangers: After losing my wife and son, I ate alone one Father’s Day – and another diner paid my bill | Australian lifestyle | The Guardian
description: I went out into the car park to see if I could spot anyone nearby who might have just left the restaurant
Author: Katie Cunningham
Headline: I went out into the car park to see if I could spot anyone nearby who might have just left the restaurant
Content: ‘I’ve got to admit that when I got to my car, I sat inside and had a good weep.’Composite: Guardian design/AlamyView image in fullscreen‘I’ve got to admit that when I got to my car, I sat inside and had a good weep.’Composite: Guardian design/AlamyKindness of strangersAustralian lifestyleThe kindness of strangers: After losing my wife and son, I ate alone one Father’s Day – and another diner paid my billI went out into the car park to see if I could spot anyone nearby who might have just left the restaurantRead more inthe kindness of strangers seriesAllen Renfrey, as told toKatie CunninghamSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIlost my wife in 2020 and my son in 2021. A few years later, I was living alone in a retirement complex when I decided to go out one night for dinner. There was a little Vietnamese restaurant nearby that my wife and I used to frequent, which had excellent food and held many happy memories. It just so happened to be Father’s Day.I dined alone, ordering my usual rice paper rolls. When I went to settle the bill, the lass behind the counter told me my meal had already been paid for.The kindness of strangers: I was hopelessly ill in China – then hotel staff offered to take my elderly father sightseeingRead more“What do you mean, paid for?” I asked.She answered that a couple who had left the restaurant a few minutes before had paid for my meal. She said the lady in that couple had remarked that it was not fair that a man had to sit alone, on Father’s Day, so they picked up the cheque.I have no idea who they were. I went out into the car park to look around and see if I could spot anyone nearby who might have just left the restaurant, but couldn’t. I’ve got to admit that when I got to my car, I sat inside and had a good weep.That lady would not have known my circumstances. She knew nothing about me – all she saw was a man eating alone on Father’s Day, and she wanted to do something about it. I am indebted to those strangers who brought a little light to a dark day.What is the nicest thing a stranger has ever done for you?Share your experienceWhat's the nicest thing a stranger has ever done for you?From making your day to changing your life, we want to hear about chance encounters that have stuck with you.Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish. For more information please see ourterms of serviceandprivacy policy.Tell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.What is your name?Where do you live (city, state and country)?Tell us a bit about the kind act you've experienced?Where and when did this encounter take place?Can we publish your response?Yes, entirelyYes, but contact me firstYes, but please keep me anonymousNo, this is information onlyAre you comfortable with a journalist contacting you about this story for potential publication?YesNoWhat is your phone number?OptionalYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.What is your email addressYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.Would you be interested in speaking to our audio and/or video teams?Audio onlyVideo onlyAudio and videoNo, I'm not interestedBy submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreExplore more on these topicsAustralian lifestyleKindness of strangersBereavementFamilyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: I ghosted a problematic friend. Now I feel terrible, but also relieved | Friendship | The Guardian
description: Your friend gave you little choice but to end your relationship. You were right to prioritise your own wellbeing
Author: Annalisa Barbieri
Headline: Your friend gave you little choice but to end your relationship. You were right to prioritise your own wellbeing
Content: Illustration: Alex Mellon/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Alex Mellon/The GuardianAsk Annalisa BarbieriFriendshipI ghosted a problematic friend. Now I feel terrible, but also relievedYour friend gave you little choice but to end your relationship. You were right to prioritise your own wellbeingAnnalisa BarbieriSun 19 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 10.07 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI had a friend whoI always felton edge around,but I tried to push past thatand give them an opportunity. They had experienced childhood traumaand had mental health issues. I put my discomfort down tohaving to be a bit more accommodating.Theyseemed incapable of maintaining friendshipsand would paint themselves as a victim, saying they constantly get ghosted.Not long ago I was on the receiving end ofan outburst for the first time. They had gone through a couple of stressful situations and I tried to be there for them. Ayear later I had other priorities and stressesthat meant I had to focus on my own life. They then snapped at me and dismissed what I had going onand threw a tantrum. When they realised they were in the wrong,they said it was due to theirtrauma and mental health issues.Ireflected on the relationship and felt a fool for going along with it when my gut was always telling me something was up.I accepted their apology andslowly stopped replying as frequently to messages, hoping they’d get the hint, buteventually I had to ghost them. I feel badbut at the same timefeelas if a weight has been lifted. How do I move on from this feeling?I wonder when we started calling “having enough of someone” “ghosting”. It shifts emphasis entirely to the person doing it rather than the reason for it. I think you feel bad because you are sensitive. But just as we wouldn’t continually put ourselves in situations that harmed us, we should be wary of people that do the same.UKCP-accredited psychotherapist Noel Bell thought you were “very self-aware and emotionally honest to reflect on that ‘on-edge feeling’ and to acknowledge it for what it really was. When we experience such a strong negative somatic reaction to someone, that could be our intuition trying to tell us something. Could it be that your empathy and compassion ended up overriding your worries? Compassionate people can often confuse healthy concern for others with tolerating toxic behaviour.”double quotation markAnger is a healthy emotion. It’s there to tell us a boundary has been crossedI feel for your friend and anyone who has had childhood trauma and mental health issues, but as adults we do have to take responsibility for our behaviour. “Having issues,” says Bell, “does not allow people a free pass to mistreat or abuse others. When your friend said that their outburst was ‘not personal’ they were trying to erase the impact of their actions on you.”I think you gave this friend a really good chance but, explains Bell: “Remember that a friendship effectively becomes an unsustainable caretaking role when one person is required to neglect their own emotional needs, life priorities and wellbeing just to keep the other person stable.”Bell felt – and I agree – that you had no other option but to walk away. “Ultimately,” he said, “you ghosted them because the compassionate approach had failed.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionI wish my son wanted to spend more time with me | Ask Annalisa BarbieriRead moreAnger is a healthy emotion. We often think of it as a failing, but it’s there to tell us a boundary has been crossed. It is, says Bell, “a sign of a healthy self-esteem. It is the resilient part of you that recognises you were treated unfairly and says ‘I deserve better’.”“You didn’t leave the friendship out of cruelty,” Bell wants you to remember, “but out of exhaustion. Choosing your own peace of mind over someone else’s emotional demands is not selfish, it’s self-care.”Think: “What else could I have practically done?” Once you realise the answer is “nothing without sacrificing my own mental health”, I think you’ll find more peace.Every week, Annalisa Barbieri addresses a personal problem sent in by a reader. If you would like advice from Annalisa, please send your problem toask.annalisa@theguardian.com. Annalisa regrets she cannot enter into personal correspondence. Submissions are subject toour terms and conditions. The latest series of Annalisa’s podcast is availablehere.Comments on this piece are pre-moderated to ensure the discussion remains on the topics raised by the article. Please be aware that there may be a short delay in comments appearing on the site.Explore more on these topicsFriendshipAsk Annalisa BarbierifeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘We noticed a login from a new device’: the message from fraudsters targeting your X account | Scams | The Guardian
description: They are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacks
Author: Samuel Gibbs
Headline: They are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacks
Content: Fraudsters want direct access to your account to commit further fraud – but there are telltale signs.Photograph: AlexPhotoStock/AlamyView image in fullscreenFraudsters want direct access to your account to commit further fraud – but there are telltale signs.Photograph: AlexPhotoStock/AlamyScam watchScams‘We noticed a login from a new device’: the message from fraudsters targeting your X accountThey are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacksSamuel GibbsConsumer technology editorSun 19 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 02.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleYou have had anXaccount for years, since it was known as Twitter. When an email arrives about a new login from a location nowhere near where you live, alarm bells begin to ring.“We noticed a login to your account from a new device. Was this you?” the email asks.The location is Arizona, but you live in London. The device is “Firefox Desktop on Mac”.“If this wasn’t you,” the email continues, “complete these steps now to protect your account.”The steps include clicking a link to change your password, which will end up with you “logged out of all your active X sessions except the one you’re using at this time” and to click another link to review the apps that have access to your account and revoke any that are unfamiliar.The steps are legitimate advice from X, but the links to reset your password, or to review app access are not. The email is fake and attempting to trick you into giving scammers your password, or direct access to your X account.“Scammers want your X username and password, or to trick you into approving a malicious link that gives them access to your account without needing your password,” says Jake Moore, a global cybersecurity adviser at ESET.Once the criminals have access to your account they will more than likely use it to attempt to commit further fraud including crypto scams, phishing attacks and misinformation campaigns.What it looks likeThe fake emails are almost an exact replica of legitimate login notifications sent by X. They include the X logo, and the same formatting, colours and copy, with correct grammar and spelling.View image in fullscreenSpot the difference: a scam email pretending to be an X new device login notification (left) next to a legitimate notification (right).Composite: Samuel Gibbs/The GuardianSmall telltale signs show the email is not legitimate, such as not including your X account handle, and being vague on the location of the login.“The two biggest giveaways are the email address it comes from, and where the links actually take you,” says Moore.Thesocial media site says: “X will only send you emails from @X.com or @e.X.com… Please know that X will never send emails with attachments, or request your X password by email … and will never ask you to provide your password via email, direct message or reply.”If you do click on a link in the email, you will be brought to a fake website designed to steal your password, or to authorise a scammer’s app to access your account directly, often under the guise of a tool for performing a “security audit” or “troubleshooting”.What to do“If you ever receive an email like this, it is very normal, but remember not to panic, and don’t click the links to divulge any personal data. Instead, open the genuine app, and if there really is a security issue, you’ll see it there,” Moore says.Check the email headers and URL links to ensure they are from the X.com domain. You can report fraudulent emails to your email provider using the built-in spam and phishing tools.“If you clicked on a link, and only opened the page, you’re probably fine,” Moore says. “But if you have ever entered your password, or a one-time passcode to a web address you didn’t check, change your password immediately and double-check you have two-factor authentication turned on.”If you suspect your X account has been compromised, follow thesocial media site’s help guide. The company says it may reset the passwords of any account it believes may have been hacked, and will send a secure link sent via email to select a new password.Explore more on these topicsScamsScam watchConsumer affairsXSocial mediafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: No car? No problem! Six cheap family days out in England by bus | Public transport trips | The Guardian
description: With bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and more
Author: Phoebe Taplin
Headline: With bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and more
Content: Norwich bus station is the starting point for trips to the Sainsbury Centre’s sculpture park and Norwich Castle.Photograph: Andrew Sparkes/AlamyView image in fullscreenNorwich bus station is the starting point for trips to the Sainsbury Centre’s sculpture park and Norwich Castle.Photograph: Andrew Sparkes/AlamyCar-free UKPublic transport tripsNo car? No problem! Six cheap family days out in England by busWith bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and morePhoebe TaplinSun 19 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTrips to waterfalls, gardens, galleries, medieval forests and prehistoric caves will make the long school holidays a lot more fun. To help families explore affordably, throughout Augustbuses in England will be freefor kids and adult fares will becapped at £3single. Or, if you arrive somewhere by train, you could buy aPlusBus ticket, which include unlimited local bus travel. Here are six ideas for family outings by bus around England.Great North Museum, NewcastleView image in fullscreenTynemouth Priory and Castle on a headland overlooking two beaches east of Newcastle.Photograph: Robert Harding/AlamyPainted mummy cases, fossilised dino teeth, a stuffed wombat, a fake merman … Newcastle’sGreat North Museum: Hancockis full of things kids might love – and entrance is free. Some of the impressive Roman altars are lit with colourful projections and there’s aplanetariumwith shows (from £2.95). This summer’s exhibitionTreasure: Hidden, Lost, Found(free, until 20 Sept) involves thoughtfully curated shiny stuff, from Roman coins to Viking silver unearthed across northern England. Lots of buses stop nearby, and abus ticket gets you 10% offin the cafe.This summer, there’s also aTales on the Tynetrail across Newcastle of giant Peter Rabbit sculptures and other, smaller “fluffles” to raise funds for St Oswald’s hospice. The waterfront hotelINNSiDE Newcastle, near the station, hosts a Tynetastic Peter sculpture, plus art and reading events. Its riversiderestauranthas a bambino menu and under-11s get free meals until 6 September. The hotel offersfamily-friendlyrooms (from £119 for four in August, room-only), activity hampers and a free soft-drink minibar.In stylish Ouseburn, a short bus ride away, theBiscuit Factorygallery hosts three further fluffles, freekids’ activities, rabbit-themed food and a roof terrace.Bus 306runs regularly all the way out toTynemouthfor an afternoon on the beach undercastle-topped cliffs. Back in the city, the art decoTyneside cinemahas selected films at £5 each week and kids’ classics over the summer.Forest fun near Hatfield,EssexView image in fullscreenThe boardwalk through Hatfield Forest’s ancient royal hunting ground.Photograph: PBP Vision/AlamyOpen every day and free to enter, the National Trust’s Hatfield Forest has 400 hectares (1,000 acres) of Frisbee-ready flowering fields and shady woodland. Incongruously close to Stansted airport, theforestwas once a royal hunting ground and is still home to hundreds of deer, some descended from Henry I’s original 12th-century herds. You can follow a boardwalk through twisting hornbeams, ramble across buttercup-carpeted meadows, build a den and hear yaffling woodpeckers.Bus 509from Bishop’s Stortford stops outside The Green Man, a short walk from the forest’s edge. Briefly follow the road signed Hatfield Forest and turn right up the path just after the bridge to find a map and gateway.The lakeside picnic tables and alfrescocafeare a delightful mile away, past cattle-grazed pastures busy with butterflies, and 1,000-year-old trees. With no car to get back to (parking costs £9), make this a linear walk.Loop through woodstoThremhall Prioryor follow theThree Forests wayfor4½ milesto the village of Hatfield Broad Oak and catchbus 305(not Sundays).In Bishop’s Stortford itself, there’s a riversidesplash pool and playgroundsnear the recently revamped castle mound.South Mill arts centrehas kids’crafts for £2andMuch Hadham Forge museumhas afree eventon 27 August, featuring a Tudor storyteller, to celebrate 450 years since their Elizabethan murals were painted.
