Half-timbered Much Hadham is 20 minutes from Stortford on hourlybus 35(not Sundays).Cave art inNottinghamshireView image in fullscreenPrehistoric rock art can be found at spectacular Cresswell Crags.Photograph: Steve Bramall/Getty Images/iStockphotoThe Welbeck Estate’swalking mapincludes an easy-going,sculpture-dotted trailtoCreswell Crags, where a bus ticket gets youfree entry to the museumand 10% off in the cafe. A stroll through the limestone gorge is spectacular, past ponds and caves where prehistoric humans lived and left rock art on the walls.Bus 209from Worksop stops every couple of hours (not Sundays) near theDukeries garden centreand Welbeck courtyard at the start of the trail.Welbeck has a freemuseumandgalleryin converted outbuildings. Highlights include the pearl earring Charles I was wearing when he was beheaded and a red chalkMadonna by Michelangelo. The award-winningHarley cafenext door serves local food (kids’ soup £5, cheese on toast £6).Half-price Harewood,WestYorkshireView image in fullscreenHarewood House is packed with kids’ activities – and art for the adults.Photograph: Tom ArberLike many big country houses,Harewoodusually charges adults well over £20 for a day’s worth of paintings, porcelain and landscaped gardens. But arrive on the frequentbus 36from Leeds or Harrogate, and you get50% off walk-up ticket prices. This gives you access to the art-filled house, theCapability Brown parklandand gardens with scented borders, waterfalls and bridges in thickets of bamboo.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionFor kids, there’s a woodedadventure playgroundwith zipwire and slides, atreehouseand awillow maze. They can also borrow a naturesafari bag, complete with map and compass, torch, binoculars and magnifying glass. And the summer holidays are packed withextra offers, all free with a ticket: den-building, gardening, statue-cleaning, dressing up, circus skills, and mid-Augustworkshops by Tibetan monks.Inside, there’s an exhibition of glamorous photos byCecil Beaton(until 4 October). Alternatively, a5-mile circular walkfrom the bus stop takes in deer, buzzards, rolling parkland and theMuddy Boots cafe. Half an hour away on the 36 bus, Leeds has the free five-storeyRoyal Armouries museumoverlooking the dock.Word-class art indoors and out in NorwichView image in fullscreenA family enjoys the outdoor sculpture trail at the Sainsbury Centre.Photograph: Kate WolstenholmeElisabeth Frink’sostrich-like structuresstalk through waterside woods. Antony Gormley’scast iron figuresstand poised on rooftops and walkways across the brutalist University of East Anglia (UEA) campus. Henry Moore’sbronze forms reclineon the lawns, and angular steelbeastsby Lynn Chadwick crouch under a model of VladimirTatlin’s tower. The freesculpture parkat theSainsbury Centreis packed with iconic names from 20th-century art and architecture and set in 140 hectares of parkland that are yours to explore, with centuries-old oaks, wild rabbits, and freefamily-friendly toursandtrails.Inside the Norman Foster-designed gallery (pay what you can), relaunched in 2023, the works are eclectic. You can wander from ancient Chinese tomb figures to a dynamic new installation via a work by Picasso. Kids might like the glass box you can stand in to“become a work of art”near John Davies’ hyper-realistic Bucket Man. The ambitioussummer exhibitionslook at the meaning of life and aspects of play. Show your bus ticket in the cafe for a10% discount.Bus 25from the station or city centre runs frequently to UEA and stops close to the Sainsbury Centre. It passes the huge square keep ofNorwich Castle, which reopened last year after a £27.5m renovation project. There are sound-and-light shows on the old stone walls of the Great Hall and a£2.50 twilight ticketif you visit an hour before closing time.Art trails, swimming spots and punt safaris, all easily accessible from Cambridge’s new train stationRead moreWildlife watching in the LakesView image in fullscreenThere’s dippers, dragonflies and red squirrels to be spotted at Aira Force waterfall in the Lake District.Photograph: Dave Porter/AlamyThe mossy, fern-fringed waterfalls atAira Forceare 45 minutes on bus508from Penrith station. The route runs beside Ullswater with views across the lake to bracken-cloaked Hallin Fell. It’s one of the trips in a new book onWhere to Watch Wildlife in Britain By Low-Carbon Transport, along with 100 other car-free trips to nature-rich sites across the UK.Look out for dippers, dragonflies and red squirrels. The area offers varied and spectacular walks, such as a pushchair- and mobility scooter-friendlystroll through the woodsat Pooley Bridge or a hike upGowbarrow Fell.Explore more on these topicsPublic transport tripsCar-free UKEngland holidaysWalkingUnited Kingdom holidaysYorkshire holidaysNottingham holidaysEssex holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Adam Lambert looks back: ‘I couldn’t pass up on joining Queen – even if the fans were protective of Freddie’ | Family | The Guardian
description: The singer and actor on the edgy artists of his youth, a controversial 2009 kiss, and his relationship with Brian May and Roger Taylor
Author: Harriet Gibsone
Headline: The singer and actor on the edgy artists of his youth, a controversial 2009 kiss, and his relationship with Brian May and Roger Taylor
Content: FlashbackFamilyAdam Lambert looks back: ‘I couldn’t pass up on joining Queen – even if the fans were protective of Freddie’The singer and actor on the edgy artists of his youth, a controversial 2009 kiss, and his relationship with Brian May and Roger TaylorHarriet GibsoneSun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAdam Lambert in 1994 and recreating the image in 2026Adam Lambert in 1994 and 2026. Later photograph: Simon Webb/The Guardian. Styling: Andie Redman. Grooming: Celine Nonon at Arlington Artists. Archive photograph: courtesy of Adam LambertBorn in Indianapolis in 1982, actor and musician Adam Lambert rose to fame in 2009 as runner-up on the eighth season ofAmerican Idol. His second album, Trespassing, made history as the first No 1 on the Billboard 200 by an openly gay male artist. He went on to front Queen + Adam Lambert from 2012, made his Broadway debut in 2024 as the Emcee in Cabaret at the Kit Kat Club, and starred as Judas in Jesus Christ Superstar at the Hollywood Bowl in 2025. His sixth solo album, Adam, is out now. He performs at London’s Roundhouse on 21 July.This was my first professional headshot. When I was 12 I would spend a lot of time driving from San Diego to Los Angeles for auditions with my mum and my brother, who also wanted to act. That guy in the plaid shirt never booked a single job. My brother did, though. He was a far cuter, more photogenic kid than I was.At this age, I was talkative and friendly. I tended to connect better with adults than children, and I was really enthusiastic about the arts – obsessed with theatre and show business. After school, I would fanatically watch videos on MTV; the fresh wave of alternative music in the 90s was like a jolt of lightning. There were dark, edgy artists, and their visuals gave me a funny feeling that I was also fascinated by, such as the video forCloserby Nine Inch Nails or anything by Daft Punk, the Chemical Brothers or Massive Attack.My self-belief was pretty stable,but that started to dismantle when puberty came along. As well as the weird new hormones, middle school introduced social expectations that I hadn’t considered before. There was this sudden segregation of tribes: boys and girls stopped hanging out, and I was suddenly conscious of my identity. Was I cool or not cool? Was I a skater or a surfer? I wasn’t anything, and I didn’t like that it was so rigid. As for my sexuality, I didn’t even know what that meant yet. I was too busy trying to blend in.double quotation markI sang Bohemian Rhapsody for the TV auditions, and my hair was jet black with a side parting – very 00s MyspaceI didn’t get into any high-end musical theatre schools as my grades weren’t quite competitive enough. Being in a classroom wasn’t for me, and I hadn’t put enough effort into the exams. Instead, I worked in theatre during the summer, and became a singer on a cruise ship when I was 19. Then came Wicked. In 2005, I got on the first national tour of the production, but left after six months as I wanted to be a rock star. When Wicked came back with a permanent production in Los Angeles, I joined again.My fantasy, or maybe delusion, that I could be a real artist was still humming in the background, so when the American Idol auditions came up, I threw myself in. My memories of that moment in front of the judges was that I was nervous; it was the last year that I could get in as I was at the age cut-off point, and I had to quit my job on Wicked to get through to the TV auditions. A lot was at stake. I sangBohemian Rhapsody, and my hair was jet black with a side parting – very 00s Myspace. It was a huge risk, but thank God it panned out.Each week, I was excited to show everyone what I could do, how much I loved to sing, but it was terrifying knowing that there were millions of people watching and that I was about to be analysed and critiqued online. The intensity gave me a lot of adrenaline – I would turn up on stage as if I was shot out of a cannon. But afterwards I was going on message boards and comment sections. I couldn’t pull myself away from it, the good and the bad comments. There were plenty of both. The ritual became masochistic.I had a lot of impostor syndromeat the start of my solo career. I was always worried and asking: is this opportunity going to be snatched away from me at any second? Am I going to be like the other people who have competed on these TV shows – is this just a flash in the pan?Then, the day before my first solo single came out, I performed at the American Music Awards. I was happy with what we had done, but afterwards I found myself at the centre of a cultural debate, as the performancefeatured a kisswith another man. The controversy was a total double standard, and I wanted to stick up for myself and say how stupid it was, and that I had done nothing wrong. But behind the scenes, the label freaked out. They felt it was a little too premature for me to be embroiled in something like that. A bunch of people got cold feet on me; a lot of radio stations and people in the business thought I was too risky to back. Especially coming off of a show like Idol, which is so family friendly. The message was that what I had done was too edgy and distasteful. It didn’t hit me right away what an impact it would have on my career. It was a crazy moment, one that should have been fun and exciting.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markI’m in my 40s now, and feel more calm. This part of my life feels like I am returning back to that kid in the photoThe first time I metQueenwas on American Idol. I had joined Brian May and Roger Taylor on stage for the finale to perform We Are the Champions, and off stage our rapport felt natural. When the dust had settled from my first solo album, there was an opportunity to perform with them at the European Music Awards. I was excited, but also aware that people were going to be protective of Queen and their memory of Freddie. Despite my apprehensiveness, it was an opportunity I couldn’t pass up. When I look back on some of those early performances, I was definitely working overtime trying to prove myself – singing every note possible in every song, and overcompensating vocally. Just like when I was on American Idol, I would search the internet afterwards to find out what people were saying. Some thought what we were doing was cool and others considered it sacrilege. Even though it hurt, it kept me humble and made me want to get better so the Queen audience would accept me.Jack Savoretti and Jemma Powell look back: ‘When she walked in I thought, ”There you are. The mother of my children”’Read moreMusic is an amazing medicine, both as a listener and a creator. Being able to get my feelings out is therapeutic, especially when you know that it’s going to connect with other people. Most recently I wrote about a breakup I went through. We were in a five-year relationship, and a week after it ended I was in a writing session. It was very raw, but having that space to express myself was so important. I am not an outwardly emotional person, I’m more of a heady kind of guy, always thinking rather than expressing.I’m in my 40s now, and feel more calm. For most of my career I have been so wrapped up in my own worth – my confidence depended on the reception to the work I was doing. The commercial success of a piece of music doesn’t control my self-esteem as much as it used to. I’ve been around for long enough to have proven myself as a performer, and I am starting to care less about what people think. In a lot of ways, this part of my life feels like I am returning back to that kid in the photo – tapping into the pure joy of creativity, where there’s no filter, no inner voice trying to sabotage me.Explore more on these topicsFamilyFlashbackAdam LambertQueenPhotographyMusicalsAmerican IdolfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: I want a place for ritual in my daughter’s life, so together we created one | Australian lifestyle | The Guardian
description: Ellie wanted a high tea. We picked a sunny afternoon in spring, not long before her 12th birthday
Author: Jackie Bailey
Headline: Ellie wanted a high tea. We picked a sunny afternoon in spring, not long before her 12th birthday
Content: ‘Ellie’s ceremony was brief and private, with just her family, her god(less)mothers and their kids.’Photograph: Senez/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Ellie’s ceremony was brief and private, with just her family, her god(less)mothers and their kids.’Photograph: Senez/Getty ImagesAustralian lifestyleI want a place for ritual in my daughter’s life, so together we created oneEllie wanted a high tea. We picked a sunny afternoon in spring, not long before her 12th birthdayJackie BaileySun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.07 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen I was about 12, I had my confirmation. I wore the same lacy white dress my terminally ill sister had worn two years earlier, with a red sash I had screen-printed at my Catholic school with a dove, symbolising the Holy Spirit.Typically, a child picks a saint’s name as their confirmation name, someone they would most like to emulate. I was permitted to take the name of someone who was still alive,Lúcia of Fátima. Her cousin had been the one to see Mary, mother of God, before dying young and innocent. Lucia, on the other hand, had been condemned to live into old age. I could relate to Lucia. My sister,living with brain cancer, had always been the religious one.Envy used to prickle me constantly. But Buddhism teaches us that if someone feels joy, we too can feel it | Jackie BaileyRead moreThis was the moment to publicly confirm my faith and commitment to the Catholic church. I had already begun to have my doubts by then; I crossed my fingers as I agreed with the priest’s creed.As a child, I may not have been sure of the dogma that I would later leave behind but it was still an important moment in my emergence from childhood. I did it for my family; I did it for my sister, who wanted to be sure that I would join her in heaven. In that sense, my confirmation fulfilled its purpose – an opportunity for me to make a sacrifice for others.When my daughter turned 12, I wanted an event that marked her transition, not so much from child to woman as from recipient to actor. She had always been a member of the community but I wanted her to think about this responsibility; what it means to co-create the world she wants to live in. So I worked with her to devise a coming-of-age ritual.View image in fullscreenEllie’s favourite foods.Photograph: Jackie BaileyThere are coming-of-age rituals across time and cultures. The Jewish rites of bar and batmitzvahs fulfil a similar role to my Catholic confirmation, in which children of about 12 or 13 commit to membership of their religious community. During a period known asRumspringa, some Amish young people–usually aged about 16–are encouraged to experience the world beyond their traditional lives. In this way, membership of their community is their choice, if they return and are baptised.Some rituals involve a little more physical risk-taking than my Catholic pronouncement of faith. For example, in Vanuatuboys might throw themselves from a towerwith a vine tied to their ankles. The Krobo people of Ghana stage atwo-day Dipoceremony for girls to mark the advent of puberty, which includes ritual bathing and dancing.double quotation markI wanted a ritual for Ellie which did not reinforce a single narrative about the world. I didn’t want her to have to cross her fingersThe sociologist and author ofReligionin Human Evolution, Robert Bellah, theorises that rituals emerged in an evolutionary context for humankind. Bellah describes ritual as a space for meaningful social play, reinforcing the norms of the group, celebrating its solidarity and forming identity. Song and dance elements are crucial, stimulating the production of the brain chemicals associated with social connection, ecstasy and joy.The theologian Karen Armstrong explains that stories can’t simply be told about a people; if they are going to stick, they have to be enacted. She offers the example of the Chinese philosopher Confucius, who emphasisedli(ritual) as a critical element of maintaining social order.Confucius provided instructions for public and private rituals, everything from everyday gestures of respect to how to serve tea to a visitor and how often the emperor should answer to heaven.I wanted a ritual for Ellie which did not reinforce a single narrative about the world. I didn’t want her to have to cross her fingers. I wanted a ritual that confirmedpossibility.View image in fullscreenThe Medicine Buddha at Ellie’s ritual.Photograph: Jackie BaileyEllie wanted a high tea. She asked for all her favourite foods set out on tiered cake platters holding fancy cheese, little sandwiches and cakes.We picked a sunny afternoon in spring, not long before her 12th birthday, warm enough for the short-sleeved white dress she never otherwise got a chance to wear. We decorated the front veranda with bunting and set up a little altar. On it, I placed a bowl of water, a gum leaf, a candle and a handful of dirt to represent the elements. I put together a book of favourite prayers, along with a little icon of Mary, who is revered in the Catholic faith of my childhood, and Medicine Buddha, who is the patron Buddha on my mother’s side of the family.I wanted Ellie to know that, even if she doesn’t adhere to these faiths, she still belongs to a long line of humans who have ritually, symbolically and physically participated in a sacred sense with the world.At my Catholic confirmation, I had recited the words I had been told. I had written vows for Ellie but she read through them and handed them back to me. She wanted to write her own.Ellie and I invited three of my female friends who had known her since she was little to be her god(less) mothers. I wanted her to have women in her life she could talk to if she didn’t want to talk to me.My confirmation had been an hour-long mass in front of the parish but Ellie’s ceremony was brief and private, with just her family, her god(less)mothers and their kids. Ellie read her vows and the women read out their promises to Ellie, to provide her with safe harbour as she transitioned back and forth, between family and community, her internal and external worlds.And then we celebrated. Our hominid ancestors once picnicked on raw meat, their safety from predators guaranteed by their commitment to watch out for each other.The ham on the sandwiches was cured, the cheese pasteurised but the sentiment was the same. Ellie had promised to look out for the planet and her people, and we had promised to look out for her.This is an edited extract from Jackie Bailey’s new book,The Outrageous Good Fortune of Living, which is published by HarperCollins and out nowExplore more on these topicsAustralian lifestyleReligion (Books)Young peopleCatholicismChristianityBuddhismParents and parentingfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The women running death salons for people of color to process grief: ‘It allows for freedom’ | Death and dying | The Guardian
description: Death salons are spaces that allow free-flowing conversations about mortality and are becoming more popular around the country
Author: Melissa Hellmann
Headline: Death salons are spaces that allow free-flowing conversations about mortality and are becoming more popular around the country
Content: ‘Death isn’t just a medical or a legal event. It’s cultural, spiritual or familial. It’s human. The way someone wants to be cared for at the end of life is deeply connected to who they are, where they come from and what they believe in.’Photograph: Isabella Kahn/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘Death isn’t just a medical or a legal event. It’s cultural, spiritual or familial. It’s human. The way someone wants to be cared for at the end of life is deeply connected to who they are, where they come from and what they believe in.’Photograph: Isabella Kahn/The GuardianDeath and dyingThe women running death salons for people of color to process grief: ‘It allows for freedom’Death salons are spaces that allow free-flowing conversations about mortality and are becoming more popular around the countryMelissa Hellmannwith photographs by Isabella KahnSun 19 Jul 2026 08.56 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTShareIn a small cream-colored room at a cemetery inPhiladelphia, Pennsylvania, Nefertiti Moor sat at the head of a semicircle. Dressed in black with her long hair in locs, she delivered instructions for the evening discussion. “It’s not death counseling or a debate,” said Moor. She invited the two women in attendance to talk freely about their cultural associations with dying and grief. Tasha Johnson, who worked in behavioral health, said that she felt desensitized after the recent deaths of several people she knew. She was grappling with “the reality that it can be anyone at any point”.The free-flowing conversation about mortality, otherwise known as a death salon or death cafe, was unique in that it wasdesigned for people of color. Death salons for marginalized communities are becoming more common around the country, with practitioners addressing the unique end-of-life concerns of queer, trans, or Black and brown people. Acting on a lifelong interest in how communities process grief, Moor became a death doula, a non-medical professional who provides emotional and practical support to people who are dying and to their loved ones.Sometimes she sits vigil with people as they’re dying, or helps them process their fears and regrets. The profession doesn’t require certification, though many doulas receivetrainingfrom established programs. As a Black woman, Moor mostly works with Black and brown people because she understands the cultural stigmas around planning for death. In the African American community, Moor said at the death salon, conversations about death are sometimes believed to summon it.Left: Raindrops on a window at the Laurel Hill Cemetery. Right: A participant designing a commemorative art piece.Left: Raindrops on a window at the Laurel Hill Cemetery. Right: A participant designing a commemorative art piece.“I see cultural norms where death isn’t openly discussed until there’s a crisis, and that can leave families scrambling to make decisions during some of the hardest parts of their lives,” Moor, 39, said. “I often see financial barriers when it comes to funeral planning or end-of-life prep. I see mistrust of the healthcare systems.” She informs families about cheaper and more environmentally-friendly alternatives to traditional funerals such asgreen burials, where bodies aren’t embalmed and are buried in shrouds instead of hardwood caskets.LGBTQ+death salons offer participants specialized support that acknowledges sexual orientation and gender diversity. Trans people face disproportionately higher rates ofviolent deaths, which queer death doula Kyra Overmier said: “informs the way that they walk through life”. She hosted a queer death salon at Laurel Hill cemetery in March. Overmier’s trans and queer clients sometimes express concern about being misgendered or referred to as theirformer namein their death. Gay couples who were ostracized from their families worry that they will feel even more alone if their partner dies.Specialized death services help people process their grief with others who relate to them. “We deserve to be seen,” Moor said. “Death isn’t just a medical or a legal event. It’s cultural, spiritual or familial. It’s human. The way someone wants to be cared for at the end of life is deeply connected to who they are, where they come from and what they believe in.”View image in fullscreenDeath always fascinated Nefertiti Moor, who viewed it as another journey in the human experience.Photograph: Isabella Kahn/The Guardian‘The OG work’Death always fascinated Moor, who from a young age viewed it as another journey in the human experience. When she was nine, her favorite uncle died by suicide, and she recalls the sense of shame permeated throughout the family. “It was very clear that a lot of adults in my life really didn’t know how to package it or talk to me about it,” Moor said. “Very early on I realized there was this fear, avoidance and silence when it comes to death.” It inspired Moor to become a mortician, but she stopped pursuing her degree after her grandfather died and she fell into a depression.Her childhood interest was renewed in 2018, when she stumbled across a video by theBlack death doula Alua Arthurabout keeping someone company while they died. Moor applied to Arthur’s death doula training program and started looking for clients on social media after she became certified.Modern-day death salons were inspired by 18th-century French salons where intellectuals discussed culture and society. In 2004, the Swiss sociologistBernard Crettazfounded the first death cafe in Neuchâtel, Switzerland. The British web developerJon Underwoodsolidified the concept by launching a website and hosting sessions in London seven years later. In the US, the mortician Caitlin Doughty helped popularize public conversations about death with the creation of the death acceptance organization,The Order of the Good Death, in 2011. Moor traces part of her family lineage to the Caribbean, where communities have long had a healthy relationship with death; she said at the Philadelphia death salon: “This is the OG work.”View image in fullscreenMoor with fellow death doula, Kyra Overmier.Photograph: Isabella Kahn/The GuardianShe often uses her Trinidadian family’s cultural practices as an example of the expansiveness of death practices. When a person dies at a hospital in Trinidad, their body is brought home and loved ones host a celebration and feast for nine days. They remove mirrors from the house so that the spirit won’t see themselves, or rearrange furniture to allow the soul to move freely. Moor informs her clients of various rituals to help them imagine how they’d like to be honored in their death.Social media has helped young Black and brown people discuss mortality with each other. Death doulas use Instagram, for instance, to educate their followers about thelegality of home funerals. OnDeathTok,a popular community on TikTok, death workers provide practical advice on end-of-life care. “It helps people realize,” Moore said, “that ‘I’m not alone, or I’m not morbid or I’m not wild for thinking about death.’”‘A final hurrah’In Fort Worth, Texas, the death worker Taylor Bell witnessed agood death, or when a person’s end-of-life wishes are honored, when her mom entered hospice care in 2013. Bell’s mother, Vicki, had a terminal cancer diagnosis and avoided chemotherapy to preserve her quality of life. Vicki curated her death experience by choosing to die at home, where she gave away her prized possessions to loved ones, and regaled them with tales from her life. “She just really did the thing,” said Bell, who’s 44. “This isn’t a death march; this is a final hurrah. I was like: ‘Oh! This is what it could be like.’” Watching her mother approach death in such a loving and inclusive way inspired Bell to pursue the profession.A few years ago, Bell, Overmier and their friend Lacy Buynak began the businessThe Art of Dyingto provide end-of-life companionship and education throughout the US. As part of the initiative, Bell hosted aqueer death cafeat the trans and gender-diverse community center Finn’s Place in Fort Worth in May 2026. One of the topics of interest was about navigating their identities – some of the attenders wondered whether they should share that they are trans with their ageing family members. They discussed, “the benefit of telling them, the possible repercussions and also the repercussions of not telling them and then they die”, Bell said. “How might they feel if they never shared that with them?”Left: Supplies used to design decorative coffins. Right: A participant doing meditative breathing exercisesLeft: Supplies used to design decorative coffins. Right: A participant doing meditative breathing exercises at Laurel Hill cemetery in Philadelphia on 11 July. Participants are encouraged to be present and in tune with their physical and emotional states entering the space.The death of Bell’s mom also inspired Overmier, 43, to pursue a career in death work. A few years beforehand, Overmier’s dad died alone without an end-of-life plan. “There was so much more that I had to grieve,” Overmier said. “I recognized this difference in dying and the fact that there’s so many other ways that people die. There’s a universal need for support and presence.”She quit her job in retail management to focus on offering death doula services in Conshohocken, Pennsylvania. One of her specialties is sitting vigil with people during their final days. “Not everybody wants someone by them when they die,” she said, “but there’s a good chunk of people who do, and sometimes they don’t have anybody. I feel like it’s an honor to be able to do that.”Death salons for marginalized communities offer a sense of belonging, said Overmier: “If you’re in a group that isn’t just your community, then you’re thinking about whether what you’re saying is going to land right, or if the person next to you is side-eyeing you in a dangerous way.“In affinity groups like this where we can come together … it just allows for this freedom.”

title: Here be dragons: does moving power to the north work? | Devolution | The Guardian
description: Andy Burnham’s recent promise to create a ‘No 10 North’ echoes BBC’s successful Out of London plan
Author: Esther Addley
Headline: Andy Burnham’s recent promise to create a ‘No 10 North’ echoes BBC’s successful Out of London plan
Content: Fifteen years on, the BBC’s decision to create MediaCity in Salford has revitalised the local area.Photograph: Mark Waugh/AlamyView image in fullscreenFifteen years on, the BBC’s decision to create MediaCity in Salford has revitalised the local area.Photograph: Mark Waugh/AlamyDevolutionHere be dragons: does moving power to the north work?Andy Burnham’s recent promise to create a ‘No 10 North’ echoes BBC’s successful Out of London planEsther AddleySun 19 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen the BBC first announced itsintention to movea significant chunk of its operation to Salford in Greater Manchester – the “Out of London” plan, as the then director general Mark Thompson called it in 2004, with a faint “here be dragons” whiff – there were plenty in the organisation who were scornful that it could ever work.Senior staff would never leave the capital. Star talent wouldn’t dream of travelling. It “didn’t take a brain surgeon”,said the Breakfast presenterand Strictly Come Dancing winner Chris Hollins, to see that the prime minister would never appear in person.Jeremy Clarkson, then presenting Top Gear,said he would rather quitthan relocate to “a small suburb with a Starbucks and a canal with ducks”. “If we ran the show from Salford, we’d be employing people from Salford,” said Clarkson. “People who were born there and thought: ‘Yes, I like this. I see no reason to go anywhere else.’ And in the world of television, that could be a genuine handicap.”Fifteen years after the BBC’s move north began in earnest, things look rather different at MediaCity, the vibrant, 200-acre media and technology hub in Salford docks that is now home not only to 3,500BBCstaff – leading significant parts of its output including its current World Cup coverage – but 250 other creative and tech businesses whose presence was galvanised by the broadcaster’s move.View image in fullscreenAndy Burnham, the former mayor of Manchester (seen here in Gravesend, Kent, on Friday), has made devolution a major theme of his premiership campaign.Photograph: Gareth Fuller/PASome think enabling people from the north-west to have broadcasting careers closer to home is a good thing. And it may soon be easier, in any event, to get the prime minister on BBC Breakfast’s Salford sofa. Andy Burnham – until recently Manchester’s mayor, as of Monday Britain’s newest PM – has said he wants to move part of his own executive office to Manchester, to create “No 10 North”.A dedicated department in the city will be charged with overseeing plans to devolve power and resources to cities and regions across the UK, in what, vowed Burnham, would become not an outpost ofLondon, but “the nerve centre of a rewired Britain”.Will it work? Details so far are sketchy and some, inevitably, have been sceptical (“Sounds performative and seems like a gimmick,” one Labour MPsniffed to PoliticsHome). But the BBC is not the only major organisation to shift significant resources and decision-making power out of London. Channel 4 is now headquartered in Leeds; the largest hub for HMRC, the UK’s tax collecting department, is in Newcastle; the Office for National Statistics (ONS) moved its head office from London to Newport, south Wales in 2007.View image in fullscreenAbout 250 more creative and tech businesses have joined the BBC at MediaCity at Salford Quays.Photograph: Mark Waugh/AlamySuccessive governments, meanwhile, have been committed to a long-term plan to move tens of thousands of civil servants out of the capital, notably at a“multi-department government hub”in Darlington, county Durham, that now houses nine departments including a major outpost of the Treasury.For Alice Webb, who was the chief operating officer overseeing the BBC’s Salford move, later becoming director of BBC North, a number of things were key in making it successful. “Yes, there was a lot of resistance, but there was also a deep, deep conviction, a very strong sense of leadership from the top of the BBC, that this was the right thing to be doing,” she says.The change had to be big enough to meaningfully shift the broadcaster’s centre of gravity, she says. Rather than moving representatives from a range of departments, the entirety of BBC Children’s, 5 Live, 6 Music and BBC Sport relocated wholesale – the last despite the fact the Olympics were due to be held in London shortly afterwards. “To a lot of people, that was a strange [thing to do]. But the reason why we moved [BBC Sport] from London a year earlier is in truth there just was not the infrastructure in the old Television Centre to support the level of broadcasting that was needed.”One former BBC exec who enthusiastically relocated from London said the technological step-change made possible by the move – as well as a more flexible way of working once out of the sometimes suffocating institutional culture – made BBC North an exciting place to work at the time. “I don’t think that would have been possible if we hadn’t moved north in such a short time. It might have happened over 10 years, but it wouldn’t have happened effectively over one year,” she says.View image in fullscreenA fifth of all Treasury staff are now based at the Darlington Economic Campus (DEC) in county Durham, which houses civil servants from nine government departments.Photograph: David Dixon/AlamyMany see the Treasury’s hub in Darlington – already described by the chancellor, Rachel Reeves, as “a No 11 of the North” – as a similar success story, albeit on a much smaller scale; according to one Treasury source, that comes down to the commitment of senior people to base themselves there.“I think this only works when there is a senior minister behind it,” says the source, who closely observed the campus’s establishment in 2021 under the then chancellor, Rishi Sunak – an enthusiastic backer of the campus who found time to visit frequently. As well as a fifth of all Treasury staff, the second permanent secretary, Beth Russell, is based in Darlington full time, “and having someone at that level sent a message to people who were still ambitious that moving wouldn’t be the nail in the coffin for your career,” says the source.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe Manchester years: how Burnham’s rebirth as ‘king of the north’ set him on road to No 10Read moreOther civil service moves have seen the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government setting up a base in Wolverhampton, and an official second HQ of the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero in Aberdeen.While only 21% of the civil service is based in London, according to Hannah Keenan, the associate director of the Institute for Government, the proportion in senior roles is closer to two-thirds, making it an ongoing challenge to move real decision-making outwards.“Civil servants understand where the power is,” she says. “That sounds quite Machiavellian, but it is a sensible response because that’s how decisions get taken. If ministers are in London, senior civil servants will be in London, so other civil servants will be London. If that power base starts to shift and ministers are spending their time elsewhere, that can start to move. I think we’ve seen Darlington artificially create that quite successfully, but it has taken quite a lot of careful planning and effort to do it.”Not every relocation is an unqualified success: while a broadening of the talent pool is one reason to move, the ONS shift from London to Wales saw 90% of staff resign rather than relocate, leading to a huge loss of institutional expertise and a measurable slump in its output, according toa later review.View image in fullscreenA former nightclub in City Square, Leeds, has been revamped to create new offices, with Channel 4 among the tenants at the Majestic building, left.Photograph: Peter Chan/AlamyChannel 4 hasfaced criticismthat the move to Leeds of its national headquarters – trailed as a “game-changer” when announced in 2019 – did not go far enough, with its most senior executives and presenters remaining in London. In response, the broadcaster said it had undergone “a significant and rapid transformation from a London-centric organisation to a UK-wide one”, with a third of its staff based in Leeds, Manchester, Glasgow and Bristol and a voluntary target to spend its main channel commissioning budget outside the capital: “Our focus has never been simply on relocating roles, but on building meaningful leadership, commissioning, creative and skills capabilities across the UK.”While there can be direct economic benefits from moving a big department or employer to a new city, according to Anthony Breach, the director of research at the Centre for Cities, it can be just as important symbolically. “It’s a signal to the wider business and policy-making communities that this is a place that is worth investing in, that we see talent and appeal in this place, that it’s worth us sticking our necks out and putting our money in this particular place.”Webb, who is now the CEO of MediaCity, agrees that successful examples of devolution of power can be key in persuading others to follow. “It can only help to have a very powerful role model to help galvanise what people instinctively know – that making sure that all parts of the country have a part to play in decision-making is the right thing to do.”Explore more on these topicsDevolutionNorth of EnglandCivil serviceManchesterBBCGreater ManchesterCounty DurhamfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: One week, two killings: Trump’s immigration crackdown turns deadly – again | ICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement) | The Guardian
description: The fatal shootings of two men, both killed in their vehicles by ICE agents, have rekindled anger over the US’s militarized deportation push
Author: Maanvi Singh
Headline: The fatal shootings of two men, both killed in their vehicles by ICE agents, have rekindled anger over the US’s militarized deportation push
Content: Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero (top) and Lorenzo Salgado Araujo.Illustration: Guardian DesignView image in fullscreenJoan Sebastián Durán Guerrero (top) and Lorenzo Salgado Araujo.Illustration: Guardian DesignThe Sunday readICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)One week, two killings: Trump’s immigration crackdown turns deadly – againThe fatal shootings of two men, both killed in their vehicles by ICE agents, have rekindled anger over the US’s militarized deportation pushMaanvi Singhin San Francisco andFabiola Cineasin HoustonSun 19 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 13.32 EDTShareLorenzo Salgado Araujo, 52, was driving to work with his brother and two other passengers in Houston,Texas, when immigration agents began tailing his car. They pulled him over and fired a fatal shot through the open passenger-side window.Six days later in Biddeford,Maine, Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero, 26, was driving around his neighborhood when agents stopped him at an intersection – right outside the laundromat where he’d often go with his three-year-old daughter – and shot him dead.The two killings in less than a week have unleashed waves of grief and outrage across the US. Demonstrators gathered in both cities to – once again – demand that US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the agency tasked with carrying out Donald Trump’s aggressive deportation scheme,remove its agentsfrom their streets.To deliver on Trump’s promise of mass arrests and deportations, immigration officers armed with military gear have been violently detaining immigrants in communities across the US – at traffic stops and outside department stores, at bus stops, warehouses, farms, schools and churches.Salgado Araujo and Durán Guerrero were the 29th and 30th people that immigration officers have opened fire on since Trump took office last year, according tothe Trace, an outlet that is tracking these shootings in itsGun Violence Data Hub. Officers killed eight of those people, among them Renee Good and Alex Pretti – the US citizens gunned down during a militarized enforcement operation in Minnesota.This week, another man fleeing immigration agents in Florida died after being struck by a truck. Twenty-two people have died in ICE custody, including inside immigration detention centers, this year.View image in fullscreenDemonstrators protest against ICE after the killing of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston, Texas, on 14 July 2026.Photograph: Mark Felix/AFP/Getty ImagesThe two latest shootings echoed each other – and those that came before.Neither victim, it turned out, was the true target of the immigration officers’ operation. Salgado Araujo, of Mexico, had lived and worked in the US for 35 years. He was a husband and the father of three US citizen children. Durán Guerrero, from Colombia, had been working multiple jobs to support his wife and daughter.Salgado Araujo’s eldest son, Ronaldo, said he saw a Facebook video of his father’s shooting – and recognized him immediately from the sound of his voice crying for help. Neighbors told the Portland Press Herald that they saw Durán Guerrero’s partner and daughter rush outside in the aftermath of his killing – the three-year-old in her Bluey pyjamas – and heard their cries ringing through the street.LoadingThe US Department of Homeland Security (DHS) – which oversees ICE and the US border patrol –quickly followed up each shooting with statements, but no evidence, that the victims were to blame. Salgado Araujo “weaponized his vehicle” against officers, the DHS said – echoing the language used after officers killed Good, and other people that agents have shot. The DHS said the officers who fired at Durán Guerrero did so while “fearing for public safety”. Witness videos of each incident have already cast doubt on these official statements.Calls for accountability: ‘This has to stop’The shootings have galvanized thousands of protesters, and led to calls from local leaders and civil rights groups for independent investigations. InHouston, thousands of protesters have marched to demand that ICE leave the city entirely and for the Houston police department to end all cooperation with the agency. In addition to demanding a full, city-led investigation, they are also asking the DHS to reveal the names of the ICE agents involved and release from immigration custody the three witnesses who were in the vehicle with Salgado Araujo when he was shot.‘Fear, intimidation, violence’: calls mount to remove ICE from US streets after agents killed two menRead moreOne week after the shooting, hundreds of protesters gathered outside Houston’s city hall, chanting: “¡Justicia para Lorenzo!” – Justice for Lorenzo! More than a hundred Houstonians signed up to urge action from the mayor, John Whitmire, and the city council during lawmakers’ first meeting since Salgada Araujo was killed.In Maine,hundredsof people poured into the streets of Biddeford and organized an evening vigil for Durán Guerrero. But many of his friends were afraid to speak publicly,accordingto Maine Public – because they feared that ICE would retaliate against them and others in the city’s Latino immigrant community. In an interview withTelemundo, Durán Guerrero’sfather, Omar, said his son “had a strong vision of getting ahead, many dreams to fulfill”.Even the scenes of mourning were familiar. At a memorial for Salgado Araujo, volunteers were sharing “know-your-rights” pamphlets with mourners and neighbors – just as volunteers had done near the site where Good and Pretti were killed.“Nobody should have to learn their rights standing at a memorial for their neighbor,” said Neidi Dominguez, the executive director of the advocacy group Organized Power in Numbers. “But that’s where we are right now.”Increasingly, advocates are grappling with a bigger question: what happens when ICE violates people’s rights with apparent impunity? Jasmine Khadem Gonzalez, an immigrant rights organizer with the Houston Democratic Socialists of America, said her training now includes instructions for documenting agents in the field – taking note of badge numbers, license plates and timestamps, and filming the agents – all in order to create a reliable record of violations.View image in fullscreenPeople protest against ICE in Houston on 8 July 2026.Photograph: Ronaldo Schemidt/AFP/Getty ImagesOn Houston’s Canal Street,where residents placed candles and flowers near the spot where Salgado Araujo was killed, people grappled with what it meant that ICE’s violence had reached them, and their community.“We watched this happen in Los Angeles last year and taught our students about it, and then in Chicago, and Minneapolis, and now it is happening to us, here,” said JoAnna Rodriguez, who teaches the history of Mexican American communities, known as Chicano history, at the local arts center she founded. She lives two blocks from where Salgado Araujo was killed. “We’ve always been fearful, and it’s only getting worse. This has to stop.”A supercharged deportation campaignThe Trump administration’s immigration enforcement apparatus is now thehighest-fundedlaw enforcement agency in the US. Last month, the president signed a bill securing $70bn in funding ICE and Customs and Border Protection (CBP), with $38bn for ICE to expand arrests, detention and deportation operations.In recent months, the administration has recruited thousands of new immigration agents, many without proper vetting andquestionable qualifications. At the same time, the government has beendismantlingthe offices tasked with investigating deaths and civil rights abuses within immigration agencies.Following public outrage over Good’s and Pretti’s killings in Minneapolis, and massive nationwide protests against the Trump administration’s militarized immigration raids in cities across the US, Trump signaled a shift in tactics. He demoted Gregory Bovino – thebombastic border patrol officialwho led armed and masked bands of agents through cities with a film crew in tow – and fired his homeland security secretary, Kristi Noem. Tricia McLaughlin, the top DHS spokesperson who had become notorious for her blatantly false press statements, also left her role.The DHS promised to equip all its agents with body cameras. Butthat has notbeen fully implemented, and the officers who apprehended Salgado Araujo and Durán Guerrero were not wearing cameras. After Durán Guerrero’s killing, the administration instructed federal immigration to pause pulling over vehicles –and almost immediately afterwards the presidentoverturnedthat pause.Many of the immigration arrests in recent months have been conducted by police, sheriffs and members of other state and local law enforcement agencies deputized by the federal government to conduct immigration sweeps. In many locales, it has become unclear to bystanders whether drivers are being pulled over due to a genuine traffic violation, or because an officer was interested in checking their immigration status.While it has become less common to see roving caravans of immigration officers in masks, the administration has not slowed its mass-deportation campaign. Officers are still detaining immigrants at workplaces, at routine check-in appointments, outside their homes or during traffic stops.The arrests are happening routinely – in most cases before neighbors and bystanders realize what is going on. “It’s just happening so quickly. So people can’t get there to observe, record and report what they saw,” said Gracie Willis, an attorney with the National Immigration Project. “And often all that’s left is an empty car sitting there.”View image in fullscreenA makeshift memorial for Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero on 14 July 2026 in Biddeford, Maine.Photograph: Ryan Murphy/Getty ImagesICE agents are targeting immigrants not only in big cities, but also in smaller cities and towns all across the US. In Iowa, advocates noticed a surge in targeted arrests at people’s residences – among those detained were a delivery service driver from Turkey and a Fijian national who had come to the US on a student visa. This month in Wisconsin, advocates noticed a surge of arrests in suburban neighborhoods – agents trailed and stopped immigrants, including those with work permits and legal status, who were driving in Milwaukee and suburban Greenfield.The New York Times reported earlier this month that immigration officials had detained more than 10,000 people in the last five days of June.In the aftermath of the killings this week, ICE released statements emphasizing that the agency was arresting the “worst of the worst” including “murderers, pedophiles, violent assailants and drug traffickers”. However, dataanalyzed by the Guardianand others show that the vast majority of people that ICE has arrested had no criminal convictions.Immigration attorneys and advocates are bracing for even more arrests to come. Arecent supreme court rulingallowing the administration to revoke a designation known as temporary protected status from Haitian and Syrian immigrants will strip legal status from about 1.3 million people. Those who aren’t able to quickly find some other legal pathway to residency in the US could be immediately arrested and deported.In Houston, residents said they would continue to push back against ICE – and protect their families and their neighbors, whose lives are on the line.“We have to stop mincing words … ICE is a domestic terrorist organization,” said Jasmine Khadem Gonzalez, the immigrant rights organizer. “They are killing our fathers on the streets in cold blood all across this country.”

title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election | Sao Tome and Principe | The Guardian
description: Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent
Author: Eromo Egbejule
Headline: Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent
Content: Vila Nova represented the Acção Democrática Independente party when he was elected as president five years ago.Photograph: Francis Kokoroko/ReutersView image in fullscreenVila Nova represented the Acção Democrática Independente party when he was elected as president five years ago.Photograph: Francis Kokoroko/ReutersSao Tome and PrincipeSão Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential electionIncumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independentEromo Egbejulein AbidjanSun 19 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 00.03 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVoters inSão Tomé and Príncipego to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.Since gaining independence from Portugal in 1975, the island country off Africa’s west coast has built a solid reputation for peaceful, competitive elections. But this year’s vote comes amid a tense political atmosphere and a lingering constitutional crisis.The president, Carlos Vila Nova, is running for a second term as an independent, rather than as the candidate for the ruling Acção Democrática Independente (ADI), whom he represented when he was elected in 2021.The tensions between Vila Nova and his former party began in January last year when he dismissed the prime minister, Patrice Trovoada. Trovoada’s replacement, the former justice minister Ilza Amado Vaz, resigned after just three days, before the current holder, Américo Ramos, took office.On Sunday, Vila Nova will be running against four other candidates including the ADI parliamentary leader, Nito D’Abreu. Another former prime minister, Jorge Bom Jesus, attempted to withdraw his independent candidacy, but missed the deadline and remains on the ballot.The main opposition party, the Movement for the Liberation of São Tomé and Príncipe, is now part of a broad coalition backing the incumbent president, despite being staunch ADI opponents. Meanwhile, an ADI faction led by Ramos is backing D’Abreu. If no candidate receives more than 50% of the vote, the election will go on to a second round.View image in fullscreenA view across the island of Príncipe.Photograph: Constantino Leite/AlamyThe businessman Domingos Monteiro, known as “Nino”, who has been president of the São Toméan Football Federation since 2015, was disqualified after the constitutional court ruled he did not meet the eligibility requirements. Monteiro was born on the island, but his parents never became naturalised citizens after moving from Cape Verde where they were born.“The constitutional court is violating the principle of equal rights … It is time for descendants born in São Tomé and Príncipe to say no to discrimination, to the culture of xenophobia and persecution,” said Monteiro.For voters, priorities include tackling government corruption, high inflation, severe youth unemployment, chronic fuel shortages, and frequent blackouts. Rivals of Vila Nova are hoping this will keep him from a second term and propel them to the presidency.Corruption in São Tomé and Príncipe “is the greatest catastrophe the country has experienced in this half-century [of independence”, D’Abreu said at thepresidential debateon Tuesday.The country is seen as a trusted western ally for security and maritime trade in the Gulf of Guinea, one of the world’s biggest maritime piracy hotspots. It also has offshore oil deposits that make a significant contribution to its economy. Observer missions from the European Union, G7+ and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries are on the ground to monitor the polls.Explore more on these topicsSao Tome and PrincipeAfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: Dozens missing after passenger ferry sinks off coast of Guyana | Guyana | The Guardian
description: Emergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disasters
Author: Natricia Duncan
Headline: Emergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disasters
Content: The Coast Guard, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers helped rescue passengers after the MV Barima sank on Saturday night.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Coast Guard, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers helped rescue passengers after the MV Barima sank on Saturday night.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesGuyanaDozens missing after passenger ferry sinks off coast of GuyanaEmergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disastersNeil Marks in Essequibo andNatricia Duncan, Caribbean correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 15.39 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA ferry carrying 116 passengers and crew sank ⁠off the coast ofGuyanain South America ⁠late Saturday, with 67 ⁠people, ​including 15 children, rescued so far, according to Guyana ⁠authorities.A massive search-and-rescue operation continues for dozens of missing passengers in what has been described by officials as one of the country’s worst maritime disasters.The ferry MV Barima left Georgetown, the capital of the English-speaking South American country, on Saturday at about 3.15pm for Port Kaituma, located in the oil-rich Essequibo region and just off the border with Venezuela.A distress call was received shortly after 11pm, prompting an emergency response involving the Guyana Defence Force Coast Guard and Air Corps, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers.Juan Edghill, the minister of public works, said preliminary investigations indicate the vessel was operating within its legal limits.“There was no engine problem or any mechanical problem. It had nothing to do with overload. It had to do with the tide,” Edghill told local reporters.He said the MV Barima was certified to carry 397 passengers and 284 tonnes of cargo. Its manifest showed it was transporting 268 tonnes of cargo, below its authorised capacity.The ship was equipped with 250 lifejackets, two lifeboats and six inflatable rafts, Edghill said.In a statement on Sunday, Guyana’s prime minister, Mark Phillips, said the search and rescue coordination centre had expanded the search area from 400 sq km (154 sq miles) to 1,070 sq km, with additional aircraft and vessels deployed alongside local fisherfolk familiar with the waters.View image in fullscreenThe emergency response involved the Coast Guard, the Air Corps and volunteer fishing vessels and private craft.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThe 67 survivors include 41 men, 11 women and 15 children.As the rescue effort continues, survivors recounted the moments when the ferry overturned in darkness.Adam Matabheek, who was travelling with his wife, brother, sister-in-law and three young nieces and nephews, said he noticed the vessel leaning several hours before it capsized.“We dismissed it as rough water and went to sleep,” he said.Moments later, the vessel rolled over. “I ended up in the water,” he recalled, saying he managed to grab a lifejacket before drifting through the night.His wife, Carlita Matabheek, who cannot swim, said she became trapped inside the overturned vessel before a lifejacket fell within her reach. “I started to pray,” she said.After calling repeatedly for her husband in the darkness, she felt someone tugging at her hair. “It was Adam,” she said.The couple and Adam’s younger brother, Nathaniel, spent the night drifting in the sea until rescuers found them after daybreak.“God cleared the skies and saved us,” Carlita said.Their relief, however, was overshadowed by tragedy.Nathaniel’s wife, Rachel Welch, and their three children – Nathaniel Jr., 8, Natalia, 5, and Ezekiel, 3 – remain among the missing. According to the family, Rachel was thrown from her hammock as the ferry capsized and tried desperately to reach her children before they disappeared into the water.Another survivor, Loureen Singh, a trader who regularly travels to Port Kaituma to sell clothing, survived by holding on to the same lifejacket as Carlita after telling her she could swim “a little”.Carlita urged her: “Hold on to me.”The two women remained together throughout the night until they were rescued.Authorities say families of all recorded passengers and crew members have been contacted as efforts continue to account for everyone aboard.Explore more on these topicsGuyanaWater transportAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Earthquake in Peru leaves at least six dead | Peru | The Guardian
description: More than 30 injured and 300 displaced after buildings collapse near Sicaya
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: More than 30 injured and 300 displaced after buildings collapse near Sicaya
Content: The local civil defence office said that the use of rustic adobe materials in construction in the area ‘contributed to the greater impact and damage’ of the earthquake.Photograph: Jhefry Sedano/APView image in fullscreenThe local civil defence office said that the use of rustic adobe materials in construction in the area ‘contributed to the greater impact and damage’ of the earthquake.Photograph: Jhefry Sedano/APPeruEarthquake in Peru leaves at least six deadMore than 30 injured and 300 displaced after buildings collapse near SicayaAssociated PressSun 19 Jul 2026 13.12 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.09 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA 5.5-magnitude earthquake shook the Andes region ofPeru, killing at least six people, authorities said Sunday. More than 30 people were injured and 300 have been displaced.The US Geological Survey reported the quake struck on Saturday at 9.24pm, with its epicentre located 2km (1.2 miles) west-south-west of the city of Sicaya in Huancayo province. It was 10km (6.2 miles) deep.Peru’s National Civil Defence Institute said in a statement that the total number of missing people is still unknown. Several buildings collapsed or suffered structural damage, including the local church and convent.View image in fullscreenPeople in the main square of Chupuro following the earthquake.Photograph: Yershon Vilca/ReutersLuis Vásquez, head of the local civil defence office, told journalists that the use of rustic adobe materials in construction in that Andean area “has contributed to the greater impact and damage”.Earthquakes are frequent in Peru, which is located in the Pacific “Ring of Fire”.Images broadcast by local media captured the anguish of victims’ relatives in one of the hardest-hit areas, the agricultural region of Chongo Bajo, where residents huddled under blankets outside severely damaged homes. Animals were also seen under the rubble.View image in fullscreenPeople walk among the debris of homes that collapsed after the earthquake struck.Photograph: Javier Ninanya/AP“My home has been destroyed,” Hermenegilda Guamalato told a local radio station as she sought a place to stay with her three children in the neighbouring district of Huayucachi.In 2007, a 7.9-magnitude earthquake struck the province of Pisco in the Ica region, killing nearly 600 people.Explore more on these topicsPeruEarthquakesAmericasNatural disastersnewsShareReuse this content

title: Australia’s life dissatisfaction levels double as ‘wellbeing poverty’ reaches new highs  | Australian economy | The Guardian
description: ABS data shows one in 10 people are not satisfied with life – but community cohesion and trust lift happiness scores
Author: Patrick Commins
Headline: ABS data shows one in 10 people are not satisfied with life – but community cohesion and trust lift happiness scores
Content: The initial surge of Australians reporting dissatisfaction with their lives came during 2020’s Covid lockdowns and has persisted over the intervening years amid soaring cost of living.Photograph: EschCollection/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe initial surge of Australians reporting dissatisfaction with their lives came during 2020’s Covid lockdowns and has persisted over the intervening years amid soaring cost of living.Photograph: EschCollection/Getty ImagesAustralian economyAustralia’s life dissatisfaction levels double as ‘wellbeing poverty’ reaches new highsABS data shows one in 10 people are not satisfied with life – but community cohesion and trust lift happiness scoresFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastPatrick ComminsEconomics editorSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNearly 2.2 million Australians 15 years and older are living below the wellbeing poverty line, with the share of people reporting very low life satisfaction doubling over the past decade.In 2025, 9.7%, or about one in 10 Australians, rated their general satisfaction with life at four or below out of 10, according to Guardian Australia’s analysis of the Australian Bureau of Statistics’s (ABS) general social survey.That compares with one in 20, or 4.8%, reporting very low overall life satisfaction in 2014.The initial surge of Australians reporting dissatisfaction with their lives came during 2020’s Covid lockdowns and has persisted over the intervening years amid soaring cost of living.Graph showing wellbeing poverty on the riseThe ABS data echoes other surveys, such as Westpac’s consumer confidence index, which has languished at around 50-year lows since failing to recover from the pandemic blow.Amid an explosion ofsupport for far-right and populist politics, those reporting life satisfaction scores of four or below are more likely to feel alienated and disempowered in their community, and expresslower support for multiculturalism, the ABS figures show.Only 15% of people experiencing wellbeing poverty feel they have a say on important matters affecting their communities, against the average of 32% across all Australians. The proportion of people who feel they have a voice in their community increases to a high of 46% among those reporting overall life satisfaction of nine or above.As One Nation’s leader, Pauline Hanson, makes headlines with her advocacy for a “monocultural” society, the ABS data show just over 60% of people who score in the lowest group on life satisfaction agree that it is a good thing for society to be composed of different cultures.That share is far below the average of 80% among Australians, and the approaching 90% among those reporting the highest life satisfaction.Stagnation and stagflationNational living standards, as measured by household disposable income per household adjusted for inflation, are at the same level as in mid-2020.Nor is the outlook encouraging: analysts expect the national economy to drop back into aper capita recessionamid astagflationary backdropof low growth and high inflation.Shane Oliver, AMP’s chief economist, said lower-income households had been hit particularly hard by the huge rise in prices for essentials, such as electricity, insurance and healthcare.“Consumer confidence seems to be chronically stuck around recessionary levels,” Oliver said.Graph showing living standards are trending downGuardian Australia haspreviously reportedresearch showing that wages for the highest paid workers over the past decade had grown at more than twice the pace of those earning the least.Oliver also pointed to extensive literature on the economics of happiness that satisfaction with life does not track higher with material wealth past a certain middle-income threshold.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailOliver said it was likely that the recent plunge in life satisfaction related to thwarted hopes and unmet expectations – much of which he believes can be traced back to dwindling hopes of home ownership.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“I suspect housing is the single biggest problem. If we managed that issue better, that would go a long way to addressing this issue,” he said.Anthea Hancocks is the chief executive of the Scanlon Foundation Research Institute, an independent body that focuses onsocial cohesion.“We know that people’s economic circumstances have a really big impact on their sense of social cohesion because it impacts their sense of self-worth,” Hancocks said.“If you feel like nobody cares and you don’t really matter, then that would make you feel like you can’t trust other people or trust institutions. And that tends to lead to not participating in their community or talking to people, so it has a big effect,” she said.Australians have lost hope in the fair go. This has profound implications for the election | Rebecca HuntleyRead moreSocial media has had “a huge impact” on Australians’ lives and how they perceive the world and their place in it, she said.Despite these undercurrents of dissatisfaction and growing financial pressures, Hancocks says social cohesion nationally has proved resilient over recent years.Scanlon’s social cohesion index has stayed steady at 78 over the past three years, although it remains considerably below the recent peak of 92 in 2021.Hancocks said connecting people to their neighbours and community was key to lifting life satisfaction.“We know that regardless of your economic circumstances, whether you are struggling or you say you are prosperous, if you live in a highly cohesive neighbourhood, then your level of happiness doubles,” she said.“That is where our strength comes from now, and most communities are actively doing things in this space.“That won’t solve the problem but building that trust is an essential component. Building national pride simply in being Australian – all of those things will help and can be the fundamental element to help people feel like someone has got their back.”Explore more on these topicsAustralian economyBusinessCost-of-living crisisAustralian politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Parents were told asbestos in play sand was low risk unless ‘pulverised’. But regulators hadn’t tested it themselves | Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Some ACCC recall notices updated to say products ‘may cause risk to health’, contrary to previous claims
Author: Catie McLeod
Headline: Exclusive: Some ACCC recall notices updated to say products ‘may cause risk to health’, contrary to previous claims
Content: Educational Colours sand products are among those recalled over concerns that they may contain asbestos.Photograph: ACCCView image in fullscreenEducational Colours sand products are among those recalled over concerns that they may contain asbestos.Photograph: ACCCAustralian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC)Parents were told asbestos in play sand was low risk unless ‘pulverised’. But regulators hadn’t tested it themselvesExclusive:Some ACCC recall notices updated to say products ‘may cause risk to health’, contrary to previous claimsFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastCatie McLeodConsumer affairs reporterSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 23.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSchools, retailers and government agencies assured parents that asbestos-contaminated play sand posed a low risk to their children’s health based on a supplier’s own assessment which was not independently verified, the Guardian can reveal.The now-contested public messaging spread quickly after a range of Educational Colours children’s sand products werepulled from shelves in November 2025 after samples were found to contain asbestos, which was followed by more than 20 similar recalls across different brands and retailers.Consumers were sold tens of thousands of boxes of potentially contaminated sand over a period of at least five years and the products were used in hundreds of schools and childcare centres in Australia andNew Zealand.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailUntil this month, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) advised toxic asbestos fibres were “unlikely” to be released into the air unless the sand was “processed by mechanical means such as crushing or pulverising”.This advice originated from a risk assessment that Educational Colours itself commissioned, which did not involve any additional testing or even a visual inspection of the contaminated products, and was never made public.The supplier’s safety claim became the official position of the Australian and New Zealand governments even though they had no additional evidence to support it.Airborne fibres found in 90% of samplesThe ACCC updated some of its recall notices last week to state the products “may cause a risk to health”, after testing by the Auckland University of Technology (AUT) found90% of craft sand samples did release asbestosinto the air when played with.Privately, the New Zealand workplace regulator said it had challenged the Australian recall statements “from the get-go” as its own testing showed the asbestos fibres were of a respirable size, according to internal emails seen by the Guardian.A WorkSafe NZ spokesperson on Friday said the regulator had consistently advised that any potentially affected sand products “must be managed, removed and disposed of as friable asbestos-containing material until proven otherwise.”Contaminated children’s play sand sold in Australia can release toxic airborne asbestos, research findsRead moreNevertheless, the “low risk” claim was adopted in both countries, with schools, health officials, government agencies, asbestos disease experts and other major retailers including Kmart all using the exact same wording to reassure consumers.Even though its contaminated sands were not supplied by Educational Colours in the first place, Kmart continues to claim its recalled products “importantly” do not contain friable asbestos and that they are “low risk” unless mechanically processed.Kmart did not respond a request for comment.Asbestos, a hazardous material which can cause terminal diseases if its crystalline fibres are breathed in, has been banned in Australia since 2003 and is not allowed to be imported except in very limited circumstances.The Educational Colours risk assessment, published on 10 October 2025 and seen by the Guardian, determined the risk to children “is negligible” because the “release of respirable asbestos fibres from the coloured sand is unlikely to occur unless [it] is further processed by mechanical means such as crushing or pulverising”.The report acknowledged the safety assurance was based solely on a discussion with an Educational Colours representative and a review of the lab results the company provided, and warned against extrapolating its findings.As the ABC first reported, an Australian laboratory found the asbestos in the sand by chance while analysing samples for staff training and machine testing – it was only reported to authorities after a lab manager mentioned it in a podcast interview.After being informed some of its products were likely contaminated, Educational Colours commissioned additional testing which found six of 14 samples contained non-respirable tremolite asbestos, documents seen by the Guardian show.The laboratory warned against making the same assumption about anything other than the samples it analysed.Furthermore, the AUT test results released this month show play sand containing tremolite asbestos can release fibres into the air, even when it’s only handled the way a child would, in a far more gentle manner than “crushing or pulverising”.The products continued to be sold to consumers and used in schools for at least another month before they were recalled, while the company hired a Melbourne-based OHS firm to do the risk assessment.Educational Colours said it had proactively recalled its products and commissioned independent NATA-accredited testing and an occupational hygiene firm who advised the risk was negligible.“We take our compliance responsibilities very seriously and remain committed to working with relevant authorities to ensure the ongoing safety of our customers and compliance with any evolving testing standards,” a spokesperson said.Officeworks relied on the report when it told consumers that there was “no identifiable safety hazard” relating to the contaminated products and they were being recalled “purely as a precautionary measure” on 12 November 2025.
