Now, what we’re doing is assuring that that oil and gas and other products can flow through the strait of Hormuz with or without Iranian cooperation, and we’ve continued to degrade their offensive military capabilities.”On the same program, a prominent Democrat continued his party’s sharp criticism of the Trump administration’s handling of the nearly five-month conflict.US senator Mark Warner of Virginia described the conflict as a “war of choice” that was launched without an imminent threat and argued the administration has failed to accomplish its previously stated objectives.“Regime change, how are we doing there? We got a worse crowd than before,” Warner said of Iran. He said the enriched uranium that the US wanted to get from Iran to prevent it from building a nuclear weapon is “still there”, adding: “Matter of fact, it would take a minimum of 15,000 troops on the ground for a week to try to take these canisters, which are quite volatile, out of their bunker.”Warner also remarked, “Getting rid of Iran’s missile and drone capability – obviously, we’ve not been successful there” because the conflict continued on Sunday. He said: “The continuation of this war, at this pace, will be a continuing disaster.”In addition to two deaths in Jordan on Friday, the US Central Command announced that another service member ‌was killed on ​Saturday “during a ​controlled detonation of ⁠unexploded ​ordnance” in ​northern Iraq. The unexploded ordnance ‌was ⁠from a “downed Iranian one-way attack ​drone”, ​Centcom said ​in ​a ⁠statementon social media on Sunday.The latest casualties in Iran for the US military came as a renewal of the campaign against Iran entered its second week, leading to intensified fighting around the strait of Hormuz. The US launched retaliatory strikes on Sunday against Iran’s Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) after the killings.After the deaths of the two US troops on Friday, Trump’s defense secretary Pete Hegseth vowed the military campaign in Iran would continue,writingon social media that the service members’ “sacrifice only stiffens our resolve”.Donald Trump himself also escalated pressure on Iran Sunday, with the president urging Congress to include Iran in pending Russia sanctions legislation. The move would further weaken thememorandum of understandingbetween the two countries in June aimed at resolving the Iranian conflict that included an agreement to end sanctions for Iran.Alluding to the unexpected death on 11 July of US senator Lindsey Graham, his ally, Trumpwroteon his Truth Social platform, “Republicans should add Iran to the Russian Sanctions Bill. That’s what Lindsey wanted to do, and it was going to happen. IMPORTANT!!!”Explore more on these topicsTrump administrationUS-Israel war on IranUS politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ICE officers to use body cameras during vehicle stops, Trump ‘border czar’ says | ICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement) | The Guardian
description: Tom Homan’s comments come amid scrutiny over federal immigration agents’ fatal shooting of two men in six days
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Tom Homan’s comments come amid scrutiny over federal immigration agents’ fatal shooting of two men in six days
Content: Tom Homan, Trump’s ‘border czar’, speaks to the media outside the West Wing of the White House in Washington DC on 14 July.Photograph: Graeme Sloan/Pool/Graeme Sloan - Pool/CNP/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenTom Homan, Trump’s ‘border czar’, speaks to the media outside the West Wing of the White House in Washington DC on 14 July.Photograph: Graeme Sloan/Pool/Graeme Sloan - Pool/CNP/ShutterstockICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)ICE officers to use body cameras during vehicle stops, Trump ‘border czar’ saysTom Homan’s comments come amid scrutiny over federal immigration agents’ fatal shooting of two men in six daysGuardian staff and agenciesSun 19 Jul 2026 13.54 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 23.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleUS Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) will require its officers to record vehicle stops with at least one body camera, said Tom Homan, theTrump administration“border czar”, on Sunday. The action comes as the agency is under renewed scrutiny after ICE officers fatally shot two men earlier this month.“They exonerate more law enforcement than they convict, and I want officers to wear body cameras because I want the American people to see what the ⁠officers saw when they took that action,” Homan said on the Fox & Friends Weekend program.Homan’s comments come after federal ​immigration agentsfatally shottwo men six days ‌apart during traffic stops in ‌TexasandMaineearlier this month. There is no body-camera footage from ICE agents involved in those incidents.‘We just want to be treated like humans’: how a Latino neighborhood is rallying after an ICE shootingRead moreIn a ‌separate interview on CBS’s Face the Nation show, Homan said money for the cameras was held up during the partial governmentshutdownearlier this year.“The cameras have been purchased right now,” Homan said. “They’re training the trainer in deploying the cameras nationwide.”The immigration agency’s extreme tactics have once again comeunderpublic scrutinyin recent days after an ICE officer on 13 JulykilledJoan Sebastián Durán Guerrero, a driver from Colombia, in the coastal Maine ‌city of Biddeford, about 15 miles (24km) south of Portland. Six days earlier, another ICE officer in Houstonfatally shotLorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Mexican national.Salgado, who wasnot the intended targetof the ICE officers, was driving his construction crew to a job site when he was pursued and stopped. Salgado had no criminal history, had lived in the US for 35 years and was close to obtaining legal status, his family has said.Less than a week later, ICE agents shot and killed Durán during another traffic stop. As with Salgado, Durán was not the target of an operation. The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) claimed officers feared for their safety. Immigrant rights activists have said Durán was authorized to work in the US and had a social security number.Homan said last week the ​agency would temporarilysuspendmost vehicle stops around the country to review procedures. Within 24 hours of Homan’s comments, Donald Trump directed federal immigration agents toresumethem.The back-to-back shootings sparkedprotestsin Maine, Houston and Boston and raised questions over ICE agents’ lack of body cameras.One week, two killings: Trump’s immigration crackdown turns deadly – againRead moreICE launched a body-camera pilot program in 2024 and deployed ⁠cameras to officers in five cities: Baltimore, Buffalo, Detroit, Philadelphia and Washington DC. The Trump ​administration moved last year to ​slow-walk the program, urging Congress to cut ​funding by 75%, Reutersreportedin January.In February, after thekillingof two US ​citizens during an immigration crackdown ‌in Minnesota, Kristi Noem, then the DHS chief, said the body-camera program would be rolled out to ICE agents nationwide “as funding is available”.The killing of Durán was the 11th fatal shooting by federal immigration officials since Trump’s second term began, including the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, according to a Guardianreviewof public reports.FBI agents will no longer be investigating confrontations with ICE agents, a New York Timesreportsaid, citing people briefed on the decision. They said written notice had been sent on Thursday of this decision. Previously, these investigation cases could yield information that could be used to prosecute ICE agents involved in violent encounters.Dani Anguiano contributedreportingExplore more on these topicsICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)Trump administrationUS politicsUS immigrationTexasMainenewsShareReuse this content

title: AI job worries grow. But some human skills can’t be replaced by machines | Gaynor Parkin and Dave Winsborough | The Guardian
description: The fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will help
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will help
Content: Given AI is such a rapidly evolving field, it’s smart to seek expert input, write psychologists Dave Winsborough and Gaynor Parkin.Photograph: Roman Lacheev/AlamyView image in fullscreenGiven AI is such a rapidly evolving field, it’s smart to seek expert input, write psychologists Dave Winsborough and Gaynor Parkin.Photograph: Roman Lacheev/AlamyThe modern mindAI (artificial intelligence)AI job worries grow. But some human skills can’t be replaced by machinesGaynor Parkin and Dave WinsboroughThe fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will helpThe modern mindis a column where experts discuss mental health issues they are seeing in their workSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTShareEarlier this year Paul* discovered via a flurry of media stories that his business group had been targeted for job cuts, alongside a number of other public-sector agencies. For him, this was the second time, after his technical management role was restructured in 2024. While that process was difficult enough, the comms this time were highlighting the integration of AI as a cost-saving tool. “They say it’s not about firing people, but hey, my whole team will go.”For Paul, the timing could not have been worse. He and his partner are expecting a child and recently bought their first house.“I’ve worked so hard to stay up to date and relevant, lots of tech training, ironically including AI, and I’ve advocated for the strategy that management roles can be assisted by AI, not replaced by them. So I feel a bit stupid now.”My most capable clients are becoming prisoners of their phones – but there is a way out | Modern MindRead moreExacerbating Paul’s distress has been the very public nature of the cuts. Headlines such as “Plans to replace thousands of public service jobs with AI” or quotes from politicians like “The overhaul will lower costs, tighten agency budgets and make government operations more efficient” have felt insulting.The experience Paul is having has been described as Fobo – fear of becoming obsolete – and it’s a very real fear.Recent commentaryhas noted that Fobo is a growing workplace anxiety. People worry their roles will become irrelevant due to technology and many aren’t sure how to prepare for the shift. This is exactly Paul’s dilemma. He sought our help to figure out his career next steps as well as how to cope with the emotional fallout of the announcements.As we explored Paul’s options, several constraints became clear. His profession has been hit hard by people cutbacks, with more expected. He could find a way to retrain for a different role or start again in an alternative industry but has no financial buffer to do so. Even if he did there are no guarantees that the AI trend for replacing workers won’t impact him in a new role.Given this is such a rapidly evolving field it’s smart to seek expert input.Don’t get stuck in doom, do prepare activelyIn April researchers at MIT Future Techpublished resultsof empirical examinations of AI’s real world impact, analysing what tasks are faster or could be replaced by AI. The researchers say predictions of doom are not accurate or helpful. Rather the takeaway from this data is for workers to view AI developments as a rising tide – you can see it coming and have time to prepare. Paul, of course, is already doing this by seeking help and thinking ahead.As for what to prepare, experts suggest that as AI automates more technical and analytical tasks, uniquely human capabilities are becoming more critical. Ina recent paperDr Tomas Chamorro-Premuzic and Dr Reece Akhtar recommend that we all cultivate the following human skills that machines can’t replace:Curiosity: Seek new learning, perspectives and interests, look outside your usual sources for inspiration. You can use AI to widen your perspective and spark ideas but stay engaged with the world beyond the screen.Humility: Grow your self-awareness. Reflect on what comes naturally, what energises you and where you get stuck. Then invite honest feedback. AI learns through feedback – you can too. Ask people you trust what to start, stop and keep doing.Emotional intelligence: Your ability to connect, show empathy and communicate with care will only become more valuable.Alongside active preparation and improving our super-talents we recommend two other strategies:Network and collaborateIn our experience people are more likely to be successful navigating a rising AI tide and cultivating uniquely human skills when they stay strongly connected with others, rather than making this a solo quest.For Paul this has meant prioritising talking with current and potential new colleagues, attending professional events and conferences, and trying reverse mentoring – linking up with younger or more tech-savvy colleagues to exchange ideas, knowledge and contacts.With his team, Paul needed to show genuine compassion for their distress, while also managing his own anxiety. A recent piece recommends that managers dotalk directly about AI, so this is the approach he is taking with them.Seek out the best organisationsWhile it may feel that organisations are holding all the cards, actively seeking out organisations that are using AI strategically as well as checking out company culture is a key consideration for Paul as he looks for future jobs. Because AI tends to accelerate organisational factors, for better or worse, the pre-existing culture canmake or break an AI rollout.For Paul – and actually for us all – the best strategy for managing Fobo is validating that the fear is real, while embracing and enhancing our uniquely human super-talents. Staying connected with others and seeking out opportunities with organisations which are using AI well will also help us to stay above the tide.*Paul is an amalgam of a few different people and details have been changed for privacyGaynor Parkinis a clinical psychologist at Button Psychology.Dave Winsboroughis an organisational psychologist with a particular interest in personalityExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)The modern mindJob lossescommentShareReuse this content

title: Why a modest US interest rate rise won’t change much for most businesses | Gene Marks | The Guardian
description: Rates may rise, but don’t be too concerned – a small increase is unlikely to change major decisions on investment or hiring
Author: Gene Marks
Headline: Rates may rise, but don’t be too concerned – a small increase is unlikely to change major decisions on investment or hiring
Content: ‘A 25-basis-point increase doesn’t meaningfully change borrowing costs for most established businesses. In fact, it changes nothing at all.’Photograph: Olena  Malik/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘A 25-basis-point increase doesn’t meaningfully change borrowing costs for most established businesses. In fact, it changes nothing at all.’Photograph: Olena  Malik/Getty ImagesUS small businessWhy a modest US interest rate rise won’t change much for most businessesGene MarksRates may rise, but don’t be too concerned – a small increase is unlikely to change major decisions on investment or hiringSun 19 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTShareWith the appointment of a newFederal Reservechair, the latest concern is that interest rates will go up in the next few months. That decision will depend on many factors, includinginflation, jobs and the economy’s overall growth. But when that happens, watch out! The media will go nuts and the president will go ballistic. Everyone treats every Fed meeting as an economic earthquake. But for most small businesses, it isn’t.A 25-basis-point increase doesn’t meaningfully change borrowing costs for most established businesses. In fact, it changes nothing at all.But say the Fed increases the federal funds rate by that amount. The result would likely be an increase in theprime rateto 7%. Most of my clients – small businesses – don’t pay the “prime” rate because, well … they’re small businesses and are generally more risky investments.Feeling bored and disconnected for your job? You may be facing workplace 'rust-out' | Gene MarksRead moreSo they often pay 1% or 2% over prime. Will this interest rate increase change their investment, borrowing and hiring decisions? No, it won’t. A business borrowing $500,000 from a bank for a five-year equipment loan, for example, would see their annual payments go up from $120,942 to $121,658.Even if the bank’s prime rate jumped to 8.5%, their annual payment would only rise to $123,099. This is not enough of a difference to discourage my clients from moving forward with a financing project.The reality is that capital markets – even for small businesses – are pretty stable right now.Some may say that startups could be squeezed. But, like always, that depends on what kind of startup we’re talking about. If you’re in tech – particularly AI – money is falling from the sky. Venture capital funding, particularly to tech companies and anyone that mentions AI,skyrocketed 51%last year to a whopping $320bn. If – like most small businesses – you’re not a VC candidate, then you’ll find that banks are still interested in you too. The Small Business Administration hasincreased credit availabilityfor many small businesses – particularly manufacturers – and is guaranteeing a significantly higher level of bank loans than in years past.Small business loan approvalsheld firmat around 52% last year (up from 46% in 2021), according to the Federal Reserve. Financing firmBiz2Credit reportedrecently that debt repayment volume increased 24% and debt coverage improved from 0.57x in Q1 2025 to 1.40x in Q1 2026, “suggesting that stronger [small and medium-sized businesses] have demonstrated a greater capacity to manage their monthly obligations”.What about consumers? Their spending is the main factor behind small business growth, and if they’re maxed out on credit that would reverberate across the economy.Still, so far, so good. Consumer spendingrose sharplylast month to a rate significantly above inflation.Delinquency ratesandbank charge-offsfor delinquent balances on credit cards have both been falling each quarter since 2024. Consumer credit remained on “solid footing” in May, according to a widely watchedcredit scoring agency report, with the data suggesting that consumers have “largely adapted to a prolonged higher-rate environment despite rising household expenses and the return of student loan payments”.This doesn’t mean that both businesses and consumers aren’t without their financing challenges. There are 34m small businesses and more than 360 million people that live here, so there’s bound to be a not insignificant percentage of people and companies that struggle to raise funds or pay their bills.Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase, the country’s largest bank,recently sharedhis concerns that the next credit cycle (a recurring economic response for when borrowers struggle to repay, defaults and losses rise, lenders tighten standards sharply, and credit becomes scarce) will hit likely harder than expected, pointing to $5.1tn in leveraged finance (private credit, high-yield bonds, syndicated loans) as the key stress point.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionToo much of an interest rate increase could certainly trigger that. But your average plumbing company that borrows $500,000 for equipment isn’t going to be much concerned about whether to buy that machine based on a quarter-point rate increase. They decide based on whether they believe demand will justify the investment.Another cause for concern is therise in US bankruptcies. The American Bankruptcy Institute recently reported that small business bankruptcy filings “jumped” 67% this past quarter over last year thanks to “persistent inflation, elevated interest rates and geopolitical instability” and warned that the more “immediate constraint is access to credit” as some lenders are becoming more cautious in extending financing due the uncertainties that continue to challenge the US and global economies.However, and like Dimon, bothBrian MoynhianandCharles Scharf– who run Bank of America and Wells Fargo, respectively – are not yet raising red flags. In its latest earnings release, Moynihan said his bank “remains watchful of evolving risks” but so far has seen “healthy client activity, including solid consumer spending and stable asset quality, indicating a resilient American economy”.Scharf reported similar findings, saying that “while markets have been volatile, we still see continued resiliency in the underlying economy and the financial health of the consumers and businesses we serve remains strong, though the impact of higher oil prices will likely take some time to materialize”.So will the Fed increase interest rates? Maybe. But I wouldn’t be too concerned. For many established small businesses, a modest increase is unlikely to alter major investment or hiring decisions.Explore more on these topicsUS small businessUS interest ratesEconomicscommentShareReuse this content

title: ‘Good growth in every postcode’: Burnham’s economic to-do list in seven charts  | Economics | The Guardian
description: From industrial strategy and defence to housing and living costs, we lay out the challenges facing the new PM
Author: Richard Partington
Headline: From industrial strategy and defence to housing and living costs, we lay out the challenges facing the new PM
Content: Andy Burnham will face public finances under pressure and time running out before the next general election.Photograph: Henry Nicholls/EPAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham will face public finances under pressure and time running out before the next general election.Photograph: Henry Nicholls/EPAEconomics‘Good growth in every postcode’: Burnham’s economic to-do list in seven chartsFrom industrial strategy and defence to housing and living costs, we lay out the challenges facing the new PMRichard PartingtonSenior economics correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham becomes prime minister as Britain contends with a series of global economic shocks and years of weak growth in living standards, fuelled by underinvestment and deep regional divisions.Before his arrival in Downing Street, the Labour leader pledged to deliver “good growth in every postcode” by transferring power from Westminster to local communities. But with the public finances under pressure, and time running out before the next general election, the task is not straightforward.Here are the areas Burnham has indicated will be his priorities in power, with charts laying out the complexities involved in achieving them.ReindustrialisationBurnham has signalled that a strengthened industrial strategy will form a central plank of his agenda to support economic growth outside London and south-east England. This includes a push to “safeguard sovereign manufacturing and production capability … in critical sectors like steel, defence, energy, food and farming”.Manufacturing jobs chartAcross rich countries, there is a growing focus on domestic manufacturing amid mounting geopolitical tensions. Rightwing populists havesought to capitalise on the consequences of deindustrialisation, targeting small towns and cities that have struggled after the closure of factories. In making promises to these communities Burnham will be hoping to win back Labour voters considering a switch to Reform UK.Britain’s industrial base has dwindled from about 30% of the economy in 1979 to about a tenth today, yet pockets of strength remain in the old manufacturing heartlands. Supporting activity in these places is core toLabour’s industrial strategy.However, some experts warn that fetishising the industrial past is not the route to modern economic prosperity, given that the UK’s comparative advantages typically lie in service sector activities. High energy costs and competition from low-cost manufacturing hubs in Asia are also big headwinds.Burnham has relied heavily on Manchester’s economic revival for his political philosophy, going as far asbranding it as “Manchesterism”. But analysts highlight the city’s success has been driven by private investment into knowledge-intensive business services, rather than reopening its cotton mills.DevolutionBritain is the most centralised G7 country for tax and spending policy, and is among the most economically unequal in the developed world. Burnham has promised to bring about the “biggest rebalancing of power the country has ever seen” – including the establishment ofa new hub for No 10 in the north, based in Manchester. He is also expected to hand powers, including over tax, to regional leaders.Tax revenue chartThis week the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development said devolution could play a key role in growing the UK economy as a whole, by driving up the output of underperforming regions. It said a coherent approach to align skills policy, infrastructure, innovation, finance and governance was an “essential” component forimproving productivityacross regions.However, others warn devolution does not guarantee stronger growth. While allowing for a tailored response to local issues, it could create inefficiencies, and will need financial firepower to be successful. Local government also lacks capacity after more than a decade of deep cuts and restructuring power will take time to implement.Cost of livingBurnham has promised to give “breathing space” as soon as he can to families struggling with the cost of living crisis, with expectations for a package of financial support that could be announced within weeks.Energy price cap chartHouseholds and businesses have faced soaring costs driven by the energy price shocks from the wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, with inflation locked above the 2% target set for the Bank of England for five years.After more than a decade of stalling real wage growth,household incomes fell during the last Conservative parliamentfor the first time in modern history. The Joseph Rowntree Foundation warns thiscould happen againwithout a change in course.Some Labour insiders believe Burnham has leeway because the Iran war is having a smaller impact than feared. The July increase in theOfgem energy price capwas smaller than after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, and comes in the summer months when consumption is lower.However, prices remain significantly higher than before the Covid pandemic and others are urging Burnham to take more radical steps on the cost of living. Policy options could include an affordable energy guarantee; rent controls, cheap bus fares, and an expansion in free school meals.Burnham has also spoken of the need for greater public control over key utilities including water, energy, and transport.Youth unemploymentUnemployment has risen to thehighest levels since the outbreak of the Covid pandemicin recent months, with young people bearing the brunt.The Iran war, elevated Bank of England interest rates and cost of living crisis weighing on the economy have hit hiring confidence.Business leaders also blame tax increasesfor making matters worse.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionYouth unemployment chartMinisters are however increasingly alarmed over a longer-term shift in youth employment, amidrising mental ill-health,the impact of social mediaandAI disruption. Against this backdrop, the number of young people not in employment, education, or training hasrisen above a million for the first time in a decade; accounting for 13.5% of all 16- to 24-year-olds.Burnham has said he will respond by pushing to strengthen technical education and the apprenticeship system. He also enters office awaiting the final reports from two major government-backed reviews:Alan Milburn’s investigation into youth worklessness, andStephen Timms’ review of disability benefits. Both have called for welfare reform.Defence spendingBurnham will need to find an extra £4.7bn over five years for defence in his first budget, after Keir Starmerannounced £15bn extra in military fundingwithout having fully identified how it will be funded.Defence spending chartAccording to the Treasury, £10.3bn will also be raised by “reallocating budget” from across government departments. Many of the decisions on how this will work in practice will form part of the challenge for the new prime minister to oversee as part of his next budget-setting and spending review process.Over the long-term Labour has also committed to spending 3.5% of GDP on defence by the middle of the next decade. However, tight constraints on the public finances and other pressures to fund public services and welfare spending will complicate the path, with theOffice for Budget Responsibilitywarning the UK is on an “unsustainable” trajectory.Social housingBurnham has pledged to oversee the “biggest council house building programme since the postwar period”. In hisspeech on the economy last monthhe linked the loss of almost 1.5m council homes since the 1980s to a rise in the number of families on social housing waiting lists to similar levels.Social housing chartUnder Starmer, Labour had already pledged£39bn to social and affordable homesand a target to build 1.5m new homes in total, but the government could struggle to hit this. Official figures show the last time more than 300,000 homes – or 1.5m over five years – were built in a single year in England was 1969. Back thenalmost half were affordable social homesbuilt by councils.Economists are however clear that greater transport investment and housing provision would have significant benefits, by helping to support labour mobility and productivity.Fiscal ruleThe biggest challenge for Burnham, as for previous prime ministers, will be how to pay for his plans. The role of his chancellor will also be crucial, with Shabana Mahmood, the home secretary,tipped to secure the role over Ed Miliband.Government borrowing chartSending a clear signal to jittery bond investors, Burnham last month said his policy agenda would be “backed with sound public finances and the discipline of our current fiscal rules”.Retaining the self-imposed borrowing and debt constraintsdrawn up by Rachel Reeveswill limit his options, but are seen as key to avoiding a market backlash that could risk further driving up the UK’s already £100bn a year bill for servicing the national debt.Reeves had put the UK on a faster path to cutting its annual borrowing levels than other G7 economies. However, with spending pressures looming, and febrile conditions in financial markets worldwide, the UK’s borrowing costs remain elevated.Explore more on these topicsEconomicsEconomic policyAndy BurnhamfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Ryan Fox crowned Open champion as heavyweights wilt under pressure | The Open | The Guardian
description: On a dramatic final day of the Open, Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of a former Black Caps captain, emerged victorious
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: On a dramatic final day of the Open, Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of a former Black Caps captain, emerged victorious
Content: Ryan Fox holds the Claret Jug after his victory in the 2026 Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreenRyan Fox holds the Claret Jug after his victory in the 2026 Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe OpenRyan Fox crowned Open champion as heavyweights wilt under pressureUSA’s Cameron Young falls short by a single shotRory McIlroy hints at DeChambeau joining Irish OpenEwan Murrayat Royal BirkdaleSun 19 Jul 2026 14.57 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat a way to enhance a family dynasty. Ryan Fox, son of a New Zealand rugby legend and grandson of one of the country’s cricket captains, is the Open champion. Three generations, three sports, one Claret Jug. Fox displayed the fortitude of Grant, his old man, to snatch victory from Cameron Young on such a dramatic Open Championship Sunday.Fox took to the 18th tee level with Young at nine under par. The American had finished more than two hours earlier, his total looking ever-more promising as others took it in turn to wilt under the heat of Royal Birkdale. Fox laughed in the face of adversity. He crashed a drive 330 yards down the fairway, flicked an iron to 11ft and holed out for a birdie three. This will trigger quite the party, including in his homeland. This Open needed something to switch the conversation fromBryson DeChambeau and a rules breach. In Fox, the tournament found a bold and deserving champion.View image in fullscreenRyan Fox celebrates after winning the Open on the final green.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianFor Young, there will be pain. A par at the last would have earned him 10 under par and, as it was to transpire, a playoff. He found the bunker from the 18th tee. A six-iron, intended to reach the front of the green, rebounded from the top of the trap and into thick grass. Young, who is becoming a major nearly man, scrambled superbly for a bogey five. It was not enough, alas.The Open 2026: Ryan Fox wins the Open – as it happenedRead moreOn a week dominated by sunburn and grass burn, some believed it was fate that Sam Burns would triumph. Instead, the 54-hole leader wobbled after reaching 11 under inside two holes. He regressed to minus eight at the turn, where he was to stay. It was Fox who was to deal best with golf’s version of scorched-earth syndrome.At one point, it looked as if matters were conspiring against Fox. His tee shot to the perilous par-three 15th hit the bank of a bunker, from where it flew back to a position where the 39-year-old had no scope to even hit his ball forward. Fox escaped with a four. He had an opportunity to reach double digits under par on the penultimate hole yet missed. In the clubhouse, a pensive Young was edging ever closer to extra holes. He was denied that by a Fox attack.Kim Si-woo had taken the lead when holing from off the green at the 6th. He remained in the equation until a poor closing stretch. Kim shared sixth with Casey Jarvis and Lucas Herbert.Tommy Fleetwood, the hometown hero, could not end his major wait at Birkdale. Still, the moment of Sunday belonged to him. He kissed his hand, waved and touched his heart in front of adoring galleries after holing out for a 72nd-hole birdie. If there really are golfing gods, he will win an Open. His finish this time was a tie for fourth. Things had started so promisingly on Sunday for Fleetwood, who triggered roars that reverberated around the course with a birdie from 73ft on the 1st.View image in fullscreenTommy Fleetwood acknowledges the love of the crowd as he walks up the fairway on his 72nd hole.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianScottie Scheffler should really have become the first man to win back-to-back Opens since Padraig Harrington here in 2008. The taking of 32 putts in round two fatally harmed the world No 1’s cause. Scheffler flew from the traps on Sunday, throwing darts towards flags and playing the first five holes in three under. He had slipped to six under on the 15th but roared back again; birdies at the 16th and 17th left him one from the lead while playing the last. Scheffler carved his tee shot right, missed the green and took three from there. A finish beside Fleetwood at minus seven was a real tale of missed opportunity. “I guess it just wasn’t my week,” Scheffler said. His consistency of results remains quite incredible.Bob MacIntyre’s latest tantrum, this time slamming his driver into the ground, received a code of conduct warning from tournament officials. “I’m going to do it for the rest of my career,” the Scot said. “I try and toe the line, but when you feel you’re playing a golf course that you can hit it pretty much anywhere and there’s no real punishment and then you do hit one slightly off line and you get the biggest punishment you can possibly get, it’s just a sore one. I’m going to react aggressively at times. Part of my DNA.” Perhaps it shouldn’t be. Professional golfers set examples.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDeChambeau was hovering on the leaderboard before a disastrous triple-bogey seven on the 11th. The record books will show a tie for 14th as his best major finish of 2026. The R&A quietly breathed a sigh of relief that he did not close only two from the lead, that being the penalty for his errant footwork on the 5th during Friday’s round.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau, hit with a two-stroke penalty on Friday, tied for 14th.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe leaving of a bunker shot by the 18th green in sand rather summed up Rory McIlroy’s Open. The Masters champion signed off with a 71 for a one-under total. McIlroy will now take a holiday before returning to competitive action in the PGA Tour’s playoffs. He was asked rather mischievously whether he would welcome DeChambeau at the Irish Open this year, as has been rumoured as a possibility. The event takes place at Donald Trump’s Doonbeg resort. “I think that would be great for the tournament, absolutely,” McIlroy said. “Having someone like Bryson at the Irish Open, it would be great for the tournament.”McIlroy’sscathing sentiment towards DeChambeauhad been the theme of Saturday at the Open. Day four proved just as eye-catching. Inside the ropes, this time. Fox has just sampled a life-changing moment.Explore more on these topicsThe OpenRory McIlroyScottie SchefflerEuropean TourPGA TourGolfUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping control | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Jonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine climb, having been woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Jonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine climb, having been woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls
Content: Remco Evenepoel celebrates as he crosses the finish line ahead of Tadej Pogacar at Plateau de Solaison.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPAView image in fullscreenRemco Evenepoel celebrates as he crosses the finish line ahead of Tadej Pogacar at Plateau de Solaison.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPATour de France 2026Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping controlDane was woken by doping control at 2am on day of stageRemco Evenepoel outsprints Pogacar to claim victoryJeremy Whittleat Plateau de SolaisonSun 19 Jul 2026 12.15 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJonas Vingegaard crashed out of the Tour de France on the first Alpine stage on Sunday, only hours after being woken up in the early hours to undergo doping controls. He will have collarbone surgery in the next few days.The double Tour winner, who alsowon the Giro d’Italia this year, climbed gingerly into a race ambulance, his right arm in a sling, after falling as the main peloton exited a roundabout in Bonneville.Tour de France 2026: Vingegaard crashes out before Evenepoel wins on stage 15 – liveRead moreVingegaard andTadej Pogacarwere subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls, outside the normal window of 6am to 11pm, before stage 15, as the race headed to Plateau de Solaison in the Savoyard Alps. Vingegaard was tested at 2am by the International Testing Agency (ITA), while Pogacar’s unexpected knock on the door came at 5am.Pogacar described Vingegaard’s 2am wake-up call as “inhuman” and suggested that it “ruined” his longstanding rival’s sleep and could have been a factor in his crash. Before the stage start on Sunday, Vingegaard said: “I was sleeping very well. It’s good that they test, but when it’s affecting performance and your sleep, I don’t think it’s so good.”Pogacar said the disturbance could “be a small percentage in why he crashed because he probably didn’t sleep good”, adding: “I know how it feels. They woke me up at five in the morning also, but at least I got my deep sleep. It’s better than if they come at two.”Pogacar’s own performance seemed unaffected by the nocturnal disturbance. Once again he led the way up the final climb, this time with his UAE Emirates XRG teammate Isaac del Toro and Remco Evenepoel in tow, moving to the front of the race on the steep final climb, just as Vingegaard, cradling his right arm, gingerly climbed the steps to the Tour’s mobile hospital.But for once Pogacar, the four-time winner, did not have the upper hand and was unable to come past Evenepoel, as the Belgian led home the sprint for the stage victory. Behind the trio, Paul Seixas and Florian Lipowitz – Evenepoel’s Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe teammate – gave chase but both lost almost a minute in the overall standings.In a statement the ITA said: “The ITA is conscious that night-time testing may be disruptive to riders’ rest and recovery. Due consideration has therefore been given to minimise as much as possible the impact testing activities have on riders.“However, the ITA, on behalf of the UCI [the governing body], has the responsibility to ensure the effectiveness of the cycling anti‑doping programme and the integrity of cycling races.”View image in fullscreenJonas Vingegaard descends earlier on stage 15.Photograph: Anne-Christine Poujoulat/AFP/Getty ImagesThe statement added that “effective testing must, in limited and justified circumstances, be capable to occur outside standard daytime hours. All testing activities conducted at the Tour are subject to the safeguards established under the world anti-doping code, the UCI anti-doping rules and French law, with due consideration for riders’ welfare, equity and fundamental rights.”While nocturnal tests are highly unusual, the UCI’s rules state that tests outside the normal hours of 6am to 11pm may be allowed if there is a “serious and specific suspicion that the rider may be engaged in doping”.Other teams confirmed that during the Tour they have also undergone tests as early as five in the morning. Pogacar said that recently the pattern of controls he had experienced had been “strange”, adding: “They’re doing different testing than usual. For example, I was counting how many controls I had at home since the first of January.“When I was at home, I was already waking up every day at six to check the doorbell because I had so many visits. I didn’t want to go for dinner or the supermarket even, because they were coming at random hours.“If this is necessary to still prove that we are riding clean, then I totally accept it and I commit to this, but it’s a bit shit when you sleep four hours in one day.“If Jonas was tested at two in the morning, he slept less than four hours, it ruined his deep sleep and then completely ruined the night. But it is what it is, and if this is what we have to do, we do it.”Evenepoel, meanwhile, who has now risen to second in the overall standings behind Pogacar, was typically outspoken. “To be honest, I would send my team tonight to wake them [anti-doping officials] at three in the night, to let them feel how it is, and to take blood,” the double Olympic champion said.“It’s inhuman and this is something we actually should not accept as athletes, because everybody knows sleep is the only recovery that we have in the night. And if that gets disturbed, it makes it even harder. I hope it doesn’t happen any more in the future because it’s quite disrespectful.”Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingJonas VingegaardTadej PogacarShareReuse this content

title: Kimi Antonelli tightens F1 title grip with Belgian GP win after Russell crashes out | Formula One 2026 | The Guardian
description: Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian Grand Prix but his Mercedes teammate George Russell was knocked out on the opening lap after a clash with Lewis Hamilton
Author: Giles Richards
Headline: Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian Grand Prix but his Mercedes teammate George Russell was knocked out on the opening lap after a clash with Lewis Hamilton
Content: Kimi Antonelli kisses the trophy after claiming victory in the Belgian GP at Spa.Photograph: Jakub Porzycki/ReutersView image in fullscreenKimi Antonelli kisses the trophy after claiming victory in the Belgian GP at Spa.Photograph: Jakub Porzycki/ReutersFormula One 2026Kimi Antonelli tightens F1 title grip with Belgian GP win after Russell crashes outRussell out on opening lap after clash with HamiltonAntonelli has 50-point lead on Mercedes teammateGiles RichardsSun 19 Jul 2026 11.02 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVictory in the Belgian Grand Prix could not have been sweeter for Kimi Antonelli, but it has cruelly exposed something of an inexplicable faultline within his Mercedes team. The Italian teenager’s joy stood in stark contrast to his teammateGeorge Russell, who was knocked out on lap one after a clash with Lewis Hamilton, for which Russell squarely blamed his team and what he sees as a weakness with his car they seem unable to rectify.Hamilton had clipped Russell at Les Combes, which the Mercedes driver considered a racing incident. Russell, however, was vehement in his belief that he should not have been competing for a corner with the Ferrari and that he had been forced to do so because he was once more enduring a lack of straightline speed. It is a deficit that notably is not affecting Antonelli, giving the conspiracy theorists a field day but more importantly posing a conundrum for Mercedes that they apparently cannot quite fathom.Formula One 2026: Kimi Antonelli wins Belgian Grand Prix to extend title lead – liveRead moreEarlier during the weekend Russell said the deficit in electrical energy deployment left him feeling powerless and, after the British driver walked from his car beached in the gravel on Sunday, he said he was numb with disappointment.The issue has plagued Russell since the Austrian GP but reached a head in Spa – not least in that his title hopes have taken another body blow. The win has given Antonelli a huge lead in the world championship, with the 19-year-old now 50 points clear of his teammate and 45 in front of Hamilton.The sense of disbelief and despondency on Russell’s face was clear afterwards. The incident was another in what has been a season of no little ill-fortune for the British driver, including an electrical failure in Montreal and an unwarranted pit‑lane speeding penalty in Monaco that kept him out of the points.After something of a resurgence in the preceding three rounds, including a win in Austria and during which Antonelli endured his own poor luck, Russell had reduced the Italian’s lead from 64 to 25 points and might have believed better fortune was to follow. However, he was left aghast in Spa, discovering – as Half Man Half Biscuit observed – that the light at the end of this tunnel was the light of an oncoming train.View image in fullscreenGeorge Russell loses control of his Mercedes after contact with Lewis Hamilton’s Ferrari on the opening lap.Photograph: John Thys/AFP/Getty ImagesThe incident had itself been relatively innocuous. Russell had started in third but had been swallowed up by the Ferraris when he lacked acceleration out of the first corner and on to the Kemmel straight. He tried to go round the outside of Hamilton at Les Combes and the Ferrari driver, down on front‑end grip because of his proximity to the car in front, found his left front wheel sliding into Russell’s right rear, pitching the Mercedes into the gravel and out of the race. Hamilton was given a five‑second penalty for causing a collision – cold comfort to Russell, who was denied the chance to score points.Russell said it was a racing incident but noted pointedly that it was his lack of pace on the straight that left him vying with Hamilton in the first place. “I just got totally swallowed up down the straight,” he said.“I lost three positions into turn five. It was a racing incident, maybe I could have gone a bit wider, maybe he could have braked a bit more. The incident shouldn’t have happened if I had the speed down the straight. Very, very disappointed. No words at the moment. I am numb to the disappointment. When it happens so often you just get used to it.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe Mercedes team principal, Toto Wolff, did his best to make a measured assessment of the travails Russell is facing but that there is an issue is clear and it is proving potentially terminal to Russell’s title hopes. Nor is he alone in his frustration. The McLaren team principal, Andrea Stella, whose team use Mercedes engines, noted that they had observed a similar unexplainable discrepancy between Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri, not because of driving style but “engine deviation”.None of which detracts from Antonelli’s superb win with a commanding drive, including coming back at the second‑placed Ferrari of Charles Leclerc, after the Monegasque driver had gained the lead through the pit stops but proved unable to resist the Mercedes as the Italian hunted him down and passed with ease.Indeed Russell was doubtless partly so frustrated because Antonelli demonstrated again with aplomb in Spa quite how comfortable he is with the car. The Italian now has six wins and has reasserted his supremacy in the title race, while Russell has but discontent and demands for answers.Max Verstappen was third for Red Bull, while Norris came back from 13th on the grid to seventh for McLaren, with his teammate Piastri fifth. Hamilton finished fourth. He was investigated post-race regarding an unsafe release in a pit stop, during which he was cleared to go and struck a mechanic still leaning over his front tyre who escaped injury. Hamilton did not receive a further penalty but Ferrari were fined.Explore more on these topicsFormula One 2026Kimi AntonelliGeorge RussellMercedes GPFormula OneMotor sportnewsShareReuse this content

title: Spain win World Cup after Ferran Torres breaks 10-man Argentina’s resistance | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Spain have won their second men’s World Cup after Ferran Torres’s extra-time strike beat a battling Argentina side who had Enzo Fernández sent off
Author: David Hytner
Headline: Spain have won their second men’s World Cup after Ferran Torres’s extra-time strike beat a battling Argentina side who had Enzo Fernández sent off
Content: Rodri, Spain’s captain and player of the tournament, lifts the World Cup trophy alongside teammates as they celebrate winning the World Cup.Photograph: Hannah McKay/ReutersView image in fullscreenRodri, Spain’s captain and player of the tournament, lifts the World Cup trophy alongside teammates as they celebrate winning the World Cup.Photograph: Hannah McKay/ReutersWorld Cup 2026Spain win World Cup after Ferran Torres breaks 10-man Argentina’s resistanceDavid Hytnerat New York New Jersey StadiumSun 19 Jul 2026 18.17 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 22.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt was the triumph of an idea, an identity. Of an unshakable and patient faith. And when it was over, Spain’s new generation having emulated the one from the 2010World Cup, all of those legendary names, there was simply delirium for them.Spain were the only team that turned up to play this World Cup final, from the first whistle to the last in extra time.Argentinawere damned by their lack of ambition even before they had Enzo Fernández sent off towards the end of the regulation time. It was extraordinary to report that they did not have an attempt on goal until the 115th minute.Spain did not let them play. Just as they have barely let any of their opponents do so during the course of this tournament. Their miserly defence has been the cornerstone of everything. They have been breached just once in their eight matches.Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time to win the 2026 World Cup – liveRead moreThe question that came to pound concerned their ability to find a way through the sky blue and white ranks. It was the only question. Spain stayed true to their style; to who they are. The possession. The cohesive movements. The control. When they found the answer, it was the moment when they knew that a second World Cup triumph was about to become gloriously real.The goal came in the 106th minute and it was nothing less than they deserved. Pedro Porro crossed deep and it was a wonderful header back by the substitute Nico Williams. It teed up another replacement, Ferran Torres, for the opportunity of his lifetime. The finish was high and true.The Argentina manager, Lionel Scaloni, looked ready to cry. It was a white knuckle ride that carried his team to this stage, defined by late goals and a never-say-die spirit. At no point in any of their knock-out games were they ahead in the 90th minute.Argentina flickered in the very closing stages. The substitute Guiliano Simeone lashed a half chance high. But there would be no successful defence of the title they won in 2022; no fairytale sign-off for Lionel Messi. He could not suppress the tears as he gazed at the Argentina support afterwards.Messi and his team had won their previous three tournaments – two Copa Américas either side of the last World Cup. They had not lost a knockout tie since the 2019 Copa América. They got what they deserved here. There was an ugly flashpoint after full time when Leandro Paredes, who had come on as a substitute, was given a red card for grabbing the Spain replacement Eric García by the throat.Argentina’s reaction in defeat was shameful. They turned their backs on the Spain players as the trophy was presented, preferring to keep their eyes on the fans behind one of the goals. Donald Trump, who did not want to leave the stage as the Spain captain, Rodri, got his hands on those six kilograms of solid gold bliss, was not the only person to make a spectacle of himself.View image in fullscreenFerran Torres smashes the ball into the roof of the net to score Spain’s extra-time winner.Photograph: Michael Zemanek/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenFerran Torres celebrates scoring the only goal of the World Cup final.Photograph: David Ramos/Getty ImagesIt was quite the show overall in the land of the spectacular show. It started with a ceremony, one to officially close the most bloated tournament there has been, which took in all manner of performances. Jennifer Hudson’s rendition of the US anthem before kick-off was certainly something; ditto Tom Cruise’s speech about the beautiful game. We kicked off six minutes late. There was plenty more at half-time. The action on the field did not keep pace.Argentina had one flicker in the early running when the Spain goalkeeper, Unai Simon, left his line to deal with a Julián Álvarez throughball for Messi. The clearance was straight to Álvarez but his first touch was poor. That was pretty much it from them in forward-thinking terms.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenEnzo Fernández sends Pau Cubarsí flying to receive a second yellow at the end of normal time.Photograph: Jeenah Moon/ReutersThe game was all about Spain’s dominance of the ball. Nobody cherishes it quite like them. Rodri set the tempo at the back of the midfield while Lamine Yamal had a few eye-catching moments. He went close at the outset. Spain finessed their patterns. They tried to wear Argentina down.Did anybody enjoy the half-time show? It was a strange detail that Madonna, one of the headline acts, did not actually perform on the pitch. Instead, there was big-screen video of her singing in the back of a car which was driven by Ronaldo and Ronaldinho.Spain pressed high. They forced turnovers. They probed. Argentina could not free themselves from the choke hold. The chances started to come for Spain after the hydration break in the second half of normal time and Argentina were indebted to their goalkeeper, Emi Martínez. He finished with 11 saves, some of them excellent. Were Argentina trying to win the World Cup without taking a shot? Scaloni could scarcely have built a lower block, although he doubled down still further in the 101st minute when he introduced Marcos Senesi for Álvarez and went to 5-3-1.Scaloni restructured in midfield, introducing Paredes in the holding role for the left-winger Nico González. The Argentina manager wanted his players to get closer to their opponents. It was not long before Paredes barged Rodri off the ball and was booked.View image in fullscreenLionel Messi’s final World Cup game, probably, ends in defeat.Photograph: Hannah McKay/ReutersFernández was guilty of losing his composure. Booked in the 83rd minute for dissent, he was asking for trouble when he left something in on Pau Cubarsí to send the Spain defender spinning. Argentina just about reached extra time when Martínez dived to repel a Lamine Yamal free-kick. His best saves before that had denied Cubarsí, Aymeric Laporte and Williams.Spain turned the screw in extra time. Martínez continued to resist, saving once again from Williams. Spain were denied when Mikel Merino, on as a substitute, was adjudged to have fouled another replacement, Nicolás Otamendi, moments before Williams swept home. It was a generous decision for Otamendi, who appeared to throw himself to the ground. Merino glanced wide from a Williams cross when gloriously placed.Spain’s pressure reached the point when the breakthrough felt inevitable. Torres provided the release, the joy. He would also run through in the 113th minute to beat Martínez again, only to be pulled back for offside. The goal he did score was all that mattered. Luis de la Fuente’s unstarry team have a second one on their shirts.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026SpainArgentinaWorld Cupmatch reportsShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before US president attempts to crash Spain celebrations | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday
Author: Alexander Abnos
Headline: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday
Content: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino during the trophy presentation ceremony after the World Cup final.Photograph: Ashley Landis/APView image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Gianni Infantino during the trophy presentation ceremony after the World Cup final.Photograph: Ashley Landis/APWorld Cup 2026Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before US president attempts to crash Spain celebrationsUS president watches Spain v Argentina matchMatch report: Spain beat Argentina in extra-timeAlexander Abnosat New York New Jersey StadiumSun 19 Jul 2026 19.27 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump andGianni Infantinowere booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony before the US president attempted to gatecrash Spain’s victory party.Trump arrived at the stadium on Marine One, with the president’s helicopter first flying over the stadium about 45 minutes before kick-off. He sat in a glassed-in section of luxury seats for the match, along with Infantino. Other dignitaries and former players sat nearby, including Spain’s king, Felipe VI, Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum.The 2026 World Cup final: Spain v Argentina – in picturesRead moreTrump was briefly shown on the video screens at the stadium after the US national anthem was played. There were scattered boos from some in the crowd but nothing like the hostile reception he has received at other sporting events during his presidency. However, when the US president and Infantino came on to the field for the presentation after Spain clinched victory, boos rang out around the stadium. The White House pool report said that ambient crowd noise, which had been sitting at approximately 78 decibels before their introduction, rose to 84 decibels when Trump entered the field.Then, after Trump presented Spain with theWorld Cuptrophy, he attempted to stand with the team at the side of the podium as they celebrated before Infantino quietly ushered him away.It was a repeat of last summer’s Club World Cup at the same stadium after Trump presented the trophy to champions Chelseaand tried to crash the celebrationsto the bemusement of the players.Trump has made several appearances at sporting events since his re-election. Last year, he attendedthe Super Bowl,tennis’s US Open,the Daytona 500and golf’s Ryder Cup. This year, he has attended several golf events, college football’s national championship game and Game 3 of the NBA finals, where he wasmet with boosfrom the New York Knicks home crowd.View image in fullscreenLionel Messi receives his runner-up medal from Donald Trump.Photograph: Alex Pantling/FIFA/Getty ImagesTrump’s appearance was the first time he has attended a match at this summer’s World Cup, which the have US co-hosted along with Canada and Mexico.At the 2022World Cupfinal, the emir of Qatar, Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani, joined Infantino to award the trophy to Lionel Messi and his Argentina teammates. Vladimir Putin awarded it to Kylian Mbappé and France after their win at the 2018 World Cup in Russia.View image in fullscreenRodri lifts the World Cup Trophy alongside Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino.Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty ImagesTrump is the first sitting US president to attend a World Cup final on home soil. When the US hosted the tournament in 1994, Trump’s predecessor Bill Clinton did not attend the game. The trophy was instead presented by Clinton’s vice-president, Al Gore.Trump hasa close relationshipwith Infantino, andcalled the Fifa presidentto lobby for a review of USA star Folarin Balogun’s controversial red-card suspension before their last-16 match. Fifa did reverse Balogun’s ban, but the political circus cast a pall over the US team, who exited a promising World Cup with defeat to Belgium.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupDonald TrumpUS politicsGianni InfantinonewsShareReuse this content

title: Lionel Messi’s World Cup fairytale ends with pitiful Argentina as final villains | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Game’s greatest player will not see his legacy sullied by defeat to Spain but his team’s display destroyed the game
Author: Pablo Iglesias Maurer
Headline: Game’s greatest player will not see his legacy sullied by defeat to Spain but his team’s display destroyed the game
Content: Lionel Messi could not help Argentina defend their World Cup trophy.Photograph: Dylan Martinez/ReutersView image in fullscreenLionel Messi could not help Argentina defend their World Cup trophy.Photograph: Dylan Martinez/ReutersWorld Cup 2026AnalysisLionel Messi’s World Cup fairytale ends with pitiful Argentina as final villainsPablo Iglesias Maurerat New York New Jersey StadiumGame’s greatest player will not see his legacy sullied by defeat to Spain but his team’s display destroyed the gameSun 19 Jul 2026 19.06 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePlaying on the grandest stage in sport for the last time,Lionel Messiseemed almost certain to provide one last memorable moment in this World Cup final.Yet as the sun ducked behind the west stand of New York New Jersey Stadium on Sunday, it became clearer and clearer that Argentina had lost the magic they had carried through this tournament. And as Spain gradually wrested control, one was left to confront reality: Messi, and Argentina, had finally run out of time, having long since given in to their worst instincts ina 1-0 defeat.This final presented a stark contrast to theArgentina– and Messi – we observed in every other match of the tournament. Almost without exception,La Albicelestehave been the tournament’s most entertaining team, not so much forhowthey’ve scored their goals but forwhenthey’ve scored them, offering a masterclass in dramatic timing. Twice they were nearly eliminated by badly outmatched opponents, results that only galvanized them.Patience beats overreliance on Messi: how the final was won and lostRead moreFinals are frequently forgettable matches that fail to entertain but even by that lowly standard the pacing of things felt dreadful. Spain were suffocating defensively and – after a forgettable first half – finally worked their way into things in the second 45, eventually breaking through in extra time, quite expectedly. Argentina? They became the first team in the near century-long history of the tournament to fail to record a shot in regular time of aWorld Cupfinal. Even when handed an additional half an hour, they never threatened.And Messi? Until this final, he had presented observers with one of the rarest opportunities in professional sport: a chance to witness greatness free of stakes, entirely devoid of any real pressure to perform. At 39, Messi has done his time and nobody could criticize his collection of hardware – as an individual or as part of a club or country. This defeat does nothing to sully a spotless legacy; a win would have only strengthened his claim as the greatest player in the history of the sport. To some Argentinians,the victory against Englandin their semi-final likely carries nearly as much weight as a victory in the final would have.Yet it has to be said: Messi was rarely involved in this match, a far cry from the player who has sometimes carried his team this summer. Much of this, of course, is down to aSpainmasterclass in defending. That alone is not always enough to contain the diminutive Argentina captain. But give Spain their due: they badly outclassed Argentina in every way, neutralizing even Messi. By the end Argentina had become what so many observers pegged them to be in the first place, sometimes unfairly: underhanded, dirty and desperate.Time has almost always been irrelevant to Messi on the field, because he so frequently bends it to his liking to elude those who defend him. The manner in which he performs at age 39 has a similar effect, tearing open a rift in time itself and transporting observers back to his glory days at Barcelona and elsewhere. Time itself has not passed him by as much as he’s marched alongside it, occasionally trailing behind a step or two but always eventually pulling even with it again.View image in fullscreenSpain kept close tabs on Lionel Messi, who had little impact on the final.Photograph: Timothy A Clary/AFP/Getty ImagesYet now we are left to face the unmistakable reality that Messi is done, with the World Cup at least – this is, it’s worth noting, what was said after he led Argentina to their first World Cup in nearly four decades in 2022 and what was assumed in 2016, when Messi briefly retired from the national team after Argentina’s defeat in the Copa América final. Back then, the dialogue surrounding this player was that he would never play for Argentina the way he did for Barcelona, or even rumblings that Messi himself wasn’t sufficiently Argentinian, having spent so much of his life in Spain. Both arguments feel laughable 10 years later.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIt remains to be seen how Argentina’s performance will be perceived back home, where expectations were understandably high. The head coach, Lionel Scaloni, and even Messi had spoken at length before the final about how much they had already achieved. This final result, they implied, would do little to sully that sense of accomplishment.This was, of course, before they took the field and more or less destroyed the game. Scaloni’s legacy, like Messi’s, was secure before this final, the coach having brought Argentina a third World Cup and a pair of Copa América titles, but this was a lost opportunity to solidify it. Two things can coexist: Scaloni is almost without question Argentina’s greatest coach, and this was one of his worst results and performances.What feels much more real, though, is a sense of loss and disappointment after this pitiful final performance, which provided Argentina – and Messi – with a perfect opportunity to put the finishing touches on their fairytale. Instead, they turned from hero to villain – and needlessly so.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026ArgentinaSpainWorld CupLionel MessianalysisShareReuse this content

title: At the World Cup of superstars, a team take home the trophy | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a system
Author: Sid Lowe
Headline: The European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a system
Content: Ferran Torres scored the only goal of the final, becoming Spain’s seventh different scorer at the tournament.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAView image in fullscreenFerran Torres scored the only goal of the final, becoming Spain’s seventh different scorer at the tournament.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAWorld Cup 2026AnalysisAt the World Cup of superstars, a team take home the trophySid Loweat New York New Jersey StadiumThe European champions conceded just one goal in the tournament and Spain’s success is a victory for a systemSun 19 Jul 2026 19.05 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.39 EDTShareRelease the pressure, let it out. All of it and all of them. Again and again they had come, again and again they had been halted but they would not be denied. A dozen different players had taken aim at Emi Martínez’s goal but somehow he was still standing and time was slipping away, yetSpainrefused to do so, so they came some more.And then, suddenly, only this wasn’t really sudden, the result instead of the purest logic, the clearest justice, Pedro Porro’s deep cross found Nico Williams at the far post and he headed back. The ball dropped deep inside theArgentinapenalty area, bounced on the turf and just kind of sat there: waiting, willing someone take this moment.Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra timeRead moreSo at 5.42pm eastern daylight time, 37 seconds into the second period of extra time, Ferran Torres did. And with Spain’s 20th shot of this final, with all his might and all his soul, he won theWorld Cup. The ball travelled at 102km/h, tearing into the net, and Torres turned and tore in the other direction, sprinting to the corner where he leapt into the air and volleyed that too. Not quite so hard as he had hit the shot, but hard. From the bench and the pitch his teammates raced towards him, liberated at last, the joy flooding out. Oh, the release.Andrés Iniesta once said that the moment the ball sat up for him in the 116th minute of the final in South Africa in 2010, he heard the silence. Maybe Torres heard it too; maybe he heard history call. More often he has heard noise. Here there was an explosion, everyone at that end of New York New Jersey Stadium going absolutely mad. Torres stood there, doing a familiar gesture: jibber-jabber hands, ears “listening” wondering where the critics were now. Then he got the biggest hug Lamine Yamal has ever given.Late in Spain’s victory over Saudi Arabia on the day that Luis de la Fuente celebrated his 65th birthday, Torres had come on as a substitute and scored the final goal – or thought he had – only for the video assistant referee to take it from him. As they reviewed the footage, Ferran had stood with his hands in front of his mouth in a praying position, muttering to himself:por favor,be a goal; por favor,be a goal.This seemed like a portrait of the pressure, of what players put up with sometimes, a picture perhaps of desperation too. Spain were four goals up already, but here he waspleadingfor it to count.In the previous game against Cape Verde, he had hit the bar. In the next game, against Uruguay, he did it again. There was something that his critics took as confirmation, something cruel in how close it was. Too often he had felt that fate somehow was conspiring against him. Turned out, it was saving something for him: the greatest goal, the greatest moment in any player’s career. Justice had been done, not just in the game, which Spain had dominated Spain’s way – at that point Argentina had not had a shot – but to this team. This family, as De la Fuente calls them, all in it together.It felt right almost that it should be Torres, a symbol of all of them – like this was another moment shared, the collective expressed in one man who hasn’t always felt it but will now be for ever beloved. Torres became the seventhdifferent Spain player to score in the US. At the World Cup of the stars, ateamtook the trophy. The European champions were the best team here, lifting that trophy having conceded just one goal, and this was a victory for a system, an ideal, a commitment to an identity. In that, it can feel almost inevitable but it is not. This was also a victory for vindication, for the belief their manager has in them and they have in each other, even if others do not. “People have been very unfair with him but he has a spectacular mentality, he keeps going, keeps working, and today life has paid him back,” Pedro Porro said of Torres. “This is for all 26 of us.”Coming into the tournament, Rodri, the captain, had not completed his rehabilitation yet. Lamine Yamal, the star, was returning from injury too, everyone waiting for him until they realised that maybe they didn’t have to. Williams, who had provided the assist, thought he wouldn’t get there: injured for much of the season, he fought back then feared Uruguay had kicked him out of the World Cup.Fabián Ruiz had missed most the season injured. Aymeric Laporte, imperious again, suffered with Athletic. Behind him, the debate around Unai Simón wouldn’t relent. Eric García and Martín Zubimendi came on here to win it: neither had had a minute yet, meaning Spain finished the competition with only two outfielders who have not played. Who had Pedro Porro and Marc Cucurella down as the world’s best full-backs before the tournament? Plenty do now. Mikel Merino, the superest of super subs, had played only 28 minutes since March, but appeared to rescue them. Not once, but twice.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAnd then, at the biggest moment of all, there was Torres.De la Fuente said that Spain had been “stung” by the doubts in those early games, the lack of recognition. “They are the best players in the world,” he kept saying, and it turned out he was right. Spain’s coach is a man of faith; faith in his footballers, his “family”. Every day over the last month he has exchanged messages with a friend Antonio, who is like a son to him, expressing the conviction they both felt. “It is written,” the messages say, and now it is.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026SpainArgentinaWorld CupanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Hits, misses and Muppets: the star-studded World Cup half-time show nobody asked for | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: It zipped along, cramming in Shakira, Madonna and Coldplay, but football fans may not be convinced of need for this mid-match spectacle
Author: Alexis Petridis
Headline: It zipped along, cramming in Shakira, Madonna and Coldplay, but football fans may not be convinced of need for this mid-match spectacle
Content: Former Brazil football players Ronaldinho and Ronaldo with Madonna during the half-time show.Photograph: Hannah McKay/ReutersView image in fullscreenFormer Brazil football players Ronaldinho and Ronaldo with Madonna during the half-time show.Photograph: Hannah McKay/ReutersWorld Cup 2026Hits, misses and Muppets: the star-studded World Cup half-time show nobody asked forIt zipped along, cramming in Shakira, Madonna and Coldplay, but football fans may not be convinced of need for this mid-match spectacleAlexis PetridisSun 19 Jul 2026 18.11 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 23.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLike hydration breaks and Donald Trump’s ability to get refereeing decisions overturned by simply phoning up the president of Fifa and complaining, the final half-time show is an innovation of the 2026World Cupthat comes with a degree of controversy attached. For one thing, its existence automatically means the World Cup final breaks the International Football Association’s 15-minute half-time mandate.For another, its obvious antecedent lies in the NFL’s Super Bowl, where the half-time entertainment has mushroomed over the decades from a few marching bands to one of the big pop events of the year.
