To its detractors, it smacks of America not so much embracing the World Cup as imposing itself upon it.View image in fullscreenShakira performs during the World Cup final.Photograph: MB Media/Getty ImagesBut before you even got to the main event, there was more musical entertainment at the final to wade through, most notably the pre-match closing ceremony, a damp squib that neither the BBC nor ITV elected to broadcast live.The 2026 World Cup final: Spain v Argentina – in picturesRead moreAnyone who hastened to iPlayer was rewarded not merely with Post Malone debuting what’s presumably the first single from his forthcoming album the Eternal Buzz, Chrome Heartbreaker (a pivot back to hip-hop-influenced pop after his recent diversion into country, should you be eagerly tracking the artistic vacillations of Post Malone’s career) and duetting with Swae Lee on their US chart-topper Sunflowers, but also an online influencer-cum-professional wrestler called IShowSpeed performing Champions, a track that gained so much traction online that Fifa felt obliged to include it on the official Fifa World Cup album.A frightful cocktail of thunderous drums and autotuned vocals, it featured lyrics that make your average football song sound as erudite and profound as the oeuvre of Leonard Cohen: “I take a breath and wait for the ref / This is the World Cup, put your hands in the air.” Alas, news of its social media virality clearly hadn’t reached the audience in theNew YorkNew Jersey Stadium. The deathly silence at its conclusion made you wonder if perhaps the TV broadcast couldn’t pick up the sound of the crowd, but no: some fireworks subsequently went off and they went audibly mental.View image in fullscreenThree members of the Muppets’ house band the Electric Mayhem performed the White Stripes’ Seven Nation Army.Photograph: James Lang/IMAGN IMAGES/ReutersThere was, it turned out, more to come. Jennifer Hudson sang the Star-Spangled Banner. Dutifully flogging a dead horse with all the professionalism he could muster, Robbie Williams performed the Fifa anthem, Desire, in the company of Laura Pausini and Nicole Scherzinger. The ballad debuted at the draw ceremony in December and – like all the musical entertainment at said event – it was overshadowed by the news that Fifa had apparently started making up awards in order to give them to Donald Trump. It vanished into obscurity thereafter, failing to chart anywhere in the world. Listening to it exhumed for another airing, you were forced to concede that the world had a point.Donald Trump arrives by helicopter to attend World Cup final alongside Gianni InfantinoRead moreAfter all that, the actual half-time show felt like a comparative triumph. You could argue that the bar had been set pretty low without much dissent, but nonetheless, it zipped along, cramming a lot into its 11 minutes by the simple expedient of paring songs down to their bare essentials and swiftly cutting between artists.Madonnaperformed a version of her 2000 hit Music – reworked over a backing track immediately recognisable as derived from the Trammps’ Disco Inferno – on a vaguely Mad Max-ish vehicle piloted by Ronaldo and Ronaldinho.Then the strains of the White Stripes’ Seven Nation Army rang out, ostensibly performed by three members of the Muppets’ house band the Electric Mayhem. There was no sign of their leader, Dr Teeth, but perhaps he was indisposed, being treated backstage after being exposed to Champions by IShowSpeed. BTS rattled through Dynamite, Shakira and Burna Boy played a truncated version of Dai Dai – the solitary World Cup song in recent memory to actually impact on the British charts – and Coldplay performed a song called Believe in Love accompanied by a children’s choir: the latter looks horrendous on paper, but was far less sugary in reality, undercut by the farty blast of a crowd of vuvuzela players.View image in fullscreenColdplay performed a song called Believe in Love accompanied by a children’s choir that was far less sugary than it could have been.Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesThe solitary misstep might have involved allowingJustin Bieberto perform a downcast acoustic ballad, Everything Hallelujah – viral hit following his performance at Coachella or not, this perhaps wasn’t the moment for introspection – but no matter: if the half-time show wasn’t sufficiently head-spinning to convince the viewer it should be a regular occurrence at every World Cup final, it worked well enough to justify its existence at least this time. Although clearly not everyone felt the same way: “I thought it was crap,” offered Wayne Rooney, when pressed for his opinions by BBC presenter Gabby Logan.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupMadonnaJustin BieberNew YorkfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Patience beats overreliance on Messi: how the final was won and lost | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Argentina tried to kill the spectacle and make it a physical battle but Spain refused to take the bait
Author: Jacob Steinberg
Headline: Argentina tried to kill the spectacle and make it a physical battle but Spain refused to take the bait
Content: Spain cut off the supply to Lionel Messi, who struggled to find space.Photograph: Christopher Neundorf/EPAView image in fullscreenSpain cut off the supply to Lionel Messi, who struggled to find space.Photograph: Christopher Neundorf/EPAWorld Cup 2026Patience beats overreliance on Messi: how the final was won and lostArgentina tried to kill the spectacle and make it a physical battle but Spain refused to take the bait and kept passingJacob Steinbergat New York New Jersey StadiumSun 19 Jul 2026 18.37 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google1Argentina try to go the distance playing physicalA worrying thought as a tepid first half drew to a close: was the half-time show going to be better than the football? This was a slog. Argentina could not match Spain’s possession approach but they had ways to level the playing field. There was physicality from Lionel Scaloni’s side, whose aim was to kill the spectacle before taking control in the second half, and they were allowed to take it to the limit by the Slovenian referee, Slavko Vincic. Alexis Mac Allister could have been booked for an early foul on Dani Olmo and the leniency, together with a pitch not conducive to playing flowing football, allowed Argentina to disrupt Spain’s passing game, creating the conditions for one of the most tediousWorld Cupfinals.2… but Spain stay calm and trust their processCredit to Spain for refusing to take the bait. The European champions maintained their composure as they won the World Cup for the second time, even though their patience was stretched by Argentina’s spoiling tactics and constant niggles.Argentinahad no way up the pitch. They had problems passing out from the back, particularly from the left. Spain squeezed them. Their pressing was expertly coordinated, stifling Argentina. Enzo Fernández and Mac Allister were outplayed in midfield and Scaloni was forced to make a change at half-time, Nico González making way after struggling on the left wing and Leandro Paredes on to provide more stability. However, the switch did nothing to alter the mood. Argentina remained a cynical eyesore. Paredes was booked for a needless barge on Rodri and was lucky not to be sent off until after full-time. In the end Argentina were caught out by their own game. The writing was on the wall when Fernández, perhaps dashing his hopes of a move to Real Madrid, was sent off for two silly bookings at the end of normal time.3Exhaustion and overreliance on Messi proves pivotalArgentina are a slow, ageing side and had already played two rounds of extra time. Their legs were heavy and their cause was not helped by them losing both of their starting centre-backs to injury. Lisandro Martínez and Cristian Romero are comfortable on the ball so it was not ideal when they went off early. It deprived Argentina of another outlet, and their reliance on Lionel Messi also hurt them.Spaincut off the supply to the 39-year-old and were too intense for him. Messi was never able to find space, never able to shake off more than one Spanish player. There was no repeat of the crosses that hurt England in the semi-final and there was an argument that Scaloni should have taken off his captain after Fernández’s dismissal. Messi stood in the middle but could not hold the ball up. He was a passenger but Scaloni removed Julián Álvarez, who might have worried Spain more with his ability to run the channels.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion4Martínez almost saves Argentina with shootoutÁlvarez’s departure was the cue for Scaloni to bring on Marcos Senesi and switch to a back five. The statistics were ridiculous. Argentina’s xG was 0.00 until conceding forced a desperate late flurry. Even then, though, the threat increased only to 0.22. It took until the 117th minute for the defending world champions to muster their first shot, Messi’s hopeful effort from long range deflected wide. Spain, defending as a team, were unruffled. Argentina had 34.9% possession and six touches in the Spain area. It was one-way traffic during the second half. Spain kept pushing – they had 20 shots to two – but poor finishing and inspired goalkeeping threatened to leave them at the mercy of a penalty shootout. Emi Martínez’s 12 saves were the most by a goalkeeper in a World Cup final and his best was a leap to push away away a Lamine Yamal free‑kick. Yet it was not enough.5De la Fuente’s substitutes make the differenceSpain were deserved winners from a moral and tactical perspective. They played all the football and proved their big-game mentality by standing up to Argentina’s nonsense. Rodri, excellent as the No 6 before making way for Martín Zubimendi in extra time, nullified Fernández and Luis de la Fuente’s substitutions were spot on. Pedri was incisive after replacing Fabián Ruiz in midfield. Argentina worked to stifle Lamine Yamal, using Nicolás Tagliafico to contain the teenager on the right wing, but De la Fuente stretched the play by bringing Nico Williams on for Álex Baena on the opposite flank. Now Spain had speed down both wings and the pressure told. Mikel Merino should have scored at least once after coming on but eventually a goal arrived. Pedro Porro sent in a deep cross from the right. Williams beat Nahuel Molina and pulled the ball back for another substitute, Ferran Torres, to score.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026SpainArgentinaWorld CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Give it up for Vozinha, Spence and the 2026 World Cup’s cult heroes | Soccer | The Guardian
description: In today’s Football Daily: Vozinha, Djed Spence and Danny Murphy, together at last!
Author: Alex Reid
Headline: In today’s Football Daily: Vozinha, Djed Spence and Danny Murphy, together at last!
Content: Vozinha, getting his due.Photograph: John Sibley/ReutersView image in fullscreenVozinha, getting his due.Photograph: John Sibley/ReutersFootball Daily newsletterSoccerGive it up for Vozinha, Spence and the 2026 World Cup’s cult heroesSign up now! Sign up now! Sign up now? Sign up now!Alex ReidSun 19 Jul 2026 09.19 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 13.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWE CAN BE HEROESEvery World Cup produces cult heroes, icons who make it possible for us to sit back and start misty-eyed recollections by saying: “Do you remember Roger Milla/Saeed Al-Owairan/Keisuke Honda/Paul the Octopus?” Well, the Geopolitics World Cup has not let us down! Here are 10 cult heroes who’ve emerged from this tournament.Yaya Sithole: a catastrophic start as the South Africa midfielder’s error led to the first goal of the GWC against Mexico, compounded by a second-half red card. Bounced back with a vital display in the win over South Korea, leading his country to the knockout round. Decades from now, people will still be talking about the Yaya Sithole redemption arc. Possibly.Vozinha:who else? Cape Verde’s players all became cult heroes, but especially the 40-year-old keeper who spent last season in the Portuguese second tier and this summer keeping out Spain as the Blue Sharks went unbeaten in the group stage, then pushed Argentina to extra time in the last 32.Vozinha’s mum: get in line – we’re all in his fan club now.Djed Spence:a toss-up with Dan “the modern-day header-beast” Burn for England’s cult hero, but let’s give it to the Spurs full-back who transformed from the hapless scourge of Thomas Tuchel’s existence in the group stage to a chin-strapped Cafu in the knockout round. Spence’s slide tackle on Giuliano Simeone (albeit sans chin-strap) will live long in the memory.View image in fullscreenDjed Spence to the rescue.Photograph: Will Oliver/EPAMatías Galarza:Paraguay’s most magnificent git was always involved despite rarely trying to play football. Almost every time we saw the midfielder he was moaning, playacting, needling opponents – truly putting the “art” into the dark arts. Also scored against Turkey and later nicked the referee’s watch and strapped it to his own wrist. Timeless ’housery.Keyne:poor Lamine Yamal, who turned a craggy 19 during the GWC, must be feeling pretty aged considering he’s been usurped by his three-year-old brother. Keyne gurning and giggling in the crowd hasdelighted fans in Spain and beyond. Considering some of the other people who’ve hogged TV cutaways, the toddler has been a welcome sight.Iván Barton:Salvadorian referee grabbed the globe’s attention when he sent off Miguel Almirón, bawling at the winger like a mum sending a misbehaving youngster to bed with no dinner. “After review, No 10, Paraguay, covered his mouth! The decision is red card!” No PlayStation either, Miguel!Eloy Room:move over, Vozinha, and make room for Eloy! Yep, another veteran goalie starred at this GWC as Curaçao bounced back from shipping seven (7) against Germany to hold Ecuador to a 0-0 draw. The 37-year-old Roommade 15 saves– a record for a 90-minute World Cup match – to earn the tiny Caribbean island their first ever point.View image in fullscreenEloy Room to the rescue.Photograph: Ed Zurga/APDanny Murphy:or should it be Bob the cat? The deadpan BBC pundit’s tale of how a family moggy once made a break for freedom inside a Royal Mail van – sparked by Oscar Bobb coming off the bench for Norway – proved so engrossing that viewers demanded, and got, several follow-ups. A purr-fect hydration-break diversion. Honk!Hossam Hassan:Egypt’s perma-angry, black T-shirted head coach was an intimidating presence – especially paired with his identical twin brother, the team director, Ibrahim Hassan. The pairhad a row with Dallas police, led the Pharaohs to the last 16, saw Egypt take a 2-0 lead against Argentina … before the final 15 minutes helped Hossam reach new heights of apoplexy.Romelu Lukaku:how can Belgium’s record goalscorer be obscure enough for cult-hero status? Well, when you polish off USA USA USA 4-1 and celebrate your 93rd international goal with a Trump dance near the corner flag in the aftermath of Balogungate. Thanks, Big Rom (Bigliest Rom?) – the planet is with you.View image in fullscreenRomelu Lukaku to the rescue.Photograph: Lindsey Wasson/APLIVE ON BIG WEBSITEJoin Rob Smyth from 8pm BST (3pm EST, 5am AEST) for hot updates from Spain 1-1 Argentina (aet; 4-5 on pens) in the GWC final. The animals below agree,though the punters in New York City disagree. Meanwhile Tom Lutz will be in situ for another liveblog mopping up all other business outside the game, which we’re tempted to call Everything But The Goal.View image in fullscreenMoo Deng, a two-year-old female pygmy hippopotamus, picks Argentina at Khao Kheow Open Zoo in ThailandPhotograph: Anusak Laowilas/NurPhoto/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenPoty the jaguar sides with the holders too at BioParque, in Rio de Janeiro.Photograph: Tita Barros/ReutersQUOTE OF THE DAY“This game will help us for sure – even if you can never fully celebrate a bronze medal. It’s the first [men’s World Cup] medal for 60 years, first [at a men’s] World Cup on foreign soil, and I hope the players can be proud of that at some point. We set ourselves the highest dream to chase, and we were very, very ambitious with our dream to make the final in the World Cup. So it’s very, very painful if you miss out, the pain will stay for a while” – England head coach Thomas Tuchel after overseeinga quite preposterous 6-4 win over Francein the Third Place Play-off.View image in fullscreenEngland with their bronzes.Photograph: Paul Childs/ReutersFOOTBALL DAILY LETTERSdouble quotation markMay I be one of the 1,057 pedants to point out that it’s little wonder Brazil lost to Poland in the 1970 TPPO (yesterday’s Football Daily) as they would have been resting players for the next day’s final against Italy (which they won 4-1 to claim their third world crown – Pelé, Carlos Alberto and all that). You must have meant the 1974 TPPO. Easy to be fatigued at this stage of the tournament; just ask Harry Kane” – Jonathan Westwood (and 1,056 others).If you have any, please send letters tothe.boss@theguardian.com.Today’s prizeless letter o’ the day goes to … Jonathan Westwood (and the 1,056 others). Terms and conditions for our competitions, when we run them, arehere.This is an extract from our daily football email … Football Daily. To get the full version,just visit this page and follow the instructions.Explore more on these topicsFootballFootball Daily newsletterfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Tempo coach Sandy Brondello suspended over ‘protected species’ comment about Angel Reese | WNBA | The Guardian
description: Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello was suspended one game after calling Angel Reese a ‘protected species’ during Friday’s loss to Atlanta
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello was suspended one game after calling Angel Reese a ‘protected species’ during Friday’s loss to Atlanta
Content: Sandy Brondello of the Toronto Tempo has been a head coach in the WNBA since 2014, winning titles with Phoenix and New York.Photograph: Graham Hughes/The Canadian Press/APView image in fullscreenSandy Brondello of the Toronto Tempo has been a head coach in the WNBA since 2014, winning titles with Phoenix and New York.Photograph: Graham Hughes/The Canadian Press/APWNBATempo coach Sandy Brondello suspended over ‘protected species’ comment about Angel ReeseCoach suspended one game by WNBABrondello apologizes directly to Angel ReeseLeague cites professionalism standardsAgenciesSat 18 Jul 2026 21.02 EDTLast modified on Sat 18 Jul 2026 21.03 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleToronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello was suspended for one game by theWNBAfor an inappropriate comment she used during a game against the Atlanta Dream on Friday.She’ll miss Monday’s game against the Las Vegas Aces.“The WNBA expects all coaches and team personnel to uphold the highest standards of professionalism and respect that are fundamental to our league,” the league said Saturday in a statement announcing the suspension.Caitlin Clark pours in career-high 45 points as Fever edge Storm in thrillerRead moreDuring the 111-92 loss to the Dream, the broadcast picked up the Tempo’s coach addressing officials after Toronto player Nyara Sabally went down with an injury after contact with Angel Reese. Sabally was called for the foul and Brondello could be heard saying Reese was a “protected species”.Brondello posted an apology on social media on Saturday.“Angel, I’m sorry,” Brondello said. “Last night, in the emotion of the moment after Nyara’s injury, I used a phrase that I shouldn’t have used, and I take full responsibility for that. My frustration was with the officiating, but my words unfairly put the focus on you.”Reese took to social media after the game, posting: “ARE WE SURPRISED?!” and tagging Brondello’s account, followed by a clown face emoji.“I also understand that my words carried an impact beyond what I intended, particularly for Black women in our league, and I’m deeply sorry for that,” Brondello posted. “I’ve spent my career competing with, coaching and learning from incredible Black women.”The coach added: “I regret that my words caused hurt to a community I respect so deeply. I have a lot of respect for you as both a player and a person, and I sincerely apologize to you, your teammates, and the Dream organization for my comment.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Weekly for the 2026 WNBA seasonSign up toWNBA 30A limited run women’s basketball newsletter dedicated to the league’s historic 30th season.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBrondello, who is in her first season with the expansion Tempo, is Australian. The “protected species” phrase is used in Australian sports to express frustration for a lack of calls against a player. However, in the US, the phrase suggests that someone isn’t human and could be seen as derogatory toward Black people.Brondello has been a head coach in the league since 2014, winning titles with Phoenix and New York.Explore more on these topicsWNBABasketballUS sportsShareReuse this content

title: US boxer Hannah Rapp struck and killed by driver while cycling, police say | US crime | The Guardian
description: Investigators arrest 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with death of Rapp who was 26
Author: Ramon Antonio Vargas
Headline: Investigators arrest 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with death of Rapp who was 26
Content: Hannah Rapp fought for the World Boxing Council women’s featherweight championship.Photograph: Harry Aaron/Most Valuable PromotionsView image in fullscreenHannah Rapp fought for the World Boxing Council women’s featherweight championship.Photograph: Harry Aaron/Most Valuable PromotionsUS crimeUS boxer Hannah Rapp struck and killed by driver while cycling, police sayInvestigators arrest 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with death of Rapp who was 26Ramon Antonio VargasSun 19 Jul 2026 12.06 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 10.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleUS boxer Hannah Rapp, who in June fought for the World Boxing Council (WBC) women’s featherweight championship, was killed on Saturday while she bicycled inTexasafter a motorist struck her with his car, according to authorities.Rapp was 26, and investigators arrested a 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with her death, the sheriff’s office of Brazos county, Texas,saidin a statement.The agency said Rapp and a fellow bicyclist were riding on a local road when Medina passed them in his car, stopped, and drove his vehicle in reverse. Medina then hit Rapp, and she was pronounced dead after emergency responders brought her to a hospital.Brazos deputies said they booked Medina on a count of manslaughter, whichTexaslaw defines as recklessly causing a person’s death. The sheriff’s office did not provide additional details, saying the “isolated” case remained under active and ongoing investigation.Word of Rapp’s death prompted tributes from her colleagues in professional boxing.“The loss of Hannah Rapp leaves us heartbroken,” WBC president Mauricio Sulaimán said in a prepared statement. “She was an exceptional boxer, but above all, an invaluable member of our boxing family.“We join in the grief that overwhelms her loved ones, her team, and all those who had the privilege of knowing and supporting her throughout her brilliant career.”Rapp hadregisteredeight victories – including five by knockout – and drawn once during her first nine pro career bouts. For her 10th outing, the Yorktown, Indiana, native challenged Tiara Brown of Fort Myers, Florida, for her world title belt in the WBC’s 126lb (57.2kg) weight class.Brown retained her belt with a unanimous decision win at Florida’s Caribe Royal Orlando resort. Nonetheless, on Saturday, the champion wrote on social media that Rapp “was the best dance partner I have ever faced as a pro”.View image in fullscreenHannah Rapp and Tiara Brown.Photograph: Harry Aaron/Most Valuable Promotions“We fought a GREAT fight for the fans from the [first] bell to the last,” Brown wrote. “It was my honor to share the ring with her.”Medina’s bond amount was not immediately set, and he remained in custody on Sunday morning, Brazos jail records show.Those jail records also showed prior arrests for Medina on counts such as deadly conduct by discharging a firearm, assault causing bodily injury, and reckless driving. Dispositions for those cases weren’t immediately available.Medina could face between two and 20 years in prison under Texas law if eventually convicted of manslaughter in Rapp’s death.Explore more on these topicsUS crimeTexasBoxingUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Kane says Tuchel will ‘learn a lot’ from England pressures after exit to Argentina | England | The Guardian
description: Harry Kane has backed Thomas Tuchel to learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat and says upcoming Nations League campaign is key to improving
Author: Ed Aarons
Headline: Harry Kane has backed Thomas Tuchel to learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat and says upcoming Nations League campaign is key to improving
Content: Harry Kane said Thomas Tuchel played ‘a big part’ in creating positive atmosphere within squad.Photograph: Catherine Ivill/AMA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHarry Kane said Thomas Tuchel played ‘a big part’ in creating positive atmosphere within squad.Photograph: Catherine Ivill/AMA/Getty ImagesEnglandKane says Tuchel will ‘learn a lot’ from England pressures after exit to ArgentinaGerman created ‘best England group for togetherness’Captain underlines importance of Nations League gamesEd Aaronsin MiamiSun 19 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHarry Kane has backedThomas Tuchelto learn from England’s World Cup semi-final defeat against Argentina and believes they must use the forthcoming Nations League campaign to gain experience of playing against the top teams.Kane was an unused substitute as England sealed third place with anentertaining 6-4 victory against Franceon Saturday – their best finish at a World Cup on foreign soil. Tuchel, who signed a contract extension before the tournament, has been criticised heavily for his tacticsagainst Argentinawhen he opted to switch to a back five to try to defend a 1-0 lead.England 2026 World Cup squad: player-by-player ratingsRead moreKane admittedEnglandhad fallen short against Lionel Scaloni’s side but described the environment Tuchel has created since succeeding Gareth Southgate in 2025 as “the best England group I’ve been a part of in terms of togetherness”.The captain said: “I know there are a lot of questions after the other night. We have to process it as players. I’m sure the manager will. It’s his first major tournament. He’ll learn a lot from the players, the situations, the pressures, the travel.“There are also a lot of things as players we can learn from the other night. For now it’s just trying to process everything. We had a lot of good things, a lot of tough situations we overcame. The group is incredible, the best England group I’ve been a part of in terms of togetherness. The boss was a big part of creating that as well.“Fans are going to be angry and we understand it. It’s a major tournament and that’s why you dream so big. When it doesn’t go your way you have to take the criticism. We’re all willing to do that, to dare to reach the biggest goal of all. We know over the next two years until we’re facing that situation again there is always going to be a question mark. It’s something we have to take and use as motivation.”England missed out on facing regular top-class opposition in the buildup to theWorld Cupafter being relegated from the top Nations League group in 2022 under Southgate. They were promoted in 2024 and will face Spain, Croatia and Czechia, starting with a rematch of the Euro 2024 final against Luis de la Fuente’s side on 26 September at Wembley. After beating France – albeit in a largely meaningless game – Kane thinks England must take the competition seriously if they are to thrive in tournaments.View image in fullscreenDefeat to an established opponent was a familiar feeling for England.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty Images“The last 30 minutes of Argentina is not the team we want to be,” he said. “We spent a lot of time over the last two years talking about that situation, to attack those moments. The bottom line is we lost anyway. It’s easy to go away and talk about it. We need to get better at actually being in those situations. We can train, play the qualifiers … I think the Nations League will be big for us, against Spain and Croatia, big teams.“Getting used to those big environments and finding our best form against those teams, like we did against France, albeit not in the most competitive match. We have to learn, improve. The coach will have improved from these learnings. I can talk as much as I want but ultimately it’s about showing it next time we’re in that situation.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionKane has been on the losing side in two World Cup semi-finals and twoEuropean Championshipfinals and he believes Tuchel is the man to help England to make the final step.“The enthusiasm he has, the emotion he brings, the tactical experience he has … it doesn’t mean you’re going to get it right every time. We want to get it right on the biggest occasions and he expects that from himself as well. He’s a fantastic coach. He not only gave the players belief but he gave the country belief that it was our year. That is why it hurts even more than other years because everyone believed we were going all the way.“Everyone has to process it. He has to process it himself. We’re still in that grieving stage, the emotional stage, and we’ll be in that for the next few weeks. It won’t be until the next camp where you think about what the future holds and what the next target is. We’ll have conversations as captain and manager and with other teammates and staff, to see where we can be better.”Explore more on these topicsEnglandHarry KaneThomas TuchelEuro 2028European ChampionshipWorld Cup 2026World CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: England 2026 World Cup squad: player-by-player ratings | England | The Guardian
description: After the team’s third-placed finish, we rate the 24 squad members who made an appearance at the tournament
Author: Ed Aarons
Headline: After the team’s third-placed finish, we rate the 24 squad members who made an appearance at the tournament
Content: Bukayo Saka, Jordan Pickford, Djed Spence and Jude Bellingham were among England’s top performers.Composite: Rex/Shutterstock; Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBukayo Saka, Jordan Pickford, Djed Spence and Jude Bellingham were among England’s top performers.Composite: Rex/Shutterstock; Getty ImagesEnglandEngland 2026 World Cup squad: player-by-player ratingsAfter the team’s third-placed finish, we rate the 24 squad members who made an appearance at the tournamentEd Aaronsin MiamiSun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGoalkeepersJordan PickfordCould he have done better with Enzo Fernández’s equaliser for Argentina? Jude Bellingham certainly thought so. But the man who has made moreWorld Cupfinals appearances than any other Englishman made some crucial saves, including a brilliant stop from Nicolas González in the semi-final.7Dean HendersonHad to wait until the third-place playoff for some action and ended up conceding four goals despite a strong performance. Will hope to challenge Pickford over next two years.7DefendersDjed SpenceEmerged as an unlikely cult hero thanks to some wholehearted performances. He will always be remembered for snubbing Thomas Partey’s handshake and his tackle on Giuliano Simeone against Argentina.8Marc GuéhiSomehow wasn’t Thomas Tuchel’s first-choice initially, although that was rectified after the Croatia game. England’s most consistent and reliable defender – the way he handled Erling Haaland was a masterclass.8John StonesArrived painfully short of match fitness and it showed in the opening game against Croatia. Much improved when called upon in the quarter- and semi-finals. A huge asset when he plays.7Ezri KonsaA few shaky moments during the group stage but recovered well and filled in capably at right-back when needed. At 28, young enough to be there again in four years.7View image in fullscreenDan Burn’s headed block from this Raúl Jiménez bicycle kick cleared the halfway line.Photograph: Ulises Ruiz/AFP/Getty ImagesDan BurnShowed his limitations against Argentina. But he will always have those two epic appearances off the bench against Mexico and Norway. No one will forget the clearing headers so powerful they ended up in the opposition’s half.7Nico O’ReillyHad his strongest game against Norway in the quarter-final when he was at his marauding best. Will be disappointed not to have scored and to have been on the pitch as a substitute when Argentina instigated their semi-final comeback.6Reece JamesSuch a good player when fit, which is sadly far too rare. Missed most of the tournament owing to a hamstring injury and England suffered when he went off against Argentina.6Trevoh ChalobahThe Chelsea player was a surprise choice to replace Tino Livramento, who pulled out injured at the last minute. Managed only six minutes against France in the end.6Jarell QuansahBrought in to solve the right-back conundrum against Panama, he was doing well until the rush of blood against Mexico and subsequent red card. Recalled for the France game and had to be replaced.5MidfieldersJude BellinghamShowed his supreme quality when it mattered and could not be more committed to his country’s cause, even if his rocky relationship with Tuchel had to be carefully managed.9Elliot AndersonDidn’t allow himself to be distracted by his £116m move to Manchester City and grew as the tournament progressed. Ran his heart out against Norway and Argentina.8View image in fullscreenMorgan Rogers (left) crossed for Anthony Gordon to put England ahead against Argentina.Photograph: Julian Finney/FIFA/Getty ImagesMorgan RogersMade the most of his limited minutes in unfamiliar roles. Showed what a good player he is by playing as one of the two central midfielders against Norway before setting up Anthony Gordon from the right wing against Argentina.8Declan RiceClearly affected by the hamstring and back problems that marred the second half of his season with Arsenal, even if his effort – as ever – could not be faulted. Needs a long rest to recharge his batteries but may not get one.6Eberechi EzeCould not have the impact he would have wished for in four substitute appearances before starting against France. Did a decent job in central midfield against Panama but was largely on the periphery when given a chance on the left in the quarter-final.6Jordan HendersonSpent 12 minutes on the pitch, in a historic appearance against Panama when he became the first England men’s player to appear at seven major international tournaments. But managed to get booked against Mexico as a substitute and break an arm celebrating the win.5ForwardsBukayo SakaThree goals and three assists despite playing through the pain of an achilles injury was a courageous effort from Arsenal’s talisman. It was a huge surprise not to see him come off the bench against Argentina and he showed what England were missing against France.8Anthony GordonA quiet start having been preferred to Rashford before responding magnificently to being dropped. Three assists and a goal against Argentina showed why Barcelona were so keen to sign him.8View image in fullscreenHarry Kane’s two goals against the Democratic Republic of the Congo saved England from an early exit.Photograph: Cristóbal Herrera/EPAHarry KaneSadly his tournament will be remembered for a largely anonymous display against Argentina rather than rescuing England against the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Will he ever have a better chance to win the World Cup?8Marcus RashfordMade a good start to his third World Cup by coming off the bench to score against Croatia, and started against Panama. But wasn’t seen again until England were chasing the game against Argentina in injury time.6Ivan ToneySat on the bench until Lautaro Martínez’s goal in added time of the semi-final. Could not make an impact in the three minutes he was given but did well against France.6Ollie WatkinsLargely a frustrated spectator despite his heroics in the Euro 2024 semi-final and arriving here in a rich vein of form. A half on the left flank against France and a brief appearance against Panama were scant reward.6Noni MaduekeSome promising moments but lacked end product in his four starts and had slipped down the pecking order by the semi-final. Needs to be much more clinical at this level.5Did not play:James Trafford and Kobbie MainooExplore more on these topicsEnglandWorld Cup 2026World CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Fifa gives fraud ‘an open door’ with betting, says Council of Europe chief  | Fifa | The Guardian
description: Alain Berset said Fifa’s deal with a prediction market company and Folarin Balogun’s reprieve meant urgent work was needed on ‘the integrity of the sport’
Author: Nick Ames
Headline: Alain Berset said Fifa’s deal with a prediction market company and Folarin Balogun’s reprieve meant urgent work was needed on ‘the integrity of the sport’
Content: The final of a controversial World Cup takes place on Sunday. Alain Berset said: ‘The celebration will end tonight. The questions will not.’Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe final of a controversial World Cup takes place on Sunday. Alain Berset said: ‘The celebration will end tonight. The questions will not.’Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesFifaFifa gives fraud ‘an open door’ with betting, says Council of Europe chiefFifa deal with prediction market company under fireBalogun reprieve showed ‘rules bend under pressure’Nick Amesin New York andPhilippe AuclairSun 19 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFifa has been accused of providing an “open door to fraud” and allowing political influence to cast doubt on the integrity of the World Cup in a stinging rebuke by the Council of Europe’s secretary general.In an open letter published to coincide with Sunday’s final, Alain Berset also called for a new integrity framework to be built before the 2030 tournament, which is mainly being staged in Europe, and warned thatFifawas embroiled in a crisis involving money and power.Fifa to announce record $15bn World Cup revenue, smashing expectationsRead moreBerset referenced the scandal surrounding Fifa’s decision to shelve the suspension of the USA striker Folarin Balogun and made apparent allusions to comments made by the Paraguayan senator Celeste Amarillaabout Kylian Mbappéand by the former Spanish prime minister Mariano Rajoyabout the France team.“The FifaWorld Cup 2026has raised question after question,” Berset wrote. “A sanction suspended under pressure, within days, with no reasons given. The authority of referees called into question. Racist abuse of players, some of it from elected officials. Betting on every pass, every card, every corner. The celebration will end tonight. The questions will not.”It is a significant intervention given Fifa and the Council of Europe signed a memorandum of understanding in 2018, which remains valid. The agreement was struck to strengthen their work together on a number of topics, including “human rights, integrity and good governance in sport”. Open criticism of one partner by another is extremely rare.Berset took aim at Fifa’s deal with ADI Predictstreet, which became the World Cup’s official prediction market partner in April. “Betting has moved from the result of a match to moments a single player can produce without changing the score,” he said. “A bet is won by making others lose. It is an open door to fraud. And thisWorld Cuphas opened the door wider.”In further reference to the lifting of Balogun’s ban by Fifa, which occurred after a phone call to its president,Gianni Infantino, by his US counterpart, Donald Trump, Berset said: “When the rules bend under pressure, every result is open to doubt.”View image in fullscreenGianni Infantino with Donald Trump at the World Cup final reception at Trump Tower on Friday 17 July in New York.Photograph: Ira L Black/Fifa/Getty ImagesInfantino has said the decision to revoke Balogun’s ban was taken independently by Fifa’s disciplinary committee “based on the applicable regulations and the specific facts”.Berset, who is Swiss, proposed a “working dialogue that starts tonight” to begin the “urgent work of strengthening the integrity of sport” before future World Cups. He referenced work by the Council of Europe – which is the continent’s intergovernmental human rights organisation – in creating a binding treaty to keep fans safe three months after the Heysel Stadium disaster in 1985, as well as further legislation set up to tackle doping in athletics and match-fixing.The letter comes a week and a half after 72 members of the European parliament wrote to the 27 EU-based football associations asking for an investigation into Infantino’s involvement in the Balogun case. It pointed to alleged breaches of political neutrality and suggested Fifa had contravened its own code of ethics.Itdoes not appear likelythat Uefa, European football’s governing body, will formally take the matter further. In June, 50 members of the European parliament wrote to Infantino and the Fifa council requesting an investigation into the awarding ofthe Fifa peace prize to Trump.Fifa was contacted for comment.This past week it was revealed that more that 200 of Fifa’s member associations haveformally backedInfantino’s attempt to be re-elected for a fourth term in March.Explore more on these topicsFifaGianni InfantinoCouncil of EuropeGamblingWorld Cup 2026Football politicsWorld CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: World Cup final: Trump booed, half-time show and everything but the football – as it happened | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The World Cup got its first half-time show starring Shakira and BTS, as Donald Trump attended his first game of the tournament – with mixed results
Author: Tom Lutz
Headline: The World Cup got its first half-time show starring Shakira and BTS, as Donald Trump attended his first game of the tournament – with mixed results
Content: 4h ago18.50 EDTWe’ll close up now. Here’s the match report from today’s game:Spain win World Cup after Ferran Torres breaks 10-man Argentina’s resistanceRead moreAnd here’s how the crowd greetedDonald Trumpand Gianni Infantino:Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before World Cup presentation ceremonyRead moreShare

title: Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra time | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Spain are the world champions after a deserved 1-0 victory over an Argentina team who barely mustered a shot
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Spain are the world champions after a deserved 1-0 victory over an Argentina team who barely mustered a shot
Content: Ferran Torres celebrates after scoring for Spain in the World Cup final against Argentina.Photograph: Michael Regan/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFerran Torres celebrates after scoring for Spain in the World Cup final against Argentina.Photograph: Michael Regan/FIFA/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra timeEnzo Fernández sent off for two bookings in normal timeVictory secures second men’s World Cup title forLa RojaMatch gallery|Golden Boot|Follow us over on TikTokMatt Hughesat New York New Jersey StadiumSun 19 Jul 2026 18.02 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSpain finally broke the dogged resistance of 10-man Argentina in extra time towin the men’s World Cupfor the second time as a drab final ended in a dramatic fashion.The Barcelona winger Ferran Torres scored the only goal of a disjointed game in the 106th minute, with Argentina’s cynical approach summed up by a mass confrontation sparked by Leandro Paredes pushing Gavi and Eric García to the ground after the final whistle, which led to the Boca Juniors midfielder following Enzo Fernández in being shown a red card.Spain win World Cup after Ferran Torres breaks 10-man Argentina’s resistanceRead moreThe Chelsea midfielder had been sent off for a second yellow card just before the end of the initial 90 minutes, which left Argentina facing an even more difficult task in hanging on against superior opponents in extra time.Donald Trump was booed when he walked out for the trophy presentation alongside Gianni Infantino, and appeared reluctant to move away from the players’ celebrations after both men handed the trophy to Spain’s captain, Rodri.In another indication of the way Infantino is positioning Fifa, the trophies for individual awards such as the player of the tournament won by Rodri were handed over by Yasir al-Rumayyan, the governor of Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund who is also the chair of Newcastle United.Lionel Messi was applauded by the entire stadium as he collected his runners-up medal in floods of tears, but Argentina’s squad then turned their backs to face their own fans behind the goal as Spain’s players received their medals, an act of petulance that encapsulated their approach.View image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Gianni Infantino pictured with Spain as the winners celebrate with the trophy.Photograph: Kevin C Cox/Getty ImagesSpain’s hard-fought victory adds theWorld Cupto their European Championship triumph two years ago and is the second time they have held both titles, after they won the World Cup for the first time in 2010 as European champions.King Felipe of Spain watched the game from Trump’s private box at the New York New Jersey Stadium in a gathering that included the leaders of the United States’ co-hosts of this tournament, the Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexican president, Claudia Sheinbaum, along with the Fifa president and other members of the Trump family.A World Cup full of thrilling matches and late comebacks ended in an underwhelming spectacle on the pitch, but also with deserved winners in Spain, who dominated completely having previously dismantled the favourites, France, in the semi-finals.View image in fullscreenArgentina’s Nahuel Molina (centre) and Leandro Paredes clash with Gavi, leading to a red card for Paredes.Photograph: Ronald Wittek/EPASpain had 20 shots to two from Argentina – both of which came towards the end of extra time as they at last pushed frantically for an equaliser their previous efforts did not merit.Without a brilliant performance from Argentina’s goalkeeper, Emi Martínez, who made a series of superb saves, Spain would have been celebrating long before substitute Torres broke the deadlock in the 106th minute. Pedro Porro’s cross to the back post was kept in play brilliantly by Nico Williams, with Torres pouncing to fire beyond Martínez and send the Spanish into raptures.Martínez had kept Argentina in the contest almost singlehandedly, but most neutrals and the many celebrities in attendance will have been pleased to see him finally beaten given his side’s cynical tactics.Argentina did not even attempt a shot until the 115th minute when Messi, making his final World Cup appearance, struck the ball at the head of Mikel Merino.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe climax of a World Cup that Infantino described on Friday as “the most incredible event in human history” appeared to be as much about show business as sport, with the spectacle on the pitch failing to live up to the razzmatazz off it for long periods.In addition to the world leaders in Trump’s box, Fifa released an 11-page list of celebrities in attendance, many of whom were also booked to perform as the US major event culture took hold.View image in fullscreenA tearful Lionel Messi on the pitch after the match.Photograph: Sarah Yenesel/EPAThe match had kicked off six minutes late owing to the pre-game schmaltz being dialled up to levels that made the Super Bowl look subdued. Jennifer Hudson gave a spine-tingling rendition of the Star Spangled Banner followed by Robbie Williams singing the rather less well known Fifa anthem, Desire.‘It was crap’: Wayne Rooney’s candour helps BBC in World Cup final TV battle | Will Unwin"Read moreMore bizarrely Tom Cruise then delivered what was no doubt intended as a stirring speech, which included such Fifa-sanctioned platitudes as: “Football is a force that unites people.”In another departure from World Cup norms the trophy was delivered to the field pre-match by two previous winners, Spain’s Andrés Iniesta and Argentina’s Mario Kempes, from what was described as a Luis Vuitton trophy trunk in one of the many endorsement deals that has helped Fifa netrevenue of $15bnfrom this tournament.The much-hyped half-time show featuring among others Madonna, Justin Bieber and the actors Jason Sudeikis and Brendan Hunt playing their characters from Ted Lasso – the first time pop stars have been permitted on the pitch during a World Cup final – meant that the interval stretched to 27 minutes and 20 seconds. That will have suited Argentina, whose tactics were based on delaying and disrupting Spain throughout.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinanewsShareReuse this content

title: Russell’s Mercedes problems ‘100% on us’, says Toto Wolff after Belgium blow | Formula One 2026 | The Guardian
description: Toto Wolff has admitted Mercedes let George Russell down after he crashed out of the Belgian Grand Prix following a collision with Lewis Hamilton
Author: Giles Richards
Headline: Toto Wolff has admitted Mercedes let George Russell down after he crashed out of the Belgian Grand Prix following a collision with Lewis Hamilton
Content: George Russell departed the Belgian Grand Prix after a first-lap crash with Lewis Hamilton.Photograph: Christian Hartmann/ReutersView image in fullscreenGeorge Russell departed the Belgian Grand Prix after a first-lap crash with Lewis Hamilton.Photograph: Christian Hartmann/ReutersFormula One 2026Russell’s Mercedes problems ‘100% on us’, says Toto Wolff after Belgium blowRussell clipped by Hamilton on lap one after power lossBritish driver 50 points behind Antonelli in standingsGiles RichardsSun 19 Jul 2026 15.06 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 16.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleToto Wolff, the Mercedes principal, has admitted the team let George Russell down at the Belgian Grand Prix. The British driver failed to score a point after a clash withLewis Hamiltonthat Russell attributed to his car’s performance not being up to scratch.The racewas won by Kimi Antonelli, who now leads his teammate Russell by 50 points in the world championship, a serious blow to the latter’s title hopes.Russell was clipped by Hamilton on the opening lap, sending him off into the gravel and ending his race. Russell said it had been a racing incident but insisted he would not have been alongside Hamilton but for his Mercedes failing to deploy sufficient power on the run to the Les Combes corner. Russell had been outspoken all weekend about a deficiency in electrical energy deployment and felt it had been hugely costly, which Wolff addressed.Kimi Antonelli tightens F1 title grip with Belgian GP win after Russell crashes outRead more“We had an issue on all Mercedes engines that we lacked the power out on the straights,” Wolff said. “That bit him [Russell] badly and it goes 100% on us.“George has 100% our support. George had a difficult weekend. Half of it is to be attributed to the power unit that wasn’t as good as it should have been and the other half may be on the driving. But we are in this together, we all make mistakes, today is the mistake of the team and another day is going to be the driver. We just need to minimise it and support each other.”Russell said the result had left him numb with disappointment, while insisting he felt he should not have been competing for the corner with the Ferraris, who had swept past him on the Kemmel straight. Wolff said he understood his driver’s strong reaction.“It’s completely understandable that the moment you’re out of the race, it’s so frustrating and the emotions boil,” he said. “We are trying to solve the issues, he’s doing everything he can to get on top of his driving. I’m going to try to put my arm around him.”Hamilton was found by the stewards to be culpable and given a five-second penalty, but he too felt it was only a racing incident and that he would speak to Russell about it.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“I was in the position where George obviously looked like he lost power and then he swooped around the outside and just had a small coming together,” he said. “I will text George later on. The last thing you ever want to do is come together with your teammate or with your old team who you highly respect.”Explore more on these topicsFormula One 2026Toto WolffGeorge RussellLewis HamiltonMercedes GPFerrariFormula OnenewsShareReuse this content

title: England beat India by 27 runs in third and deciding men’s cricket ODI – as it happened | Cricket | The Guardian
description: England beat India by 27 runs in a high-scoring contest at Lord’s to secure their ⁠first bilateral one-day international series victory against the world’s No 1-ranked side since 2018
Author: Tim de Lisle
Headline: England beat India by 27 runs in a high-scoring contest at Lord’s to secure their ⁠first bilateral one-day international series victory against the world’s No 1-ranked side since 2018
Content: 8h ago14.57 EDTAli MartinAfter a cascade of wickets during the much-criticised Test here six weeks ago came a landslide of one-day international runs. In the end, despite a ferocious century from Rohit Sharma that turned Lord’s into his personal playground, England had enough on the board to seal a 2-1 series victory against India.Harry Brook must have thought it was mission accomplished for his side at the halfway stage, with Ben Duckett’s 141 from 135 balls having powered them to 387 for three. Both were men’s ODI records at Lord’s, with Duckett having surpassed Viv Richards and the unbeaten 138 made during the World Cup in 1979.But amid speculation about his international future – reports of a recent tap on the shoulder – Sharma erupted with a display of controlled brutality. His 138 from 110 balls, first allied with another glossy 77 from Shubman Gill, had India tracking with the target and their vocal supporters dreaming of a famous heist.That was until Jacob Bethell, fresh from 91 with the bat, finally slid one of his left-arm tweakers past a sweep shot and into the stumps to see India 262 for two in the 39th over. It was the key moment in a run chase that eventually saw the tourists finish on 360 for seven and go down by a margin of 27 runs.England take India ODI series after Duckett’s ton and Bethell’s brilliant star turnRead moreShare

title: England take India ODI series after Duckett’s ton and Bethell’s brilliant star turn | Cricket | The Guardian
description: Rohit Sharma made 138 for India but England’s 387-3 secured a 27-run win as Ben Duckett hit 141 and Jacob Bethell made 91 and bowled seven overs for 49
Author: Ali Martin
Headline: Rohit Sharma made 138 for India but England’s 387-3 secured a 27-run win as Ben Duckett hit 141 and Jacob Bethell made 91 and bowled seven overs for 49
Content: Ben Duckett and Jacob Bethell made explosive contributions to England’s record score.Photograph: Graeme Wilcockson/Focus Images Ltd/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenBen Duckett and Jacob Bethell made explosive contributions to England’s record score.Photograph: Graeme Wilcockson/Focus Images Ltd/ShutterstockCricketEngland take India ODI series after Duckett’s ton and Bethell’s brilliant star turn3rd ODI: England, 387-3, bt India, 360-7, by 27 runsRohit Sharma hits searing century but India fall shortAli Martinat Lord'sSun 19 Jul 2026 14.55 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter a cascade of wickets during the much-criticised Test here six weeks ago came a landslide of one-day international runs. In the end, despite a ferocious century from Rohit Sharma that turned Lord’s into his personal playground, England had enough on the board to seal a 2-1 series victory against India.Harry Brook must have thought it was mission accomplished for his side at the halfway stage, with Ben Duckett’s 141 from 135 balls having powered England to 387 for three. Both were men’s ODI records at Lord’s, with Duckett having surpassed Viv Richards and the unbeaten 138 he made during the World Cup in 1979.England beat India by 27 runs in third and deciding men’s cricket ODI – as it happenedRead moreBut amid speculation about his international future – reports of a recent tap on the shoulder – Sharma erupted with a display of controlled brutality. His 138 from 110 balls, first allied with another glossy 77 from Shubman Gill, had India tracking with the target and their vocal supporters dreaming of a famous heist.That was until Jacob Bethell, fresh from 91 with the bat, finally slid one of his left-arm tweakers past a sweep shot from Sharma and into the stumps to leave India at 260 for two in the 39th over. It was the key moment in a run chase in which the tourists finished on 360 for seven and lost by 27 runs.Brook later credited the head coach, Brendon McCullum, for the introduction of Bethell, having nipped off the field for an over to seek his advice. The pair have built up a strong rapport, with T20 and ODI series wins against India this summer. But it has also pushed Brook forward as a candidate for the Test captaincy under the next head coach in that format.“It’d be tough to do [all three jobs] but I’m not saying it’s impossible,” Brook said. “I’m extremely pleased with the way we’ve performed in this series after going 1-0 down – the fight, camaraderie and belief we have shown to win is credit to the team and the coaches.”One of the differences on the day was Jos Buttler’s incendiary, unbeaten 41 from 13 balls at the back end of England’s innings, while Brook was also blessed with more bowling options than his opposite number. Among them was Sam Curran, whose array of cutters and moon-balls claimed four for 75 and shut down Virat Kohli’s maiden half-century at Lord’s for 74.Brook had also won a handy toss, even if Gill, without Jasprit Bumrah due to a knee injury sustained in ­Cardiff, said he would have bowled first. In Bumrah’s absence, only Prasidh Krishna adjusted quickly enough first thing – and even then his figures were set alight at the tail end by Buttler’s ultimately match-winning cameo.This dizzying match began with a minute’s silence for the late Sir Garfield Sobers and during the first couple of hours it looked like a fitting century might follow. Bethell, who swapped Barbados for a scholarship at Rugby School with a personal ­reference from Sobers, fell nine runs short of his second in the format.View image in fullscreenRohit Sharma’s swashbuckling innings proved in vain.Photograph: Henning von Jagow/Action Plus/ShutterstockStill, as one half of a 192-run ­opening stand with Duckett, Bethell had helped to set a record by an ­England pair against India – any wicket – and the platform for this monster total. Bethell struck 11 fours and pulled two mighty sixes for his 93-ball 91, dampening concerns about his recent promotion to opener in the process.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe SpinSubscribe to our cricket newsletter for our writers' thoughts on the biggest stories and a review of the week’s actionPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDuckett was at his impish best, the more passive initially – 64 balls for his half-century compared to Bethell’s 52 – before kicking on with Joe Root in a 101-run second‑wicket stand. His only real flirtation with danger, before plopping a catch to Prince Yadav, was also his only six as KL Rahul tumbled over the rope with ball in hand.The England of old might have pushed out Buttler ahead of Root but the latter’s busy unbeaten 74 from 48 balls proved no impediment. Following scores of 76 not out at Edgbaston and 99 not out in Cardiff, England’s No 3 has been no average Joe in this series – and has scored 360 runs since his last dismissal in the format.And the fireworks followed regardless. After Brook came and went for 14, Buttler arrived in the mood for mischief. He slapped four fours and cleared the rope three times to see 82 runs flow from the last five overs. At that stage, India looked as cooked as Gooram Brar’s figures of none for 97 – the costliest in a Lord’s ODI.It was clear, however, that unlikeEngland’s crapshoot victoryin whites against New Zealand last month, wickets would be tricky to come by and even containment was going to be a struggle. Gill was in sublime touch from the outset, holding the pose after lacing his cover-drives as he and Sharma put on 147 for the first wicket.Adil Rashid eventually prised out Gill lbw in the 25th but it was not until Sharma’s demise – followed by Curran’s quickfire removals of Ishan Kishan and Shreyas Iyer – that Brook could breathe a little easier. The final overs may have been an anticlimax but the journey to get there was both brilliant and bonkers.Explore more on these topicsCricketEngland cricket teamIndia cricket teamnewsShareReuse this content

title: My hands were all over the place for ‘surreal’ Open-winning putt, says Ryan Fox | The Open | The Guardian
description: Ryan Fox admitted he could not feel his hands as he lined up his winning putt in the 154th Open Championship at Royal Birkdale
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: Ryan Fox admitted he could not feel his hands as he lined up his winning putt in the 154th Open Championship at Royal Birkdale
Content: Ryan Fox is the first New Zealander since Bob Charles in 1963 to win the Open.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreenRyan Fox is the first New Zealander since Bob Charles in 1963 to win the Open.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianThe OpenMy hands were all over the place for ‘surreal’ Open-winning putt, says Ryan FoxFox sinks winning putt on 18th for first major title‘It’s going to be big back home in New Zealand’Ewan Murrayat Royal BirkdaleSun 19 Jul 2026 16.25 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRyan Fox has said he had no ­sensation in his hands as he hit the winning putt in the 154th Open Cham­pionship. Fox, who claimedhis first major title, became the first New Zealander since Bob Charles in 1963 to win the Open. He did so in dramatic style, with a birdie putt on the final hole sufficient for a 68 and 10-under-par total which was enough to beat Cameron Young by one stroke.Fox was typically brisk in his approach to playing the 18th. Reflecting on the moment of victory, though, he explained the state of his emotions. “You can’t feel your hands, I know that,” he said.“My focus with the putter all day was, just take a really deep breath before I hit it and just try to hit a line and I did that on 18. I was shaking. I was just trying to get it up there near the hole.Glory eludes hometown hero Tommy Fleetwood in his dream made realityRead more“It looked pretty straight and thankfully it dived in that left edge. It’s crazy. I don’t really know what to think. I don’t know how I ­managed to keep the putter face square enough for that to go on line but I’ll take it.”Fox’s inward half of 34 included two birdies in the last three holes. “I said to my caddie through six or eight holes: ‘I feel a lot better than I thought I would feel right now,’” he said. “I played really solid early. To play that last hole how I did, I stood on the tee and said to my caddie: ‘Dean, we’re going to try to win this.’ I hit two perfect shots. I’m not sure how I hit the putt. As I said, my hands were all over the place. I was just trying to get it towards the hole somewhere. It’s surreal that it went in.”Fox, the son of the All Black rugby legend Grant Fox, had not recorded a major top‑10 finish before this and missed the cut in the Open of 2025. “You dream of this moment,” he said. “I don’t know whether I ever thought it was going to become a reality. Now it is and to have my name etched in the history of the game is pretty awesome. I think it’s pretty awesome for New Zealand as well. It’s going to be big down there.“I appreciate all the support from down there at home. It’s going to be a lot of people pretty tired Monday morning at work after staying up all night to watch. I’m glad it was worthwhile for them.”Explore more on these topicsThe OpenEuropean TourPGA TourGolfUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘We noticed a login from a new device’: the message from fraudsters targeting your X account | Scams | The Guardian
description: They are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacks
Author: Samuel Gibbs
Headline: They are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacks
Content: Fraudsters want direct access to your account to commit further fraud – but there are telltale signs.Photograph: AlexPhotoStock/AlamyView image in fullscreenFraudsters want direct access to your account to commit further fraud – but there are telltale signs.Photograph: AlexPhotoStock/AlamyScam watchScams‘We noticed a login from a new device’: the message from fraudsters targeting your X accountThey are out to steal your password to commit further fraud such as crypto scams or phishing attacksSamuel GibbsConsumer technology editorSun 19 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 02.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleYou have had anXaccount for years, since it was known as Twitter. When an email arrives about a new login from a location nowhere near where you live, alarm bells begin to ring.“We noticed a login to your account from a new device. Was this you?” the email asks.The location is Arizona, but you live in London. The device is “Firefox Desktop on Mac”.“If this wasn’t you,” the email continues, “complete these steps now to protect your account.”The steps include clicking a link to change your password, which will end up with you “logged out of all your active X sessions except the one you’re using at this time” and to click another link to review the apps that have access to your account and revoke any that are unfamiliar.The steps are legitimate advice from X, but the links to reset your password, or to review app access are not. The email is fake and attempting to trick you into giving scammers your password, or direct access to your X account.“Scammers want your X username and password, or to trick you into approving a malicious link that gives them access to your account without needing your password,” says Jake Moore, a global cybersecurity adviser at ESET.Once the criminals have access to your account they will more than likely use it to attempt to commit further fraud including crypto scams, phishing attacks and misinformation campaigns.What it looks likeThe fake emails are almost an exact replica of legitimate login notifications sent by X. They include the X logo, and the same formatting, colours and copy, with correct grammar and spelling.View image in fullscreenSpot the difference: a scam email pretending to be an X new device login notification (left) next to a legitimate notification (right).Composite: Samuel Gibbs/The GuardianSmall telltale signs show the email is not legitimate, such as not including your X account handle, and being vague on the location of the login.“The two biggest giveaways are the email address it comes from, and where the links actually take you,” says Moore.Thesocial media site says: “X will only send you emails from @X.com or @e.X.com… Please know that X will never send emails with attachments, or request your X password by email … and will never ask you to provide your password via email, direct message or reply.”If you do click on a link in the email, you will be brought to a fake website designed to steal your password, or to authorise a scammer’s app to access your account directly, often under the guise of a tool for performing a “security audit” or “troubleshooting”.What to do“If you ever receive an email like this, it is very normal, but remember not to panic, and don’t click the links to divulge any personal data. Instead, open the genuine app, and if there really is a security issue, you’ll see it there,” Moore says.Check the email headers and URL links to ensure they are from the X.com domain. You can report fraudulent emails to your email provider using the built-in spam and phishing tools.“If you clicked on a link, and only opened the page, you’re probably fine,” Moore says. “But if you have ever entered your password, or a one-time passcode to a web address you didn’t check, change your password immediately and double-check you have two-factor authentication turned on.”If you suspect your X account has been compromised, follow thesocial media site’s help guide. The company says it may reset the passwords of any account it believes may have been hacked, and will send a secure link sent via email to select a new password.Explore more on these topicsScamsScam watchConsumer affairsXSocial mediafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Government use of automated AI decision-making to be curbed under new Australian rules | Australian politics | The Guardian
description: New national plan is accompanied by Labor push for digital duty of care legislation
Author: Tom McIlroy
Headline: New national plan is accompanied by Labor push for digital duty of care legislation
Content: Federal departments and agencies still use automated decision-making in some service delivery functions, prompting fears AI systems could replace more human-led processes.Photograph: Tom Merton/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFederal departments and agencies still use automated decision-making in some service delivery functions, prompting fears AI systems could replace more human-led processes.Photograph: Tom Merton/Getty ImagesAustralian politicsGovernment use of automated AI decision-making to be curbed under new Australian rulesNew national plan is accompanied by Labor push for digital duty of care legislationFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastTom McIlroyPolitical editorSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe use of AI in automated decision-making by government departments and agencies will be subject to tough rules under a new national plan, expected to extend to consumer protections, workplace safety and privacy.As the Albanese government grapples with the rapid growth in the use of artificial intelligence and a boom in datacentre construction, senior ministers have begun work to draw up new rules to ensure safety is built into AI processes inside government, prioritising fairness, accuracy and transparency.Federal departments and agencies continue to rely on automated decision-making in some service delivery functions, prompting fears increasingly sophisticated AI systems could replace more human-led processes.Though the saga ofthe illegal robodebt schemerevealed the unreliability of some automated processes, the rise of AI is already dramatically changing the way data is used across the economy.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailLabor said that the attorney general, Michelle Rowland, will lead development of the new rules – coming as AI giants including Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft and Google eye major developments in Australia.The government is also progressing legislation to create a digital duty of care, designed to put the onus on AI companies to build in safety features and proactively address potential harm.That work is being led by the communications minister, Anika Wells.Albanese’s AI speech was a good start. Now Australia must confront these bigger questions | Julianne SchultzRead moreA second round of reforms to privacy laws is also planned, billed as efforts to “responsibly strengthen, modernise and simplify Australia’s personal data protection laws”. It will include rules for personal data protection.The final element of a new work plan announced on Monday will include a focus on AI safety in the workplace.The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, used a major speech last week to announce plans fora new office of AI within his department, part of plans for a whole-of-government approach to managing and maximising opportunities from the new technological frontier.He promised “the strongest possible protection” for Australian creatives against misuse of their work and announced plans for rules around large energy-intensive datacentres.Expected to be legislated from next year, the national rules will govern where the massive facilities can be built.The assistant minister for science, technology and the digital economy, Andrew Charlton, said on Sunday that the pace of datacentre construction was already proving challenging for governments around the world.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“We already have more than 200 datacentres in Australia,” he told ABC TV.“Those datacentres consume electricity equivalent to about 2% of our energy grid and there are projections that could grow very rapidly over the next 10 years, potentially even triple over the next 10 years.“The government has been very clear that we are implementing this world-first AI datacentre standard, and that standard will make sure that local communities are supported, that they don’t face the negative impacts of these datacentres looming over them, being too close to houses, too close to schools, making sure they don’t push up power prices for Australians, making sure they don’t consume drinking water.”But the deputy Liberal leader, Jane Hume, told Sky that the government’s approach to regulating AI was insufficient, and described Albanese’s speech as lacking detial.“Talking about regulating AI by introducing rules for datacentres is a little bit like saying, ‘Well, I’ve regulated the fishing industry by setting up a licensing regime for building boats’,” she said.“There’s so much more to it than that.“Overregulating them could, in fact, be a problem because it’s not just about setting up certainty, it’s also about alternative opportunities.”Explore more on these topicsAustralian politicsAI (artificial intelligence)Anthony AlbaneseComputingLabor partynewsShareReuse this content

title: Victoria announces new social media ‘demasking’ powers for accounts accused of vilification | Victoria | The Guardian
description: New laws would give Vcat power to force social and AI platforms to identify anonymous users in move premier says will protect children
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: New laws would give Vcat power to force social and AI platforms to identify anonymous users in move premier says will protect children
Content: As well as the new ‘demasking’ power, the Victorian reforms would lower the legal threshold to sue social media and AI platforms for negligence causing psychiatric harm to children.Photograph: Aap Image/AAPView image in fullscreenAs well as the new ‘demasking’ power, the Victorian reforms would lower the legal threshold to sue social media and AI platforms for negligence causing psychiatric harm to children.Photograph: Aap Image/AAPVictoriaVictoria announces new social media ‘demasking’ powers for accounts accused of vilificationNew laws would give Vcat power to force social and AI platforms to identify anonymous users in move premier says will protect childrenGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastAustralian Associated PressSun 19 Jul 2026 03.22 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 02.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSocial media companies could be forced to identify anonymous accounts accused of online vilification, under new laws being proposed in Victoria.The Victorian premier, Jacinta Allan, announced a suite of social media reforms on Sunday, saying families needed new ways to protect their children online.But the opposition has suggested the laws might not see the light of day before November’s state election, despite Allan insisting they would be introduced as a priority.Under the proposed changes – the first for an Australian state – the Victorian civil and administrative tribunal (Vcat) would be granted “demasking” powers to order platforms to reveal the identities of account holders in those cases.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailThe state government will also lower the legal threshold to sue social media and artificial intelligence platforms for negligence causing psychiatric harm to children.Current laws require families to prove a child has suffered a permanent impairment of at least 10% before pursuing damages.These assessments are made by medical practitioners, who apply calculations to estimate how significant a given injury is.The changes would scrap that threshold for suits brought on behalf of minors, with further changes for adult victims to be considered.Marilyn Bromberg, an associate professor of law at the University of Western Australia who specialises in social media regulation, said the changes were a step in the right direction but should go further.So-called “demasking” powers in some overseas jurisdictions had been shown to discourage harmful behaviours in some cases, she told AAP.‘Enforcement mode’: Australia must take fight to tech giants to make social media ban stick, experts warnRead moreThere was no reason the reforms should not be extended to other forms of online behaviours, including defamation and cyberbullying, she said.“It’s a brave start, but I don’t think it goes far enough,” she said.Bromberg also welcomed moves to make it easier for families to pursue negligence claims.“The harms that social media can cause young people have been substantiated by compelling, peer-reviewed research,” she said.Stronger protections would help to build on other measures, including Australia’s world-leading minimum age requirements for social media, she said.Allan said the laws, which would build on Victoria’s existing anti-vilification regime, were being drafted.“We’re going to use every
