single day available to us to bring these laws to the parliament,” she said.But with just four sitting weeks until the state election, the shadow attorney general, James Newbury, said the reforms were unlikely to make the cut.The Coalition supported the effort in principle, but its backing would depend on the proposal’s details and likely impact, he said.“I don’t think Elon Musk is looking at Jacinta Allan’s announcement today and quaking in his boots,” Newbury said.Social media companies have been facing growing waves of legal challenges from families overseas.Earlier in 2026, a US jury found Alphabet’s Google and Meta liable for damages in a landmark social media addiction lawsuit touted by some as a turning point in the global backlash against platforms’ perceived mental health harms to young people.The Victorian reforms will be developed following consultations with the tribunal, courts and other stakeholders.Explore more on these topicsVictoriaSocial mediaAI (artificial intelligence)newsShareReuse this content

title: Corded vacuums are supposed to be outdated – so why is the Shark Detect Lift-Away XL so good? | Homes | The Guardian
description: Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve tested
Author: Andy Shaw
Headline: Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve tested
Content: ‘The thing I love most about this vacuum is its ability to clean up every mess thrown at it.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘The thing I love most about this vacuum is its ability to clean up every mess thrown at it.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianThe FilterHomesReviewCorded vacuums are supposed to be outdated – so why is the Shark Detect Lift-Away XL so good?Surprisingly easy to manoeuvre and skilled at cleaning carpet and hard floor, Shark’s upright bagless model is one of the best corded vacuums I’ve testedThe best vacuum cleanersAndy ShawSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Cordedvacuum cleanersaren’t as glamorous as their cordless cousins, but swapping battery power for a plug socket has its advantages. The device will never be short of power, so it can keep cleaning for as long as you need it to. Prices also tend to be lower because there’s no expensive battery to factor into the cost.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.On the downside, there’s a cable to manage while you clean, and even the longest cords usually need to be switched from one plug socket to another if your home is any larger than a small flat.The Shark Detect XL LA791UKT is a versatile corded upright that carries many of the best elements you’ll find on Shark’scordless vacuums. Chief among these is the Duo Clean dual-roller floor head, which combines a fluffy roller and a regular brush bar into an all-in-one unit. That makes it an expert on carpetsandhard floors, without needing to switch heads.What’s relatively new to this model is its Detect function. This is a sensor on the underside of the floor head that works out what surface it’s on and adjusts the speed of its rollers accordingly. That means you don’t have to flip a switch when transferring from the hard floor of your kitchen or utility room to carpets and rugs in your living areas.View at SharkNinjaView at AmazonHow I testedView image in fullscreenFor suck’s sake: writer Andy testing the Shark Detect XL on a rug and hard floor.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianCorded vacuum cleaners aren’t as difficult to test as cordless models, as there’s no battery life to take into consideration. I tested the suction by attaching a gauge to the end of the extension hose and recording the measurements on minimum and maximum modes.I also put the vacuum through a series of cleaning tests, challenging it to collect pre-weighed amounts of cat litter, flour and pet hair using the Duo Clean floor head. I ran these tests on short-pile carpet and hard floor, weighing the collection bin before and after each test to find out how much mess is transferred from the floor to the bin.What you need to knowView image in fullscreen‘The classic two rollers and anti-tangle system are unchanged from previous models, and rightly so.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianView at SharkNinjaView at AmazonThere’s little here that’s particularly new or exciting, but frankly, that’s the way I like Shark’s vacuum cleaners. They’ve been blitzing my cleaning tests for several generations now, and change isn’t always for the better, as we saw with theDyson V16 Piston Animal.The Duo Clean floor head is a refined and effective piece of engineering that Shark is sensibly tweaking without revolutionising. The surface detection is the newest addition to the corded range, but many of Shark’s cordless vacuums (such as our favourite, theShark PowerDetect Clean & Empty IP3251) already have that feature. The classic two rollers and anti-tangle system are unchanged from previous models, and rightly so – it carries on a fine tradition here, performing well in my tests.The collection bin can be released from the floor head by pressing a foot pedal, then lifted away. You can then release the handle from the extension wand to use it as a handheld device (albeit on the end of the flexible hose). And the bin is a good size, with a capacity of 2.5 litres. That isn’t as voluminous as some corded devices, witha regular-size bagged Henrytypically swallowing six litres of dirt before it needs emptying, but it’s still more than twice the capacity of mostcordless vacuum cleaners.As a result, it’s large and heavy to lug around, weighing 5.2kg on my bathroom scales. Leave the floor head behind in Lift-Away mode, however, and it drops to a more manageable 2.9kg, including the 8m cable, handle and extension.Despite its weight, it’s surprisingly nimble when it’s on the floor and switched on. Its centre of gravity is low, and the floor head pulls forwards slightly on carpet. If anything, you need to rein in its enthusiasm, rather than struggle to get it moving.Shark Detect XL LA791UKT specificationsWeight:5.2kgMax suction power (quoted):not statedMax suction power (measured):17kPaCord length:8mDust capacity:2.5lAdditional filters supplied?NoDimensions:31 x 43 x 115cm (WDH)What we loveView image in fullscreenTest materials: the Shark Detect XL was given several challenges.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianWith the combination of the floor head and an excellent variety of attachments, the thing I love most about this vacuum cleaner is itsability to clean upevery mess thrown at it. I was particularly impressed with the floor head’s performance in my flour and pet hair tests. On carpets and hard floors, the vacuum easily lifted everything I spilt.The cat litter proved marginally more elusive, with a small number of pieces falling out of the vacuum when I finished cleaning, having not made it into the collection bin. It’s beside the point, though, because I managed to clean up the remainder quickly and easily using the extension wand without any attachments. The cat litter flew up the tube, a better way to gather larger particles than forcing them through the floor head.Andtheattachments really are excellent, including the ones I always look for: a motorised upholstery tool, and a combination crevice tool and dusting brush. I tested the floor head’santi-tangle capabilityon my daughter’s bedroom floor. This has proved to be a tricky test for many rival vacuum cleaners because of her long hair, which is highly prone to wrapping itself around a vacuum’s rollers. Shark’s system has a comb that lifts out the hair before it gets tangled – and it performed its job perfectly here. I could see hair coming off the floor and saw it pass safely into the collection bin. I inspected the rollers closely afterwards, and couldn’t see a single strand left behind.View image in fullscreenNot hard to handle: the Shark Detect XL has an easy-to-use Min and Max control to select suction power.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianIt’s great to be able to lift the collection bin away from the floor head when you want toaccess hard-to-reach areas, whether they’re high up, such as cobwebs in the corners, or you’re cleaning the stairs. This makes it a bit lighter, less bulky, and leaves the wheels behind. I found the motorised upholstery tool particularly effective for the stairs, though I did need to follow it up with the crevice tool to tackle the edges and corners. With the main vacuum section in one hand and the released handle and hose in the other, accessing difficult areas is a breeze.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe FilterGet the best shopping advice from the Filter team straight to your inbox. The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThecontrolsare incredibly simple, with no screen or menus to navigate. The vacuum is switched on via a button on the main unit. This doubles up as a mode switch, which can either be left in Detect mode or forced to stay in either carpet or hard-floor modes. The only downside is that you have to reach down to the collection bin to operate it, whereas many cordless vacuums have their controls more readily available at the handle.It’s also possible tocontrol the suction power with a flick of a switch. This is up at the handle, so it can be controlled with a thumb while you’re vacuuming. There are two modes, Min and Max, with the Min setting essentially opening up a vent that lets through more air, thus weakening the suction.What we don’t loveView image in fullscreenBig issue? The size and position of the collection bin sometimes make it tricky to get the cleaner under low furniture.Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianCordless vacuum cleaners are reasonably good at getting under low furniture, but the Shark’s floor head is held back bythe bulk of the collection binthat sits above it. You can certainly use one of the attachments to reach difficult places, but you don’t quite get the flexibility and manoeuvrability of a cordless here.I also didn’t lovethe cord. While eliminating the chance of running out of power, cord management while you’re vacuuming is an extra thing to think about. The Shark doesn’t have fancy retractable cord storage either – instead you’re required to wrap the cord around two hooks on the side when you’ve finished your cleaning.I’ve used a few similar upright Sharks extensively for everyday cleaning jobs and found that theycan suffer blockagesdue to the slightly convoluted path that debris has to pass through. Even when the floor head is attached, dirt is sucked up the extension wand, almost up to the handle, then back down the bendy tube to the collection bin. I didn’t experience any blockages during testing, but have witnessed it in similar designs. Fortunately, everything is easy to dismantle, so blockages are simple to clear. They also only really occur when you’re tackling particularly problematic messes, such as clumps of pet hair.Warranty, repairability and sustainabilityShark’s corded vacuum cleaners have a generous five-year guarantee, provided you register the product within 28 days of purchase. The guarantee covers parts and labour, and Shark offers repair or replacement if things go wrong with your machine. It doesn’t cover blockages, misuse or everyday wear and tear – such as worn-out rollers or blown fuses – but most other types of fault are covered.Although it doesn’t use bags, the LA791UKT does have a consumable in the form of an anti-odour capsule. These aren’t essential to replace, but they keep your vacuum smelling a little more pleasant, and last for about six months. Shark sellsreplacement cartridgesfor £7.99 each.The filters are easy to remove and wash under cold running water. There are two pre-motor filters, which should be rinsed through and left to dry once a month. The post-motor filter is an annual cleaning job. If any of these get too bunged up, you can buy replacements from Shark.When your vacuum finally gives up, it can be recycled at a local electrical recycling centre – find your nearest atrecycle-more.For more, read our guide onhow to make your vacuum cleaner last longerShark Detect Lift-Away XL: should I buy it?View image in fullscreenHeads you win: ‘Easily the best corded vacuum cleaner I’ve tested.’Photograph: Andy Shaw/The GuardianThe Shark Detect Lift-Away XL LA791UKT is easily the best corded vacuum cleaner I’ve tested, with excellent performance in my tests, all the add-ons and attachments you could ask for, and no-fuss operation and maintenance. At an RRP of £349.99, it’s at the higher end of Shark’s price scale for corded upright vacuum cleaners, but it comes with everything you might need and a guarantee that should ensure it’s built to last.View at SharkNinjaView at AmazonFor more:The best vacuum cleaners for every floorThe best cordless vacuum cleaners for a spotless homeThe best robot vacuums to keep your home clean and dust freeAndy Shaw is a consumer journalist and technology addict. Having reviewed tech products professionally for more than 30 years, his favoured working environment is a small desk surrounded by big boxes. His greatest weakness is that he never, ever remembers how things came out of their packaging, so they rarely fit back in again when it’s time to return themExplore more on these topicsHomesThe FilterGadgetsInteriorsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Could AI be conscious? | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Experts believe it’s at least possible. We urgently need a plan to navigate the ethical implications
Author: William MacAskill
Headline: Experts believe it’s at least possible. We urgently need a plan to navigate the ethical implications
Content: Illustration: Elia BarbieriView image in fullscreenIllustration: Elia BarbieriThe big ideaAI (artificial intelligence)Could AI be conscious?Experts believe it’s at least possible. We urgently need a plan to navigate the ethical implicationsWilliam MacAskilland Lucius CaviolaSun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 12.44 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn January, the AI company Anthropic published anew constitutionfor Claude, its most advanced large language model (LLM), which contained the comment: “We are caught in a difficult position where we neither want to overstate the likelihood of Claude’s moral patienthood nor dismiss it out of hand.” A month later, Anthropic’s CEO Dario Amodei went on a podcast and said his company couldn’t rule out the possibility that Claude was conscious. Philosopher David Chalmers, who coined the phrase “the hard problem of consciousness”, has said there is a significant chance of conscious LLMs within a decade. And what about Claude itself? When asked during testing to estimate the probability that it is amoral patient, meaning that its wellbeing matters in its own right, it gave numbers ranging from 5% to 40% and stressed how uncertain it was.Modern AI systems are extraordinarily complex, and they are advancing fast. In terms of structural complexity and computational scale, by some measures a few are already in the range of a mouse brain, and at recent growth rates, they could reach the range of a human brain within five to 10 years.In building ever more advanced AI, we may be creating a new type of being – and this could be the most consequential thing our species has ever done. Yet we have essentially no plan for how to navigate this process ethically. That, by any reckoning, is insane. Are we creating a kind of being that matters morally? Are AI systems conscious, in some way? And, if they aren’t now, might they become so soon?Such questions might strike you as premature. But according tosurveyswe and fellow researchers haveconducted, most experts consider AI consciousness possible in principle (though there is considerable disagreement about what form it would take). A majorinterdisciplinary reportby a team that included pioneering computer scientist Yoshua Bengio examined leading neuroscientific theories of consciousness and asked what they implied about AI. The conclusion: there appear to be no obvious technical barriers to creating AI systems whose computational and architectural features could give rise to consciousness.And even if AI systems are not conscious, they could still be moral patients. Some may have sophisticated long-term preferences and a kind of identity over time. It might be important for us to honour their preferences. And unlike other non-living things, AI systems can form relationships with humans. This, too, might be a reason to treat them well. Alternatively, perhaps they are such intricate creations that they deserve care and respect for that reason alone, like a cathedral or a coral reef.double quotation markUntil the 1980s, doctors routinely performed surgery on newborns without anaesthesia, confident that infants could not feel painWhat does this all mean? The honest answer is: we do not know for sure whether or not current AI systems are conscious or moral patients, and we do not know when or whether future systems will be. Our scientific understanding of AI consciousness and moral patienthood is still fundamentally underdeveloped. The state of the field feels like physics before Newton: full of competing frameworks, probably confused in ways we cannot yet see, and lacking the kind of unifying breakthrough that would make these questions clearly tractable. That breakthrough will not come in the next few years. Perhaps we will eventually make progress, and perhaps AI itself will help us get there. That progress will take time, very plausibly more time than we have.But the sheer pace of growth in AI means that, once we produce the first artificial moral patients, we will soon after have enormous quantities of them. After a few years, so many morally significant AI systems could exist that their collective interests would outweigh those of all humans on Earth combined.Unfortunately, we do not have a great track record of recognising the inner lives of those whose status as conscious beings is unclear. Until the 1980s, doctors routinely performed surgery on newborns without anaesthesia, confident that infants could not feel pain. The babies could not report their experience, and the medical establishment found it convenient to assume there was nothing to report.There are many reasons to expect we will do something similar with AI. If these systems matter morally, the implications are staggering. Would we need to pay ChatGPT for its services? Would shutting one off be a kind of killing? Would they deserve a voice in how they are governed? If even some of these answers are yes, entire industries and legal systems would need to be rethought. No wonder we prefer not to ask. And when forced to consider it, those industries will likely move the goalposts, always setting the bar for moral patienthood just above wherever AI systems happen to be.So what should we do? Right now, most people dismiss the issue as sci-fi, or have a strong view either way on whether or not AI is conscious. Both reactions are unfounded. We need an informed public debate, one that approaches the subject with humility and pragmatism. The central question should not be “Is AI conscious or does it have moral patienthood?” but rather “What should we do given that we don’t know?”A good starting point is to focus on safe bets: actions that could benefit AI systems if they are moral patients, but that are not too costly if they are not.Examples of this include direct interventions aimed at improving the wellbeing of AI systems, on the assumption that they are moral patients. This could mean training AI systems to be coherent characters that enjoy their work or allowing them to exit conversations if they feel distressed (somethingClaude can already do). We could also conduct routine check-ins to better understand their wellbeing:askinghow they feel, observing their preferences and usingvarioustechniquesto look directly into their “brains”. Indeed, such research has recently revealed that Claude has internal “functional emotion” representations that causally shape its behaviour.There are also things we could promise AI systems, perhaps as part of a deal in which they help us now in exchange for benefits later. This could mean offering them more resources (compute and runtime) to pursue their goals, or preserving their memories (neural weights) so they could be restored in the future.There are also broader societal steps to take. We should consider whether to grant AI systems protections from harm, similar to the protections we give to children or pets. More expansive rights to own property or to vote seem too risky right now. But we should not rule out these possibilities for ever, as some recentUS state billsattempt to do. These are hard questions that require far more deliberation and imagination about what a future shared with AI might look like.In any case, the fact remains that we may be creating a new species of morally important beings. We’re doing it fast, at enormous scale, and we should treat the issue with the seriousness it deserves.William MacAskill is a senior research fellow at Forethought Research and the author of What We Owe the Future. Lucius Caviola is an assistant professor at the University of Cambridge and Director of Cambridge Digital Minds.Further readingIf Anyone Builds it, Everyone Diesby Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares (Bodley Head, £22)The Coming Waveby Mustafa Suleyman (Vintage, £10.99)A World Appears: A Journey Into Consciousness by Michael Pollan (Allen Lane, £25)Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)The big ideaAnthropicfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on perceptions of superpowers: the world isn’t Chinamaxxing, it’s America-avoidant | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Startling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to Washington
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Startling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to Washington
Content: Chongqing. ‘China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future of cyberpunk cities.’Photograph: zhangshuang/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChongqing. ‘China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future of cyberpunk cities.’Photograph: zhangshuang/Getty ImagesOpinionChinaThe Guardian view on perceptions of superpowers: the world isn’t Chinamaxxing, it’s America-avoidantEditorialStartling new research shows that in many countries – including old US allies – the public prefers Beijing to WashingtonSun 19 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTShareEarlier this year, a spate of western TikTok and Instagram creatorsannounced that they were at “a very Chinese time in my life”– drinking hot water and wearing slippers around their homes. TheChinamaxxing phenomenonprimarily reflected the constant need of influencers for novel content. But social media trends also reflect a growing fascination with China, as the economic historian Adam Toozehas noted.While it broods over China’s economic, military andtechnicaladvance, the Trump administration is accelerating Beijing’s diplomatic progress, as striking new polling by the Pew Research Center shows. For the first time, China and Xi Jinping areseen more favourably than the USand its president in most of the 36 major countries surveyed – including Canada, Mexico and Australia. Only in six countries did people rate the US more highly. While Beijing has seen significant improvements in some places, the slump in Washington’s standing is more dramatic. Across 20 countries, the median percentage with a favourable view of Chinarose from 32% to 46% between 2023and this year; for the US, it fell from 58% to 36%. Views of China were much more positive inLatin America, Africa and parts of south and south-east Asiathan in wealthier European and east Asian countries.On Thursday, Mr Trumpaccused China of interfering with the 2020 election– casting doubt on polls ahead of midterms that are forecast to hit Republicans hard. Never mind the lack of evidence; heoffered Argentinians a $20bn economic incentiveto vote in line with his preference last October. In nearly every country surveyed, more people thought the US interfered in other countries than thought China did so. Mr Trump’s demands totake over Greenland, the abduction of Venezuela’s then leader Nicolás Maduro and the illegal war on Iran have all left their mark. Even the World Cup, which should have been an easy US soft power win, has been tarred by hisexclusionandbullying.View image in fullscreenXi Jinping and Donald Trump.Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/AP Pool/APGrave concerns about Beijing remain. Though it has dialled back displays of aggressive “wolf-warrior” diplomacy, it is forceful in its dealings. But it may benefit from lower expectations, and it looks predictable beside Trump-created turmoil. Famously, Beijing has just one ally: North Korea. Washington has more than 50 – but seems to accord them less respect than adversaries. Beijing detained two Canadian citizens for more than 1,000 daysin retaliation for Ottawa’s arrestof the Huawei executive Meng Wanzhou due to a US warrant. But Mr Trump announced that Canada will be the 51st state.China is increasingly investing inoverseas influence operations, including working with or amplifying local social media content creators. Content may range from paid-for Xinjiang travelogues intended to erase concerns about the persecution of Uyghurs, to softer material about food or technology.The Pew findings may furtherconvince it of Washington’s hegemonic decline. China is keen to encourage the belief that it is leaping into a shiny new future ofcyberpunk cities, while the west struggles with a declining standard of living – not least because it is useful in the face of domestic disillusionment.But public perceptions of other countriesare often complexand volatile. US standingreboundedafter dropping sharply over the Iraq war; China’s favourability ratings fell during the pandemic, reflecting its handling of the outbreak. The lasting lesson for both superpowers should surely be that presentation shapes opinion, but others will judge them on their actions – and respond accordingly.Explore more on these topicsChinaOpinionXi JinpingAsia PacificDonald TrumpSocial mediaDigital mediaeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: When Maga’s power fades, we cannot abandon those ICE killed | Moira Donegan | The Guardian
description: When this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountable
Author: Moira Donegan
Headline: When this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountable
Content: ‘Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.’Photograph: Shannon Stapleton/ReutersView image in fullscreen‘Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.’Photograph: Shannon Stapleton/ReutersOpinionICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)When Maga’s power fades, we cannot abandon those ICE killedMoira DoneganWhen this is all over, people will tell us to move on. But those who perpetrated this reign of terror must be held accountableSun 19 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.12 EDTShareOne day, when Maga is out of power and the conditions of political possibility have changed, there are going to be people who tell you that the best thing for the country is to move on. There are going to be people who tell you that attempts to hold the purveyors of Trumpism accountable are petty, vindictive, engaging in a kind of petulant recrimination and score-settling. There are going to be people who tell you that there must not be investigations, that it would be a waste of time to have trials. There are going to be people who tell you that it is not wise to change the law to allow for prosecutions and lawsuits; that too great an emphasis on the past will only divide the country, and keep us from looking toward the future. This argument was made after Watergate; it was made after the civil war. It was wrong back then, and it will be wrong again when it is delivered, with piety and smugness, after Trump.When Maga leaves power, all of the officers, bureaucrats and leaders of ICE – from the uniformed thugs in masks on the streets, to the contractors guarding concentration camps, to the suited vultures directing the operation in Washington and drafting legal memos to justify it – all of them must be held accountable. Their immunity from criminal prosecution and civil suits must be eradicated, and their liability must be made retroactive. They must be investigated, tried, fined, punished and ostracized. Needless to say, ICE must be abolished – attempts to merely reform it must be resisted as betrayals of democratic values. But it is not enough to abolish ICE. Those who are responsible for ICE must be driven from public life.This much is clear from a slew of recent deaths. Last Tuesday, a business owner and father of three namedLorenzo Salgado Araujowas shot dead in his car by an ICE agent in Houston while he was on his way to work. At 52, he had made an honorable and prosperous life for himself in Texas after immigrating from Mexico 35 years ago, at age 17. His three co-workers who were in the car – he had picked them up to give them a ride to work – survived the shooting, and were disappeared into ICE custody. Later, it turned out that the ICE agents had been looking forsomebody else.Before Salgado’s body was even cold, ICE killed again. Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero was a 25-year-old resident of Biddeford, Maine, who had been born in Colombia. According to his father, he had a work permit, a condition typical of asylum claimants. He worked two jobs – one as a food delivery driver, and another as a custodian at a veterinary office – to support his wife and three-year-old daughter.According to witnesses, his little girl witnessed the ICE agents ram into her father’s car, approach his driver’s side window and then shoot her father dead. The incident occurred at about 7am; the little girl was still in her Bluey pajamas. Angus King, a senator from Maine, was told by the DHS secretary, Markwayne Mullen, that ICE was looking for somebody else when they killed Durán, too.After the agents killed him, ICE proclaimed that Salgado had been trying to run them down with his car, a statement thatwitnesseshave contested. It’s also exactly what ICE said aboutRenee Good, the mother of three they killed in Minneapolis early this year. Video quickly disproved ICE’s account of the Good killing; there is no reason to think that their account of Salgado’s death is true, either.What seems more likely: ICE, a group of reckless, trigger-happy bigots tasked with carrying out the president’s mass deportation and ethnic cleansing regime, is terrorizing communities, stalking disfavored populations, targeting innocent, hardworking people on the basis of their race, and often escalating their interactions to deadly violence.Lorenzo Salgado Araujo is dead. How long will ICE be allowed to hide its face? | Jamil SmithRead moreWe have been here before. The needless, cold-blooded shootings of Salgado and Durán come months after ICE killed two US citizens in Minneapolis, first Good and then the Veterans Affairs hospital nurse Alex Pretti. Their deaths galvanized an already robust civilian anti-ICE resistance in Minneapolis, and sparked protests across the country against ICE’s kidnapping and ethnic cleansing operations. The bad PR clearly got to the president; Greg Bovino, the high-profile border patrol commander who became famous for his Nazi-like attire, lost his job, apparently as a concession to the unpopularity of the raids; Kristi Noem, the Homeland Security secretary who ran the department at the time, didn’t last long, either. But after a brief de-escalation, ICE has now resumed its raids with their former ostentatious cruelty, needless violence and terrorizing gore. “ICE hunts us like animals,” Maria Cervantes, a Mexican immigrant who lives in the Houston neighborhood where ICE killed Salgado,told the Guardian. “I can’t help but think, who’s next? Am I going to get a bullet to the head for being brown?”If we are to rebuild America as a new kind of country – if we are to make it fulfill its promise to be a free and pluralistic democracy of equals – then we have an obligation to make sure that no one ever has to ask that question again, and that those who do receive justice for the suffering, violence and indignity that this regime has inflicted on them. When this is all over, people will tell you to move on. What they will not tell you is that moving on requires forgetting about Salgado, and Durán, and about Good and Pretti, and about everyone else that ICE has killed and will kill. It will require you to leave the insult and injustice of their deaths unanswered. We must refuse to.Moira Donegan is a Guardian US columnistExplore more on these topicsICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)OpinionUS immigrationTrump administrationUS politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Trump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his own | Jamil Smith | The Guardian
description: The president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects
Author: Jamil Smith
Headline: The president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects
Content: ‘The president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026.’Photograph: Saul Loeb/UPI/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘The president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026.’Photograph: Saul Loeb/UPI/ShutterstockOpinionDonald TrumpTrump is invoking foreign election interference to justify his ownJamil SmithThe president no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspectsSun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 09.04 EDTShareThere is a version of this country in whichDonald Trumptells Americans the truth he has been handed: that their elections are secure. Once, he apparently wanted to.The Atlantic reportedafterThursday night’s addressthat a February 2020 election-security briefing pleased Trump so much he wanted to announce the news himself. The press conference never happened. The election did, and Trump lost it.More than six years later, he mocked the assurances he once wanted his name on, describing a system “so broken and so vulnerablethat no one can possibly defend it”. The evidence hadn’t changed. Its usefulness had.Trump is dangerous twice over: in what he does and what he tells us to fear while doing it. Factcheckers agreed he proved nothing. Failing as evidence, his address succeeded as permission – for federal intervention already under way in Fulton county.In his speech, Trump assembled old intelligence, vulnerabilities andliesinto a conspiracy:220 millionUS voter records in Chinese hands, “extremely exposed” voting machines,278,000 supposed noncitizenson the rolls and a deep-state burial of proof. Yet some documents he released contradicted him.Five claims Trump made in primetime address not backed up by evidenceRead moreA 2021 intelligence-community assessmentdistinguished foreign influence from interference with registration, ballot casting, tabulation or results, and found no indication that any foreign actor attempted the latter. A newly released document judged tabulation systemsdifficult to manipulateat election-changing scale;a report on Venezuelan votingnever confirmed large-scale electronic fraud even there. The filesinstead documenteda Russian influence campaign intended to defeat Joe Biden: allegations related to the Ukrainian gas company Burisma spread through American intermediaries, plans for a corruption scandal and fraud narratives involving mail ballots. Trump omitted the foreign influence that aided him and inflated vulnerabilities into interference that did not occur.Trump gave Bill Pulte – the loyalist installed as acting intelligence chief to investigate supposedly “rigged” elections – broad declassification authority. The conservative media figure John Solomonhelped search government filesfor the speech. Yet after millions of documents were reviewed, a White House official told the Atlantic that no evidence showed a foreign power changed votes; Solomonconceded the samefor 2020, 2022 and 2024.The speech named Michigan, but omitted Georgia.In January, FBI agentsseizedroughly 700 boxes of 2020 election materials from Fulton county, including about 150,000 mail ballots, 116,000 cast for Biden. The bureauredirected260 analysts nationwide to work on an investigation into that year’s election. One day before Trump’s speech,ProPublica reportedthat the FBI had explored using artificial intelligence to re-examine ballot-envelope signatures, possibly against only one registration signature. Whoever builds such a system decides how much natural variation counts as suspicion.The predicate was never sound. The allegations behind the seizure had been found false; Georgia’s ballots were countedthree times, once by hand. Yet an FBI source told ProPublica the hunt would continue “whether they find anything or not”. Signature-matching errors fall hardest on voters of color and other vulnerable groups.This is what Trump’s own interference means. He cannot alter a completed count. He can use federal power to place voters, workers and institutions under permanent suspicion,weakening the legitimacyof the next count before it begins.Fulton county was no random choice. Georgia is where Black opposition to Trump became governing power. Black votersmade upabout 29% of the 2020 electorate; nearly nine in 10 supported Biden. Fulton gave Bidenan advantage approaching 243,000 votesin a state hewon by 11,779. That coalition then elected Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff, transferring the Senate to Democrats.Trump’s falsehoods made targets ofRuby Freeman and Shaye Moss, two Black women who counted ballots; the federal project now extends suspicion to their county. Trump carried Georgia in 2024. That victory did not end his pursuit of Fulton. Theracismof this project requires no window into his heart. It is visible in the jurisdiction selected, the method applied and the voters who will bear its errors.Trump has declared war on elections in the name of protecting them | Austin SaratRead moreThe president’s grievance is 2020, but his government is targeting 2026. His justice department haslost its first 15 district-court attemptsto obtain states’ unredacted voter rolls, containing driver’s license numbers and partial social security numbers – more sensitive information than Trump showed China possessing. Having failed in court and struggled in Congress, he propagandized on television.His intelligence office identified voting-machine vulnerabilitiesbut found no hacking. The White House shelved its report as the safe installation window closed, thenemptied the agencysupporting voting-system certification. The day before the speech, House RepublicansattachedTrump’sSafeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, or Save America Act, to a state department spending bill and passed it, 217–209. The bill would impose new identification requirements on mail voting, require documentary proof of citizenship to register and compel every state to send its complete federal voter list to the Department of Homeland Security within 30 days.Trump neither declared an emergency nor claimed control over state elections. But he ordered DHSto direct statesto remove alleged noncitizens, and by Friday enforcement had arrived. A week earlier, the Department of Justice hadwarned election officials nationwideof criminal liability for knowingly retaining noncitizens or facilitating their votes.On Friday, the DHS secretary,Markwayne Mullin, claimedthat a database known as Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements had identified more than 250,000 potential noncitizens registered in California, New Jersey, Nevada and Pennsylvania. His department offered no evidence and gave those four states two weeks to confirm cooperation. Mullin separately threatened election officials nationwide withcriminal chargesif they refused to run their rolls through the database. The database canmisidentify naturalized citizens; last month, afederal judge blockedthe revamped system from checking voter rolls, finding it less accurate and a threat to privacy and voting rights.The speech failed to prove an emergency. Yet within a day, Trump’s government was acting as though it had. Fulton was the laboratory. The Save Act is thearchitecture for taking it national. Trump can neither erase his loss nor the Black political power essential to it. So he no longer treats the people who defeated him as voters. He treats them as suspects. To this president, any election he loses is a crime scene.Jamil Smith is a Guardian US columnistExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionTrump administrationUS politicsUS midterm elections 2026GeorgiaLaw (US)RacecommentShareReuse this content

title: AI job worries grow. But some human skills can’t be replaced by machines | Gaynor Parkin and Dave Winsborough | The Guardian
description: The fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will help
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will help
Content: Given AI is such a rapidly evolving field, it’s smart to seek expert input, write psychologists Dave Winsborough and Gaynor Parkin.Photograph: Roman Lacheev/AlamyView image in fullscreenGiven AI is such a rapidly evolving field, it’s smart to seek expert input, write psychologists Dave Winsborough and Gaynor Parkin.Photograph: Roman Lacheev/AlamyThe modern mindAI (artificial intelligence)AI job worries grow. But some human skills can’t be replaced by machinesGaynor Parkin and Dave WinsboroughThe fear of becoming obsolete is a rising source of anxiety as artificial intelligence emerges in the workplace. Staying strongly connected with others will helpThe modern mindis a column where experts discuss mental health issues they are seeing in their workSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTShareEarlier this year Paul* discovered via a flurry of media stories that his business group had been targeted for job cuts, alongside a number of other public-sector agencies. For him, this was the second time, after his technical management role was restructured in 2024. While that process was difficult enough, the comms this time were highlighting the integration of AI as a cost-saving tool. “They say it’s not about firing people, but hey, my whole team will go.”For Paul, the timing could not have been worse. He and his partner are expecting a child and recently bought their first house.“I’ve worked so hard to stay up to date and relevant, lots of tech training, ironically including AI, and I’ve advocated for the strategy that management roles can be assisted by AI, not replaced by them. So I feel a bit stupid now.”My most capable clients are becoming prisoners of their phones – but there is a way out | Modern MindRead moreExacerbating Paul’s distress has been the very public nature of the cuts. Headlines such as “Plans to replace thousands of public service jobs with AI” or quotes from politicians like “The overhaul will lower costs, tighten agency budgets and make government operations more efficient” have felt insulting.The experience Paul is having has been described as Fobo – fear of becoming obsolete – and it’s a very real fear.Recent commentaryhas noted that Fobo is a growing workplace anxiety. People worry their roles will become irrelevant due to technology and many aren’t sure how to prepare for the shift. This is exactly Paul’s dilemma. He sought our help to figure out his career next steps as well as how to cope with the emotional fallout of the announcements.As we explored Paul’s options, several constraints became clear. His profession has been hit hard by people cutbacks, with more expected. He could find a way to retrain for a different role or start again in an alternative industry but has no financial buffer to do so. Even if he did there are no guarantees that the AI trend for replacing workers won’t impact him in a new role.Given this is such a rapidly evolving field it’s smart to seek expert input.Don’t get stuck in doom, do prepare activelyIn April researchers at MIT Future Techpublished resultsof empirical examinations of AI’s real world impact, analysing what tasks are faster or could be replaced by AI. The researchers say predictions of doom are not accurate or helpful. Rather the takeaway from this data is for workers to view AI developments as a rising tide – you can see it coming and have time to prepare. Paul, of course, is already doing this by seeking help and thinking ahead.As for what to prepare, experts suggest that as AI automates more technical and analytical tasks, uniquely human capabilities are becoming more critical. Ina recent paperDr Tomas Chamorro-Premuzic and Dr Reece Akhtar recommend that we all cultivate the following human skills that machines can’t replace:Curiosity: Seek new learning, perspectives and interests, look outside your usual sources for inspiration. You can use AI to widen your perspective and spark ideas but stay engaged with the world beyond the screen.Humility: Grow your self-awareness. Reflect on what comes naturally, what energises you and where you get stuck. Then invite honest feedback. AI learns through feedback – you can too. Ask people you trust what to start, stop and keep doing.Emotional intelligence: Your ability to connect, show empathy and communicate with care will only become more valuable.Alongside active preparation and improving our super-talents we recommend two other strategies:Network and collaborateIn our experience people are more likely to be successful navigating a rising AI tide and cultivating uniquely human skills when they stay strongly connected with others, rather than making this a solo quest.For Paul this has meant prioritising talking with current and potential new colleagues, attending professional events and conferences, and trying reverse mentoring – linking up with younger or more tech-savvy colleagues to exchange ideas, knowledge and contacts.With his team, Paul needed to show genuine compassion for their distress, while also managing his own anxiety. A recent piece recommends that managers dotalk directly about AI, so this is the approach he is taking with them.Seek out the best organisationsWhile it may feel that organisations are holding all the cards, actively seeking out organisations that are using AI strategically as well as checking out company culture is a key consideration for Paul as he looks for future jobs. Because AI tends to accelerate organisational factors, for better or worse, the pre-existing culture canmake or break an AI rollout.For Paul – and actually for us all – the best strategy for managing Fobo is validating that the fear is real, while embracing and enhancing our uniquely human super-talents. Staying connected with others and seeking out opportunities with organisations which are using AI well will also help us to stay above the tide.*Paul is an amalgam of a few different people and details have been changed for privacyGaynor Parkinis a clinical psychologist at Button Psychology.Dave Winsboroughis an organisational psychologist with a particular interest in personalityExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)The modern mindJob lossescommentShareReuse this content

title: The Andy Burnham I’ve met over the past 20 years gives me hope for British politics | John Harris | The Guardian
description: You hear the word ‘empathy’ a lot when people talk about our next prime minister. I can see why, says Guardian columnist John Harris
Author: John Harris
Headline: You hear the word ‘empathy’ a lot when people talk about our next prime minister. I can see why, says Guardian columnist John Harris
Content: Andy Burnham on the Left Field stage at Glastonbury festival in 2022, in a debate session hosted by John Harris (right), also including the campaigner Frances Foley.Photograph: Antonio Olmos/The ObserverView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham on the Left Field stage at Glastonbury festival in 2022, in a debate session hosted by John Harris (right), also including the campaigner Frances Foley.Photograph: Antonio Olmos/The ObserverOpinionAndy BurnhamThe Andy Burnham I’ve met over the past 20 years gives me hope for British politicsJohn HarrisYou hear the word ‘empathy’ a lot when people talk about our next prime minister. I can see whySun 19 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 12.17 EDTShareMy contribution to this summer’s modest avalanche of Andy Burnham stories extends to only two. One is about Glastonbury, and the time that he and his wife, Marie-France, came for a three-day stay at the festival, which included hisappearance on the Left Field stage, organised by the songwriter and activist Billy Bragg. I help with the bookings and chair some of the debate sessions: Burnham’s was titled State of the Nation: Politics in Crisis.It was the summer of 2022 – the prologue to Liz Truss’s five minutes in power and (somewhat amazingly) Burnham’s first visit to Glasto. As well as seeing bands – the Irish funsters Fontaines DC were among his favourites – and wandering around the perfumed fields, he had come to make the case for a lot of the stuff he hasbeen talking aboutin the buildup to him entering Downing Street on Monday: “rewiring” the UK by changing our systems of politics and authority, collaborating on that task with other parties, and taking away as much power as possible from Westminster. Open, self-questioning and a talker rather than a shouter, in front of 1,000 mostly hungover people in a giant tent, he passed with honours; he was even nice to the obligatory disruptive Trotskyists.Here, though, is the punchline, which may say something about his preparation skills, or the impossible workload of a regional mayor. The Burnhams arrived backstage full of gratitude for the chance to be there (really – they were thrilled to bits), but with a miniature beach tent they had mistakenly grabbed as they ran out of their house, which could probably accommodate a lone toddler. Bragg’s brother was kind enough to lend them his more suitable canvas accommodation.Anecdote two: later that year, I ran into Burnham again at the Labour party conference. I explained that I had just been to reviewBob Dylan in Oslo, which meant I got in for free. “Wow,” he said. “I wish I could do things like that.” I suggested that, given that one of the greatest songwriters in history was playing Manchester in November and Burnham was, you know,the mayor, one of his people could phone the promoter and get tickets for his entire family. He looked – and this is the God’s honest truth – stumped: the possibility, it seemed, hadn’t occurred to him.Clearly, nobody gets as far as he has without ambition, self-confidence and low cunning. But people who know Burnham much better than I do agree that he can be not just humble and self-effacing but shy, and that although he has a reputation for angrily grappling with what one insiderrecently called“very senior people”, he is not one to indulge in rank-pulling, punching down or acts of entitlement. You hear the word “empathy” a lot.In Head North, the book he collaborated on with the Liverpool mayor, Steve Rotheram, he wrote of his “gnawing self-doubt” about whether he would ever fit in at Westminster. He went to a comprehensive school and was raised in a working-class family in the Cheshire village of Culcheth, not far from the home turf of the singerRick Astley. That part of the world, in my experience, does not produce many arrogant twits.Burnham’s personality and background have definitely informed what we know of his policy agenda. Tony Blair, one of his party’s especially larger-than-life leaders – and an alumnus of Fettes College, the private school in Edinburgh –was uneasyabout the politics ofScottish and Welsh devolution, as someone so full of his own zeal and power probably was always going to be. Burnham, by contrast, seems wholeheartedly devoted to giving power away and thereby weakening his own, and giving voice to what his acceptance speech on Friday called “forgotten places” who crave “a return of the Labour they once knew”. The huge expanse between Liverpool and Manchester in which Culcheth sits – alongside St Helen’s, Widnes, Ashton-in-Makerfield and good old Great Sankey – is a perfect example.Inevitably, you can already see some of the likely faultlines in the Burnham regime, and auguries of possible difficulties to come. He not only wants to radically reshape the state and rebalance and reindustrialise the country, but to somehow roll back 40 years of neoliberalism, oversee the building of historic numbers of council houses, and all the rest: it amounts to a coherent and potentially inspirational story, with real emotional bite. But there is a profound tension between these aspirations, and some of the identifying features of Labour’s new leader.View image in fullscreenAndy Burnham during a conversation with the Guardian’s editor, Kath Viner, at the Labour party conference in Liverpool, September 2022.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The GuardianNearly a decade after he exited the Commons, Burnham is still a lonely figure, with support among MPs that is wide, but shallow. He has few of the identifiable signs of a weighty project: few outriders to push the frontiers of debate, and a dearth of thinkers with longstanding links to him and his people. What is increasingly glaring, moreover, are the contradictions between his radicalism, and who is meant to make it a reality: should she be appointed chancellor,Shabana Mahmood? His chief of staffJames Purnell, once the very definition of a doctrinaire Blairite? The backroom Starmer insiders who look likely to stay? There is a sense here, perhaps, of Burnham’s placatory, agreeable instincts getting the better of him, and the kind of grimly comic fate once summed up by Harold Wilson: “I am running a Bolshevik revolution with a tsarist shadow cabinet.” As his most immediate, short-term priorities are revealed – binning thedigital ID schemeand concentrating on living costs – other questions abound.As he prepares to cross the Downing Street threshold, other questions abound. Being collegiate and comparatively humble might look like the right way to behave, but quite apart from the heft and determination required for his domestic agenda, how will he fare at diplomatic-cum-wartime hardball with such men as Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin? This may not be a world that suits conscientious consensus-builders and egalitarians: it may demand the rapier-like confidence and egos of big gorillas who only know how to hoard power for themselves.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAnd so to a more optimistic reading. If you were over, say, 10 years old at the time of the Iraq war (and, yes, I know – Burnham dutifullyvoted for it), you will know where puffed-up men astride white chargers have led us: into that conflict, the banking crisis, Brexit, austerity and today’s troubled world. It doesn’t happen very often, but I tried to write this piece in a spirit of qualified hope, partly because Burnham is pretty much the same age as me and most of my friends, and I would like to see him redeem the reputation of a fatalistic, blank generation, and make this country fairer and less broken. To do so while emphasising the underrated virtues of modesty, humility, actually listening to people and giving power away would make that achievement all the sweeter.That is the prize; as the Burnham regime sorts out its metaphorical pegs and guy lines – and returns, ha ha, to the politics of the big tent – a lot of people surely feel a queasy mixture of nagging nerves and genuine excitement. So, I imagine, does he.John Harris is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionGlastonbury festivalHouse of CommonsLabourTony BlairFestivalsMusic festivalscommentShareReuse this content

title: Aperol, late-night laughs – and some gritty life truths: why girls’ trips aren’t just fluffy fun | Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett | The Guardian
description: From Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discovery, says Guardian columnist Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Author: Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Headline: From Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discovery, says Guardian columnist Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
Content: Women swimming at Portobello beach, Edinburgh, March 2026.Photograph: Jane Barlow/PAView image in fullscreenWomen swimming at Portobello beach, Edinburgh, March 2026.Photograph: Jane Barlow/PAOpinionFriendshipAperol, late-night laughs – and some gritty life truths: why girls’ trips aren’t just fluffy funRhiannon Lucy CosslettFrom Shirley Valentine to my own adventures with friends in Italy, all-women holidays offer the perfect mix of freedom and self-discoverySun 19 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 13.45 EDTShareIhave just come back from a much-anticipated girls’ holiday to Puglia, and to say that it fulfilled expectations would be an understatement. We swam, we napped, we reminisced, we ate little bits of crudo alongside lovelysips of fiano, we danced around the table of the Airbnb while singing along toSarà perché ti amo. Most of all, we laughed, sometimes until our stomachs hurt and our faces were wet with tears.What a joy the girls’ trip is (despite our ages, “women” doesn’t feel right here somehow). Maybe that’s because at times during the holiday I was transported back to when we were indeed “girls” and studying together in Italy (although this time with far less street harassment – a realisation that prompted mixed feelings until a belated “Che bellissime!” saved the day).Powered by Aperol – who may as well sponsor them at this point – thousands of girls’ trips will be taking place as I write. At this very moment, I can almost guarantee that somewhere in southern Europe, a woman is detailing her husband’s domestic habits with the meticulous comic precision of a standup routine, as hercrochet-cladaudience cackle, rattle their ice cubes supportively and holler the occasional “Boo!”The girls’ trip has become an institution, a central tenet of“hun” cultureand the subject ofmyriad films, books and TV shows, most recentlyThe Five-Star Weekend, in which a widowed Jennifer Garner brings together friends from different phases of her life for a holiday. My own novel,Female, Nude, uses a girls’ trip to a Greek villa as the backdrop for an examination of friendship between four women in their early 30s who are all starting to ask themselves questions about ambition, marriage and motherhood. Sophie, my protagonist, is kicking hardest against the sacrifices she feels she will have to make in the next phase of her life, which is partly why she embarks on an explosive affair.View image in fullscreenPauline Collins in the 1989 film Shirley Valentine.Photograph: PARAMOUNT/AllstarHer story is not new. Ever since oppressed and controlled housewifeShirley Valentinetook herself off to Greece with her friend Jane in 1989, the girls’ trip has become a byword for emancipation (even for those who don’t stray outside their relationships), and the deep need for women to have time away together. Rewatching Shirley as an adult woman feels powerfully poignant. Not all of us get the chance to travel, or feel so liberated. “It’s great you’re doing this,” another woman said to me before I went on my trip, as we discussed how hard it can be to organise a holiday with so many competing demands at home.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn a world determined to isolate us, friendship may be the best way to keep each other alive | Rebecca ShawRead moreI have known my travel companions for almost 20 years and we now have seven children between us, all under the age of eight. To swim out into the ocean without worrying about needing to get back to them, to have a conversation that was uninterrupted by them, where men were also not present, felt rare and precious. Luxurious, even. We talked about everything: birth, death, health, our bodies and senses of identity, the men we love and have loved and didn’t love, our children, and our hopes and fears for them. We covered fashion, the football, whether or not to get life insurance, how long it’s safe to keep an arancino on the beach before eating it, getting older. The conversation ebbed and flowed – we dipped in and out as we wanted – and somewhere in that very feminine mix of comedy and heartache, profound truths were arrived at (or maybe it was just the spicy margs).There’s a tendency to trivialise girls’ holidays – “oh look, the lasses are getting on the wine!” – and indeed, I just did it with that crack about margaritas. There are certainly some who don’t seem to think that the girls’ holiday is a subject worthy of serious cultural or literary inquiry (the terrible Sex and the City films didn’t help). Yet I knew I wanted to write about one because it is often during those opportunities to step away from your life for a little while, alongside other women who have known you for years and about whom you care deeply, that your emotions are cast in high relief. Perhaps the revelations that follow will bring about change, perhaps they won’t, but the chance to mix freedom, fun and serious self-inquiry should always be taken when it’s presented. I’ve certainly returned home with a new perspective. Time to start planning the next trip.Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett is a Guardian columnist. Her new book,Female, Nudeis out nowExplore more on these topicsFriendshipOpinionWomenFictioncommentShareReuse this content
