title: ‘We were in danger of running it into the ground’: Brittany Howard on fleeing fame, fighting Trump and the epic return of Alabama Shakes | Alabama Shakes | The Guardian
description: The soul-rockers achieved worldwide success before surprising everyone by deciding to quit while they were ahead. Now they’re back with a new album railing against the state of contemporary America
Author: Dave Simpson
Headline: The soul-rockers achieved worldwide success before surprising everyone by deciding to quit while they were ahead. Now they’re back with a new album railing against the state of contemporary America
Content: Blue sky thinking … Alabama Shakes (left to right): Zac Cockrell, Brittany Howard and Heath Fogg.Photograph: Bobbi RichView image in fullscreenBlue sky thinking … Alabama Shakes (left to right): Zac Cockrell, Brittany Howard and Heath Fogg.Photograph: Bobbi RichAlabama ShakesInterview‘We were in danger of running it into the ground’: Brittany Howard on fleeing fame, fighting Trump and the epic return of Alabama ShakesDave SimpsonThe soul-rockers achieved worldwide success before surprising everyone by deciding to quit while they were ahead. Now they’re back with a new album railing against the state of contemporary AmericaSun 19 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn the autumn of 2024,Alabama Shakesshowed no sign of ending their indefinite hiatus – and nobody was asking them to. Seven years had passed since the blues-soul-rock band, who exploded out of Athens, Alabama in 2009, had last shared a stage. Their transatlantic Top 10 2012 debut Boys & Girls announced them; the million-selling 2015 follow-up Sound & Color went to No 1 in the US and won them four Grammys. Their fanbase included Bruce Springsteen, Robert Plant and Barack Obama. But by 2017 they were physically and creatively spent, and they stopped. Then, in December 2024, with almost no warning, they played their first show in more than seven years.“We had a friend in Tuscaloosa who had a brewery, but it wasn’t doing so well after Covid,” explains singer Brittany Howard. “He called me and said he was going to do a fundraiser and asked if I’d like to perform. I said, ‘For sure.’” But then she started reminiscing, remembering how that particular friend had been a huge help to the band, not just her personally. She felt the band owed him something, collectively. “So I called the fellas,” she smiles. “‘Do y’all wanna perform at this thing – like, all of us, together?’ And they instantly said yes.”Soon afterwards, they found themselves rehearsing songs they hadn’t played in years. Then, on 18 December 2024, they performed at the 1,000-capacity Bama Theatre, to a rapturous reception. Guitarist Heath Fogg remembers that show well. “It felt like an out-of-body experience, to me,” he says. “Just really happy, really thankful … a feeling of euphoria in the purest form. I hadn’t played those songs since our last gig. I hadn’t even listened to them unless one came on in a restaurant or something. Then at the show we knew so many people in the crowd but we hadn’t told anyone we were playing. So it was a total surprise – and so exciting.”View image in fullscreen‘It feels good and honest’ … Howard on stage in Leeds earlier this month.Photograph: Alexander Cropper/RedfernsHoward knew that it couldn’t stop there. “It felt very natural to think: ‘Let’s get together again. Let’s rehearse. Let’s write songs. Let’s go on tour,’” she says. “I naively thought we’d do some shows in clubs and theatres, but we’ve been playing some of the biggest shows we’ve ever played, which is just baffling to me, but wonderful.”Part of the answer is a song. Released in April this year, American Dream – a gospel-tinged snapshot of a country coming apart and refusing to despair about it – went viral on TikTok and Instagram. Over the next fortnight, Howard found herself back at the centre of the cultural conversation after a decade of highly acclaimed but less impactful solo work. Describing lyrics – which address gun violence, the climate crisis, abortion rights, low wages and even Trump’s White House renovations – as “a snapshot of 2026”, the singer spoke of her desire that future generations would hear it and think: “‘Yeah, shit was crazy back then, but we made it through.’”The song’s success is all the more remarkable given the dire state of protest music in 2026. With honourable exceptions such as Bruce Springsteen’s Streets of Minneapolis, a powerful response to the Ice killings of Renée Good and Alex Pretti, or Lucinda Williams’s gently raging The World’s Gone Wrong, most attempts to tackle the ruinous state of Trump’s US fall short, either because they are banal or too divisive, failing to identify the rot in a way that resonates on both sides of the divide. Overnight, though, Alabama Shakes rediscovered their cultural lane – a band almost uniquely capable of being furious and hopeful in the same breath, articulating the feelings of millions of people. As Howard puts it, “We’ve probably touched on these subjects before, but not to the extent that we are now.”double quotation markWe hit the ground running, then were in danger of running the thing into the groundWe meet in Leeds, the day before they play the city’s cavernous Millennium Square – their first gig in Europe for a decade. Howard, who the following night will take the stage in a striking glittering furry ballgown, is dressed down and very jetlagged. Fogg is too, but has been out exploring the city, where they have never played before, and is endearingly excited about having visited Leeds’s oldest ale house, Whitelock’s. “Built in 1715!” he exclaims, in a lovely soft Alabama drawl. Bassist Zac Cockrell doesn’t join us, and is possibly still sleeping.Alongside old favourites such as Hold On – 225m Spotify streams and counting – the gig will unveil songs from their fabulous forthcoming third album, I Must Be Dreaming, a radical departure from their previous big-lunged retro soul. It’s more ethereal, even psychedelic in places, with songs about relationships, the current state of their home country and the pace and dissonance of modern life.“I feel there’s quite a difference between the first album and the second,” Howard says, “but there’s a thread between them and I feel the same about this album. We’re 10 years older and more experienced, but going to unpredictable places has always been the fun part of this group.” Dreamlike opener Tea Time is built round, of all things, flutes. The similarly titled Time has a full-on psychedelic jam. The album’s first single, Another Life, morphs from R&B into hazy, frazzled rock and packs a huge, euphoric chorus: “Can we try again? Can we try in another life?”“It’s a way of saying: ‘This relationship has run ashore,’” Howard says, “‘but maybe in the next life it will all work out. I bid you adieu, but just for now.’”Is the song, on some level, even subconsciously, partly about the band? “Maybe, when I think about it, because it is unusual to come back like this,” she considers. “Cos it feels good and honest. I’m not being forced to do this. When we started the group it felt like it was cool to make music with your friends: ‘What kind of music do you like? How are we going to fit together?’ Coming back together was the same: ‘What are you into now? What have you been listening to?’”The trio went to the same high school, but didn’t really know each other. Then Howard noticed Cockrell’s At the Drive-In T-shirt and thought he might like the music she’d been making and want to play together. “I approached him in class and played him a CD in his Honda Civic, which was a very vulnerable thing to do,” she remembers. “He was smashing through the tracks and I was thinking, ‘Uh oh, he’s not liking this.’ But then he went, ‘This is cool. Let’s do it.’” Fogg, three years above them, had left college when he came onboard, and the three bonded over rock, punk, R&B and old music they’d picked up from their parents: Dionne Warwick, Elvis, Motown.View image in fullscreenShake it off … The band at Bonnaroo festival in Manchester, Tennessee.Photograph: Savannah Lauren ShealyHoward can pour her heart out like a classic, old-school soul singer, but she started as a drummer, gravitating to other instruments through necessity and only realising she had a voice when her mother heard her tape and said: “I didn’t know you could sing.” She describes her childhood in Limestone County as “slow-paced”, among “pastures, little birds and wildfires. Everybody knew each other. You had some kids who rode horses or drove tractors to school, and people grew soya beans, so we played in fields of yellow.” Not that she lacked harder life experience for songs. Howard is gay, mixed-race – her white mother and Black father separated when she was eight after her 13-year-old sister Jaime died from retinoblastoma. The singer has previously said that the incident triggered “a raincloud over our family”, but named her 2019 solo album Jaime to honour the sister who taught her piano. Howard herself survived the disease, although it left her partly blind in one eye.With the band giving her an outlet, they played local bars, old folk’s homes, shows a 90-mile drive away in Birmingham or Nashville, anywhere that would have them, eventually taking off after the likes of Drive-By Truckers and Jack White asked the band to support them. “We’d play to anyone who would listen even if we lost money,” says Howard. “All we wanted was to be in a band and make an album. We never thought people would fall in love with it like they did.” She admits to some guilt about the multitudes of “super-talented” people who never get a break. “It’s crazy to think I was plucked from millions. So many people don’t get to have a voice.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe GuideGet our weekly pop culture email, free in your inbox every FridayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionToday, the Shakes still pinch themselves at experiences such as performing for President Obama at the White House. “[When] I shook his hand, it felt like it reached the middle of my arm,” smiles Fogg. “Michelle gave us a great, sincere hug. Each of us have a photo of that moment.” In 2015, Howard performed Get Back with Paul McCartney during his headline set at Lollapalooza, trading vocals with the revered Liverpudlian. “You forget you’re talking to a Beatle. I don’t know where he gets his energy from. The last time I saw him was at an after-party at 4am. I was like: ‘What are you doing here?!’ He just doesn’t stop.”It was a heady rise, and a product of its moment: the early 2010s were perhaps the last period in which a major label and the young streaming platforms worked in concert to mint new stars, and the Shakes – a retro-soul band with a colossal playlist-friendly single – were obvious beneficiaries. But by 2017, with babies due in two of the band’s households, Howard felt that after “hitting the ground running and running very hard, we were in danger of running the thing into the ground. Stopping must have looked crazy from the outside, but this group’s always been about protecting and caring about each other.” So they took a break.Howard didn’t exactly go quietly, making the twice Grammy-nominated Jaime and the acclaimed What Now (2024), but also relishing being able to “cut the grass, do the garden, scoop the litter box and just do normal tasks”. In 2019, she married musician Jesse Lafser; the marriage lasted barely a year. “It was very quick,” Howard grins. “Can we go to the next topic?” We can, but it’s even thornier. In 2020, the band’s original drummer, Steve Johnson, received a suspended sentence and probation for violating a domestic violence protection order and was later arrested over wilful abuse of a child. Those charges were dropped.The band re-formed without him. “I guess you can’t take a long journey like we have without expecting some things to happen,” Howard says. Her reluctance to fully address the subject is as obvious as it is understandable. “I put myself in his shoes, I wouldn’t want someone out there talking about me.”Today, Howard says that her experiences have made her a gentler, more patient person. “I’ve always had that fight in me, but I’m a bit more contemplative than I was,” she says. “I know I can’t change everything in the world.” Not that she doesn’t want to try. Another new song, the jazzily soulful I Feel Hope Coming, works the same seam as American Dream, but with even more positivity.Alabama Shakes review – US rockers’ first UK gig in a decade is suffused with hope for the futureRead more“My perspective is that I see a lot of people outraged, people in the streets protecting those who don’t have very much from those who have everything,” she says. “But outrage leads to protest which leads to people talking to each other. To me, that’s hopeful. The American people don’t want war. We want to be able to feed ourselves, clothe our kids and dream dreams for them and have freedom of movement like anybody else. That’s what those songs are about.”In a few hours’ time, she’ll express such sentiments on stage. Her hope is that songs such as American Dream and I Feel Hope Coming can soundtrack a moment: the turning of the tide she sees happening now as Americans start to reject hate and division and turn towards freedoms, social justice and accountability.“I look around and see all these people who were dissatisfied finally pointing the finger at people who are making it that way,” she says. “People finally coming together and saying: ‘I was wrong. Let’s do something about it.’” Fogg concurs: “Hope can be a catalyst. First we hope, then we make it happen.”I Must Be Dreamingis released on 28 Auguston Island Records.Explore more on these topicsAlabama ShakesPop and rockfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘People say they’ve conceived to my music’: Tricky’s honest playlist | Tricky | The Guardian
description: The trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?
Author: Rich Pelley
Headline: The trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?
Content: ‘Songs I loved years ago I can still listen to now’ … Tricky.Photograph: Steve GullickView image in fullscreen‘Songs I loved years ago I can still listen to now’ … Tricky.Photograph: Steve GullickHonest playlistTricky‘People say they’ve conceived to my music’: Tricky’s honest playlistThe trip-hop star doesn’t believe in getting jiggy to songs and would choose Cyndi Lauper at karaoke. But which classic reminds him of his mum?As told toRich PelleySun 19 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe first song I fell in love withI was going out with this Jamaican girl when we were about 15, and would listen to Night Nurse by Gregory Isaacs all the time. We had a daughter together when we were 17 – she’s 35 now and works as a social worker in Bristol – so that song has always stayed with me.The song that makes me cryAstral Weeks by Van Morrison, because it reminds me of my mum and growing up in Bristol. He sings about his mum dressing him and showing him pictures, and my mum was exactly like that. In every childhood photo I’m in a shirt and tie and looking proper.The first single I boughtI remember getting on the bus from Knowle West into town to buy the Specials album. I was obsessed with the artwork and the sleeve notes. I sat on the bus reading everything on the way home and listened to it for three days straight.The song I know all the lyrics toI can still rap every word of Check Out My Melody by Eric B and Rakim off the top of my head, 40 years later.The best song to play at a partySong 2 by Blur, because of that riff. I still listen to it now. I was listening to it yesterday in my hotel room. I put on my headphones, smoked some hashish and played Song 2.The song I can no longer listen toThere honestly isn’t one. Songs I loved years ago – Purple Rain by Prince or Fight the Power by Public Enemy – I can still listen to now.The song I do at karaokeI can’t say I’ve ever done karaoke, but if I had to, I’d probably do True Colors by Cyndi Lauper.The song that gets me up in the morningRelease Yo’ Delf by Method Man gets me moving.The best song to have sex toPeople have been telling me for years: “Me and my partner have sex to your music.” Some people have even said they’ve conceived to my music, which is all very flattering. But I don’t think sex and music even go together, so it’s always seemed a bit weird to me.The song that changed my lifeI loved Prince, but I knew I could never sing like him. Then I heard Too Much Too Young by the Specials and saw them live. Terry Hall and Neville Staple were singing about the life I knew. Suddenly it felt possible. They made me realise I could do that, too.The song I secretly like, but tell everyone I hateBreathe Me by Sia is fucking gorgeous. I don’t really know any of her other stuff, which is bad, because I probably should.The song I’d like played at my funeralI’m never going to die, so there’s no need to think about that.Tricky’s album When It’s Silent is outnow.Explore more on these topicsTrickyHonest playlistHip-hopDance musicElectronic musicfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘What has the BBC ever done for me?’ Romesh Ranganathan reboots corporation’s 1985 licence fee plea | BBC licence fee | The Guardian
description: As with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David Jason
Author: Tara Conlan
Headline: As with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David Jason
Content: Romesh Ranganathan in the Queen Vic with punters including David Jason, Adam Woodyatt  and Sue Perkins.Photograph: BBCView image in fullscreenRomesh Ranganathan in the Queen Vic with punters including David Jason, Adam Woodyatt  and Sue Perkins.Photograph: BBCBBC licence fee‘What has the BBC ever done for me?’ Romesh Ranganathan reboots corporation’s 1985 licence fee pleaAs with the John Cleese original, comedian is supported by famous faces from Claudia Winkleman to David JasonTara ConlanSun 19 Jul 2026 12.02 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 15.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJohn Cleese’s 1985 advert for the BBC in which he asks a pub full of stars “What has the BBC ever given us?” is one of the most famous examples of the corporation making the case to the public for the value of paying the licence fee.In the advert, Cleese is complaining to the bartender that he has just bought his TV licence. “Fifty-eight quid for the privilege of sitting in your own home, watching your own television set. It’s diabolical. I mean, what’s the BBC ever given us for 58 quid?” he says, before being put right by a string of punters, ranging from David Attenborough and John Humphrys to Terry Wogan and David Jason.Now, over 40 years later, as the corporation cranks up its campaign for the charter that allows it to operate to be renewed, it has rebooted the advert with anew musical versionfronted by Romesh Ranganathan and directed by Dougal Wilson, the man behind Paddington in Peru and the John Lewis Christmas ad campaigns.The comedian stars as a disgruntled licence fee payer grumbling in EastEnders’ Queen Vic pub to a host of household names including Chris Martin, Claudia Winkleman, David Jason and Greg James.View image in fullscreenClaudia Winkleman pulls a pint of The Traitor’s Ale.Photograph: BBCThe comedian then has a whistle-stop song and dance tour of the BBC’s shows and services, featuring about 90 famous faces, from Alan Carr to Cate Blanchett, from Hey Duggee to Clive Myrie.The BBC faces what its director general, Matt Brittin, described recently as“a moment of real jeopardy”due to a decline in licence fee payments and the rise of rivals such as YouTube, TikTok and the streamers. The number of people paying the BBC’s licence fee fell faster than expected in the last year, with half a million more households opting out of the payment.To illustrate what could be lost, near the end of the advert Ranganathan is propelled to the backstage of Strictly Come Dancing to meet Louis Theroux, who says: “You know, none of this has to exist,” as he puts his hand on the lever of a giant on/off switch marked BBC, “if we don’t want it to”.View image in fullscreenLouis Theroux has his hand on the BBC’s on/off switch.Photograph: BBCThe film is being premiered on TV during the World Cup final and was launched on social platforms on Sunday morning. It has had some positive reaction from viewers, but a lukewarm response from some quarters, withthe Telegraphcomplaining that the selection of BBC offerings presented were “notably middlebrow” and theTimes complainingthat – as is “sorely emblematic of so much TV content these days on the BBC, but also elsewhere” – it was a rehash of an old idea.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWhat's OnGet the best TV reviews, news and features in your inbox every MondayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRomesh Ranganathan: ‘My greatest fear? My children ending up anything like me’Read moreThere have been a number of BBC promotional films over the years aimed at encapsulating its public service offering in a way that informs, educates and entertains.They include star-studded versions of theBeach Boys’ God Only KnowsandLou Reed’s Perfect Day. But Cleese’s ad (itself based on Monty Python’s Life of Brian sceneWhat Have the Romans Ever Done For Us?) washighlighted recently by Chris Packhamas “genius” while promoting an open letter in defence of the corporation.Ranganathan defended the rationale behind the new version, saying: “I strongly, strongly believe that the BBC should exist and, look, don’t get me wrong, I don’t think it’s without its flaws.”“There have definitely been times and issues where I wished it could have dealt with things differently – but I feel incredibly passionate about the fact that we should have it and it adds so much to our lives. And I’m really proud of it.”“I really do feel strongly that we should have a BBC and I think that there is nothing else that does what the BBC does.”He said when he was asked to front the film, “I was very surprised that they’d chosen me and very honoured, but I thought it was a no-brainer, really.”Growing up, it was the BBC’sGoodness Gracious Methat made him believe he could have a performing career, he said: “I didn’t think people that looked like me could do comedy. I was doing standup as a little kid and then you watch something like Goodness Gracious Me and you think, yeah, I could do that.”The advert acknowledges that the BBC has done a lot for Ranganathan himself, with Myrie pointing out he fronts The Weakest Link, Sara Cox reminding him he hosts a Radio 2 show and Chris McCausland highlighting his “hip-hop podcast on Sounds”.Explore more on these topicsBBC licence feeBBCTelevision industryTelevisionRomesh RanganathanMatt BrittinnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘There was absolutely no chance of being a kid’: new exhibition explores how war shapes children’s lives | Children | The Guardian
description: Childhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilities
Author: Nadia Khomami
Headline: Childhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilities
Content: John Hajdu outside the Imperial War Museum in London. John is loaning his beloved teddy for the Childhood in War exhibition, which opens on 31 July.Photograph: Richard Ash/© IWMView image in fullscreenJohn Hajdu outside the Imperial War Museum in London. John is loaning his beloved teddy for the Childhood in War exhibition, which opens on 31 July.Photograph: Richard Ash/© IWMChildren‘There was absolutely no chance of being a kid’: new exhibition explores how war shapes children’s livesChildhood in War examines what life looks like when play, education and family life is disrupted by hostilitiesNadia KhomamiArts and culture correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen John Hajdu was three, his parents gave him a teddy bear small enough to fit into his hands: 86 years later, it is the only surviving object from his childhood.The bear, which the 89-year-old simply calls “teddy”, stayed with him as his father was taken to a labour camp, his mother sent to a concentration camp, and he hid in a non-Jewish neighbour’s cupboard before being forced into the Budapest ghetto.From 31 July, teddy will go on public display for the first time as part ofChildhood in War, a new exhibition at the Imperial War Museum, London, examining how conflict shapes children’s lives.Displayed in a recreated second world war bedroom, the bear sits among toys, books and personal possessions that ask visitors to consider what childhood looks like when play, education and family life are disrupted by war.View image in fullscreenJohn Hajdu and his family. Now a grandfather of four, John spends much of his time speaking in schools about surviving the HolocaustThe exhibition combines hundreds of objects with immersive environments, including a full-size railway carriage, a wartime classroom and a cold war living room and kitchen, alongside testimonies from children living through conflicts in places including Ukraine, Sudan and Afghanistan.For Hajdu, teddy represents a childhood interrupted. Before the war, his life was typical of a well-to-do, middle-class Jewish family. By 1944, after years of antisemitic persecution in Hungary, his father had been sent to a labour camp and Hajdu and his mother had been forced into a designated yellow-star house.double quotation markTeddy did, and still does, mean so much to me. It was my only connection to a family life I barely rememberedJohn Hajdu“We could only take with us what we could carry, and that meant just a few pieces of clothing and teddy,” he says. “There was absolutely no chance of being a kid. I had no other toys or books. We were only allowed out in the afternoon wearing the yellow star. I couldn’t meet other children. We weren’t allowed in parks, cinemas or swimming pools or cafes. I was completely isolated.”The exhibition explores exactly those everyday losses. A reconstructed classroom shows how war disrupts children’s education through objects from conflicts including the second world war, Northern Ireland, Afghanistan and the former Yugoslavia.View image in fullscreenThe red knitted jumper given to Jochewet Heidenstein by her parents before she left Berlin for Britain.Photograph: © IWM (EPH 3909).There are comics warning children about landmines and unexploded ordnance. Visitors can also climb into an evacuee train carriage containing letters sent home and Kindertransport objects, including a red knitted jumper given to Jochewet Heidenstein by her parents before she and her sisters fled Berlin for Britain.In October 1944, Hajdu’s mother was arrested and taken to Mauthausen concentration camp in Austria. Believing she would never return, Hajdu’s aunt rushed him into the cupboard of a non-Jewish neighbour.“I had teddy in my pocket,” he says. “I was in complete darkness and had to keep completely quiet. It seemed like a very long time, which was an incredibly frightening experience for a seven-year-old … By that time I was able to comprehend what happened without fully understanding the enormity of the situation.”View image in fullscreenA child refugee’s drawing depicting the 1998–99 conflict in Kosovo.Illustration: © The rightsholder (EPH 3008)He later moved with his aunt into the Budapest ghetto, sharing a flat with more than 20 strangers. “There were no children. We survived on horse meat and bread. Dead bodies lay piled up on the streets, having died of cold, starvation or disease. I was always hungry. Again, I had teddy in my pocket.”The ghetto was liberated by Soviet troops in January 1945, just before retreating German forces planned to destroy it. Miraculously, Hajdu was reunited with both his parents. His mother survived Mauthausen after US troops liberated the camp, but returned with broken ribs, smashed teeth and lasting psychological scars. “I was convinced I’d never see my parents again,” he says.A decade later, after the Soviet suppression of the Hungarian uprising, Hajdu and his mother walked 40km (25 miles) and crossed an icy river under cover of darkness to reach Austria. They arrived in Britain in 1957 on a Red Cross train, with teddy travelling with them.Throughout the exhibition, stories such as Hajdu’s sit alongside contemporary accounts from children living through war today. Visitors can explore a 360-degree virtual tour of an underground school in Ukraine created by one of its pupils, read poems written by Ukrainian children for Unicef’sPoems for Peace project, and seedrawings by children in Sudanthat were later submitted as evidence of alleged war crimes to the international criminal court.A poem by a 15-year-old Afghan girl describes the moment the Taliban returned to power in Kabul in August 2021: “I witnessed how that day darkened not only Kabul’s sky, but also the dreams and hopes of so many.”View image in fullscreenAlina and Artem, aged nine and from the city of Kharkiv in Ukraine, sit on a makeshift sofa in the underground car park that serves as a bomb shelter. Their parents have done their best to make it homely.Photograph: Aleksey Filippov/Unicef/UN0642196/Aleksey Filippov. All rights reservedThe exhibition also examines how childhood can become politicised, with material from the Hitler Youth and Soviet Komsomol movements.“We wanted to talk about the impact of war on children’s lives,” says curator Fran Singleton. “With more conflicts in the news at the moment, it felt like the right time to create an exhibition that offers families a space to come together and discuss these important topics.”‘Into the arms of strangers’: child refugees of Nazi Germany rememberRead moreFor Hajdu, allowing teddy to leave his home for the first time has been surprisingly difficult.“Teddy did, and still does, mean so much to me. It was my only connection to a family life I barely remembered when I was three,” he says. “I find it difficult to look at the desk where he normally sits.”Now a grandfather of four, Hajdu spends much of his time speaking in schools about surviving theHolocaust. As the number of survivors dwindles, he hopes younger generations will continue telling these stories.“I tell them, ‘When you go home, talk to your grandparents and parents. Find out whether they’ve gone through traumas, whether it’s in Asia or Africa.’“My small story is an example that you can survive. Unfortunately, people are suffering all over the world. We are getting older, and fewer and fewer Holocaust survivors are here to tell these stories. It’s absolutely vital that the younger generation takes on what we’re doing.”Explore more on these topicsChildrenMuseumsHolocaustSecond world warfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Bad Bunny concert in Milan abandoned because of hailstorm | Bad Bunny | The Guardian
description: Fans evacuated from sold-out open-air show as organisers promise full refunds
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Fans evacuated from sold-out open-air show as organisers promise full refunds
Content: Fans rush out of Bad Bunny concert in Milan due to hailstormBad BunnyBad Bunny concert in Milan abandoned because of hailstormFans evacuated from sold-out open-air show as organisers promise full refundsAgence France-PresseSun 19 Jul 2026 05.57 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 06.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAn open-air show by the Puerto Rican music starBad Bunnywas abandoned in Italy on Saturday night because of a violent hailstorm, but the organiser said ticketholders would be reimbursed.Videos posted on social media showed large hailstones hitting people at the sold-out show, along with strong winds and driving rain, after the star began his set at Milan’s Snai La Maura Hippodrome.“Due to adverse weather conditions, including thunderstorms and hail, Bad Bunny’s concert scheduled for 18 July at the Snai La Maura Hippodrome in Milan was interrupted and the area was safely evacuated,” the ticket operator Live Nation wrote on its website on Sunday.All ticketholders would receive full refunds, it added.Explore more on these topicsBad BunnyItalyEuropenews

title: Sphinxes, movie stars and 3m books: inside Los Angeles’s beloved art deco library | Los Angeles | The Guardian
description: The showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institution
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institution
Content: The library opened in 1926 and has been described as ‘a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences’.Photograph: Damian Dovarganes/APView image in fullscreenThe library opened in 1926 and has been described as ‘a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences’.Photograph: Damian Dovarganes/APLos AngelesSphinxes, movie stars and 3m books: inside Los Angeles’s beloved art deco libraryThe showcase library’s centenary was celebrated by thousands who filled the still-relevant public institutionJames Bartlett in Los AngelesSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 17.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe central library in downtownLos Angeleshas seen its fair share of colorful moments.The architectural gem, which turned 100 this week, has been the backdrop to the epic street battle between Pacino, De Niro et al in Heat, and waswhere the originalGhostbusterscame across their first ghost, a friendly older lady librarian who was anything but. Television shows such as Moonlighting; Murder, She Wrote; and LA Law filmed there too, when characters were researching or investigating a case.‘A counter-narrative of history’: Schomburg Center’s 100 years of celebrating Black cultureRead moreBut the library has also been touched by the darker side of LA life. The “Night Stalker” serial killer Richard Ramirezcame here in 1985and asked for books on astrology and torture. Ever helpful, the librarian directed him to the correct department and thought little more about the unsettling man with rotten teeth – until he recognized him when he was arrested several days later.Then there was the still-officially unsolved 1986 arson, which destroyed 400,000 volumes and shut the library for seven years.“People think that libraries are quiet, but they really aren’t. They rumble with voices and footsteps and a whole orchestral range of book-related noises,” wrote Susan Orlean in The Library Book,her 2018 bestseller about the central library.That was certainly true this past week, when the author was one of the estimated 12,000 people who swelled through the corridors, halls, courtyards, rotunda, reading rooms and sculpture garden in celebration of its 100th anniversary.View image in fullscreenThe Los Angeles central library has been a set location for films and TV shows, and a research center for a serial killer.Photograph: Eric Draper/APThe art deco-era library, according to L Is for Librarian: The ABCs ofLos AngelesCentral Library, houses nearly 3m books and 89 miles of shelves.The library’s most dazzling and Instagram-worthy feature is the central rotunda – cathedral-like and lined with Dean Cornwell’s romanticized murals of California history. Visitors’ eyes are drawn up to the zodiac chandelier with its stained-glass globe and zodiac signs.“It remains one of the city’s great civic spaces, a place that’s used, loved and valued every single day,” says Stephen Gee, the author of Los Angeles Central Library: A History of Its Art andArchitecture.“It’s one of the few places in modern Los Angeles where everyone is welcome, regardless of their background or circumstances. It doesn’t ask who you are or what you earn.”After outgrowing several smaller downtown locations, the municipal dream of a monumental downtown library was financed through a $2.5m municipal bond measure approved in 1921.View image in fullscreenThe Bison Hunt frieze on the exterior of the Los Angeles central library.Photograph: University of Southern California/Corbis/Getty ImagesDesigned by the architect Bertram Goodhue, the central library officially opened its doors in 1926.“Goodhue promised a library that was ‘Spanish in style – at least in spirit’,” noted Gee in his book, “but what he ultimately created was something far more original: a remarkable fusion of Spanish, Egyptian, Byzantine and Islamic influences.”View image in fullscreenThe Lodwrick M Cook Rotunda inside the LA central library.Photograph: Barry Winiker/Getty ImagesThat fusion is apparent whether you approach the library from Hope Street, which was the original, mural-lined tunnel entrance, or through the sculpture and fountain-spotted Maguire Gardens.Towering over the main entrance are Hesper and Phosphor, the ancient Greek morning and evening stars; Hesper holds a scroll with the names of eastern philosophers, Moses, Buddha and Mohammad.Most noticeable to the first-time visitor is the colored mosaic pyramid on the building’s roof, which features a sunburst and is topped by a handheld torch symbolizing the light of learning.The Egyptian theme is also seen in two black-gold sphinxes, which rest at the top of a staircase on either side of a female statue representing civilization.One of the longest lines at this week’s centennial festival was for a vintage injection-mold vending machine, whichproduced small replica wax modelsof those sphinxes.Christina Rice, the senior librarian of the library’s vast photo collection and a Central 100 co-chair, christened the whirring, jukebox-like machine “Mold-A-Central”, and obtaining one had been on her wishlist for years.“About a dozen years ago, we received an inquiry in the photo collection from a patron looking for images of these machines,” Rice said.It unleashed a flood of childhood memories for Rice, who recalled getting animal models at the LA zoo and admits that she “became a little bit obsessed with them”. After finally tracking down one of the machines, she had it installed for the Central 100 event. “It will be a permanent fixture, and we’ll be changing out the molds periodically,” she said.View image in fullscreenA crowded reading room in the LA central library in 1933.Photograph: University of Southern California/Corbis/Getty ImagesElsewhere, people took former catalogue cards and used them to clack out poems on vintage typewriters, while down in the history and genealogy department the cards in the huge family names cabinet were being eagerly thumbed through.“People do love card catalogues,” says Kelly Wallace, the department’sCaliforniahistory specialist. “I find that Los Angeles has come of age in terms of taking an interest in its own history. Every day, people come to the library to research the history of their families, homes, neighborhoods and the city around them.”View image in fullscreenLos Angeles central library on 19 July 2010.Photograph: Ted Soqui/Corbis/Getty ImagesRice explains that the library is as relevant as ever, despite those who might declare libraries “obsolete”. She mentions how the central library provides services for job hunters and those seeking a path to citizenship or looking to learn a new language. “We have a program for adults to earn their high school diploma, loads of resources to get teens prepared for college and literacy assistance for patrons of all ages.”On the science, technology and patents floor is the Octavia lab, which offers 3D printing, digitizers, converters, vinyl cutters, large-format printers – and sewing and embroidery machines. The facility is named after a former frequent patron, the late Octavia Butler, the renowned science fiction author who said: “Public libraries … are the open universities of America.”The daily business of the library continued during the events, but the most popular book of the day was Luceros y Penumras (roughly translated as “starlight and shadows”) by Daniel González. At 31-by-20ft and 11ft tall, it’sthe world’s largest pop-up book, according to the library, and the 1,800lb work wascarefully assembledin the center of the rotunda.But perhaps the best news of the day? Five hundred and eighty-eight people were issued new library cards.Explore more on these topicsLos AngelesLibrariesArchitectureWest CoastCaliforniafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Lashes, langers, bozzers and belly bachelors: a new book decodes Cork’s local slang | Ireland | The Guardian
description: Sex, drink and religion are common motifs in the ‘extraordinarily rich’ colloquial vernacular of Ireland’s second city
Author: Rory Carroll
Headline: Sex, drink and religion are common motifs in the ‘extraordinarily rich’ colloquial vernacular of Ireland’s second city
Content: In Cork’s colloquial jargon, a drinker dodging paying their round is engaging in ‘immaculate conception’, while someone who needs the toilet after only three pints is a ‘premature ejaculator’.Photograph: Design Pics Inc/AlamyView image in fullscreenIn Cork’s colloquial jargon, a drinker dodging paying their round is engaging in ‘immaculate conception’, while someone who needs the toilet after only three pints is a ‘premature ejaculator’.Photograph: Design Pics Inc/AlamyIrelandLashes, langers, bozzers and belly bachelors: a new book decodes Cork’s local slangSex, drink and religion are common motifs in the ‘extraordinarily rich’ colloquial vernacular of Ireland’s second cityRory Carroll, Ireland correspondentSun 19 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 14.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIf Des MacHale had to nominate a favourite from the lexicon of insulting and inexplicable terms that comprise Cork slang, it would have to be “langer”.Depending on tone and context it can mean idiot, drunkenness or penis, a versatility that baffles outsiders and further enhances the word’s value. “Langer is an absolutely beautiful word. I’m very fond of it,” says MacHale.Hence its inclusion in the title of his new book,Langers & Lashes: A Compendium of Cork Slang, which bills itself as the most comprehensive collection of the crude, playful, savage and ingenious wordplay that inflects speech inIreland’s second city.“It’s an extraordinarily rich area that most dictionaries avoid,” MacHale says. “It’s a very real phenomenon of language that hasn’t been taken terribly seriously until now.”In a foreword, the publisher, Mercier, said it had added explanatory notes to highlight some terms’ “historical context and outdated social views” and that inclusion did not imply endorsement. “By retaining these terms as artefacts of the past, we aim to encourage contemporary Cork speakers to embrace the city’s renowned wit while rejecting harmful stereotypes.”Sex, drink and religion are common motifs. A “premature ejaculator” – a term attributed to the late Cork actor and comedian, Niall Tóibín – is a fellow who must go to the gents after drinking only three pints. “Immaculate conception” describes drinkers who dodge paying their round. “Lash” is an attractive woman.Cork – known inIrelandas the rebel county and also the people’s republic of Cork – uses local slang more than other parts of the island, says MacHale. “Cork slang is very different from Dublin slang, and from Belfast, and from Galway. It’s much more extensive.”MacHale is an unlikely chronicler. The 80-year-old academic is from County Mayo and his background is not language but mathematics. “I’ve been here for about 55 years so I’m still regarded as a blow-in.”The University College Cork emeritus professor has authored dozens of books onpuzzlesand humour, including a book of Kerryman jokes that continues to sell 50 years after it was first published. “A mathematician likes to collect, classify and put things together,” MacHale explained.View image in fullscreenDes MacHale’s book of Cork slang was published in July.Photograph: Rory Carroll/The GuardianHis Cork slang credentials are bolstered by having five Cork-born children, including the actor Dominic MacHale, who plays Sergeant Healy in the BBC sitcomYoung Offenders, which is about hapless delinquents.For the compendium MacHale drew on two earlier books about Cork slang, which are no longer in print, and conversations with Cork residents. “Nearly everybody you meet has got a new word,” he says. “I’m not sure why but women seem to be a lot better at remembering the words and using them than men.”Under A, there is “all-a-bah”, a warning you give when someone is about to vomit. A person who is “all Gillette” is dolled-up.Brussels sprouts are “balls of the cabbage”. To be ill is to feel “like a small hospital” or to be “barely above ground”. A “wooden suit” is a coffin. “Bazz” is female pubic hair, not to be confused with “bazzer”, a cheap, self-administered haircut, or “bozzer”, an attractive person.To “be doggy wide” is to be very careful. A “belly bachelor” is an opportunist who cultivates friendships for free meals. To tell someone to get lost, you say: “bite the back of me bollix”.To “lob the gob” is to try to kiss someone. A “martyr for the quare thing” is someone with a strong libido. To “fertilise the stars” is to urinate in a field and to “raise the froth” is to urinate copiously.A “dullamoo” – derived from ag dul amú, Irish for getting lost – is an unreliable person and a “gedgemeen” is a small, unhappy person. Not all the terms were necessarily coined inCorkbut are commonly heard there, said MacHale.“Tosser” – defined as a synonym for “langer” – made a controversial appearance in parliament this week. During a clash with the taoiseach,Micheál Martin, the opposition leader,Mary Lou McDonald, said: “Jesus, he really is a tosser,” a remark that entered the official Dáil record.Explore more on these topicsIrelandEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: TV tonight: the biggest names in country make a noise in Nashville | Television & radio | The Guardian
description: Carrie Underwood and Kathy Mattea celebrate 100 years of Grand Ole Opry. Plus: controversial comedy in Unacceptable. Here’s what to watch this evening
Author: Ali Catterall
Headline: Carrie Underwood and Kathy Mattea celebrate 100 years of Grand Ole Opry. Plus: controversial comedy in Unacceptable. Here’s what to watch this evening
Content: Belting lungs … Carrie Underwood at Grand Ole Opry on Sky Arts.Photograph: Chris Hollo/Hollo Photographics Inc.View image in fullscreenBelting lungs … Carrie Underwood at Grand Ole Opry on Sky Arts.Photograph: Chris Hollo/Hollo Photographics Inc.TV tonightTelevision & radioTV tonight: the biggest names in country make a noise in NashvilleCarrie Underwood and Kathy Mattea celebrate 100 years of Grand Ole Opry. Plus: controversial comedy in Unacceptable. Here’s what to watch this eveningAli Catterall,Hollie Richardson,Hannah J DaviesandPriya ElanSun 19 Jul 2026 01.15 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 01.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGrand Ole Opry: Opry Live7pm, Sky Arts“The show that made country music famous” (according to its own branding), the Grand Ole Opry celebrates its centennial season. Striding on to the stage in Nashville this weekend are Carrie Underwood (belting lungs and contemporary-style hits); Grammy‑winning Kathy Mattea (character‑driven, intimate storytelling); and Suzy Bogguss (folk and Americana influences).Ali CatterallBorder Force: America’s Gatekeepers7pm, U&DaveA depressing look at the work of customs and border protection officers on the US/Mexico border. The week’s cases include a female pedestrian with an outstanding warrant for larceny returning from a day in Mexico, and two women caught in a car with illegal diet pills stashed in it.Hollie RichardsonBeethoven’s Fifth at the Proms9pm, BBC FourA specially curated Proms night, presenting archive performances of Beethoven’s “supercharged” Symphony No 5 in C minor, Mussorgsky’s Night on a Bare Mountain and Strauss’s audacious Don Juan. Petroc Trelawny and Georgia Mann host, the BBC Symphony Orchestra essays the bangers, and the baton-wagglers are Marin Alsop and Elim Chan.ACThe Piano9pm, Channel 4View image in fullscreenUnstoppable! … The Piano on Channel 4.Photograph: Nic Serpell-Rand/Channel 4 / Nic Serpell-RandLast year’s fire next to Glasgow Central station meant that the city’s amateur pianists travelled to London to play for Claudia, Mika and this week’s guest judge Jamie Cullum. Among them are Paul, whose right hand lost its function after a brain tumour operation at 17 – but he didn’t let it stop him.HRLove Island9pm, ITV2What a messy week it’s been! Lola and Sean gritted their teeth as they “willingly” left the villa before they could be dumped, while Ellie has also walked after days of tears. With the final imminent, surely it’s time for the couples to put on a united front?Hannah J DaviesUnacceptable9pm, TLCThe panel show that dares comedians to say the unsayable in front of a live audience continues. Captains Richard Ayoade and Joanne McNally are accomplished provocateurs – and joining them this week for full-frontal assaults on received wisdom are Alex Brooker, Kiell Smith-Bynoe, Lou Sanders and Sarah Keyworth.Phil HarrisonLive sportWorld Cup Football: Spain v Argentina, 7pm, BBC One/ITV1The final at New York/New Jersey Stadium.Explore more on these topicsTelevision & radioTV tonightfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: No car? No problem! Six cheap family days out in England by bus | Public transport trips | The Guardian
description: With bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and more
Author: Phoebe Taplin
Headline: With bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and more
Content: Norwich bus station is the starting point for trips to the Sainsbury Centre’s sculpture park and Norwich Castle.Photograph: Andrew Sparkes/AlamyView image in fullscreenNorwich bus station is the starting point for trips to the Sainsbury Centre’s sculpture park and Norwich Castle.Photograph: Andrew Sparkes/AlamyCar-free UKPublic transport tripsNo car? No problem! Six cheap family days out in England by busWith bus fares capped at £3 and free for kids in August, it’s the ideal time for wildlife watching, woodland walks, world-class art and morePhoebe TaplinSun 19 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTrips to waterfalls, gardens, galleries, medieval forests and prehistoric caves will make the long school holidays a lot more fun. To help families explore affordably, throughout Augustbuses in England will be freefor kids and adult fares will becapped at £3single. Or, if you arrive somewhere by train, you could buy aPlusBus ticket, which include unlimited local bus travel. Here are six ideas for family outings by bus around England.Great North Museum, NewcastleView image in fullscreenTynemouth Priory and Castle on a headland overlooking two beaches east of Newcastle.Photograph: Robert Harding/AlamyPainted mummy cases, fossilised dino teeth, a stuffed wombat, a fake merman … Newcastle’sGreat North Museum: Hancockis full of things kids might love – and entrance is free. Some of the impressive Roman altars are lit with colourful projections and there’s aplanetariumwith shows (from £2.95). This summer’s exhibitionTreasure: Hidden, Lost, Found(free, until 20 Sept) involves thoughtfully curated shiny stuff, from Roman coins to Viking silver unearthed across northern England. Lots of buses stop nearby, and abus ticket gets you 10% offin the cafe.This summer, there’s also aTales on the Tynetrail across Newcastle of giant Peter Rabbit sculptures and other, smaller “fluffles” to raise funds for St Oswald’s hospice. The waterfront hotelINNSiDE Newcastle, near the station, hosts a Tynetastic Peter sculpture, plus art and reading events. Its riversiderestauranthas a bambino menu and under-11s get free meals until 6 September. The hotel offersfamily-friendlyrooms (from £119 for four in August, room-only), activity hampers and a free soft-drink minibar.In stylish Ouseburn, a short bus ride away, theBiscuit Factorygallery hosts three further fluffles, freekids’ activities, rabbit-themed food and a roof terrace.Bus 306runs regularly all the way out toTynemouthfor an afternoon on the beach undercastle-topped cliffs. Back in the city, the art decoTyneside cinemahas selected films at £5 each week and kids’ classics over the summer.Forest fun near Hatfield,EssexView image in fullscreenThe boardwalk through Hatfield Forest’s ancient royal hunting ground.Photograph: PBP Vision/AlamyOpen every day and free to enter, the National Trust’s Hatfield Forest has 400 hectares (1,000 acres) of Frisbee-ready flowering fields and shady woodland. Incongruously close to Stansted airport, theforestwas once a royal hunting ground and is still home to hundreds of deer, some descended from Henry I’s original 12th-century herds. You can follow a boardwalk through twisting hornbeams, ramble across buttercup-carpeted meadows, build a den and hear yaffling woodpeckers.Bus 509from Bishop’s Stortford stops outside The Green Man, a short walk from the forest’s edge. Briefly follow the road signed Hatfield Forest and turn right up the path just after the bridge to find a map and gateway.The lakeside picnic tables and alfrescocafeare a delightful mile away, past cattle-grazed pastures busy with butterflies, and 1,000-year-old trees. With no car to get back to (parking costs £9), make this a linear walk.Loop through woodstoThremhall Prioryor follow theThree Forests wayfor4½ milesto the village of Hatfield Broad Oak and catchbus 305(not Sundays).In Bishop’s Stortford itself, there’s a riversidesplash pool and playgroundsnear the recently revamped castle mound.South Mill arts centrehas kids’crafts for £2andMuch Hadham Forge museumhas afree eventon 27 August, featuring a Tudor storyteller, to celebrate 450 years since their Elizabethan murals were painted. Half-timbered Much Hadham is 20 minutes from Stortford on hourlybus 35(not Sundays).Cave art inNottinghamshireView image in fullscreenPrehistoric rock art can be found at spectacular Cresswell Crags.Photograph: Steve Bramall/Getty Images/iStockphotoThe Welbeck Estate’swalking mapincludes an easy-going,sculpture-dotted trailtoCreswell Crags, where a bus ticket gets youfree entry to the museumand 10% off in the cafe. A stroll through the limestone gorge is spectacular, past ponds and caves where prehistoric humans lived and left rock art on the walls.Bus 209from Worksop stops every couple of hours (not Sundays) near theDukeries garden centreand Welbeck courtyard at the start of the trail.Welbeck has a freemuseumandgalleryin converted outbuildings. Highlights include the pearl earring Charles I was wearing when he was beheaded and a red chalkMadonna by Michelangelo. The award-winningHarley cafenext door serves local food (kids’ soup £5, cheese on toast £6).Half-price Harewood,WestYorkshireView image in fullscreenHarewood House is packed with kids’ activities – and art for the adults.Photograph: Tom ArberLike many big country houses,Harewoodusually charges adults well over £20 for a day’s worth of paintings, porcelain and landscaped gardens. But arrive on the frequentbus 36from Leeds or Harrogate, and you get50% off walk-up ticket prices. This gives you access to the art-filled house, theCapability Brown parklandand gardens with scented borders, waterfalls and bridges in thickets of bamboo.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionFor kids, there’s a woodedadventure playgroundwith zipwire and slides, atreehouseand awillow maze. They can also borrow a naturesafari bag, complete with map and compass, torch, binoculars and magnifying glass. And the summer holidays are packed withextra offers, all free with a ticket: den-building, gardening, statue-cleaning, dressing up, circus skills, and mid-Augustworkshops by Tibetan monks.Inside, there’s an exhibition of glamorous photos byCecil Beaton(until 4 October). Alternatively, a5-mile circular walkfrom the bus stop takes in deer, buzzards, rolling parkland and theMuddy Boots cafe. Half an hour away on the 36 bus, Leeds has the free five-storeyRoyal Armouries museumoverlooking the dock.Word-class art indoors and out in NorwichView image in fullscreenA family enjoys the outdoor sculpture trail at the Sainsbury Centre.Photograph: Kate WolstenholmeElisabeth Frink’sostrich-like structuresstalk through waterside woods. Antony Gormley’scast iron figuresstand poised on rooftops and walkways across the brutalist University of East Anglia (UEA) campus. Henry Moore’sbronze forms reclineon the lawns, and angular steelbeastsby Lynn Chadwick crouch under a model of VladimirTatlin’s tower. The freesculpture parkat theSainsbury Centreis packed with iconic names from 20th-century art and architecture and set in 140 hectares of parkland that are yours to explore, with centuries-old oaks, wild rabbits, and freefamily-friendly toursandtrails.Inside the Norman Foster-designed gallery (pay what you can), relaunched in 2023, the works are eclectic. You can wander from ancient Chinese tomb figures to a dynamic new installation via a work by Picasso. Kids might like the glass box you can stand in to“become a work of art”near John Davies’ hyper-realistic Bucket Man. The ambitioussummer exhibitionslook at the meaning of life and aspects of play. Show your bus ticket in the cafe for a10% discount.Bus 25from the station or city centre runs frequently to UEA and stops close to the Sainsbury Centre. It passes the huge square keep ofNorwich Castle, which reopened last year after a £27.5m renovation project. There are sound-and-light shows on the old stone walls of the Great Hall and a£2.50 twilight ticketif you visit an hour before closing time.Art trails, swimming spots and punt safaris, all easily accessible from Cambridge’s new train stationRead moreWildlife watching in the LakesView image in fullscreenThere’s dippers, dragonflies and red squirrels to be spotted at Aira Force waterfall in the Lake District.Photograph: Dave Porter/AlamyThe mossy, fern-fringed waterfalls atAira Forceare 45 minutes on bus508from Penrith station. The route runs beside Ullswater with views across the lake to bracken-cloaked Hallin Fell. It’s one of the trips in a new book onWhere to Watch Wildlife in Britain By Low-Carbon Transport, along with 100 other car-free trips to nature-rich sites across the UK.Look out for dippers, dragonflies and red squirrels. The area offers varied and spectacular walks, such as a pushchair- and mobility scooter-friendlystroll through the woodsat Pooley Bridge or a hike upGowbarrow Fell.Explore more on these topicsPublic transport tripsCar-free UKEngland holidaysWalkingUnited Kingdom holidaysYorkshire holidaysNottingham holidaysEssex holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: How to make Russian salad – recipe | Food | The Guardian
description: Cooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dish
Author: Felicity Cloake
Headline: Cooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dish
Content: Felicity Cloake's Russian salad.Photograph: Robert Billington/The Guardian. Food styling: Loïc Parisot.View image in fullscreenFelicity Cloake's Russian salad.Photograph: Robert Billington/The Guardian. Food styling: Loïc Parisot.Felicity Cloake's masterclassFoodHow to make Russian salad – recipeCooked vegetables, pickles and good mayonnaise are key components of this traditional but adaptable dishFelicity CloakeSun 19 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn much of the world, including Russia itself, this richly satisfying collation is known assalade olivier, after the 19th-century Belgian-born chef credited with its creation. Its wide popularity is testament to its adaptability and essential deliciousness: robust enough for picnics and packed lunches, it also keeps well and is great paired with just about anything, from fish to a simple green salad.Prep40 minCook15 minServes4For the salad200g waxy potatoes(ie, about 4 small ones)100g carrots(ie, 1 medium one)100g turnip(about ½ medium one, or 2 extra potatoes, if you prefer)Salt50g green beans2 eggs(optional)75g frozen peas2 large gherkins, plus 2 tbsp of their pickling liquid1 tbsp capers, rinsed if necessary50g ham, or pressed tongue or cooked chicken (optional, see step 9)1 small bunch dill, or chives, finely choppedFor the homemade mayonnaise (alternatively, use 3 tbsp ready-made)1 egg2 tsp dijon mustard1 generous pinch salt1 tbsp white-wine vinegar, or lemon juice180ml groundnut oil, or light olive or sunflower oil25ml extra-virgin olive oil(optional)1 A note on the ingredientsMayonnaise aside, the exact make-up of this salad is entirely up to you, though I would recommend including something starchy (potatoes are the usual base, but you might prefer to use sweet potato, parsnips or yam) and a selection of other flavours and textures, with a preference for cooked, rather than raw ingredients.View image in fullscreen2 Prep the root veg and beansStart by peeling the potatoes, carrot and turnip, and top and tail the beans. Cut the carrot and turnip into large chunks, but keep the potatoes whole, unless any are particularly large. Other common additions include cauliflower, broad beans and beetroot (though add the latter as a garnish at the end, or it will turn the mayonnaise pink).3 Boil the potatoesCooking each vegetable separately might seem a faff, but in such a simple dish it’s really worth doing to ensure that each element is perfect. Put the potatoes in a pan of cold salted water, bring to a boil and cook until tender; try not to overcook them or they’ll be hard to cut neatly. Drain and leave to dry in the hot pan.View image in fullscreen4 Cook the rest of the veg and eggsMeanwhile, bring another pan of salted water to a boil, drop in the beans and cook for two minutes. Scoop out into a large bowl filled with cold water (or the sink). In the same pan of boiling water, repeat with the carrots and turnip, then hard-boil the eggs for 10 minutes. Put the peas in a colander or sieve and pour boiling water over them to thaw.5 A note on the mayoThis salad is bound only by mayonnaise (a vegan one, if necessary), so it’s important that it tastes good. If there’s a jarred sort you like, by all means use that, but I really think it’s worth making from scratch for this dish. These days, I’m more likely to go for the stick-blender option than the traditional sort (though do feel free to get out the whisk if you wish).View image in fullscreen6 Start the mayoTo make stick-blender mayonnaise (this will give you more than you need here, but it keeps well), put the whole egg, mustard, salt and vinegar in a jar or glass that’s just wider than the head of the blender. Pour the lighter oil on top, leave for a minute to settle, then plunge in the blender so the head sits on the base of the jar.7 Finish the mayoTurn on to high speed and, without lifting the blender head, whizz until you see mayonnaise billowing out from the sides of the head. Gently tilt the jar this way and that, and gradually introduce more oil. Very slowly lift up the head of the blender through the oil until it’s all blended in. Whisk in the extra oil, taste and adjust the seasoning.View image in fullscreen8 Chop the veg and eggsDrain, dry and chop all the vegetables (except the peas, of course) into roughly 1cm dice or lengths, then mix these in a large bowl. Cut the gherkins (or other pickles) into 1cm dice and add to the bowl with two tablespoons of their pickling vinegar, the capers and peas. Peel the eggs (doing so underwater makes the process easier) and coarsely grate or dice into the salad.View image in fullscreen9 Finishing touchesCut the meat, if using, into strips (tofu or drained beans or pulses would be a good plant-based alternative; cooked fish or seafood such as brown shrimp, smoked trout or white crabmeat are also a pleasing addition). Finally, stir in the chopped herbs and about three tablespoons of the mayonnaise – you need only just enough to coat – then serve, or cover and store in the fridge.Explore more on these topicsFoodFelicity Cloake's masterclassSaladVegetablesPotatoesEggsHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Vieni, Birmingham – ‘A happy hubbub – you cannot pipe this in’ – restaurant review  | Food | The Guardian
description: Vieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often fail
Author: Grace Dent
Headline: Vieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often fail
Content: Vieni, Birmingham: ‘It isn’t remotely intimidating, and you are actually being fed’.Photograph: Jack Spicer Adams/The GuardianView image in fullscreenVieni, Birmingham: ‘It isn’t remotely intimidating, and you are actually being fed’.Photograph: Jack Spicer Adams/The GuardianGrace Dent on restaurantsFoodReviewVieni, Birmingham – ‘A happy hubbub – you cannot pipe this in’ – restaurant reviewVieni dances a fine line where British Italian restaurants often failGrace DentSun 19 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Sun 19 Jul 2026 05.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVieni, an Italian restaurant in the Jewellery Quarter of Birmingham, is small, Sicilian and scrappy. The Instagram bio of its chef and founderAngelina Adamoreads, “Bite off more than you can chew and learn how to fucking chew”, which feels like a battle cry for almost everyone stepping into hospitality, especially independent places like Vieni, where the waters are choppy and your skillset needs to be varied. Arancini with one hand, signing off payroll and ordering loo paper with the other.Vieni, which is about a mile or so from the city’s Bull Ring, is a million miles fromAlbert’s Schloss, Big Mamma’sLa Bellezzaand the multitude of vast, impersonal pleasure machines that have spread their legs in Brum city centre. Of course, a giganticRosa’sserving pad thai andTattuwith a plastic purple flower ceiling all have their place, and make people happy, but I have joy in my heart for places like Vieni.View image in fullscreen‘Hearty, not trendy’: Vieni’s barbecued whole sea bass, with courgette and basilPrior to this opening, Adamo was running the kitchen atThe Circle Loungeinside Birmingham Hippodrome, serving weary thesps and parents seeking refuge from Robin Hood the pantomime, with plates of marvellous arancini. She has now moved to this tiny, beloved nook on the edge of the newly emergingGoodsyarddevelopment, serving coils of Sicilian fennel sausage withcacio e pepebutter beans, fregola with clams, and an eccentric, ever-changingdolceof the day that is worth the train fare alone. Note: many property developers claim to be building in the quaintly named Jewellery Quarter but are actually miles away; Vieni is just beside the tiny station. En route I bought vintage clip-on earrings from a man in a bullet-proof kiosk, while on the corner a sandwich board offered gold grills for your teeth from £60, with free moulding and postage.Vieni’s menu is hearty, not trendy – it’s not glorified picky bits and puzzling sharing plates. It is, instead, 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne in slices as a starter; Pyefleet oysters with British strawberry and champagne vinegar; seabass crudo with peaches, basil and green chilli; and ruffled ribbons ofmafaldinewith vodka tomato sauce. We snuck in on a Saturday at around half past four, myself and the weary Charles, and grabbed a window table for a Sicilian Slapper, draught Poretti and Limonata Radler, and a glass of Luscombe’s lightly sparkling rhubarb pop. Soon we were ordering a round of 1946 gildas and a plate of majoram polenta bites. We’d popped in for snacks; now we were begging them to serve us dinner.I needed one of the pea, broad bean and pecorino arancini that Adamo is so adept at; these aren’t your piddly quail’s egg-sized arancini so popular in Britain. This was a magnificent object, perched in a puddle of tomato sauce and heaped with pecorino. Next up, that 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne: lots of depth, lots of softness. The ultimate cuddle on a plate.View image in fullscreen‘An ultimate cuddle on a plate’: Vieni’s 32-hour ox cheek crispy lasagne.Vieni has that thing that pleases me most in restaurants: a happy hubbub. You cannot pipe this in or orchestrate it. A happy restaurant hubbub manifests when diners are genuinely happy with their s’mores toasted marshmallow gelato, or, as we had, a large barbecued sea bream with peperoncino ’nduja butter. Diners were chuffed with their burrata, melon and Brummie honey, littered with mint and pistachio, or their whole globe artichoke with hazelnut pangrattato. This wasn’t likeKawan, who you may remember from last week, where the atmosphere was of people who had been very politely mugged.View image in fullscreenDolce of the day: Vieni’s Sicilian lemon and  vanilla cannolo.Vieni dances a fine line where British-Italian restaurants often fail. It’s not the Spaghetti House or a red-sauce mum-and-pop restaurant; it sits closer in spirit toMoroon Exmouth Market, London, but it isn’t remotely intimidating and you are actually being fed. The menu is ever changing – that dolce of the day, a s’mores toasted marshmallow gelato when we visited, for example, seems now to be a maraschino cherry gelato with a lattice shortcrust. That’s one of the sexiest things I’ve written this year.Grab a table. Bite off more than you can chew. Then eat the bloody lot.Vieni, 41 Pitsford Street, Goodsyard, B18 6LJ, 0121 806 0080; vieni.co.uk. Open lunch Weds–Sat noon-2pm, dinner Tue-Sat 5pm-9pm, plus brunch Sat 9.30am-11.45am (10am-2pm Sun). About £50 a head for three courses, plus drinks and service.Explore more on these topicsFoodGrace Dent on restaurantsRestaurantsfeaturesShareReuse this content
